An automated 6-stage verification pipeline converts free-form questions into ground-truth answers: (1) task normalization, (2) context injection from scene metadata, (3) LLM-based entity extraction, (4) canonicalization and label sanitization, (5) rule-based rejection, (6) deterministic geometry execution (point-cloud operators, camera-pose arithmetic, depth projection). The Spatial-Grounded Policy Co-Evolution mechanism trains a single Qwen2.5-VL policy under GRPO. In Round 1, the model acts as a Questioner, generating observation-question pairs rewarded by DGE validity (f_valid) × observation quality (f_obs, scored by a GPT-OSS-120B text judge) plus format compliance. In Round 2, the same model acts as a Solver: for valid questions, reward is accuracy against DGE ground truth; for invalid questions (which are recycled rather than discarded), reward is explanation quality for the invalidity reason — converting all rollouts into learning signal. A task-adaptive scheduler maintains per-task historical accuracy ā_k, sets sampling weight w_k = max(δ, 1 − ā_k) with δ = 0.05, and normalizes over the feasible task set, producing curriculum emergence without manual difficulty sequencing. Three critical design decisions: (i) shared questioner/solver parameters so solver gradients improve visual perception and questioner gradients deepen geometric reasoning — this mutual benefit is well-motivated by the shared spatial representation; (ii) invalid questions yield explanation rewards rather than being discarded — justified by ablation; (iii) observation quality is scored by a text-only judge on the textual description, not a vision model, reducing system complexity — a reasonable but unvalidated simplification. Key Results Experiments run on Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct using ScanNet, ScanNet++, and ARKitScenes (~4K scenes, 4,365 training contexts). Training uses 1 node × 8 H100 (80 GB) GPUs. Evaluation spans nine benchmarks: VSI-Bench (primary), EmbSpatial, ViewSpatial, RealWorldQA, V-STAR, SpatialViz, STARE, CoreCognition, MMStar. SpatialEvo achieves the highest average across all nine benchmarks at both scales: 51.1 (3B) and 54.7 (7B). On VSI-Bench, SpatialEvo scores 39.2 (3B) and 46.1 (7B), outperforming SpatialLadder (35.4, 3B; 45.4, 7B) and SpaceR (36.0, 3B; 36.8, 7B). General capability is preserved: MMStar scores 55.2 (3B) and 62.5 (7B), close to the untuned baseline (47.5 / 52.1 averaged) and notably above SpatialLadder (45.8 / 54.9). The most counter-intuitive finding is that annotation-dependent baselines (SpatialLadder, ViLaSR) collapse on V-STAR to ~36, far below the baseline of 74.9 / 78.5, indicating static training introduces distributional shift that destroys video spatio-temporal generalization — a degradation SpatialEvo avoids. Ablation confirms the DGE physical grounding is the dominant contributor: removing it drops average to 49.6 and VSI-Bench to 18.8 for 7B. Adaptive scheduling vs. uniform sampling: 46.1 vs. 44.4 at Iter 4 on VSI-Bench, with the scheduler up-weighting Rel. Dir. (21.8%) and Rel. Dist. (18.7%) and down-weighting Room Area (12.5%) and Obj. Size (13.4%). Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The DGE as a geometric oracle for self-evolving VLM training is genuinely novel — no prior work has operationalized deterministic physical computation as the reward signal in a spatial VLM self-play loop. The co-evolution mechanism under DGE constraints and the recycling of invalid questions as explanation-reward signal are also new. The GRPO backbone and shared-parameter questioner/solver pattern draw on existing self-play literature (VisPlay, V-Zero, MM-Zero), but the deterministic grounding is the differentiating contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough: three architectural dimensions (w/o Questioner, w/o Solver, w/o Physical Grounding, w/o Adaptive Scheduler) and two reward-component dimensions (w/o Validity Reward, w/o Observation Reward, w/o Explanation Reward) are each tested on the full 9-benchmark suite. No error bars or multiple-seed results are reported — this is a common omission in large-scale VLM RL papers but weakens confidence in margin sizes. Code is released on GitHub and model weights on Hugging Face (both linked on the first page). Hyperparameters are fully tabulated (Tables 8 and 9). The auxiliary GPT-OSS-120B judge used to score observation quality at training time is a dependency that could create reproducibility issues if that model changes. The paper is an arXiv preprint (April 2026); results are not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (system + experiments). - Practical: Training requires 8× H100 (80 GB) GPUs and high-fidelity indoor 3D assets (point clouds, calibrated camera poses); inference uses standard RGB images only, which is commercially viable, but constructing the DGE for a new environment requires the full 3D reconstruction pipeline. - Efficient: Online GRPO training to ~20K unique QA pairs (~12 epochs equivalent); no SFT warm-start is used by default, which saves a training stage; the auxiliary GPT-OSS-120B judge is called at every training step for observation scoring, representing a non-trivial inference cost not quantified in the paper. - Effective: Evidence across nine benchmarks and two model scales, with ablations confirming each component's contribution, credibly shows the method solves the stated problem of geometry-grounded self-evolution for indoor 3D spatial reasoning. - Scalable: Not tested beyond 7B parameters; the DGE is inherently limited to scenes with dense point clouds and calibrated poses, restricting applicability to ScanNet-family indoor environments; outdoor, egocentric, or dynamic scenes are explicitly excluded and untested. The work delivers a software artifact (code + trained model weights released). Strengths - The DGE paradigm cleanly eliminates the systematic bias that afflicts all model-consensus self-evolving methods; the ablation where removing physical grounding drops VSI-Bench from 46.1 to 18.8 is a sharp, credible validation of the core claim. - Recycling invalid questions as explanation-reward signal is an underappreciated design that converts geometrically unsolvable rollouts — normally wasted compute — into productive supervision for constraint learning. - The task-adaptive scheduler's emergent curriculum (hardest categories receive more weight without manual prescription) is demonstrated quantitatively across four iterative rounds with and without the scheduler, showing sustained monotonic improvement with the scheduler versus stagnation without it. - General capability is preserved (MMStar 62.5 at 7B) while static-dataset baselines collapse on out-of-distribution benchmarks (V-STAR ~36), suggesting the DGE-grounded approach avoids the catastrophic forgetting and distribution shift that plague annotation-dependent fine-tuning. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly confront three limitations: (1) dependency on high-fidelity 3D assets confines the framework to static indoor scenes with complete point cloud reconstructions and calibrated camera poses, excluding outdoor and dynamic environments; (2) sensitivity to entity parsing quality — LLM extraction errors propagate through the verification pipeline; (3) sensitivity to point cloud quality — reconstruction artifacts degrade continuous-valued tasks (absolute distance, object size). These are confronted honestly and without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All 16 DGE tasks are metric or topological geometric predicates; purely semantic spatial relations (affordance, navigability, containment, region membership) are absent, so the claim of general 3D spatial intelligence remains partial. - Several evaluation benchmarks (VSI-Bench, EmbSpatial) draw from the same ScanNet scene family used for training, creating potential distribution overlap; the paper does not analyze held-out scene splits specifically. - The observation quality judge is GPT-OSS-120B, a third-party model invoked at every training step; no ablation tests a weaker or open-weight judge, so the sensitivity of the method to this auxiliary model is unknown. - Only 3B and 7B model scales are tested; the self-evolving curriculum is justified partly by scalability arguments, but no experiment tests whether the gains hold or shrink at 13B or larger. - The SpatialLadder baseline is reproduced under SpatialEvo's training setup, but SpaceR and Spatial-SSRL are evaluated using their authors' released checkpoints without retraining — a minor but real methodological asymmetry in the comparison. Bottom Line SpatialEvo makes a clean, well-ablated, and reproducible contribution to self-evolving spatial VLM training; the DGE paradigm is the right idea and the experiments credibly support it, making this required reading for anyone training embodied or spatial VLMs — with the important caveat that practical deployment remains limited to environments where dense 3D reconstructions are available.
Recommended 2604.14144 PDF
#18 — Highly Innovative (77)

Understanding Evolving Graph Structures for Large Discrete-Time Dynamic Graph Representation

vldb.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (85) is the standout — thorough ablations across 8 datasets, 10-run mean±std, PVLDB peer review, public code, ablations of 5 structural variants plus pipeline. Novelty (70) — principled integration of influence-score neighbor store replacing adjacency matrices. Completeness (80) and Resilience (75) solid given formal theorems and honest gaps (transductive-only, ρ tuning). Impact (80) — only method scaling to Stack-Overflow on single GPU, released GitHub artifact, 94× over TGL. Non-Obviousness (70) for the structural-cohesiveness+recency+path-decay unified score. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*85+0.15*70+0.15*80+0.15*75+0.10*80 ≈ 77.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 85 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 80 Resilience 75 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces UnderGS, a scalable GPU-resident framework for discrete-time dynamic graph (DTDG) representation that replaces adjacency-matrix neighbor management with a temporal-cohesive neighbor store, achieving up to 9× training speed-up and 31.36% average accuracy improvement over nine baselines across eight datasets. - Why it's important: Adjacency-matrix-based neighbor management for DTDGs consumes O(T|V|²) memory, prevents GPU-native batch processing, and captures only binary connectivity — making existing methods unable to scale to graphs with millions of edges or to faithfully represent structural evolution across snapshots. - How it does it: A GPU key-value store retains the top-K neighbors per node scored by a composite temporal influence score combining structural cohesiveness (node importance via subgraph density), path-length decay (α₁^(l-1)), and snapshot recency decay (α₂^(t-t')), together with a late-snapshot gradient aggregation mechanism that prevents information leakage during batch training. - The good: UnderGS is the only evaluated method that scales to the Stack-Overflow (2.6M nodes, 63M edges, 92 snapshots) and Wiki-Talk (1.1M nodes, 7.8M edges) datasets without out-of-memory errors, while simultaneously outperforming all baselines on both AUC and MRR tasks. - The missing: All experiments run on a single RTX 3090 (24 GB); the paper makes no claim about multi-GPU performance, and scalability beyond this hardware — or behavior on graphs with denser temporal dynamics than the tested datasets — is unevaluated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and engineers working on DTDG representation learning, temporal GNNs, or scalable graph ML infrastructure; it provides a principled, hardware-aware alternative to adjacency-matrix neighbor management with strong empirical evidence. Problem & Motivation: Discrete-time dynamic graphs model systems (retail, finance, social networks) that evolve through discrete snapshots, where each snapshot is a static graph. The central challenge for representation learning is neighbor management: existing approaches either maintain a full adjacency matrix per snapshot — consuming O(T|V|²) memory and restricting scalability — or preprocess multiple adjacency matrices into node sequences for sequence models, incurring hours of preprocessing and CPU–GPU transfer overhead (e.g., FALCON requires 2.2 hours on Reddit-title). Both lines fail simultaneously: snapshot-focused GNN approaches lose cross-snapshot temporal context, while sequence-based approaches lose intra-snapshot structural context. Neither line scales to graphs with millions of nodes or hundreds of snapshots because they cannot batch-process efficiently on a single GPU. The gap is a neighbor management strategy that captures structural evolution, preserves temporal context, and fits in GPU memory without preprocessing. Key Idea: The core insight is to replace the adjacency matrix with a GPU-resident key-value store that, for each node, retains exactly K neighbors selected by a temporal influence score — a single scalar that fuses three quantities: structural importance of the neighbor (cohesiveness of its local subgraph), path-length discount (closer hops score higher), and snapshot recency discount (more recent interactions score higher). Because the store caps at K entries per node, total memory drops from O(T|V|²) to O(|V|K), independent of snapshot count. The store is maintained incrementally as new snapshots arrive: L-hop traversals compute influence scores for candidates; new neighbors displace incumbents if their scores are higher; a snapshot decay pass reduces all stored scores at snapshot boundaries. This design allows direct GPU access for message passing without preprocessing, and the influence score serves as an aggregation weight replacing degree normalization. Methodology: UnderGS consists of four interacting components. (1) Temporal influence score: For node vᵢ(t) and neighbor vⱼ(t') interacting over l hops, Iᵢⱼ(t) = Cⱼ(t') · α₁^(l-1) · α₂^(t-t'), where Cⱼ(t') is the node importance defined as a sum of exponential terms over the interaction counts within vⱼ's subgraph, α₁ ∈ (0,1) is the path-decay hyperparameter, and α₂ ∈ (0,1) is the snapshot-decay factor. Node importance distinguishes structurally cohesive neighbors from peripheral ones, which degree alone cannot. The paper proves three formal properties: Snapshot Decay (Theorem 4.6 — more recent neighbors are preferred when structurally comparable), Cohesive Priority (Theorem 4.7 — neighbors in cohesive subgraphs are retained over sparser ones), and Path Decay (Theorem 4.8 — direct neighbors score higher than equidistant distant ones). (2) Temporal-cohesive neighbor store: A GPU key-value store with O(1) lookup and update per node; initialized from scratch and updated incrementally per batch or snapshot; reduces storage from O(T|V|²) to O(|V|K). K=16 and L=2 in all experiments. (3) Time-aware filter: For nodes with sparse current-snapshot interactions, historical neighbors can dominate. A Bernoulli mask with probability ρ (tuned by dataset density: ρ=0.3 for high density, ρ=0.5 for low density) randomly zeros out historical neighbor influence scores before aggregation, shifting focus toward current interactions. (4) Late-snapshot gradient aggregation: Each snapshot G(t) is partitioned into B(t) mini-batches; losses are computed independently per batch using binary cross-entropy on link prediction labels, but gradients are accumulated across all batches of the snapshot and applied once after the full snapshot is processed (θ ← θ − η · (1/B(t)) ΣᵦᵢB(t) ∇L^b_{G(t)}). This prevents later batches from being influenced by parameter updates from earlier batches within the same snapshot (information leakage). The framework is compatible with six neural network architectures (GCN, GIN, GAT, GraphSAGE, MLP Mixer, Transformer), using influence scores as aggregation weights in lieu of degree normalization; message embedding Mᵢⱼ(t) = [Zⱼ(t-1) ‖ eᵢⱼ(t') ‖ φ(t-t')]Wₘ concatenates node embedding, edge features, and a time encoding. Key Results: Evaluated on 8 datasets spanning multiple domains and scales (UCI-Message: 1,899 nodes; Stack-Overflow: 2.6M nodes, 63M edges, 92 snapshots). Baselines are 9 models: 4 GNN-based (EvolveGCN-H, EvolveGCN-O, Roland, WinGNN) and 5 sequence-based (DGNN-LSTM, DGNN-GRU, DGNN-Transformer, SimpleDyG, FALCON). All experiments use a single GeForce RTX 3090 (24 GB RAM). Key quantitative outcomes: (1) AUC (link prediction): UnderGS achieves best results on 7/8 datasets; up to 13.56% improvement over the best baseline; e.g., 99.97% AUC on Bitcoin-OTC vs. 99.75% for second-best. Only Roland is competitive on small datasets. (2) MRR (link ranking): UnderGS achieves best on all 8 datasets; average 59.20% improvement; e.g., 95.01% MRR on Bitcoin-Alpha vs. 36.20% for second-best (SimpleDyG). (3) Training efficiency: Up to 9× overall speed-up vs. best baseline; up to 6× per-epoch speed-up. (4) Scalability: 4 of 9 baselines (DGNN variants, FALCON) OOM on 5+ datasets; only UnderGS completes all 8 including Stack-Overflow and Wiki-Talk. (5) vs. TGL (multi-GPU framework): UnderGS achieves 94× per-epoch sampling speed-up, 10× overall training speed-up, and 24.07% better AUC+MRR on average — while running on a single GPU. Ablation (Table 3): removing snapshot decay drops MRR by up to 82.30%; removing path decay drops by up to 26.02%; replacing node importance with k-core drops MRR by 9.29% on average; the time-aware filter contributes 4.35% on average. Training pipeline ablation (Table 4): pipeline yields 6.93–10.40× overall speed-up and 3.65–10.44× per-epoch speed-up vs. snapshot-by-snapshot training. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The temporal influence score combining structural cohesiveness, path decay, and snapshot decay as a unified neighbor selection criterion is genuinely new; the GPU-resident incremental neighbor store that replaces adjacency matrices in GNN training is a novel infrastructure contribution; the late-snapshot gradient aggregation addressing information leakage in DTDG mini-batch training is a targeted engineering advance. The individual components (influence-weighted aggregation, GPU key-value stores, gradient accumulation) are known; the novelty is their integration into a principled, theoretically grounded neighbor management framework for DTDGs. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough: 5 structural variants tested across all 8 datasets (Table 3); training pipeline ablation on 2 datasets (Table 4); compatibility tested across 6 neural networks with and without influence scores (Figures 6–7). Experiments are repeated 10 times and mean ± standard deviation is reported. Code and data are publicly released at https://github.com/dani0108/UnderGS (PVLDB Artifact Availability badge confirmed). The paper was published in PVLDB Vol. 19, No. 5, 2026 (peer-reviewed). Hyperparameters (K=16, L=2, α₁=0.1, α₂=0.05, filter ratio 40%) are reported; ρ is density-dependent (ρ=0.3 or 0.5) which introduces a tuning step not fully automated. The embedding dimension is fixed at 128 and batch size at 200 for all models, ensuring fair comparison. Theoretical proofs for the three core properties are deferred to a technical report (Sections 2.1 and 2.2 thereof); the main paper contains proof sketches, which limits independent verification from the paper alone. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (built framework evaluated by deployment on real datasets). - Practical: Runs on a single commodity GPU (RTX 3090, 24 GB) on all 8 tested datasets including the 63M-edge Stack-Overflow graph. - Efficient: Neighbor store reduces memory from O(T|V|²) to O(|V|K); eliminates preprocessing (2.2 hours for FALCON on Reddit-title); achieves 6–9× training speed-up over the best baselines; per-epoch sampling is 94× faster than TGL. - Effective: Outperforms all 9 baselines on AUC (7/8 datasets) and MRR (8/8 datasets) with statistically meaningful margins (error bars reported); ablations confirm each component contributes materially, not just the aggregate. - Scalable: Tested at up to 2.6M nodes and 63M edges (Stack-Overflow), 7.8M edges with 324 snapshots (Wiki-Talk); no experiment exceeds this scale; behavior at 10×–100× scale on billion-edge graphs is not tested, though the O(|V|K) theoretical bound is favorable. The paper delivers a software artifact (GitHub repository with source code and data). Strengths: - The temporal influence score is principled and theoretically grounded with three proven properties (Theorems 4.6–4.8), giving the neighbor selection mechanism a formal foundation absent in prior adjacency-matrix approaches. - The scalability gap versus competitors is substantial and not marginal: UnderGS is the only method completing all 8 datasets on a single GPU, including graphs where every alternative OOMs; the 94× sampling speed-up over TGL (a multi-GPU system) is the most striking empirical result. - Compatibility with six heterogeneous neural architectures (message-passing and non-message-passing) without architectural modifications demonstrates genuine generality of the neighbor store abstraction. - The late-snapshot gradient aggregation addresses a concrete information-leakage failure mode in DTDG batch training that prior work ignores, and ablation (Table 4) shows it yields 6.93–10.40× speed-up over snapshot-by-snapshot training. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper does not include a dedicated limitations section. The conclusion notes that UnderGS targets single-GPU settings, contrasting it with multi-GPU approaches; no explicit failure modes or scope boundaries are stated. The density-dependent filter probability ρ is acknowledged implicitly as a dataset-specific hyperparameter but not discussed as a limitation. The authors do not discuss behavior on highly skewed degree distributions (power-law graphs), adversarially structured temporal graphs, or directed weighted graphs beyond those tested. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Hyperparameter sensitivity for ρ: The time-aware filter probability is set per dataset based on an undisclosed density threshold (10⁻⁴). The paper does not report a sensitivity analysis for ρ, leaving unclear how sensitive performance is to this choice and how a practitioner would set it without labeled validation data. - Single-hardware evaluation: All experiments use one specific GPU. The O(|V|K) memory claim is theoretically favorable, but the paper does not demonstrate behavior when the neighbor store itself exceeds GPU memory (e.g., billion-node graphs), nor does it discuss CPU-offload strategies for this regime. - Transductive setting only: All downstream tasks (link prediction, link ranking) are evaluated on edges within the training graph's node set. Inductive generalization to entirely unseen nodes at inference time is not evaluated, limiting applicability to dynamic graphs with frequent node arrivals. - Theoretical proof completeness: The three key theorems are proved in a separate technical report not included in the PVLDB submission; the main paper contains only proof sketches, which a peer reviewer cannot independently verify from the paper alone. - No comparison to hyperbolic or geometry-aware DTDG methods (e.g., the authors' own prior work on hyperbolic temporal graphs, refs [23, 47]) which have shown strong performance on sparse social graphs — an omission that slightly narrows the baseline comparison. Bottom Line: A practitioner building DTDG representation learning pipelines gains a concretely useful contribution here — UnderGS offers a principled, GPU-efficient replacement for adjacency-matrix neighbor management with strong empirical support across 8 diverse datasets and publicly released code, making it the current method of choice for single-GPU DTDG training at scale.
Search Results
#19 — Highly Innovative (76)

EdgeFlow: Fast Cold Starts for LLMs on Mobile Devices

Yongsheng Yan, Jiacheng Shen, Xuchuan Luo, Yangfan Zhou · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (80) is the driver — concrete C++ runtime delivering 4.07× TTFT reduction on commodity Android hardware, with promised open-source release. Rigor (78) strong: clean component-isolating ablations, NPU-constraint-aware baseline augmentation, WikiText-2 perplexity comparisons across multiple quantization schemes; reduced for single-device evaluation, no error bars, and preprint status. Novelty (72) solid: co-design of NPU-constraint-aware adaptive quantization + SIMD packing + fine-grained CPU-NPU pipeline is genuinely new. Non-Obviousness (75) good — the finding that quantization alone yields only 3.3% TTFT improvement (packing is the bottleneck) is non-obvious. Completeness (78) and Resilience (72) strong with honest limitation acknowledgment around Hexagon-specificity and modest decoding gains. Weighted: 0.15*72 + 0.15*78 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*72 + 0.30*80 ≈ 76.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 78 Resilience 72 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EdgeFlow, a mobile LLM inference framework that reduces cold-start latency (time-to-first-token, TTFT) by up to 4.07× through co-designed NPU-aware adaptive quantization, a SIMD-friendly weight packing format, and a fine-grained dynamic CPU-NPU pipeline. - Why it's important: Mobile LLMs like Llama3 8B incur 9.1-second TTFT on state-of-the-art NPU frameworks during cold starts, exceeding the ~7s user tolerance threshold and making conversational LLM applications on phones practically unusable. - How it does it: The core mechanism assigns different bit-widths to per-output-channel weight groups based on their importance (cosine-distance relative error), respecting Hexagon NPU quantization constraints, then packs those mixed-precision weights into a SIMD-register-aligned stripe layout for fast unpacking, and schedules CPU/NPU operators at individual-operator granularity with dynamic task stealing. - The good: EdgeFlow reduces TTFT by 4.07× over llama.cpp and 1.63× over the augmented llm.npu baseline at comparable model accuracy (-0.03% average at 5-bit), with ablations cleanly isolating the contribution of each component. - The missing: All evaluation is on a single device (Xiaomi 15 Pro, Snapdragon 8 Elite), covering models up to 8B parameters, with no error bars on latency measurements — generalizability to other NPU vendors, device tiers, or larger models is undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Essential reading for mobile systems engineers targeting NPU-accelerated LLM inference on Android; less relevant if the deployment target is cloud, server, or non-Hexagon hardware. Problem & Motivation Deploying LLMs on mobile devices for privacy and offline access is increasingly viable due to mobile NPUs (e.g., Qualcomm Hexagon), but existing inference frameworks suffer from severe cold-start latency — the delay incurred when an application invokes an LLM whose weights are not resident in DRAM and must be loaded from flash. On llm.npu (ASPLOS 2025), the TTFT for Llama3 8B under 128 input tokens reaches 9.1 seconds. Prior work on mobile LLM inference focused on inference throughput during execution, not on the cold-start loading path. The root bottleneck identified in this paper is suboptimal flash bandwidth utilization: existing frameworks uniformly quantize all weights to a single precision (e.g., INT8) to meet NPU hardware constraints, wasting bandwidth on unimportant weights. Memory prefetching approaches (e.g., reserving 4 GB in advance) can hide this latency but impose 25% of total DRAM as overhead on constrained devices. Key Idea The key insight is that per-output-channel weight importance varies substantially, so assigning lower bit-widths to unimportant channels reduces the bytes transferred from flash without proportionally degrading accuracy — but only if the quantization scheme respects NPU hardware constraints (symmetric uniform quantization, per-output-channel granularity for weights, per-tensor for activations). The activation-to-weight variance transfer (NPU-aware smoothing) and a SIMD-register-aligned packing format are the enabling mechanisms that make this insight deployable without dequantization overhead canceling the bandwidth savings. Methodology EdgeFlow has two phases. In the offline phase, NPU-aware adaptive quantization uses a relative error (RE) metric defined as the cosine distance between a weight channel and its quantized-dequantized version, approximated analytically as RE(W, B) = (max|W|²) / (2^(2B) · E[W²]), avoiding explicit quantization of all channels at all bit-widths. A greedy O(C log C) bit-width allocation algorithm minimizes total RE subject to a budget constraint on average bit-width (1–8 bits per channel), provably yielding the global optimum because RE is additive and strictly decreasing with more bits. NPU-aware smoothing transfers per-channel activation variance to weights via diagonal scaling matrices (fused into adjacent normalization/linear operators at no runtime cost), making the per-tensor activation quantization constraint tractable. The resulting mixed-precision weights are stored in a SIMD-friendly packing format: each weight group is decomposed into primitive weightlets (1-, 2-, and 4-bit components), stored in an interleaved stripe layout that allows ARM Neon 128-bit registers to unpack up to 8 weightlets in parallel with 3 SIMD operations (AND, SHIFT, OR), averaging 0.48 SIMD instructions per weight. In the online phase, a synergistic granular pipeline performs fine-grained operator-level (not subgraph-level) CPU/NPU placement — all INT8 matmuls to NPU, all normalization/activation/residual operators to CPU in FP16. Dynamic operator scheduling uses position-guided priority (earlier prompt chunks get higher priority, reducing NPU bubbles by unlocking downstream tasks sooner) and CPU task stealing (CPU proactively executes NPU-queued tasks when NPU queue length exceeds a threshold of 5, determined by CPU/NPU matmul time ratio). Critical design decisions: (1) per-output-channel granularity is hardware-constrained and well-justified; (2) the greedy allocation's optimality relies on additive independence of per-channel RE — a local proxy that is theoretically sound but may diverge from end-to-end accuracy at very aggressive bit-widths; (3) the task-stealing threshold is heuristically set. Key Results All experiments run on a Xiaomi 15 Pro (Snapdragon 8 Elite SoC, 16 GB RAM, 512 GB flash). Models tested: Llama3 8B, Mistral 7B, Phi3 3.8B, Qwen1.5 1.8B. Benchmarks: LAMBADA, WinoGrande, OpenBookQA, MMLU, HellaSwag. Baselines: llama.cpp (CPU, mmap), MNN (CPU, sequential load), llm.npu augmented with materialization and overlapping (ASPLOS 2025 baseline). At 4-bit average: EdgeFlow reduces TTFT by 4.07× vs. llama.cpp, 2.53× vs. MNN, 1.63× vs. llm.npu+. At 7-bit: 3.32×, 2.28×, 1.47×, respectively. At 5-bit default configuration (accuracy comparable to llm.npu INT8, -0.03% average): TTFT speedup is 1.55× over llm.npu+. WikiText-2 perplexity at 5-bit on Llama3 8B: EdgeFlow 17.27 vs. CMPQ 18.22 vs. SmoothQuant 99.68 vs. shadow outlier >10k. Ablation (Llama3 8B, Phi3 3.8B): quantization alone reduces TTFT by only 3.3% over INT8 (packing bottleneck remains); adding SIMD packing reduces TTFT by up to 1.36×; adding granular pipeline reduces TTFT by a further 3.5% (short prompts) to 12.7% (long prompts). Packing format: 1.42× loading speedup over INT4/INT8 mixed format; 6.17× unpacking speedup over K-Quant. Pipeline: position-guided priority lowers NPU bubble rate by 82.2%; task stealing further reduces execution time by 11.3% and CPU bubble rate by 61.6%. Decoding efficiency: 1.13× speedup on Qwen1.5 1.8B, 1.00× on Mistral 7B vs. llm.npu. Energy during cold start: 12.9% lower total, but 17.6% higher average power (due to CPU task stealing). Accuracy comparison vs. SmoothQuant, shadow outlier, CMPQ across 4–7 bits on Llama3 8B: EdgeFlow improves by 16.55–38.23%, 13.72–63.13%, and 0.67–13.55%, respectively. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The co-design of NPU-hardware-constraint-aware adaptive quantization with a SIMD-friendly packing format and fine-grained dynamic CPU-NPU scheduling for mobile cold-start optimization is genuinely novel; individual components (mixed-precision quantization, SIMD unpacking, CPU-GPU pipeline balancing) exist in prior work, but explicit NPU constraint modeling as a first-class design constraint — and the integration of all three for cold-start specifically — has not been done before. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and clearly structured (Fig 11, Fig 14), isolating each of the three components; this is a credibility strength. Quantization baselines (SmoothQuant, CMPQ) are adapted to NPU constraints before comparison, avoiding unfair advantages. No error bars or variance across runs are reported for TTFT, which is a significant omission for a latency paper where thermal throttling and flash controller variability matter. Evaluation is on one physical device. Code is not yet released (paper promises open source "in the near future"). This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.09083v1); results are not peer-reviewed. No multiple-seed reporting for accuracy benchmarks. Deployability (4-property check) SYSTEMS profile (built C++ runtime + Python quantization utilities, ~8K lines of code, evaluated by deployment on physical hardware). - Practical: Runs on commercially available Android hardware (Xiaomi 15 Pro, Snapdragon 8 Elite) using Qualcomm QNN SDK; no specialized hardware required. - Efficient: Achieves 12.9% lower cold-start energy vs. llm.npu while reducing TTFT by 1.55× at 5-bit; the 17.6% higher instantaneous power from CPU task stealing is offset by shorter execution time. - Effective: Evidence directly demonstrates TTFT reduction on the stated problem (cold start TTFT) with accuracy within -0.03% of the INT8 baseline, not just a proxy metric. - Scalable: Tested only on models ≤8B parameters on one high-end device; behavior on 13B+ models, lower-memory devices (<8 GB RAM), or flash storage with different bandwidth characteristics is unverified and not discussed. The system delivers a software artifact (C++ runtime + Python tooling) but is not yet open-sourced. Strengths - The NPU-constraint-aware quantization formulation is principled: the relative error metric has a closed-form approximation, the greedy algorithm has a provable optimality guarantee under the additive RE assumption, and NPU-aware smoothing absorbs activation variance without runtime cost. - The SIMD-friendly packing format directly eliminates both the read amplification problem of INT4/INT8 mixed format and the unpacking bottleneck of K-Quant, achieving a superior latency-accuracy Pareto point (3.05× average improvement in combined load+unpack time). - Ablations are unusually honest: the result that quantization alone gives only a 3.3% TTFT improvement (because packing is the bottleneck) is a non-obvious negative finding that the authors report transparently. - Baseline comparisons are strengthened by augmenting llm.npu with materialization and overlapping before comparison, ruling out the most obvious catch-up optimizations. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note that EdgeFlow targets Qualcomm Hexagon NPU and that adapting to other platforms (Apple Neural Engine, MediaTek) requires engineering effort. They acknowledge that decoding efficiency gains are modest (1.13× on Qwen1.5 1.8B, 1.00× on Mistral 7B) because decoding was not the primary optimization target. They report the 17.6% higher average power from CPU task stealing. These limitations are confronted directly and framed appropriately — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single-device, single-run evaluation: all latency numbers come from one physical unit; no variance, no multiple runs, no thermal-state sensitivity analysis. Flash storage speed and thermal throttling can shift TTFT by >10% in practice. - Model scale ceiling at 8B: with mobile hardware improving, 13B+ deployment is approaching viability; the paper offers no analysis of whether flash bandwidth savings scale proportionally or whether NPU DRAM bandwidth becomes the new bottleneck at larger sizes. - The greedy RE proxy's gap from end-to-end accuracy at 4-bit is not characterized — the ablation shows accuracy comparisons against baselines but not a comparison of greedy-allocated vs. oracle bit-width assignments. - Total-session energy (cold start + multi-turn decoding) is not reported, which matters more than cold-start energy alone for real battery-constrained scenarios. - No evaluation on Apple devices or MediaTek NPUs despite both being high-volume mobile platforms; the claimed generalizability "with engineering effort" is not empirically supported. Bottom Line A mobile systems practitioner targeting Hexagon NPU-accelerated LLM inference on Android will find concrete, validated, and implementable techniques in this work — but should treat the single-device, no-error-bar evaluation and the unreleased codebase as reasons to verify independently before committing to this architecture.
Search Results 2604.09083 PDF
#20 — Highly Innovative (76)

PipeLive: Efficient Live In-place Pipeline Parallelism Reconfiguration for Dynamic LLM Serving

Xu Bai, Muhammed Tawfiqul Islam, Chen Wang, Adel N. Toosi · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (75): first open-source live in-place PP reconfiguration system with novel KV patching and layer stacking. Rigor (70): thorough ablations but single 2-GPU testbed, no error bars, preprint, code release status unclear. Non-Obviousness (80): VM-live-migration adaptation to KV cache and layer-stacking fragmentation fix are genuinely non-obvious. Completeness (75): honest limitation acknowledgment, but no comparison against HydraServe/FlexPipe. Resilience (70): 2-GPU scope is a real vulnerability. Impact (80): working vLLM-based system with measurable gains. Weighted: 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.30*80 = 75.75 ≈ 76.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PipeLive, an open-source LLM serving system built on vLLM that enables live, in-place pipeline parallelism (PP) reconfiguration — dynamically redistributing model layers and KV cache across GPUs without interrupting ongoing inference. - Why it's important: Static PP configurations are profiled offline and fixed for the serving lifetime, causing up to 20–30% throughput degradation when workload characteristics shift (e.g., from prefill-heavy to decode-heavy), a particularly acute problem in heterogeneous GPU deployments. - How it does it: PipeLive co-designs a five-phase reconfiguration coordinator with a redesigned non-contiguous KV cache layout (extending PagedAttention), layer stacking to align KV blocks with GPU memory allocation granularity, and incremental KV patching (inspired by VM live migration) to continuously synchronize KV states during migration, reducing final cutover to a brief atomic switch. - The good: KV patching reduces service interruption from seconds to ~10ms across all tested migration sizes, and PipeLive achieves 36% and 33% composite performance score gains over the best static baseline on LLaMA-70B and Qwen3-30B respectively. - The missing: All experiments use exactly two GPUs (A100 + L40S); behavior at 4, 8, or more GPUs — where coordination, deadlock avoidance, and migration bandwidth contention compound — is not evaluated. - Read it or not: Systems researchers and practitioners building heterogeneous or serverless LLM serving infrastructure should read this; it is the first complete, open-source treatment of in-place PP reconfiguration with a working implementation and honest ablations. Problem & Motivation: LLM serving over heterogeneous GPU clusters uses pipeline parallelism to partition model layers across devices. The optimal layer-to-GPU assignment depends on workload characteristics: prefill-heavy traffic (long inputs, short outputs) favors assigning more layers to compute-strong GPUs, while decode-heavy traffic (short inputs, long outputs) favors GPUs with high memory bandwidth. Existing serving systems (vLLM, SGLang, DistServe) profile a single PP configuration offline and hold it fixed. When workload mix shifts — as in serverless platforms or multi-tenant deployments — the static assignment can degrade TTFT or TPOT by 20–30% compared to the workload-optimal configuration. Reconfiguration via service restart discards in-flight requests and incurs minutes of downtime. The core gap is that no prior system supports live, in-place PP reconfiguration: redistributing layers and KV cache across existing GPUs while inference continues. Key Idea: The central insight is that PP reconfiguration's three hard sub-problems — (1) GPU memory is full of weights and KV cache leaving no room for new layers, (2) KV cache is physically contiguous in PagedAttention so resizing requires expensive copy, and (3) KV states must stay consistent during migration without blocking inference — can each be solved by a corresponding mechanism: KV cache shrinking via block-level allocation, layer stacking to align KV block sizes with GPU allocation granularity (eliminating fragmentation from dynamic sizing), and incremental KV patching (dirty-bitmap-driven continuous synchronization) that converges KV state divergence to a negligible residual before a brief atomic cutover. Methodology: PipeLive implements a five-phase reconfiguration protocol coordinated by a centralized Reconfiguration Coordinator. Phase 1 computes an intermediate configuration Cᵢₙₜ — the union of source and target layer sets per GPU — and checks feasibility under current KV block budget; if infeasible, reconfiguration aborts. Phase 2 performs KV cache shrinking by defragmenting live blocks (pointer-only operations, <1ms) and releasing freed blocks. Phase 3 launches asynchronous, non-blocking weight loading and KV state migration concurrently with ongoing inference. Phase 4 performs convergence monitoring: the coordinator tracks Tₛₒₕₑd (last scheduled token) and Tₐₚₚₗᵢₑd (last synchronized KV index) on each GPU; once the gap drops below threshold τ (set to 50 tokens on InfiniBand at 100 Gbps), it triggers Phase 5 — an atomic SyncAndCommit that switches to the target configuration, deletes obsolete layers, and reclaims KV memory. Critical design decisions: (a) Non-contiguous KV block access stores resolved GPU addresses directly in the block table rather than remapping virtual→physical at each inference step (avoiding PagedAttention's per-step remapping overhead); (b) Layer stacking packs k=4 layers' KV blocks into one 2MB GPU physical allocation unit, reducing effective KV block size by 4× and cutting fragmentation from ~44% to ~5%; (c) KV migration uses dedicated lower-priority NCCL communicator groups separate from the pipeline forwarding group, with a two-phase handshake protocol to prevent circular-wait deadlocks when migration and pipeline communication share overlapping GPU pairs. The KV patching dirty-bitmap drain-and-transmit loop runs continuously until the coordinator detects convergence and commits. Key Results: Evaluated on a two-GPU heterogeneous testbed (NVIDIA A100 80GB + NVIDIA L40S 48GB, InfiniBand 100 Gbps) with LLaMA-70B and Qwen3-30B. A pattern-shifting benchmark of 200 requests alternates between prefill-heavy (input=512, output=16) and decode-heavy (input=128, output=512) workloads at high request rates (3 req/s for LLaMA-70B, 5 req/s for Qwen3-30B). PipeLive achieves +36% composite performance score over the best static baseline (Prefill-Optimal) for LLaMA-70B and +33% for Qwen3-30B. For LLaMA-70B specifically, PipeLive reduces TTFT from 12,623ms (Prefill-Optimal) to 2,390ms and achieves TPOT of 346ms vs. 916ms (Decode-Optimal) — because Prefill-Optimal overloads L40S memory during decode phases. KV resizing enables sustaining 2.5× higher request rates without KV overflow compared to reconfiguration with KV resizing disabled. KV patching alone improves TTFT by up to 49.7% and TPOT by up to 29.5% compared to KV patch disabled; enabling asynchronous weight loading additionally improves TTFT by up to 72.4%. Stop time is held to ~10ms across all migration sizes, whereas both baselines (async-WL disabled, KV patch disabled) incur stop times that grow substantially with migrated layer count. Baselines are static configurations selected as workload-optimal by exhaustive profiling — the strongest achievable fixed-PP alternatives. No open-source dynamic PP reconfiguration system exists for direct comparison; closest prior work (HydraServe, FlexPipe, DynaPipe) is described but not benchmarked against with numbers. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. PipeLive is the first complete, open-source system for live in-place PP reconfiguration in LLM serving — no prior system supports this capability. The KV patching mechanism (incremental dirty-bitmap-driven synchronization of KV states across GPUs during live migration) and the layer stacking technique (packing multiple layers' KV caches into a single GPU physical allocation unit) are novel algorithmic contributions. The non-contiguous PagedAttention extension and deadlock-avoiding NCCL two-phase handshake are engineering contributions applied in a genuinely new setting, not simple combinations of existing ideas. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and thorough: layer stacking factor k is swept (1, 2, 4, 8, 16), KV patching is disabled/enabled independently, asynchronous weight loading is disabled/enabled, and synchronous vs. asynchronous migration modes are compared. Multiple request rates are swept for each configuration. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported — the 200-request benchmark is a single run. Code is described as implemented in ~5,000 lines of Python and CUDA/C++ on top of vLLM and FlashAttention; the paper does not state whether the code is publicly released or provide a repository URL. The evaluation testbed has only two GPUs, which limits generalizability. No vendor self-reported numbers; all measurements are first-party. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2604.12171v1, April 2026); it has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): SYSTEMS paper. - Practical: Runs on commercially available hardware (NVIDIA A100 and L40S) with standard interconnect (InfiniBand); built on the widely deployed vLLM framework. - Efficient: Service interruption reduced to ~10ms vs. seconds in naive approaches; KV resizing compaction involves pointer updates only (<1ms); layer stacking reduces fragmentation from 44% to ~5% with factor 4, recovering nearly half of previously wasted KV memory. - Effective: End-to-end results on LLaMA-70B and Qwen3-30B under realistic pattern-shifting workloads demonstrate PipeLive consistently outperforms all three static configurations across TTFT, TPOT, and throughput simultaneously, not just on one metric. - Scalable: Not tested beyond two GPUs; the paper explicitly defers multi-GPU scaling, and coordination complexity, NCCL group management, and migration bandwidth contention are all uncharacterized at larger pipeline depths. PipeLive delivers a software artifact (system implementation on vLLM + FlashAttention); repository release status is not stated. Strengths: - The KV patching mechanism is a genuinely novel adaptation of VM live migration semantics to GPU KV cache state, converting a blocking seconds-long synchronization barrier into a continuous background activity with a ~10ms terminal pause — a concrete, measurable advance over prior approaches. - The layer stacking technique elegantly resolves the fragmentation problem that arises when block-level KV allocation is used: aligning logical KV block size with the 2MB GPU physical allocation granularity by packing multiple layers eliminates ~88% of wasted memory (from 44% loss to ~5% loss) without changing the total KV capacity. - The five-phase reconfiguration protocol is formally specified with primitives (Collective and Synchronization), a well-defined intermediate configuration, and a published algorithm (Algorithm 1); this is more rigorous than typical systems papers. - Ablations cleanly isolate each component's contribution and reveal non-obvious interactions: disabling KV resizing causes sharp TTFT degradation even at moderate load (request rate > 1), demonstrating that KV resizing is load-bearing, not cosmetic. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper explicitly states it does not address when or why to trigger reconfiguration, nor how to determine optimal source/target PP configurations — these policy decisions are deferred to future work. The evaluation testbed is limited to two GPUs. The deadlock avoidance two-phase handshake adds protocol complexity. Layer stacking reduces reconfiguration granularity (migrations must occur in multiples of k), limiting fine-grained layer redistribution. The authors confront these limitations directly and proportionately. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Scaling behavior beyond two GPUs is entirely uncharacterized — with N GPUs, the number of GPU pairs requiring KV migration grows as O(N), NCCL group management complexity increases, and the two-phase deadlock avoidance handshake must coordinate more concurrent transfers; none of this is analyzed or measured. The 200-request benchmark with no repeated trials or error bars makes it difficult to assess result variance — a single pattern-shifting sequence of 200 requests may not be representative of production traffic distributions. The policy question (trigger timing, target config selection) is not just deferred but entirely absent: without a reconfiguration policy, the mechanism cannot be used autonomously, and the paper provides no guidance on how to build one or what its overhead would be. HydraServe, FlexPipe, and DynaPipe are described in related work but not benchmarked directly, leaving open the question of whether a less-invasive alternative (e.g., FlexPipe's inflight refactoring) might close the gap at lower implementation complexity. No repository URL is provided, raising reproducibility concerns for a systems paper. Bottom Line: PipeLive solves a real, well-defined problem — live PP reconfiguration — with technically sound mechanisms and honest ablations, and any team deploying LLMs across heterogeneous GPUs at variable workload intensity should read it; the policy question (when and how to trigger reconfiguration) is the most important remaining gap before the system can operate fully autonomously.
Search Results 2604.12171 PDF
#21 — Highly Innovative (76)

Sparse Contrastive Learning for Content-Based Cold Item Recommendation

Gregor Meehan, Johan Pauwels · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Strong overall: SIGIR'26 accepted, public code, 5-run averages with paired t-tests, four diverse datasets, consistent large gains (up to 57.7% R@20, 148.7% Gini). Novelty (72) genuine — CF-free framing with α-entmax Fenchel-Young loss for cold-start is new. Rigor (85) high with ablations across α values and training regimes. Non-Obviousness (75) — the gradient-sparsity argument for uninformative negatives is principled. Completeness (75) and Resilience (75) solid; authors confront scalability limits honestly. Impact (75) — released code, lightweight encoder. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*85 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*75 ≈ 76.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 85 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 75 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SEMCo (Sampled Entmax for Cold-start), a purely content-based contrastive training framework for cold-item recommendation that replaces sampled softmax loss with a sparse α-entmax generalization and extends it via offline or online knowledge distillation in the content space. - Why it's important: CF-based cold-start methods inherit popularity bias and erratic predictive behavior because they force alignment between semantically different content features and behavioral CF embeddings; SEMCo sidesteps this structural mismatch while also improving item-exposure fairness without any explicit fairness regularization. - How it does it: A content encoder projects multimodal item features into a latent space where item-item cosine similarity correlates with user preference; training uses α-entmax Fenchel-Young loss to zero gradients for uninformative in-batch negatives, and an optional distillation stage transfers item-item similarity structure from a larger teacher encoder to a smaller student. - The good: Sparsemax SEMCo-Online achieves up to 57.7% Recall@20 gain over the best existing cold-start baseline (M4A-Onion) and up to 133.7% MDG@20 gain (Electronics), with statistical significance (paired t-test, p < 0.05) across four multimodal datasets spanning e-commerce, music, and micro-video. - The missing: The method constructs user embeddings via matrix multiplication over the full item catalog (RY), which the authors acknowledge creates scalability concerns for large catalogs but only gesture at a mitigation; no experiments at catalog scales beyond ~63K items are provided. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners working on cold-start or fairness-aware recommendation who want a principled, CF-free alternative to generative/dropout-based methods; the sparse loss idea is clean and the fairness benefit comes for free. Problem & Motivation: Collaborative filtering recommenders fail to produce useful predictions for unseen items (the cold-start problem). The dominant remedy — training a content encoder to map item features into a pre-trained CF model's embedding space — is compromised by an information gap: CF embeddings encode behavioral co-occurrence patterns while content features encode semantic attributes. Prior work (Meehan & Pauwels, RecSys 2025) shows this mismatch causes generative cold-start models to imitate the popularity bias of the supervisory CF model, producing erratic and unfair recommendations. The problem is felt acutely by platforms introducing many new items (music streaming, e-commerce) where cold items must receive meaningful exposure. Key Idea: Instead of bridging content and CF representations, discard CF embeddings entirely and train the content encoder so that item-item cosine similarity in the content latent space directly correlates with user-item interaction patterns. This reframes cold-start as a contrastive learning problem over item similarities, making sampled softmax a natural loss — then replacing softmax with α-entmax to zero out gradient contributions from uninformative in-batch negatives, yielding a cleaner training signal for the content encoder whose representations are less flexible than learned CF embeddings. Methodology: User preference scores are computed as RB = R(YY⊤) = (RY)Y⊤, where Y = f(X₁,...,X_M) is the L2-normalized content encoding matrix. For new items, preferences reduce to dot products between content encodings and user embeddings RY, enabling efficient inference. The content encoder f(·) applies a linear-BN-ReLU layer per modality, fuses modalities via a two-layer MLP attention module (softmax weights), and projects to a final L2-normalized embedding. Training uses Sampled Entmax loss (Eq. 7): for each user in a batch, logits are cosine similarities to all batch items and the target is the one-hot positive item; the α-entmax Fenchel-Young loss zeros gradients for items below a learned threshold, concentrating updates on relevant negatives. Three critical design decisions: (1) computing α-entmax rather than softmax — well-justified via gradient sparsity argument and validated empirically; (2) knowledge distillation in content space rather than from a CF teacher — justified to avoid the information gap, though the justification is more intuitive than formally proven; (3) online vs. offline distillation — the online variant uses EMA teacher outputs as student targets, providing curriculum-style supervision, and is shown empirically superior for Sparsemax but harder to tune. The combined objective is L_Total = L_Distill + λ L_SEM (Eq. 9), with distillation operating on intra-user item pairs for more informative supervision. Key Results: Evaluated on Clothing (39K users, 23K items, text only), Electronics (192K users, 63K items, image+text), M4A-Onion (8.8K users, 44K items, audio+text), and Microlens (98K users, 17K items, image+text+video). Baselines: ALDI, CLCRec, GAR, GoRec, Heater — all current CF-alignment or contrastive cold-start methods, with FREEDOM as the warm backbone. Sparsemax SEMCo-Online beats the best baseline by 7.0% R@20 (Clothing), 39.7% R@20 (Electronics), 57.7% R@20 (M4A-Onion), and 12.5% R@20 (Microlens). MDG@20 gains are even larger: up to 133.7% on Electronics, reflecting substantially improved coverage over the item population. Gini diversity improvement reaches 148.7% on M4A-Onion. Within SEMCo variants, α > 1 (Entmax or Sparsemax) consistently outperforms α = 1 (softmax) in the base and distillation settings. Online distillation outperforms offline, particularly for Sparsemax, where <10% of probability matrix entries are non-zero during training. Training time overhead from α-entmax over softmax is only 10–20%. All improvements are statistically significant by paired t-test (p < 0.05) with one exception (Entmax SEMCo-Offline, Clothing R@20). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The contribution is genuinely novel in combining: (a) purely content-based (CF-free) framing of cold-start via item-item similarity factorization, and (b) α-entmax sparse Fenchel-Young loss as a drop-in replacement for sampled softmax in contrastive RS training, and (c) content-space knowledge distillation (teacher-student both content encoders, not CF-to-content). Entmax has been used in session-based RS attention but not in cold-start or content-based RS training. The CF-free framing is not new in principle but its systematic execution with this loss family and the fairness benefit analysis is novel. Rigor & Reproducibility: Code is publicly released (https://github.com/gmeehan96/SEMCo). All results are averages over five runs at optimal hyperparameters; statistical significance is confirmed via paired t-tests. Ablations cover all α values (softmax, 1.5-entmax, sparsemax) and all training regimes (base, offline distillation, online distillation). No error bars or standard deviations are reported in Table 2, only mean results — a minor weakness given five runs. Hyperparameter search ranges are deferred to GitHub rather than stated in the paper. The paper is accepted at peer-reviewed SIGIR '26, not merely a preprint, though this is the author's version (arXiv:2604.12990v1). No evaluation on the authors' own proprietary dataset; all datasets are public and pre-processed via the MMRec framework. One potential concern: Clothing visual features are excluded following prior work findings that they hurt performance, which is acknowledged and cited. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The content encoder is a shallow MLP; all experiments use pre-computed feature vectors from frozen pre-trained models (BERT, MusicFM, etc.), so inference requires only a small encoder and dot products — runs on commodity hardware. - Efficient: Training overhead is 10–20% over sampled softmax; the output dimension is 64 with hidden size 192, making the student model very lightweight, though the full-catalog matrix multiplication RY scales linearly with |I| and can be a bottleneck for very large catalogs. - Effective: Evidence on four diverse public benchmarks with strong baselines and statistical tests supports that the method solves the cold-start ranking problem better than alignment-based alternatives; the fairness gains are a meaningful side benefit. - Scalable: Maximum catalog size tested is ~63K items (Electronics); the authors acknowledge that full-catalog RY multiplication is a scalability concern for larger catalogs and suggest batching active items as a mitigation, but this is not experimentally validated. The paper stops at paper + code form, with no deployed system reported. Strengths: - Conceptually clean motivation: the information gap between CF and content spaces is a genuine structural problem, and the pure content-based framing cleanly sidesteps it rather than patching it. - The fairness improvement (up to 148.7% Gini diversity gain) is a concrete, practically valuable side effect that requires no extra regularization, contrasting favorably with post-hoc debiasing approaches. - Sparse α-entmax loss is well-motivated mechanistically (gradient sparsity for uninformative negatives) and backed by empirical validation across α values — the ablation showing <10% non-zero entries in Sparsemax probability matrices during training directly supports the hypothesis. - Code is released and hyperparameter details are public, enabling reproducibility. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note that scalability is a concern for large item catalogs due to the full-catalog matrix multiplication, and suggest a batching mitigation. They also note that online distillation is harder to tune than offline. Future work on missing modality scenarios is identified as out of scope. The authors confront these limits directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) No error bars or confidence intervals are shown in Table 2 despite five-run averaging — the magnitude of variance across runs is not visible to the reader. (2) The user cold-start problem is not addressed; the method assumes warm users throughout, which limits applicability to fully cold settings. (3) The α hyperparameter is not tuned in the experiments — only three fixed values (1, 1.5, 2) are tested, leaving open whether intermediate values could improve results. (4) All content features are pre-computed from frozen external models (BERT, MusicFM); the sensitivity of SEMCo to the quality of these upstream representations is not analyzed — a practitioner deploying this without access to strong pre-trained models would want this. (5) The Electronics dataset is 5-core filtered, which removes many true cold items and may make the simulated cold-start test set easier than real deployment conditions. Bottom Line: Practitioners building cold-start recommenders — particularly those concerned about inherited popularity bias or item exposure fairness — will find SEMCo a practically superior and principled alternative to CF-alignment methods, backed by consistent gains across four diverse benchmarks with released code.
Search Results 2604.12990 PDF
#22 — Highly Innovative (76)

Towards Long-horizon Agentic Multimodal Search (15 ▲ )

Yifan Du, Zikang Liu, Jinbiao Peng, Jie Wu, Junyi Li, Jinyang Li, Wayne Xin Zhao, Ji-Rong Wen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (85) drives strongly — UID-based context offloading is a clean architectural solution with demonstrated model-agnostic gains (+35.7 on MMSearch+ for Seed-1.8 without fine-tuning), code promised. Novelty (80) — file-based UID mechanism applied to multimodal agentic search is genuinely new. Non-Obviousness (75) — decoupling visual perception from reasoning storage is a non-obvious design. Rigor (65) — targeted ablations, but no error bars, heuristic α=0.8. Completeness (70) and Resilience (70). Weighted: 0.15*80 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.30*85 = 74. Strongest contribution in this cluster on practical impact.
systems Novelty 80 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 85
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LMM-Searcher, a long-horizon multimodal deep search framework that offloads visual assets to an external file system referenced by lightweight UIDs, paired with a data synthesis pipeline and SFT-trained 30B agent that scales to 100-turn interactions. - Why it's important: Multimodal search agents accumulate images in context across dozens of turns, causing token explosion that either degrades performance or forces lossy image discarding, blocking the scalability of real-world multimodal search. - How it does it: Every retrieved image is saved to disk and replaced in context with a textual UID; a dedicated fetch-image tool lets the agent pull specific images back into context on demand, decoupling heavyweight visual perception from lightweight long-horizon reasoning. - The good: Applying the LMM-Searcher framework to Seed-1.8 (without any fine-tuning) improves MMSearch-Plus from 11.0 to 46.7 (+35.7 points), demonstrating the framework's generalizability independent of the fine-tuned agent. - The missing: The model merging interpolation weight α=0.8 is set empirically with no ablation, and no analysis covers failure modes at very long horizons (e.g., UID reference drift, fetch-image latency at scale, or multi-image chain-of-thought coherence). - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building multimodal agentic search systems who need a principled context management strategy; the framework generalization results across GPT-5 and Seed-1.8 are the most practically useful finding. Problem & Motivation: Multimodal deep search agents must iteratively gather visual and textual evidence across many tool-call turns to answer complex, cross-modal questions. Each retrieved webpage may contain multiple images, and naïvely inserting raw image tensors into the context window causes token counts to grow unmanageably over 20–100 turns. Prior solutions either summarize and discard intermediate images (losing fine-grained perceptual signal needed for visual reasoning) or retain them at full cost (causing context explosion). Text-based context compression methods do not transfer to images because images have fundamentally different representational structure. The result is that existing multimodal search agents saturate around 20–30 interaction turns, failing on long-horizon benchmarks like MM-BrowseComp that require extended reasoning chains. Key Idea: The core insight is to decouple visual perception from reasoning storage by treating images as external files rather than in-context tensors. Assigning each retrieved image a persistent UID converts the heavy visual space into a lightweight textual address space; the agent keeps UIDs in its reasoning chain indefinitely at near-zero token cost and invokes a fetch-image tool only when fine-grained visual inspection is actually required, enabling arbitrarily long search histories without compounding image token overhead. Methodology: The framework has three interconnected components. (1) File-based multimodal data management: a strict injective mapping f: I → U assigns each retrieved image a unique UID (implemented as the image URL when available). An intercepting middleware layer automatically indexes all images from any document D returned by search or browse tools, saves them to disk, and serializes the document by substituting raw images with their UIDs before the agent sees the context. (2) Extended agentic tool interface: tools are redesigned around UID-based visual references and organized as a coarse-to-fine perception funnel — search tools (google_search, visual_search, image_search) return thumbnails and UIDs; scrape_website retrieves a UID-serialized page; fetch_image loads the full image for a given UID into context; zoom_in applies a spatial crop and produces a new UID. (3) Agentic training pipeline: single-hop visual questions are synthesized from multimodal webpages (entity extraction + image-conditioned question generation); multi-hop questions are generated by iteratively expanding a directed knowledge graph from a core entity and fuzzifying leaf nodes to prevent shortcut retrieval; rejection sampling against Seed-1.8 within 40 turns and 64K context yields 12,736 training trajectories; Qwen3-VL-Thinking-30B-A3B is fine-tuned with cross-entropy loss masked over tool responses; the trained model is merged with MiroThinker-1.7-mini at α=0.8 via parameter interpolation to inject language-based search scaling capabilities. Key Results: LMM-Searcher-30B is evaluated on four benchmarks: MM-BrowseComp (MMBC), MMSearch-Plus (MMSearch+), VisBrowse, and MMSearch. At 30-turn evaluation, it achieves 22.3/32.9/42.0/71.0 respectively (avg 42.1), versus the closest open-source competitor REDSearcher-MM-30B at 23.5/26.6/-/72.9. With context management enabled and 100 turns, scores improve to 30.1/34.8/48.3/72.3 (avg 46.4), establishing best published open-source results on MMBC and MMSearch+. Framework generalization experiments show that placing GPT-5 inside the LMM-Searcher tool interface (without any fine-tuning of the framework) boosts its MMSearch-Plus score from 17.2 to 34.8 (+17.6 points), and placing Seed-1.8 inside the framework raises MMBC from 21.4 to 35.1 (+13.7) and MMSearch-Plus from 11.0 to 46.7 (+35.7). Interactive scaling curves show monotonically increasing accuracy out to 100 turns on MMBC and VisBrowse, with no saturation. Tool ablation shows that removing fetch-image drops average performance from 53.3 to 48.2 (-5.1), with the largest impact on VisBrowse (58.0 → 48.5), while MMSearch drops only 2.2 points, confirming that this benchmark is less image-perception-intensive. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET. The file-based UID context management mechanism applied to multimodal agentic search is a genuine architectural novelty not seen in prior work; the multi-hop cross-modal query synthesis pipeline with graph fuzzification is a new data generation method; the 12K trajectory dataset is a new resource. The SFT training and model merging are standard techniques applied in a new configuration. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and targeted: Table 4 ablates data source combinations (incrementally adding open-source VQA, textual queries, and synthesized queries); Table 5 ablates the fetch-image tool specifically. Error bars and significance tests are not reported. Model merging weight α=0.8 is asserted without ablation — the paper explicitly defers a rigorous study of model merging to future work. Code is promised at https://github.com/RUCAIBox/LMM-Searcher but is not yet released (the paper is a preprint dated 14 Apr 2026). The base model Qwen3-VL-Thinking-30B-A3B and training data (LLaMA-Factory, MiroFlow) are publicly available, making reproduction plausible but not yet verified. The evaluation protocol difference (30 vs. 100 turns, context management on/off) is explained but adds ambiguity when comparing to baselines that do not use identical configurations. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: The 30B model requires multi-GPU inference, but the framework components (file system, UID serialization, tool interface) are lightweight and run on standard server infrastructure. - Efficient: By replacing image tensors with UIDs, the framework substantially reduces per-turn token cost; fetch-image adds latency per visual inspection call, but this is bounded by the number of active visual reasoning steps rather than all retrieved images. - Effective: Evidence supports the stated goal: the framework improves 5 out of 5 tested base models, with gains concentrated on long-horizon benchmarks; the fetch-image ablation directly confirms visual signal preservation is the mechanism. - Scalable: Accuracy improves monotonically to 100 turns with no plateau on MMBC; behavior beyond 100 turns is not tested, and UID collision or file system management at very large scale (thousands of images per session) is not addressed. The paper delivers a software framework (described in detail) with promised open-source release; no trained model weights are released at time of preprint. Strengths: - The UID-based visual offloading is a clean architectural solution to a concrete, well-quantified bottleneck (context explosion at 20+ turns); the mechanism provides a hard guarantee of no information loss, unlike heuristic discarding approaches. - Framework generalization results across GPT-5, Seed-1.8, and Qwen3-VL-30B provide strong evidence that the context management design is model-agnostic, not merely tuned for the fine-tuned LMM-Searcher agent. - The multi-hop graph construction with fuzzification produces training data that demonstrably requires more fetch-image and visual-search calls (shown via tool call distribution analysis in Figure 4), indicating the synthesis pipeline genuinely targets the intended capability gap. - Interactive scaling curves showing monotonic improvement out to 100 turns quantify a key claimed property rather than merely asserting it. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note that the model merging weight α=0.8 was chosen heuristically and that a rigorous study of model merging is left for future work. No other limitations are explicitly stated; the conclusion is uniformly positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The 100-turn evaluation keeps only the most recent 5 tool call results (a DeepSeek-V3.2 strategy borrowed wholesale), but no analysis of how much information is lost from truncated earlier turns or whether this interacts with UID retention in the reasoning chain. - Comparison to proprietary models (GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Kimi-K2.5) is included but these are run under the LMM-Searcher framework only for Seed-1.8 and GPT-5 in Table 3; Gemini and Kimi are tested only in the direct-answer and default agent workflow conditions, leaving an open question about ceiling performance under the same framework. - The synthesized training data is validated via a teacher model (Seed-1.8) trajectory rollout, but the quality filter is pass/fail within 40 turns — no human evaluation of query naturalness or answer correctability is reported. - VisBrowse performance at 30 turns (42.0) and 100 turns (48.3) is not explicitly compared against the best closed-source agent baseline because Gemini/Kimi/GPT results are absent for VisBrowse in the main table. - Model merging via linear interpolation is a blunt instrument; the extent to which it may introduce interference between multimodal and language search capabilities is unanalyzed. Bottom Line: Practitioners building long-horizon multimodal search agents will find the UID-based context management mechanism and the framework generalization results (especially +35.7 points on MMSearch+ for Seed-1.8 without fine-tuning) immediately actionable and worth implementing ahead of the code release.
Recommended 2604.12890 PDF
#23 — Highly Innovative (76)

Learning to Learn with Language Models

escholarship.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Three peer-reviewed contributions (TMLR/ICLR/ACL) with strong execution. Novelty (75) — each chapter offers a genuine methodological advance (algorithm-as-output transformer, cross-modal ICL via projectors, self-supervised CoC distillation). Rigor (80) — ablations, error bars, public code/models, diverse benchmarks, though TabPFN baseline missing and judge-model circularity noted. Non-Obviousness (80) — depth-generalization, unsupervised fMRI decoding, and search-to-SFT+DPO distillation are each non-obvious. Completeness (75) and Resilience (70) solid, with acknowledged constraints (256 points, single source domain). Impact (80) — three released codebases and reproducible recipes. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*80 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*80 ≈ 76.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This dissertation introduces three systems — MetaTree, Vector-ICL, and AgenticLU — that collectively demonstrate LLMs can be meta-learners: producing interpretable learning algorithms, performing in-context learning over continuous non-text vectors, and improving long-context reasoning through self-generated clarification chains. - Why it's important: LLM in-context learning has been almost entirely studied over discrete tokens; extending meta-learning to algorithm emulation, heterogeneous continuous data, and self-supervised long-context reasoning opens the door to general-purpose adaptation systems that do not require expensive human annotation or stronger teacher models. - How it does it: Each chapter employs a different meta-learning strategy: (1) a transformer trained on 10.8M decision trees from GOSDT and CART with a two-phase curriculum to adaptively select splitting strategies; (2) lightweight embedding projectors aligned to LLM representation space via next-token prediction, enabling cross-modal ICL; (3) inference-time tree search over Chain-of-Clarifications paths distilled into a model via SFT + DPO, amortizing expensive search cost into single-pass inference. - The good: MetaTree consistently achieves average rank 1.34±0.84 on 91 held-out datasets at depth 2 versus GOSDT's 2.68±1.20 and CART's 3.30±0.90, generalizing to deeper trees never seen in training; Vector-ICL (finetuned) reaches 98.16% on SST2 and 97.28% on IMDB, beating both few-shot ICL and soft prompt tuning; AgenticLU attains 97.8% answer recall on NarrativeQA at search depth 3 and consistently outperforms strong long-context baselines up to 128K tokens. - The missing: MetaTree is bounded to 256 data points and 10 features per call due to transformer sequence-length constraints; Vector-ICL evaluates only single-token encoders and does not test instruction-tuned encoders; AgenticLU training data derives solely from NarrativeQA, and cross-dataset generalization of the distilled CoC behavior is not systematically ablated. - Read it or not: Researchers working on meta-learning, multimodal in-context learning, or long-context LLMs should read the individual chapters (each published at peer-reviewed venues: TMLR 2024, ICLR 2025, ACL 2025); practitioners needing a unified theoretical framing of these three directions will also find the dissertation introduction and Chapter 4 synthesis valuable. Problem & Motivation: Standard LLM in-context learning operates over discrete text tokens and produces predictions — not reusable algorithms, not multimodal reasoning, not self-improving behavior. Three concurrent gaps exist: (1) Classical decision tree induction commits to a single greedy or globally optimized strategy, cannot adapt based on dataset context, and the globally optimal tree problem is NP-hard; (2) ICL is inapplicable to continuous domains like time-series, molecular graphs, or brain fMRI signals that lack natural textual representations; (3) LLMs nominally supporting 128K-token contexts exhibit sharp performance degradation as context grows (Llama3.1-8B-Instruct drops from ~60% to ~40% accuracy on HotpotQA from 8K to 128K tokens), reflecting a gap between nominal and effective context size. Each chapter targets one of these gaps, and the thesis unifies them under a single claim: LLMs possess general-purpose meta-learning capacity that extends beyond pattern matching over seen demonstrations. Key Idea: Each chapter has a distinct core insight. MetaTree: a transformer can internalize the decision of when to use a greedy versus a globally optimized split by training on labeled examples from both algorithms and selecting trees by validation performance — effectively learning an algorithm selector.
