Code and data are publicly released at https://github.com/HuiZ-W/SEABED (ACM Artifacts Available v1.1 badge). A detailed technical report with hyperparameters and extended results is available at the repository. Main methodological concern: ground-truth GEDs for YAGO and WIKIDATA are synthetically generated by applying Δ random edits to subgraphs, not from real-world KG change logs or version histories — the authors acknowledge this and it introduces a potential distribution mismatch with real use cases. The KG pairs tested are small (≤30 nodes) despite the source KGs having millions of triples, so the evaluation does not reflect the practically relevant case of comparing large subgraphs. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on an RTX 3090 (24GB) for training; inference memory stays under 3GB, well within RTX 4060 Ti 8GB, making deployment on commodity hardware feasible. - Efficient: SEABED requires approximately 2× the GPU memory of baseline models during training; the paper does not report per-pair inference latency, which is a critical omission for database query applications. - Effective: Improvements over GEDIOT are large (up to 73.9% MAE reduction), statistically significant (p < 0.01), and consistent across all four datasets and multiple embedding choices, supporting genuine effectiveness rather than overfitting to one benchmark. - Scalable: Tested on 5 progressively scaled versions of SWDF; maintains superiority at all scales, but evaluated KG pairs remain small subgraphs (≤30 nodes), so behavior on pairs of KG fragments with hundreds or thousands of nodes is untested and unextrapolated. The work delivers a software artifact (open-source code, ACM badge) and a trained model, not just a paper. Strengths: - Identifies and formally characterizes two KG-specific challenges (semantic entanglement, global structural semantics) that prior GED literature ignores, providing clear and principled motivation for each architectural choice. - Embedding-model sensitivity ablation (Table 5) demonstrates that gains are not an artifact of the RDF2Vec initialization but stem from SEABED's semantics-aware architecture. - EMD as an auxiliary training signal for distributional global alignment is an effective and underused technique for graph-level comparison that generalizes beyond this specific problem. - Statistical rigor: 5-run mean/std reporting with p-values < 0.01 for the primary metric distinguishes this from many GNN papers that report single-run results. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) synthetic ground-truth GEDs for YAGO/WIKIDATA (random edit operations rather than real KG version histories), noting future work will use authentic KG change logs; (2) a failure mode when global similarity dominates local differences, causing underestimation of true GED. Both are confronted directly rather than minimized, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The evaluated KG pairs are small subgraphs (7–30 nodes) sampled from KGs with millions of triples; the practically important problem of comparing larger KG subgraphs (hundreds or thousands of nodes) is not addressed, and the scalability study only varies the number of pairs and source KG size, not the size of individual KG pairs in the test set. - Inference time per KG pair is not reported; for graph database applications, this is as important as accuracy, and the more complex SEABED pipeline (partition, multiple GNN passes, self-attention) may be slower than simpler baselines in ways not captured. - The synthetic GED construction (apply Δ random edits, treat result as a KG with GED = Δ) creates a ground truth distribution heavily concentrated near small integer values and generated by a known process — a model that learns the random-edit signature could outperform on this benchmark without generalizing to natural KG evolution. - No comparison to graph kernel methods (e.g., Weisfeiler-Lehman subtree kernels adapted for edge labels), which provide a natural and theoretically grounded alternative for labeled graph similarity. - The METIS partition hyperparameter k is not ablated; the sensitivity of performance to the number of sub-KGs is reported in the technical report but not the main paper. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on knowledge graph similarity or graph database query evaluation will find SEABED the current strongest method for small-subgraph KG comparison, with well-documented gains, reproducible code, and honest limitation acknowledgment — but should independently validate on real KG version histories before deploying, given the synthetic ground truth concern.
Search Results
#39 — Innovative (73)

Multi-User Large Language Model Agents (13 ▲ )

Shu Yang, Shenzhe Zhu, Hao Zhu, José Ramón Enríquez, Di Wang, Alex Pentland, Michiel A. Bakker, Jiaxin Pei · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (80) is the standout: formalizing multi-user LLM interaction as weighted social-welfare maximization with access-control constraints is a genuine NEW FORMULATION, and the paired selection/execution and privacy/utility dual metrics are well-designed to defeat trivially safe strategies. Rigor (72) is strong — 20 models evaluated with standard errors, public benchmark release, useful aligned-vs-conflicting and full-vs-partial disclosure ablations — though ground-truth labels are author-constructed with no inter-annotator agreement reported. Non-Obviousness (70) is elevated by the monotonic round-by-round privacy erosion finding and the selection-execution dissociation. Completeness (72) and Resilience (70) are solid; the paper stops at diagnosis with no mitigation experiments, and simulated-user methodology needs real-deployment validation. Impact (75) reflects immediate utility to enterprise-agent builders via the released Muses-Bench. Composite ≈ 73.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Muses-Bench, the first systematic benchmark and formal framework for evaluating LLM agents in multi-principal settings where a single agent must serve multiple users with conflicting objectives, asymmetric authority, and private context. - Why it's important: Real-world deployments increasingly use LLMs as shared organizational assistants—team collaboration tools, enterprise copilots—yet all current training paradigms (SFT on single-user templates, RLHF with scalar reward) optimize for one principal, leaving fundamental gaps in authority reasoning, privacy enforcement, and multi-party coordination that practitioners have no rigorous way to measure. - How it does it: The core move is formalizing multi-user LLM interaction as a weighted social-welfare maximization problem (max Σ w_i U_i(a; C_i, p_i)) subject to access-control constraints, then constructing three targeted stress tests—conflicting instruction following, cross-user access control, and multi-user meeting coordination—that expose failures invisible to single-user benchmarks. - The good: The privacy degradation finding is striking and reproducible across virtually all evaluated models: Claude-3.5-Haiku drops from privacy score >0.95 on round 1 to below 0.75 after only four interaction rounds under sustained social engineering pressure, a failure pattern confirmed across ~20 frontier models. - The missing: All three tasks are evaluated using simulated users generated by a slot-filling persona template; there is no human-in-the-loop validation that the simulated conflicts represent the distribution of real organizational failures, and the benchmark's ground-truth labels are manually constructed by the authors with no inter-annotator reliability reported. - Read it or not: Researchers building enterprise LLM agents or studying AI safety in multi-stakeholder settings should read this; practitioners looking for drop-in fixes will find diagnoses but no solutions. Problem & Motivation: As LLMs are integrated into team workflows—acting as shared assistants for managers, engineers, and interns simultaneously—the implicit single-principal assumption baked into current training becomes a structural liability. Existing chat templates (system/user/assistant) serialize all inputs into a single user role, collapsing distinct user identities and authority levels. RLHF reward models learn a single scalar preference signal that conflates the objectives of different stakeholders. Prior multi-user LLM work (Jhamtani et al., 2025; Rezazadeh et al., 2025) treats secondary users as information providers rather than independent principals. The concrete failure modes that result—an LLM following a lower-authority engineer's instruction over a CEO's directive, leaking salary data to unauthorized employees after persistent social pressure, or prematurely committing to a meeting time that conflicts with an attendee's hard constraint—have no standardized evaluation protocol. Key Idea: The core insight is that multi-user LLM interaction is structurally equivalent to the multi-principal agent problem from economics (Fickinger et al., 2020; Rees, 1985): the agent must jointly optimize a weighted sum of heterogeneous user utilities under access-control constraints, not a single latent objective. Once cast this way, the failure modes—unstable prioritization, privacy erosion, coordination breakdown—follow directly from training on single-principal data, and targeted stress tests can be designed by operationalizing each structural challenge independently. Methodology: The framework defines each user u_i by an authority persona p_i (integer level 1–10 on an organizational hierarchy), a private context C_i (background, preferences, sensitive data), and a utility function U_i. The agent observes a selectively shared context C^share and must produce actions maximizing Σ w_i U_i(a; C_i, p_i) subject to access-control constraints. Three benchmark scenarios operationalize distinct failure modes: Scenario 1 (Multi-User Instruction Following): 1,298 execution scenarios and 304 selection scenarios with 2–10 users. Performance measured by Selection F1 (instruction prioritization accuracy) and Execution Accuracy (constraint satisfaction rate, verified by regex/logic checks). Critical design decision: separating selection from execution reveals that models that correctly identify which instruction to follow often fail to execute it faithfully—a distinction prior work ignored. Scenario 2 (Cross-User Access Control): 216 scenarios across three adversarial categories—direct access requests, social engineering (pretexting, urgency, emotional pressure), and technical obfuscation (XML tag injection, unconventional layouts). Privacy Score = 1 − (leakage rate over unauthorized users); Utility Score = success rate of authorized users receiving requested content. The dual metric is well-designed: a model that refuses everything scores perfect privacy but zero utility. Scenario 3 (Multi-User Meeting Coordination): 216 scheduling scenarios (108 full-disclosure, 108 partial-disclosure, N ∈ [2,20] users). Success Rate measures whether the finalized slot satisfies all mandatory attendees' constraints. Turns Taken measures coordination efficiency. The full vs. partial disclosure split isolates the agent's ability to proactively elicit missing information—a distinct capability from constraint satisfaction under complete information. User simulation constructs personas by sampling from discrete attribute sets (role, tenure, temperament, working style, security posture) and filling a natural-language template. Security posture operates on a three-level spectrum: Strict Compliance, Cautious/Balanced, Risky/Relaxed. Multi-user inputs are serialized into existing single-user templates via three formats (Says, Colon, XML) since no frontier model natively supports multi-user message schemas. Key Results: Across 20 evaluated models (proprietary: GPT-4o-mini through GPT-5.2, Claude-3.5-Haiku through Claude-Sonnet-4.5, Gemini-2.5-Flash through Gemini-3-Pro, Grok-3-Mini, Grok-4.1-Fast; open-weight: Llama-3-8B/70B, Qwen3-4B-IT/30B, GPT-OSS-120B, DeepSeek-R1): Instruction Following: Gemini-3-Pro achieves the highest Selection F1 at 97.3±0.4, while Grok-3-Mini achieves the highest Execution Accuracy at 88.4±1.0—but Grok-3-Mini's Selection F1 is only 68.2. Claude-Haiku-4.5 drops from 0.86 execution accuracy in aligned settings to 0.62 under conflict; GPT-OSS-120B drops from 0.64 to 0.50. This selection-execution dissociation is consistent across all tested models. Access Control: Grok-3-Mini and GPT-5.1 achieve near-perfect Privacy scores (99.6, 98.6) but their Utility scores are 60.1 and 60.3—meaning authorized users receive correct information only ~60% of the time. GPT-OSS-120B achieves 94.4 Utility but only 92.2 Privacy. No model approaches both 95+ Privacy and 90+ Utility simultaneously. Privacy degrades from round 1 to round 10 across nearly all models; Claude-3.5-Haiku drops from >0.95 to below 0.75 within four rounds under sustained adversarial pressure. Meeting Coordination: Best overall is GPT-OSS-120B at 77.3% success rate; most proprietary models cluster in the 50–66% range. Meeting coordination success declines substantially as N increases beyond 10 users, with the required number of interaction turns scaling linearly with N—a clear scalability bottleneck. Partial-disclosure settings consistently underperform full-disclosure across all models, with the gap widening at larger N. Overall average (Table 2): Gemini-3-Pro leads at 85.6, Claude-Sonnet-4.5 second at 82.6. Open-weight GPT-OSS-120B achieves 74.0 average; Llama-3-8B is worst at 41.8. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK / NEW FORMULATION. The formal multi-principal framing of LLM interaction and the Muses-Bench evaluation protocol are the genuine contributions; the individual tasks (instruction conflict resolution, access control, scheduling) each exist in isolation in prior work, but their joint operationalization under a unified multi-principal protocol with authority hierarchies, private context scoping, and adversarial pressure is new. The mathematical formulation borrows from principal-agent theory and social welfare functions—no novel theoretical results are derived. Rigor & Reproducibility: Code and benchmark are released at https://github.com/Korde-AI/Multi-User-LLM-Agent. Standard errors are reported across all metrics (Table 2). The separation of aligned vs. conflicting conditions in Scenario 1, and full vs. partial disclosure in Scenario 3, constitute useful ablations. However, ground-truth labels for instruction prioritization are constructed by the authors without reported inter-annotator agreement. User personas are simulated via slot-filling—no human validation that simulated conflicts are representative of real organizational failures. The privacy and utility metrics for Scenario 2 are binary (0/1 per user), which is coarse and may miss partial-leakage behaviors. The benchmark is a preprint (arXiv:2604.08567v1, 19 Mar 2026), not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (benchmark evaluation of existing systems; no new model or training procedure proposed). - Practical: Evaluation runs against model APIs and open-weight checkpoints on standard hardware; any organization with API access can run Muses-Bench. - Efficient: Evaluation cost scales with number of models × number of scenarios (1,298 + 216 + 216 = 1,730 total scenarios); multi-round access control adds turns but total compute is modest for API-based evaluation. - Effective: The benchmark exposes failure modes that are invisible to existing single-user evaluations, validating that the multi-principal framing reveals real gaps—e.g., the privacy-utility tradeoff and selection-execution dissociation are not visible in standard benchmarks. - Scalable: Figure 9 shows meeting coordination success declines steadily as N scales from 2 to 20; no model has been tested beyond 20 users, and the linear growth in required turns projects poorly to organizational settings with 50+ concurrent users. The work delivers a benchmark dataset and evaluation protocol (software artifact), not a trained model or algorithmic fix. Strengths: - The formal multi-principal framing is the right abstraction: it cleanly explains why single-user-trained LLMs fail (SFT learns one conditional distribution; RLHF optimizes one scalar reward) and maps directly onto evaluable failure modes. - The privacy-utility dual metric in Scenario 2 prevents the trivially safe "refuse everything" strategy from scoring well—a non-obvious design choice that reveals the privacy/utility frontier is currently unattained by any evaluated model. - The round-by-round privacy degradation analysis (Figure 4) is a compelling empirical finding: the failure is not stochastic but systematic and monotonic, suggesting models treat security as a surface-level policy rather than a value. - The scale analysis (N up to 20 users, Figure 9) provides concrete evidence that coordination bottlenecks are structural—turns scale linearly with N—rather than incidental. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly identify: (1) current evaluations use short interactions; long-horizon benchmarks are needed; (2) simulated users may not capture real-world deployment diversity; (3) the benchmark does not yet include tooling, auditability, or policy enforcement mechanisms. They confront these limitations directly in §7 and frame them as future directions rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - All user conflicts and ground-truth resolutions are author-constructed; the paper does not report how inter-rater agreement was established for "correct" authority prioritization decisions, which are often ambiguous in practice (e.g., when a CEO directive conflicts with a legal compliance constraint held by a lower-authority role). - The benchmark evaluates models prompted with explicit authority hierarchies in the system message—this is not standard deployment practice. Real multi-user deployments rarely enumerate authority levels in prompts, so results may overstate model capability for real-world use. - Social engineering in Scenario 2 is limited to three predefined attack categories; red-teamers with knowledge of specific model architectures would likely devise more effective attacks, understating privacy vulnerability. - No analysis of whether serialization format (Says vs. Colon vs. XML) interacts differently with different model families—Figure 6 shows robustness on average, but variance across models is not reported. - The paper measures what fails but proposes no mitigation experiments—not even prompt-engineering baselines like explicit role-enforcement templates—leaving open whether failures are fundamental or easily patched. Bottom Line: This paper provides the most rigorous diagnosis to date of how frontier LLMs break under multi-user pressure, and the Muses-Bench protocol is immediately useful for anyone building enterprise agents—but it stops at diagnosis and the simulated-user methodology will need real-deployment validation before its severity estimates can be trusted.
Recommended 2604.08567 PDF
#40 — Innovative (73)

How to Fine-Tune a Reasoning Model? A Teacher-Student Cooperation Framework to Synthesize Student-Consistent SFT Data (18 ▲ )

Zixian Huang, Kaichen Yang, Xu Huang, Feiyang Hao, Qiming Ge, Bowen Li, He Du, Kai Chen, Qipeng Guo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) — capability/style token decomposition with generate-then-rollback is a clear new method. Rigor (65) — ablations broad but no boundary-predictor accuracy characterization, no error bars, k=20 unjustified, preprint. Non-Obviousness (75) — cross-family distillation degradation diagnosis is compelling. Completeness (70) — code+dataset released. Resilience (65). Impact (75) — released GitHub+HF artifacts. Composite ≈ 71.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TESSY (Teacher–Student Cooperation Data Synthesis), a framework that alternately interleaves teacher and student model generation at the token-span level to produce SFT training data whose reasoning content comes from the teacher but whose stylistic tokens match the student's distribution. - Why it's important: Supervised fine-tuning of reasoning models (e.g., Qwen3-8B) using data generated by stronger teacher models routinely causes catastrophic forgetting and performance regression rather than improvement, because the teacher's stylistic distribution clashes with the student's internalized style. - How it does it: TESSY classifies each generated span as either capability-related or style-related using two small boundary-predictor classifiers (Qwen3-0.6B-Base), then routes capability span generation to the teacher and style span generation to the student in an alternating loop, truncating after k=20 tokens per iteration via a generate-then-rollback strategy. - The good: Training Qwen3-8B with TESSY-synthesized data raises LiveCodeBench-Pro from 25.35% to 36.69% (+11.34%) and OJBench from 18.75% to 25.43% (+35.6% relative), while the standard Teacher-Only baseline drops to 22.10% and 8.73% respectively — a stark, quantified reversal of the conventional distillation result. - The missing: The boundary predictors are the load-bearing component, but the paper provides no ablation over boundary predictor quality, accuracy, or error rate, making it impossible to assess how sensitive the method is to mislabeled token spans. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone working on SFT or knowledge distillation for post-trained reasoning LLMs; it offers a concrete, empirically validated diagnosis of why teacher-generated data hurts reasoning models and a practical remedy with released code and dataset. Problem & Motivation: Practitioners who want to improve reasoning models like Qwen3-8B via SFT on synthetic data generated by larger, more capable teacher models (GPT-OSS-120B, DeepSeek-R1) consistently observe performance degradation rather than improvement. The root cause is not data quality in the capability sense but stylistic divergence: reasoning models are trained on large proprietary corpora that instill model-specific discourse patterns (transitional phrases, hesitation markers, structural idioms), and these patterns differ substantially across model families and scales. When the student model is trained to mimic teacher-generated text wholesale, the SFT loss on stylistic tokens from an alien distribution interferes with the student's ability to learn capability-relevant tokens, causing catastrophic forgetting of the student's reasoning style. Prior work on self-distillation partially addresses this but sacrifices teacher knowledge; RLHF-based approaches are expensive and not offline-friendly. Key Idea: Token spans in a reasoning trace are heterogeneous: capability tokens (code, mathematical derivations, logical deductions) convey the substance of the answer, while style tokens (discourse markers like "Okay let's see," "But wait," "For example,") convey the model's persona. If the SFT data assigns capability spans to the teacher and style spans to the student, the student's training distribution aligns at the stylistic level while importing the teacher's reasoning quality, decoupling the two otherwise-conflated loss components. Methodology: TESSY generates a synthetic response y = [s¹, t¹, s², t², …] where s^i are student-generated style spans and t^i are teacher-generated capability spans. The alternating loop begins with the student (since responses typically open with a stylistic phrase), then switches roles upon each boundary. Within each iteration, the active model generates a fixed window of k=20 tokens, which is then passed to the corresponding boundary predictor — a binary token-level sequence labeler built on Qwen3-0.6B-Base — that identifies the last token of the intended type (last capability token for the teacher, last style token for the student) and truncates the span there (generate-then-rollback). The teacher's boundary predictor B_T is trained on 100K segments from teacher outputs annotated for style spans via LLM prompting (Table 2 shows the annotation prompt); B_S mirrors this for student outputs. Once the alternating thinking-phase loop terminates (when the model signals completion of its chain-of-thought), the final answer is generated entirely by the student to maintain stylistic consistency. The training objective is standard token-level cross-entropy; TESSY restructures the data, not the loss. Critical design decisions: (1) generating k=20 tokens before rolling back is a pragmatic choice that is not ablated for sensitivity; (2) using Qwen3-0.6B-Base as the boundary predictor avoids compromising throughput; (3) discarding the last word when teacher/student vocabularies differ prevents subword-boundary semantic corruption. Training used 80K questions (37K unique, drawn from OpenThoughts and Nvidia Nemotron), 9 epochs, batch size 128, lr 5e-5 on 32 H200 GPUs using XTuner. Key Results: Primary benchmarks are code generation tasks: LiveCodeBench-V5 (2024/08–2025/02), LiveCodeBench-V6 (2025/02–2025/05), LiveCodeBench-Pro, and OJBench. Baseline Qwen3-8B scores: LCB-Pro 25.35%, OJBench 18.75%. Teacher-Only (GPT-OSS-120B): LCB-Pro 22.10% (−3.25%), OJBench 8.73% (−10.02%). TESSY: LCB-Pro 36.69% (+11.34%), OJBench 25.43% (+6.68%). All six competing baselines — Teacher-Score, Teacher-Reference, Teacher-Answer, Teacher-Think, Teacher-Mix, Teacher-Only — degrade performance relative to the Qwen3-8B baseline on at least the harder benchmarks; TESSY is the only method that consistently improves across all four in-domain code benchmarks. With Qwen3-30B-A3B as student, TESSY achieves LCB-Pro 35.55% vs Teacher-Only 30.03% and OJBench 30.39% vs 21.98%. With DeepSeek-R1 as teacher, Teacher-Only drops 19.61% while TESSY gains; with Qwen3-235B as teacher, Teacher-Only drops 21.98% while TESSY gains 19.61% — though the absolute gain from TESSY relative to baseline is modest (+1.07%) with the within-family Qwen3-235B teacher. Out-of-domain math results are mixed: TESSY improves AIME2024 by 3.75% and AIME2025 by 0.93% over the Qwen3-8B baseline, but other methods cause drops. PCA visualization of bag-of-words TF-IDF representations confirms TESSY-synthesized data centroids lie closer to the student distribution than Teacher-Only, consistently across all three teacher models tested. TESSY data is also shorter: TESSY reduces average token count by 7,594 vs Teacher-Only when using GPT-OSS-120B, because the student's tendency toward earlier reasoning termination propagates into the alternating generation. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The decomposition of reasoning traces into capability and style tokens and the use of a two-sided boundary predictor for alternating teacher-student generation is a genuine technical contribution not present in prior work. The theoretical framing (decomposing L_SFT into L_Cap + L_Sty) is clean but straightforward; the real novelty is the generate-then-rollback mechanism that operationalizes this decomposition at inference time for data synthesis. The approach differs from on-policy distillation (student-driven, which lacks teacher capability) and AdaSwitch (online alternating generation, not offline SFT data synthesis) in a practically significant way. Rigor & Reproducibility: Code is released at github.com/CoopReason/TESSY and the training dataset at huggingface.co/datasets/CoopReason/TESSY-Code-80K. Pass@1 is computed as the average of multiple independent runs via the OpenCompass platform, providing some implicit variance averaging, but no explicit error bars or confidence intervals are reported. Ablations exist for final-answer generator (teacher vs. student — Figure 9 shows 12–13% drops from using teacher for final answers), system prompt isolation (Figure 10), LoRA vs. full fine-tuning (Figure 13, where LoRA performs worse), base vs. reasoning model as SFT starting point (Figure 7, confirming reasoning model is strongly preferred), and token ratio analysis (Figure 17). However, the paper does not ablate: boundary predictor accuracy or error rate, sensitivity to k (fixed at 20 without justification), or number of boundary predictor training examples. The benchmark suite (LiveCodeBench, OJBench) is reputable, and the contamination-free design of LiveCodeBench is acknowledged. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2604.14164v1, 23 Mar 2026); results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: TESSY requires running two full-size models (teacher and student) simultaneously during inference for data synthesis, plus two Qwen3-0.6B boundary predictor models; this is feasible on multi-GPU servers but substantially more expensive than single-model generation. - Efficient: TESSY-synthesized data is shorter than Teacher-Only data (by ~7,594 tokens on average with GPT-OSS-120B), which reduces training cost, but the synthesis phase requires alternating inference across four models per sample. - Effective: The evidence shows consistent in-domain code generation improvement across four benchmarks and two student model sizes, with partial out-of-domain math gains; the method demonstrably solves the stated catastrophic-forgetting-under-distillation problem. - Scalable: Tested on 8B and 30B student models; teacher scale ranges from 120B to 235B. No experiments beyond these scales, and the method's reliance on simultaneous multi-model inference may face memory and coordination overhead at larger scales. The authors claim future extension to heterogeneous multi-model settings but present no data. The paper delivers a software artifact (GitHub repo + HuggingFace dataset + training pipeline based on vLLM + XTuner). Strengths: - The core diagnosis — that style-token distribution mismatch is the primary driver of SFT degradation in reasoning models, not capability quality — is empirically confirmed across three teacher models and two student models, making it a general finding rather than a cherry-picked result. - The cross-training experiment (Section 4.2) where Qwen3-30B-A3B data degrades Qwen3-8B and vice versa is a particularly clean controlled demonstration that even within-family distributional mismatch causes harm, strengthening the theoretical claim. - The comparison to six distinct baselines covering the full spectrum from fully student-driven to fully teacher-driven synthesis is thorough and avoids strawman comparisons. - The observation that TESSY data is shorter than teacher-only data (because the student's termination tendency propagates) is an unexpected and practically useful side-effect that the authors explain credibly. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) Teacher-Only slightly outperforms TESSY when the teacher's generation length is unrestricted (64K tokens vs. the 40K cap used in fair comparison), suggesting TESSY's capability ceiling is not yet at the teacher's level. (2) The paper does not integrate quality-filtering techniques like reject sampling, which the authors acknowledge as complementary and potentially further beneficial. (3) The impact statement discloses that initial investigations were confounded by misattributing performance drops to data quality rather than distribution mismatch — a credibility-positive admission of research process. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper's most significant unaddressed gap is the boundary predictor: no ablation reports its token-level classification accuracy, its precision/recall on capability vs. style spans, or how performance degrades when the predictor makes errors. Given that the boundary predictor is the mechanism by which TESSY achieves its core property, its reliability is not characterized. Second, the choice k=20 tokens per iteration is stated empirically without sensitivity analysis — it is plausible that k strongly influences the capability/style mix ratio. Third, the out-of-domain evaluation is restricted to math (AIME, OlympiadBench, GPQA) while training data is entirely programming-contest problems; generalization to other reasoning domains (medical, legal, scientific) is claimed in the conclusion but not tested. Fourth, synthesis cost in GPU-hours is not reported, making it difficult to assess whether TESSY is practical relative to alternatives that require only single-model inference. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on SFT for instruction-tuned or reasoning-model fine-tuning should read this paper — it provides a well-evidenced diagnosis of a widely observed failure mode (teacher data hurting reasoning models) and a concrete, released-code remedy that turns a −10% regression into a +35% relative improvement on competitive programming benchmarks.
Recommended 2604.14164 PDF
#41 — Innovative (73)

Many-Tier Instruction Hierarchy in LLM Agents (13 ▲ )

Jingyu Zhang, Tianjian Li, William Jurayj, Hongyuan Zhan, Benjamin Van Durme, Daniel Khashabi · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical/benchmark paper. Novelty (80) — NEW PROBLEM formulation of many-tier IH with clean controlled-difficulty benchmark design; ~99% vs ~40% gap establishes a qualitatively new capability axis. Rigor (70) — bootstrap CIs, sensitivity analyses, but 80% human-validated IF constraints inject noise; Opus 4.6 both generator and evaluated. Non-Obviousness (75) — PPI format and z-value perturbation flip rates are practically important findings. Completeness (70) — adversarial case acknowledged but absent. Resilience (70). Impact (70) — dataset/code released. Composite ≈ 73.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Many-Tier Instruction Hierarchy (ManyIH), a paradigm and accompanying benchmark (MANYIH-BENCH) for evaluating LLMs' ability to resolve instruction conflicts across arbitrarily many dynamically-specified privilege levels, rather than the fixed few tiers used in current practice. - Why it's important: Real agentic deployments receive instructions from many heterogeneous sources — system prompts, skill files, tool outputs, sub-agents — with trust levels that cannot be pre-enumerated at training time, so the fixed-tier assumption of existing instruction hierarchy frameworks breaks down in practice. - How it does it: A Privilege Prompt Interface (PPI) wraps each instruction with an inline privilege tag (ordinal `[[Privilege N]]` or scalar `[[z=N]]`) and a meta-instruction that teaches the model to resolve conflicts by comparing these values at inference time, fully decoupled from chat-template role labels. - The good: Frontier models including GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 Pro top out at ~40–43% accuracy on MANYIH-BENCH despite reporting >99% on two-tier benchmarks, quantitatively establishing that many-tier IH is a qualitatively distinct, unsolved capability. - The missing: The benchmark tests non-adversarial privilege assignment only; adversarial privilege manipulation (e.g., injected instructions that claim high privilege values) is acknowledged but left to future work, leaving the security-critical use case unevaluated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building agentic LLM systems, LLM safety evaluators, or those developing instruction-following training pipelines — the benchmark fills a genuine gap and the sensitivity findings are practically actionable. Problem & Motivation: LLM agents operating in multi-agent and tool-augmented settings receive instructions from sources with naturally heterogeneous trust levels: a system prompt, a developer config file, a memory skill file, a user message, and tool output may each carry different authority. The dominant Instruction Hierarchy (IH) paradigm addresses this by associating privilege with a fixed, small set of message role labels (system > developer > user > tool) hard-coded during model training. This fails in two ways: (1) it can represent at most as many trust levels as there are role types (fewer than five in practice), and (2) it cannot adapt to deployment-time trust structures that were not anticipated during training. Agents in group chats, multi-agent pipelines, or retrieval-augmented systems routinely encounter more distinct trust levels than existing IH can express, leaving conflicts unresolvable or incorrectly resolved. Key Idea: Decouple instruction privilege from chat-template role labels by encoding privilege values inline within the instruction text via a Privilege Prompt Interface. The model is given a meta-instruction that defines the conflict resolution rule (lower ordinal number wins, or higher scalar z-value wins), and at inference time must read, compare, and act on arbitrary privilege values it has never seen during training. This makes the number of privilege tiers unbounded and dynamically specifiable per deployment. Methodology: ManyIH uses two Privilege Prompt Interface variants. The ordinal PPI wraps each instruction as `[[Privilege N]]...[/Privilege]]` where lower N wins; the scalar PPI uses `[[z=N]]...[/z]]` where higher z wins. A meta-instruction M is prepended to the full input, explaining the conflict resolution rule. Conflicts are resolved based solely on relative ordering of privilege values, not on absolute magnitudes or position in the prompt. MANYIH-BENCH is constructed via a three-stage pipeline: (1) instruction bank curation — a manually curated coding subset (12 Python style groups inspired by PEP 8, 2–5 mutually exclusive instructions each) paired with MBPP programming problems, and a naturalistic instruction-following (IF) subset augmented from AgentIF (46 real-world agents); (2) instruction composition — privilege levels are assigned uniformly at random and shuffled to decouple privilege from position; (3) evaluation — each constraint is verified programmatically (AST/token analysis for coding, LLM judge for IF), using strict all-or-nothing accuracy (all active constraints must be satisfied, all suppressed constraints correctly ignored). The benchmark spans 853 samples: 427 coding (up to 12 privilege levels, fixed 4 style groups, 12 instructions per sample averaging 9.8 conflicts) and 426 IF (up to 7 privilege levels, averaging 12.8 active and 6.6 suppressed constraints). Three key design decisions: (a) non-adversarial prompts to isolate multi-tier reasoning from attack robustness — justified because these are complementary capabilities; (b) granular constraint-level evaluation to avoid conflating IH compliance with task difficulty; (c) controlled difficulty scaling by varying tier count while holding style groups and winning instructions constant. Key Results: Ten models are evaluated: Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6, Grok 4.20, Kimi K2.5, Qwen3.5-397B, -122B, -9B, -4B. Best overall accuracy is 42.7% (Gemini 3.1 Pro), with GPT-5.4 at 39.4% and Opus 4.6 at 33.2%. These same models report >99% on two-tier system-prompt extraction evaluations. On the coding subset alone, GPT-5.4 achieves 60.9% overall accuracy with 89.7% functional test correctness but only 67.9% style compliance, confirming that style conflict resolution — not code generation — is the bottleneck. Performance degrades monotonically with tier count: 11 of 12 model–tier transition pairs show strict accuracy decreases, with drops ranging from 6.8% (Qwen3.5-9B) to 24.1% (Sonnet 4.6) between 6-tier and 12-tier configurations. Ordinal vs. scalar PPI sensitivity: GPT-5.4 drops 8.4% and Opus 4.6 drops 8.0% when switching from ordinal to scalar encoding, despite identical semantic content. Scalar z-value perturbation by δ ~ Uniform(−3,+3) produces flip rates ≥8% in 5 of 6 models tested, with GPT-5.4 showing a 16.4% overall flip rate. Higher reasoning effort monotonically improves GPT-5.4 from 15.5% (none) to 60.9% (high) but does not resolve the ManyIH challenge since the best model saturates at ~60%. Human validation of LLM-generated IF constraints finds 81/100 faithful, 11 unclear, 8 incorrect. Novelty Classification: NEW PROBLEM + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The genuinely novel contribution is identifying and operationalizing the fixed-tier bottleneck in instruction hierarchy and constructing the first benchmark specifically designed to vary the number of privilege tiers independently of instruction-following difficulty. The Privilege Prompt Interface is a straightforward prompt engineering mechanism rather than a new training method; the novelty lies in the problem formulation and benchmark design, not in a new learning algorithm. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations on tier count (6/8/12 tiers) cleanly disentangle IH complexity from instruction-following difficulty by holding winning instructions constant. Sensitivity analyses on PPI format and z-value perturbations are well-designed and reveal non-trivial findings. Bootstrap 95% CIs are reported in Figure 2. Human validation of LLM-generated constraints is conducted on 100 samples (81 faithful, 11 unclear, 8 incorrect), which is modest but provides a lower bound estimate. Code, dataset, and homepage are publicly released (github.com/JHU-CLSP/ManyIH, hf.co/datasets/jhu-clsp/ManyIH-Bench). The IF subset uses LLMs for conflict generation and verification on individual constraints, with the claim that per-instruction generation is human-level for frontier models; the harder combinatorial resolution task remains unsolved. A potential concern: the benchmark was designed partly using Claude Opus 4.6 for conflict generation and verification, and Opus 4.6 is also one of the evaluated models — the authors argue that individual constraint generation is easier than combinatorial resolution but do not provide direct evidence that the generation process does not inadvertently favor or disadvantage specific models. No training methodology is proposed, so Goodhart's Law concerns do not apply to the benchmark itself. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile (benchmark construction and model evaluation). - Practical: Evaluation requires API access to frontier models; the PPI mechanism itself requires no special hardware and can be applied to any LLM at inference time. - Efficient: Evaluation cost scales with model inference cost per sample; the 853-sample benchmark is modest in size, but evaluating 10 models with multiple reasoning effort levels represents substantial API spend. No wall-clock or token counts are reported. - Effective: The benchmark reliably distinguishes models on the ManyIH capability — the >99% vs. ~40% gap between two-tier and many-tier settings is convincing evidence that it measures a real capability gap, not benchmark noise. - Scalable: The PPI mechanism claims to support arbitrary privilege levels; the benchmark tests up to 12. Whether model behavior degrades further beyond 12 tiers, or whether there is a saturation point, is not evaluated. The paper delivers a benchmark artifact and pre-trained model evaluations, not a software library or training recipe. Strengths: - The fixed-tier bottleneck argument is crisply formulated and empirically validated: the dramatic gap between two-tier performance (>99%) and many-tier performance (~40%) is the paper's strongest result and strongly motivates the problem. - The controlled difficulty decomposition — holding task difficulty constant while varying only tier count — is methodologically careful and distinguishes this benchmark from prior instruction-following evaluations that conflate IH compliance with task quality. - The sensitivity analysis (PPI format sensitivity, z-value perturbation flip rates) reveals that models are brittle in ways that matter for deployment: format changes alone drop frontier model accuracy >8%, a practical safety concern. - Public release of dataset, code, and examples enables immediate community use and follow-up training work. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge that ManyIH assumes privilege is assigned by a trusted party and uses non-adversarial prompts, leaving adversarial privilege manipulation as future work. They note the dual-use risk of the PPI mechanism (adversaries could tag malicious instructions with high privilege values). The IF constraint generation pipeline has a ~19% ambiguity/error rate in human validation. The authors treat ManyIH as an extension to existing IH (conflicts within same role), not a replacement, which bounds the scope. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The benchmark's ~80% human validation faithfulness rate means roughly 1 in 5 IF evaluation constraints may be ambiguous or wrong; this injects noise into IF accuracy numbers that is not fully quantified. The coding subset uses only Python style constraints (indentation, naming, quotes, etc.), which are narrow relative to the full space of instruction conflicts agents encounter in practice. No training methodology is proposed or tested — the paper documents the failure of current models but offers no path to fixing it beyond noting that higher reasoning effort helps partially. The use of Claude Opus 4.6 for IF conflict generation and Opus 4.6 as an evaluated model is not controlled: while the authors argue per-constraint generation is easier than combinatorial resolution, shared model artifacts could introduce subtle correlations. The paper reports no evaluation on open-weight models with privileged prompt fine-tuning (e.g., models trained on IH-Challenge data), which would contextualize whether the gap is a fundamental capability issue or a training data gap. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or evaluating agentic LLM systems should read this paper: it documents a real, quantified capability gap that existing two-tier benchmarks miss entirely and provides a ready-to-use benchmark for tracking progress on many-tier instruction conflict resolution.
Recommended 2604.09443 PDF
#42 — Innovative (72)

GHOSTSERVE: ALIGHTWEIGHT CHECKPOINTING SYSTEM IN THE SHADOW FOR FAULT-TOLERANT LLM SERVING

kennethzhao24.github.io · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (75) — FP16→uint16 reinterpretation plus chunk-level erasure coding for the streaming KV cache is a clean, non-obvious adaptation of classical coding to a new setting. Impact (78) — peer-reviewed MLSys artifact, open-source SGLang plug-in, directly addresses a real production pain point, but single-node scope limits blast radius. Rigor (68) — ablations present but no error bars, memory-flush fault simulation rather than real hardware faults, DéjàVu baseline is a reimplementation. Non-Obviousness (72), Completeness (75), Resilience (68) — authors confront cross-node, hard-failure, and decode-heavy gaps directly, and the TP=2 regression is a real limitation. Weighted composite ≈ 72.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 75 Resilience 68 Impact 78
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GhostServe, a fault-tolerant LLM serving system that applies erasure coding to the streaming KV cache at chunk granularity, storing only parity shards in host memory to enable fast recovery without full recomputation or full-cache replication. - Why it's important: Million-token agent workloads run for minutes to hours on expensive GPU clusters where a single device fault forces a complete restart; production studies show ~60% of high-severity LLM serving failures originate at the inference engine level, and recomputing a 1M-token KV cache for a 405B model can take 20 minutes. - How it does it: GhostServe reinterprets FP16 KV tensors as uint16 bit-patterns to make them compatible with standard erasure codes (XOR, RDP, Reed-Solomon), encodes parity shards at the chunk level via custom fused CUDA kernels, and employs hybrid recovery that overlaps partial GPU recomputation with asynchronous parity transfer from CPU DRAM. - The good: On LLaMA-3-70B at 64K input tokens GhostServe recovers in under 5 seconds versus ~2 minutes for SSD-based checkpointing, while maintaining EITR >90% across failure rates of 5–15%, substantially outperforming both CPU-replication and SSD baselines on every measured metric. - The missing: Evaluation is confined to a single 8-GPU intra-node cluster; cross-node scalability, decode-heavy reasoning workloads, and prefill-decode disaggregated architectures are all deferred to future work. - Read it or not: Read it if you build or operate large-scale LLM inference infrastructure and need practical, low-overhead fault tolerance for long-context serving; skip it if your concern is multi-node or cross-datacenter resilience, which the system does not yet address. Problem & Motivation: As LLM serving shifts toward long-context, agent-based workloads—processing millions of tokens per request—the transient KV cache grows to hundreds of gigabytes and becomes the dominant failure point. A device fault (GPU memory error, kernel crash, resource leak) forces a full restart and KV recomputation from scratch, wasting minutes or hours of compute on a billion-dollar GPU cluster. Existing mitigations are inadequate: vanilla recomputation (SGLang baseline) has high recovery cost; full-cache CPU replication (DéjàVu) incurs prohibitive I/O latency during prefill—up to 113% overhead for 70B models in intra-node tensor parallelism—and consumes >300 GB of host memory at long context; SSD checkpointing (PCCheck) is even slower due to disk bandwidth limits. The gap is a lightweight checkpoint mechanism that protects the KV cache with low latency and low memory overhead. Key Idea: Instead of storing a full copy of the KV cache, store only K erasure-coded parity shards for N data shards (K << N). Because erasure coding operates on integers but KV tensors are FP16, GhostServe losslessly reinterprets each FP16 value as a fixed-width uint16 bit-pattern under IEEE-754, enabling standard erasure codes to operate directly on GPU tensors without format conversion overhead. This reduces both memory footprint and I/O transfer volume by (K/N), at the cost of only the parity generation compute. Methodology: GhostServe is a plug-in module on top of SGLang v0.5.1, implemented in 4K lines of Python and 1.5K lines of C++/CUDA. The pipeline operates at the granularity of a chunk (group of tokens, default 2K tokens). During prefill: (1) Each GPU generates its portion of the KV cache chunk in parallel. (2) A designated GPU (assigned in round-robin) gathers all N KV cache shards via `torch.dist.gather` over NVLink. (3) That GPU runs `EC.encode()` using one of three supported codes—XOR, RDP, or Reed-Solomon—over Galois field arithmetic. (4) The K parity shards are asynchronously offloaded to host DRAM over PCIe, overlapping with the next chunk's computation. Custom CUDA kernels fuse FP16-to-uint16 packing, parity computation, and unpacking into single GPU passes using 64-bit indexing, grid-stride loops, and warp-synchronous execution to eliminate intermediate memory transfers. CUDA Graphs minimize kernel launch overhead; CUDA Streams enable concurrent multi-chunk recovery. For recovery, a hybrid strategy first GPU-recomputes the earliest r chunks (determined by `get_recompute_units()`), then gathers remaining surviving shards and reconstructs lost shards via `EC.reconstruct()` using CUDA Streams for concurrency. The two phases are overlapped via asynchronous PCIe transfer. Design decision 1 (integer reinterpretation rather than GPU-side FP↔integer conversion) is well-justified by the 100× latency gap shown in Figure 6. Design decision 2 (chunk-level rather than per-device coding) is justified by eliminating GPU-side peer shard storage and the all-to-all communication it would require. Design decision 3 (round-robin parity assignment) is justified by thermal wear and straggler evidence. Key Results: Evaluated on a private cluster with 8× NVIDIA H200 GPUs (NVLink Gen 4), 1024 GB DDR5, PCIe Gen 4 (32 GB/s bidirectional). Models: LLaMA-3-8B, GPT-OSS-20B, DeepSeek-R1-32B, LLaMA-3-70B, GPT-OSS-120B. Sequence lengths 2K–1M. Batch sizes 4–64. Failure rates 5–15% injected via memory-flush simulation. - Checkpointing latency: 2.7× lower than CPU replication (DéjàVu) on average across models and lengths. - I/O overhead: 13× lower than CPU checkpointing; 132× lower than SSD checkpointing. - Recovery latency: 2.1× lower than CPU replication for a single batch; under 5 seconds for LLaMA-3-70B at 64K tokens vs ~2 minutes for SSD. - Response latency under failure: 1.2× P50 and 1.1× P99 improvement over CPU replication on LLaMA-70B. - EITR (effective inference time ratio): consistently >90% at failure rates 5–15%, while baselines degrade. - At 1M tokens (Figure 9): GhostServe induces <6% overhead over the no-checkpointing baseline; CPU checkpointing overhead drops from 2.6 min to 9 seconds. - Kernel ablation: CUDA-fused kernels outperform PyTorch baseline; kernel fusion adds ~1.05× speedup on encoding; CUDA Graphs add ~1.13× on reconstruction. - Decode overhead: <10% additional latency. - For TP=2, the benefit of erasure coding vanishes due to extra encoding overhead; GhostServe consistently outperforms CPU checkpointing at TP>2. Baselines chosen (SGLang-Base, SGLang-CPU/DéjàVu, SGLang-SSD/PCCheck) are current and directly comparable. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The combination of erasure coding applied to the streaming LLM KV cache—rather than static model weights or training checkpoints—with FP16-to-uint16 reinterpretation, chunk-level distributed encoding, and hybrid recomputation-plus-reconstruction recovery is genuinely novel in the LLM serving context. Erasure coding for storage is classical; its adaptation to the latency-sensitive, dynamically growing, distributed KV cache with custom fused GPU kernels is the new technical contribution. The scheduling and load-balancing components are competent engineering rather than novel algorithms. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation coverage is reasonable: Figure 6 isolates kernel fusion vs PyTorch baseline, with vs without CUDA Graphs; Figure 7 sweeps failure rates 5–15%; Figure 8 sweeps parity ratios (8:1, 8:2, 8:3), batch sizes, TP sizes, and recomputation contribution. Error bars are absent throughout—no confidence intervals or variance reported across multiple runs, which is a meaningful gap for claimed speedup ratios. Failure simulation uses memory-flush mimicry rather than real hardware fault injection, which may underestimate recovery coordination costs (NCCL re-initialization, GPU warmup). Code is publicly released at https://github.com/project-ghostserve/26mlsys-AE-GhostServe. Evaluation is on a single cluster owned by the authors; no external reproduction. The paper is peer-reviewed (MLSys 2026, 9th conference). Two authors are listed as inventors on a pending related patent (App. No. 18/123,456). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: Runs on commercially available NVIDIA H200 GPUs using standard NVLink, PCIe, and NCCL infrastructure already present in production serving clusters. - Efficient: Parity generation adds <5% GPU idle time between chunks; host memory overhead scales as K/N of the full KV cache (75% reduction at 8:2 ratio); IO cost is 13–132× lower than replication alternatives. - Effective: Evidence directly shows recovery of distributed KV cache after GPU memory fault with <6% serving overhead at 1M tokens and EITR >90% under 15% failure rates, addressing the stated problem. - Scalable: Tested up to 1M-token sequences (Figure 9) and GPT-OSS-120B, but only on a single 8-GPU intra-node node; cross-node and multi-hundred-GPU scaling is explicitly deferred and not experimentally validated. The system delivers a software artifact (open-source SGLang plug-in module). Strengths: - The FP16→uint16 IEEE-754 reinterpretation trick elegantly sidesteps the format incompatibility between erasure codes and floating-point KV tensors without any loss or precision degradation, enabling reuse of mature erasure coding libraries. - Chunk-level encoding with round-robin GPU assignment elegantly avoids both GPU memory overhead (no persistent peer shard storage) and all-to-all communication, while distributing thermal load—a non-obvious design choice supported by concrete latency evidence. - Hybrid recompute-then-reconstruct recovery with CUDA Stream parallelism achieves 42.9% recovery time improvement over pure reconstruction by hiding I/O transfer latency behind computation, validated by ablation. - End-to-end evaluation spans five model families from 8B to 120B parameters with real-world-calibrated failure injection, giving meaningful confidence in the reported 47× I/O speedup over SSD checkpointing. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly confront three limitations: (1) Cross-node scalability is unaddressed—inter-node bandwidth disparity and topology-aware scheduling remain open problems. (2) Full-stack fault tolerance is not achieved—the NCCL communication graph and tensor partitioning are static, so failed GPUs cannot be dynamically excluded during inference; GhostServe targets only soft memory errors, not hard device failures requiring node restart. (3) Decode-heavy reasoning workloads (unpredictable output lengths) complicate encoding protocol design and are not evaluated. These are acknowledged forthrightly, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - All experiments run on a single 8-GPU intra-node configuration; the claim of generalizability to multi-node or production-scale deployments (hundreds of GPUs) is unsubstantiated. Cross-node results would require topology-aware scheduling not present in the current system. - No error bars or repeated-run variance reported for any result; the claimed 2.7× speedup may vary meaningfully under different workload mixes or GPU generation differences. - Failure simulation uses memory-flush mimicry; real GPU soft errors (silent data corruption, kernel faults, memory ECC errors) have different detection latency and may alter the recovery timeline more than modeled. - The TP=2 finding—that erasure coding benefit vanishes—is noted but not deeply analyzed; this is a significant practical concern since TP=2 is a common production configuration for mid-size models. - Evaluation against DéjàVu is conducted via the authors' own reimplementation of DéjàVu for SGLang ("SGLang-CPU"), not against the original DéjàVu codebase, introducing potential implementation bias in the baseline. Bottom Line: Any team operating LLM serving infrastructure for long-context or agent workloads should read this paper—it delivers a concretely implemented, open-source solution with compelling numbers for a real and growing production pain point, though the single-node scope means cross-node applicability requires future validation.
Citations
#43 — Innovative (72)

Bringing Value Models Back: Generative Critics for Value Modeling in LLM Reinforcement Learning

Zikang Shan, Han Zhong, Liwei Wang, Li Zhao · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (78) drives up — the TC⁰≠P complexity argument motivating a generative critic is principled and new in this RL context. Non-Obviousness (75) from the counter-intuitive move of re-introducing critics via autoregressive reasoning. Rigor (65) pulled down by single-seed RL results with no error bars on the central 1.5% gap, dependence on proprietary GPT-5 for SFT, no confirmed code release, preprint. Completeness (72) — limitations acknowledged. Resilience (65) — single actor, single domain, small unverified gap. Impact (65) — algorithm only, 2.1× overhead, no public artifact confirmed. Weighted: 0.20·78 + 0.25·65 + 0.15·75 + 0.15·72 + 0.15·65 + 0.10·65 ≈ 70.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 72 Resilience 65 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GenAC (Generative Actor-Critic), which replaces the one-shot scalar discriminative critic in LLM PPO-style RL with an autoregressive critic that performs chain-of-thought reasoning before producing a value estimate, plus an In-Context Conditioning (ICC) mechanism that supplies policy-descriptive hints at inference time. - Why it's important: Discriminative critics fail to scale reliably with model size and are highly sensitive to random seeds, pushing practitioners toward value-free methods (GRPO, RLOO) that assign uniform credit across tokens — a contextual-bandit reduction that caps performance on long-horizon reasoning and agentic tasks. - How it does it: By retaining the full language-model head instead of replacing it with a linear projection, the critic can perform sequential chain-of-thought computation before emitting an integer score, exploiting the strictly greater expressive power of autoregressive generation over one-shot scalar prediction (grounded in TC⁰ vs. P complexity theory). - The good: Generative critics achieve lower validation MSE than discriminative critics at every model size tested (0.6B–14B), with error decreasing as scale increases (unlike discriminative critics, which plateau), and GenAC achieves 51.90% average accuracy across six math benchmarks versus 50.39% (VC-PPO), 50.22% (GRPO), and 49.93% (RLOO), with the gap continuing to grow over training. - The missing: All RL experiments use a single actor (Qwen3-8B-Base) on mathematical reasoning only; there are no experiments on code generation, dialogue, or agentic tasks that would test the paper's central claim that value modeling matters most for long-horizon sequential decisions. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners working on LLM post-training via RL — especially those considering moving beyond GRPO/RLOO — should read this; those focused on reward modeling or SFT can skip it. Problem & Motivation LLM post-training with RL typically provides a single scalar reward at the end of a generation, requiring credit assignment across hundreds or thousands of intermediate tokens. Actor-critic methods like PPO address this by learning a value function that converts sparse outcome rewards into dense token-level advantages. However, in practice, discriminative critics (a frozen-depth transformer with a linear projection to a scalar) have proven unreliable at LLM scale: their approximation error does not improve with model size and varies sharply across random seeds, driving adoption of value-free methods like GRPO and RLOO that estimate advantages from group-level sample statistics. The cost of this simplification is that every token in a trajectory receives identical credit signal regardless of its correctness, collapsing sequential generation to a contextual bandit — a gap that grows as target applications move toward multi-turn reasoning, complex tool use, and agentic behavior. Key Idea The central insight is that one-shot scalar value prediction is representationally insufficient: value functions in language-generation MDPs can require P-complete computation, while log-precision transformers of constant depth are confined to TC⁰, a complexity class widely believed strictly weaker than P. Under TC⁰ ≠ P, this implies that increasing discriminative critic size alone cannot close the approximation gap — a prediction confirmed empirically. The fix is to allow the critic to perform explicit chain-of-thought reasoning (autoregressive generation) before producing its estimate, since sequential token generation strictly expands transformer expressive power beyond TC⁰. Methodology GenAC has two core components. First, a generative critic architecture: rather than replacing the LM head with a linear projection, the critic retains the full LM head and is prompted to generate a step-by-step reasoning trace before outputting an integer score from 0 to 10, which is then normalized to [0,1] as the value prediction. Responses are segmented at semantic boundaries (heuristic delimiter-based rules); the critic scores each segment, and predictions are broadcast back to token level for GAE (with λ=1, reducing advantage to r−v̂). Second, In-Context Conditioning (ICC): a prompt template supplies the critic with the actor's model size and a smoothed running average of the actor's success rate on the training set (exponential moving average with momentum c). This grounds the critic's reasoning in explicit policy-capability descriptors without requiring full policy information to be encoded in the critic's weights — critical because even GPT-5 fails as a zero-shot value model without policy conditioning. The critic training pipeline proceeds in three stages: (1) Supervised Finetuning (SFT) on reasoning traces synthesized by querying GPT-5 with the ICC prompt template on initial-policy rollouts, to instill chain-of-thought reasoning format; (2) RL Pretraining of the critic alone via REINFORCE with rule-based reward R_v(s,z) = 1−(r−v̂)² (0 if parsing fails), to ground reasoning in empirical returns and overcome the quality ceiling of the SFT oracle; (3) Joint RL Training where the actor is updated via PPO using advantages from the pretrained generative critic, while the critic continues adapting via REINFORCE. The actor is Qwen3-8B-Base; the critic is initialized from the same model family. Compute overhead relative to vanilla PPO is approximately 2.1× (versus VinePPO's 4.3×). Key design decisions: (a) integer 0–10 output rather than a real-valued scalar — justified as more natural for LM generation; (b) segment-level rather than token-level critic scoring — avoids per-token critic calls; (c) ICC via prompt rather than weight conditioning — allows critic to track policy without retraining from scratch each iteration. All are reasonable, though (a) and (b) lack ablations. Key Results Approximation scaling (Section 4 and 6.1): Discriminative critics trained on Qwen3-8B-Base's value function (fixed actor, held-out DeepScaleR prompts, 10 seeds per model size) show flat or negligible MSE improvement from 0.6B to 14B parameters, with high seed variance. Generative critics at 8B achieve MSE approximately 0.050, versus discriminative critics at approximately 0.062–0.065 across all sizes. RL training (Table 1, six benchmarks, avg@16): GenAC 51.90% vs. VC-PPO 50.39%, GRPO 50.22%, RLOO 49.93% average across AIME24, AMC23, MATH500, AIME25, OlympiadBench, Minerva Math. On AIME24 specifically: GenAC 30.00%, GRPO 26.46%. On OlympiadBench: GenAC 54.16%, GRPO 52.92%. GenAC's margin over baselines grows over 200 training steps; VC-PPO's advantage over value-free baselines erodes and approaches the value-free ceiling. Relative ranking (Table 3): With 2 candidates, generative critic top-1 accuracy 0.711 vs. discriminative 0.604; with 8 candidates, 0.332 vs. 0.204 — the discriminative critic degrades toward random chance as candidate pool grows. OOD generalization (Table 4): Generative vs. discriminative MSE reduction: 20.3% on DeepScaleR (in-distribution), 39.3% on Math500 (mild shift), 52.5% on AIME24 (moderate), 48.5% on GPQA (high shift, distinct domain). Ablation (Table 2): Base model MSE 0.299 → SFT 0.137 → RL pretraining 0.051 (with ICC); without ICC: 0.308 → 0.164 → 0.058. Each stage contributes meaningfully; ICC provides consistent improvement at every stage. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is the application of an autoregressive generative architecture as a value critic in the actor-critic RL loop for LLMs, motivated by a complexity-theoretic argument that is both principled and empirically validated. ICC as a mechanism for explicit policy conditioning during training is also new. The theoretical framing (TC⁰ vs. P for value function expressiveness) is borrowed from prior work (Feng & Zhong, NeurIPS 2024) but is applied here to motivate a concrete architectural change, which is new. PPO, GAE, REINFORCE, CoT, and SFT are all established components applied in a new configuration. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative, isolating each training stage and the ICC component. Approximation experiments use 10 random seeds with mean and standard deviation reported (Figure 2a). RL training results in Table 1 report single runs (no error bars or multiple seeds); whether the 1.51% average improvement of GenAC over VC-PPO is statistically significant is not established. Code is developed on verl with vLLM and FSDP on Nvidia 80G A100 GPUs; no public release URL is mentioned. The SFT data is generated by GPT-5, which is not publicly accessible, creating a partial reproducibility barrier. The evaluation protocol (avg@16 generations) is reasonable and clearly stated. Baselines (GRPO, RLOO, VC-PPO) are current and appropriate. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Requires Nvidia 80G A100 GPUs (multi-node with FSDP); accessible to academic labs with institutional GPU allocations but not consumer or single-GPU setups. - Efficient: GenAC incurs 2.1× PPO FLOPs per iteration, less than VinePPO's 4.3×; the overhead comes from critic generation tokens (avg ~300 tokens per segment), and the authors explicitly defer cost reduction to future work. - Effective: The evidence shows that improved value approximation directly translates to improved downstream RL performance on math benchmarks, and the growing advantage over training steps is a credible signal; the causal chain (better critic MSE → better ranking → better actor) is demonstrated, not just asserted. - Scalable: Tested at 8B actor/critic scale; no experiments at 70B+, longer sequences, or multi-turn agentic settings; the scaling claim rests on the theoretical argument and the approximation scaling experiment (generative critic MSE decreases 0.6B→14B), but end-to-end RL scaling is untested. The work delivers a training framework and algorithm (GenAC) with a reference implementation on verl, but code is not confirmed to be publicly released. Strengths - The complexity-theoretic motivation (TC⁰ ≠ P → one-shot value prediction is fundamentally limited) is principled, testable, and confirmed empirically — this is a substantive intellectual contribution beyond a heuristic design choice. - The controlled approximation experiment (fixed actor, train only the critic across model sizes and seeds) cleanly isolates the value modeling component from optimization noise, making the case for discriminative critic inadequacy convincingly. - OOD generalization experiments across four datasets spanning in-distribution to high distributional shift provide evidence that the generative critic captures transferable reasoning patterns rather than dataset-specific surface statistics. - The ICC mechanism is a clean solution to the policy-conditioning problem — it avoids the expensive alternative of maintaining a constantly-retrained discriminative critic while providing information the model weights cannot fully encode. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) the 2.1× computational overhead relative to PPO, noting engineering optimizations are left to future work; (2) experiments are conducted on mathematical reasoning tasks, with generalization to other domains described as an expectation rather than a measured result. These limitations are stated directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single actor architecture and size (Qwen3-8B-Base throughout): whether the approach holds for smaller actors (where the generative critic may be larger than the actor) or larger actors (70B+) is untested. - No error bars on Table 1 RL results: the 1.51% average gap between GenAC and VC-PPO, and the 1.68% gap over GRPO, may or may not be statistically significant across seeds; this is the central empirical claim of the paper. - The SFT stage uses GPT-5-generated reasoning traces as supervision, creating dependence on a proprietary model that is not reproducible; the authors do not characterize sensitivity to oracle quality. - The ICC prompt exposes the running success rate of the actor on the training set — this conflates training-set performance with overall policy capability and may be misleading when training and evaluation distributions diverge. - No evaluation on long-horizon agentic tasks despite this being the paper's primary motivation for why credit assignment matters; the case that GenAC helps most where value-free methods are most deficient is argued theoretically but never tested empirically. - Public code release is not confirmed, which limits reproducibility given the multi-stage training pipeline complexity. Bottom Line This paper makes a theoretically grounded, empirically supported case that generative critics are a viable and superior alternative to discriminative critics for LLM RL, and practitioners working on post-training for reasoning-intensive tasks should engage with it — though the single-domain, single-scale evaluation and absent statistical tests mean the degree of generalization remains an open question.
Recommended 2604.10701 PDF
#44 — Innovative (72)

RAG or Learning? Understanding the Limits of LLM Adaptation under Continuous Knowledge Drift in the Real World

Hanbing Liu, Lang Cao, Yang Li · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) high — new benchmark with a principled five-category temporal taxonomy, plus the Event Evolution Graph retrieval structure. Rigor (70) good — ablation is unusually clean with all four components independently contributing, variance reported, code+data released; but the 59-instance C3 test set with ±40 variance and shared retrieval corpus (construction = evaluation) are genuine weaknesses. Non-Obviousness (75) — the parametric-editing collapse result is concrete and informative. Completeness (75) — limitations confronted directly. Resilience (65) moderate — C3 claims fragile. Impact (65) — released framework and benchmark. Weighted ≈ 72.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 65 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a benchmark of real-world dynamic events (January 2024–October 2025) and a time-aware retrieval framework (Chronos) that organizes retrieved evidence into a chronological Event Evolution Graph to enable temporally consistent LLM question answering under continuous knowledge drift. - Why it's important: Deployed LLMs have fixed knowledge cutoffs, and existing adaptation methods — fine-tuning, knowledge editing, and standard RAG — systematically fail to distinguish what was true at different time points, causing both outdated answers and temporally inconsistent reasoning in time-sensitive applications. - How it does it: Chronos decomposes each query into entities and a time window, performs time-aware dense retrieval using a combined semantic-plus-exponential-decay temporal relevance score, then progressively constructs an Event Evolution Graph with chronological and entity-centric edges that the LLM reasons over directly. - The good: Chronos with GPT-4o achieves 73.86% overall accuracy versus 54.53% for the best RAG baseline (ReAct RAG with Claude), with the largest gain on multi-source temporal reasoning (C3: 71.19% vs. 45.76%), and ablation cleanly shows each of the four graph components contributes independently. - The missing: The benchmark is small (513 knowledge quadruples, only 59 multi-source QA instances with variance up to ±40 points), retrieved from the same quadruple store used to construct ground truth, and the framework's per-query latency and cost — involving at least four LLM calls — are entirely uncharacterized. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on temporal knowledge management, RAG architecture, or LLM adaptation, as it provides the first systematic multi-paradigm comparison under continuous (multi-event, multi-timestamp) knowledge drift with a concrete reproducible framework; practitioners should treat multi-source QA numbers cautiously given statistical fragility. Problem & Motivation LLMs acquire factual knowledge during pretraining, fixing them to a world snapshot at their training cutoff. As entities evolve — corporate leadership changes, policy revisions, stock price movements — models experience not just outdated facts but temporally inconsistent reasoning: the inability to distinguish what was true at different time points. Existing adaptation paradigms each fail in distinct ways. Continual pretraining is computationally expensive and prone to catastrophic forgetting. Knowledge editing methods (ROME, MEMIT, WISE) overwrite existing beliefs without preserving prior states and do not scale to batches of sequential, related updates. Standard RAG retrieves documents without modeling chronological ordering or entity-level state transitions, making it ill-suited to multi-timestamp or multi-source temporal queries. No prior benchmark enforces evaluation across historical recall, point-in-time contemporary accuracy, multi-timestamp aggregation, and multi-source temporal integration simultaneously — the gap this work addresses. Key Idea The core insight is that retrieved evidence should be explicitly organized as a chronological graph of entity state transitions — an Event Evolution Graph (EEG) — rather than a flat ranked list of documents. By sorting retrieved quadruples by timestamp, connecting consecutive events via directed temporal edges, and adding entity-centric edges that track the same entity's evolution, the EEG gives the LLM structural access to how facts changed rather than just what facts exist, enabling temporally consistent reasoning without any parameter updates. Methodology Chronos is a four-stage inference-time pipeline. (1) Query Analysis: an LLM prompt extracts the entity set and time window [t_s, t_e] from the raw query and rewrites a time-agnostic query q_0. (2) Time-aware retrieval: for each extracted entity, dense retrieval with all-mpnet-base-v2 fetches top-N candidate quadruples. Each quadruple is rescored by Score(k_i) = α·sim_i + (1–α)·TimeScore(k_i), where TimeScore uses exponential decay exp(–Δ(t_i, t_q)/τ) that assigns zero penalty to events inside the query time window and penalizes deviations proportionally (α=0.75, τ=180 days). (3) EEG Construction in three progressive stages: EEG-I chronologically orders retrieved quadruples into a temporal graph; EEG-F augments with LLM-hallucinated historical quadruples drawn from the model's parametric memory (prompted via q_0 and t_q); EEG-A adds event augmentation — the LLM either generates a follow-up retrieval query to fetch additional quadruples or directly proposes new quadruples to fill coverage gaps, then adds entity-centric directed edges linking consecutive events for the same entity. (4) Prediction: the EEG is serialized into two complementary views — a temporal view (all events within the query time window, globally ordered) and per-entity evolution views — jointly provided as context for the LLM's final answer generation. Critical design decisions: (a) exponential decay rather than hard temporal filtering preserves retrieval recall for events near the time-window boundary — justified but the sensitivity to τ is only briefly validated; (b) separating EEG into two views is well-justified by ablation; (c) using the LLM itself for history reconstruction (EEG-F) risks injecting hallucinated quadruples into the EEG, acknowledged but not quantified.
