If the Validation Agent systematically accepts tasks that are solvable in Gemini-3-Pro's reasoning style, the benchmark may be inadvertently biased toward that model's capabilities; no model-independence analysis of validation decisions is provided. - Per-task generation cost (API spend, wall-clock time) is unreported, making it impossible to assess whether WebForge's automation is economically viable for practitioners who want to extend the benchmark to new domains. - The anti-cheating Base64 encoding is trivially reversible if an agent reads the data.json file and decodes it; the paper relies on deceptive codes as the primary defense against sophisticated agents, but does not test whether current frontier models can exploit the encoding. - Intra-domain task diversity is determined by the Plan Agent's LLM creativity and may exhibit systematic topical clustering (e.g., Consumer Transaction tasks may overrepresent e-commerce booking workflows), but no within-domain diversity analysis is reported. Bottom Line: WebForge delivers a genuine and timely contribution — the first automated pipeline that simultaneously achieves realism, reproducibility, and scalability for browser-agent benchmarking with multi-dimensional diagnostic capability — and any researcher building or evaluating web agents should read it, though the correlated difficulty dimensions and unquantified generation cost warrant scrutiny before treating the benchmark's capability profiles as ground truth.
Search Results 2604.10988 PDF
#50 — Innovative (72)

CocoaBench: Evaluating Unified Digital Agents in the Wild (29 ▲ )

CocoaBench Team, Shibo Hao, Zhining Zhang, Zhiqi Liang, Tianyang Liu, Yuheng Zha, Qiyue Gao, Jixuan Chen, Zilong Wang, Zhoujun Cheng, Haoxiang Zhang, Junli Wang, Hexi Jin, Boyuan Zheng, Kun Zhou, Yu Wang, Feng Yao, Licheng Liu, Yijiang Li, Zhifei Li, Zhengtao Han, Pracha Promthaw, Tommaso Cerruti, Xiaohan Fu, Ziqiao Ma, Jingbo Shang, Lianhui Qin, Julian McAuley, Eric P. Xing, Zhengzhong Liu, Rupesh Kumar Srivastava, Zhiting Hu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (80) drives — open-sourced benchmark+scaffold with infrastructure-agnostic design and clear capability differentiation. Novelty (65) — the infrastructure-agnostic task specification is a principled but incremental benchmark design refinement. Rigor (60) — LLM-judge conflict of interest (Claude Sonnet annotates its own failures), no inter-annotator κ, no CIs on 153 tasks across 9 domains. Completeness (70), Resilience (65) — proxy verifier soundness not empirically measured. Non-Obviousness (65) — the 64% coding allocation correlation is a useful finding. Weighted: 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.30*80 = 69. Useful benchmark but evaluation methodology has gaps.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CocoaBench, a benchmark of 153 human-authored tasks that evaluate unified digital agents on long-horizon problems requiring flexible composition of vision, search, and coding capabilities, alongside Cocoa-Agent, a lightweight shared scaffold for controlled model comparison. - Why it's important: Existing agent benchmarks test capabilities in isolation (CLI-only, GUI-only, or single-domain), leaving systematic evaluation of general-purpose agents on compositional, multi-modal digital tasks essentially unmeasured. - How it does it: Each task is specified only by a natural-language instruction and an automatic evaluation function over final outputs — no fixed tool ecosystem or infrastructure — forcing agents to reason about which capabilities to compose and how. - The good: Even the best system (GPT-5.4 under Codex) achieves only 45.1% success rate, and open-source leaders (Kimi-k2.5, Qwen3.5-397B-A13B) reach only 11.8% and 9.8%, demonstrating that the benchmark is genuinely hard and exposes real capability gaps across three distinct failure dimensions. - The missing: The evaluation relies on proxy outcome verifiers rather than full trajectory verification for action-centric tasks; it is possible for an agent to arrive at a correct answer via an incorrect execution path and pass, or via a correct path with a spurious final-state artifact and fail. - Read it or not: Researchers building or evaluating general-purpose LLM agents should read this; it is the most structurally principled open benchmark for compositional digital agency currently available, and the failure taxonomy alone is worth the read. Problem & Motivation: LLM-based agent systems — SWE-Agent, UI-TARS, OpenClaw, ChatGPT Agent Mode — are increasingly marketed as unified digital assistants, yet existing benchmarks evaluate them on isolated capability slices: OSWorld (GUI + VM), SWE-bench Pro (code + repo), BrowseComp (web search only), TerminalBench-2 (CLI only), MCP Atlas (fixed tool APIs). None simultaneously require and measure vision, web search, and code execution on the same task. Researchers and developers building unified agents therefore lack an evaluation surface that reflects the compositional demands of real digital work tasks such as shopping, scheduling, research synthesis, and data extraction across heterogeneous sources. Key Idea: The core insight is that benchmark tasks should be specified by (instruction, evaluation function) pairs with no prescribed tool ecosystem, so that agents must reason about which of vision, search, or coding capabilities to invoke and in what order — and evaluation remains automatic and reproducible by measuring final outputs rather than action sequences. Where correct outputs strongly imply correct execution (e.g., a precise price extracted from a live website after reasoning about user dietary constraints), proxy outcome verification is sound. This design makes CocoaBench both infrastructure-agnostic and scalable. Methodology: Task construction proceeded by identifying realistic everyday digital scenarios (research, entertainment, shopping, business, etc.), then instantiating 3–5 concrete tasks per scenario, yielding 153 tasks across 9 domains. Each task was peer-reviewed for ambiguity, shortcut resistance, and external resource stability. Each task ships with an automatic evaluation script; for action-centric tasks, proxy outcome verifiers are designed so that the correct final output strongly implies correct execution. Resources include 17 hosted websites, W&B logs, ChatGPT conversation transcripts, and Costco receipts — all curated for long-term stability. Tasks are annotated for required capabilities: 83.0% require vision, 86.3% search, 56.2% coding, with 98% requiring multiple capabilities simultaneously. Cocoa-Agent is a ReAct-based scaffold built on AIO Sandbox (Docker container providing browser, shell, filesystem, and code execution). It exposes 39 strongly-typed tools across five categories: Vision (17 browser interaction + screenshot tools), Search (11 DOM-level content access tools), Coding (9 file manipulation + shell + code execution tools), and one Control tool (task_complete). The scaffold was evaluated across six model backbones: Claude Sonnet 4.6 (thinking high), GPT-5.4 (thinking high), Gemini-3.1-pro (thinking high), Gemini-Flash-3.0, Kimi-k2.5 (1T param MoE, 32B active), and Qwen3.5-397B-A13B (397B param MoE, 13B active). Five existing agent products were also evaluated as complete systems: ChatGPT Agent Mode, OpenClaw (instantiated with GPT-5.4 and Claude Sonnet 4.6), Codex, Claude Code, and OpenAI Deep Research. All runs used a 30-minute wall clock budget and 50 interaction turn maximum. Error analysis covered 712 failure trajectories from 918 total task attempts under Cocoa-Agent. Failures were annotated by Claude Sonnet 4.6 as judge into three hierarchical categories: Reasoning & Planning (E1 — incorrect reasoning, imprecision, format error), Tool & Execution (E2 — infinite loops, anti-bot barriers, tool result hallucination), and Visual Grounding (E3 — visual detail failures, visual knowledge gaps, missing visual perception due to DOM opacity). Key Results: Under existing agent products, the best-performing system is GPT-5.4 under Codex at 45.1% (matching GPT-5.4 under OpenClaw also at 45.1%). Claude Sonnet 4.6 under Claude Code or OpenClaw reaches 25.5%–34.0%. OpenAI Deep Research reaches only 3.3%, likely due to mismatch between its deep-search orientation and CocoaBench's action-oriented tasks. Under the controlled Cocoa-Agent scaffold, Claude Sonnet 4.6 leads at 36.6%, followed by GPT-5.4 at 30.7%, Gemini-3.1-pro at 19.6%, Gemini-Flash-3.0 at 15.7%, Kimi-k2.5 at 11.8%, and Qwen3.5-397B-A13B at 9.8%. Cost per task ranges from $0.50 to $2.50; Codex achieves the best accuracy-cost Pareto trade-off at $0.75/task vs. $1.09 for OpenClaw and $2.31 for Cocoa-Agent (all with GPT-5.4). Completion time ranges from 380s to 3400s, with no strong positive correlation between time/cost and accuracy. Tool call analysis across 6 Cocoa-Agent backbones shows code_execute (~7,799 calls) and shell_execute (~4,050) dominate; GPT-5.4 allocates 64.0% of tool calls to coding (vs. Kimi-k2.5 at 26.4%), which correlates with performance. Coding-oriented scaffolds (Codex, Claude Code) generalize well to this compositional benchmark despite being designed for software engineering tasks. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the design principle: infrastructure-agnostic, capability-compositional task specification with automatic outcome-based evaluation, applied to unified digital agency at a meaningful task count (153). The Cocoa-Agent scaffold is competent engineering rather than algorithmically novel. The failure taxonomy is a useful empirical contribution. The benchmark design idea — outcome-based proxies over trajectory verification — is an incremental but well-executed refinement of prior benchmark methodology. Rigor & Reproducibility: Task quality control included peer review of all instructions, reference answers, and evaluation functions, plus pilot agent runs to distinguish agent failures from task design issues. The benchmark, all 153 task instructions, evaluation scripts, and the full Cocoa-Agent implementation are open-sourced (cocoabench.github.io). Error analysis covers 712 of 918 failure trajectories — near-complete coverage. No ablations over task design choices are reported (e.g., what happens if the 30-minute budget is doubled, or if tasks with anti-bot-barrier failure modes are excluded). Error annotation uses an LLM judge (Claude Sonnet 4.6), which introduces potential bias since Anthropic's model annotates failures of competing systems including itself. No inter-annotator agreement statistic is reported for the failure taxonomy. The proxy outcome verifier design is described qualitatively but no formal analysis of its soundness rate is provided. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a benchmark + evaluation infrastructure, evaluated by deployment and agent experiments). - Practical: Tasks run on standard cloud infrastructure; Cocoa-Agent requires a Docker container with browser/shell access, which is commercially available. - Efficient: Per-task cost $0.50–$2.50 and time 380–3400s; feasible for research groups but non-trivial to run at scale across all models and all tasks simultaneously. - Effective: The benchmark successfully differentiates strong from weak models and surfaces three distinct failure dimensions — it clearly measures something real about unified digital agent capability. - Scalable: 153 tasks is a small set; performance estimates carry high variance per task. The benchmark design supports extension, but the current task count limits statistical power for fine-grained comparisons. The reliance on externally hosted resources introduces brittleness as websites change. The paper delivers a software artifact: open-source benchmark tasks, evaluation scripts, and Cocoa-Agent scaffold code. Strengths: - The infrastructure-agnostic design (task = instruction + evaluation function) is principled and meaningfully advances beyond benchmarks tied to fixed tool ecosystems or VMs. - The automatic evaluation functions with proxy outcome verifiers enable scalable, human-free evaluation while retaining realistic task complexity — a genuine methodological advance over benchmarks requiring LLM judges or human raters. - The failure taxonomy (Reasoning & Planning / Tool & Execution / Visual Grounding) is specific and empirically grounded in 712 annotated trajectories, with concrete examples that make it actionable for system builders. - The finding that coding-heavy tool use patterns (64% for GPT-5.4 vs. 26% for Kimi-k2.5) strongly correlate with performance is a non-obvious, useful empirical result about how strong agents decompose compositional tasks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that external resource stability is a concern (websites can change, breaking tasks) and that they have "made their best effort" to ensure stability, without providing a formal mechanism. They acknowledge that Cocoa-Agent is not the strongest-performing scaffold, and that their results may not generalize to other agentic infrastructures. They do not claim Cocoa-Agent represents the ceiling for any backbone model. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) The LLM-as-judge for error annotation uses Claude Sonnet 4.6, one of the evaluated models — this is a direct conflict of interest for characterizing Claude's failure modes vs. GPT-5.4's. No inter-rater reliability is reported. (2) 153 tasks across 9 domains yields ~17 tasks per domain on average; confidence intervals on per-domain or per-capability-combination results are not reported, making fine-grained comparisons statistically fragile. (3) The proxy outcome verifier soundness rate (what fraction of passing agents actually followed a valid execution path) is not empirically measured. (4) No analysis of whether tasks are solvable by non-agentic prompting (e.g., a single GPT-5.4 call with tool use) to establish the true lower-bound contribution of multi-turn agentic scaffolding. (5) The benchmark is static and frozen; there is no stated plan for versioning as models improve past the current 45% ceiling. Bottom Line: Practitioners building unified digital agents should read this paper — CocoaBench is currently the most structurally sound open benchmark for compositional agent evaluation, and the 45% ceiling even for the best available model establishes that the problem is far from solved.
Recommended 2604.11201 PDF
#51 — Innovative (72)

Turing Test on Screen: A Benchmark for Mobile GUI Agent Humanization (19 ▲ )

Jiachen Zhu, Lingyu Yang, Rong Shan, Congmin Zheng, Zeyu Zheng, Weiwen Liu, Yong Yu, Weinan Zhang, Jianghao Lin · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (85) high — defines a new problem (GUI agent humanization), new benchmark/dataset at scale (~280M events), new MinMax formulation with three theorems. Non-Obviousness (80) — counter-intuitive non-additivity of strategies and Pareto frontier are genuine findings. Rigor (65) — thorough combinatorial ablations and public code/data, but single device, no error bars, no adaptive detector, no neural-detector sanity check. Completeness (65) and Resilience (55) limited by absent adaptive-detector analysis and single-device scope. Impact (70) — released dataset, benchmark harness, and wrapper open a new evaluation axis the field will likely adopt. Weighted: 0.20*85 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*70 ≈ 70.
empirical Novelty 85 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 65 Resilience 55 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the Agent Humanization Benchmark (AHB), a framework that formalizes mobile GUI agent detection evasion as a MinMax adversarial game, collects a large-scale touch-dynamics dataset from humans and LMM-based agents, and evaluates four humanization strategies on the trade-off between imitability and task utility. - Why it's important: Autonomous GUI agents are blocked by platform anti-bot defenses that use behavioral biometrics — an obstacle the field has ignored almost entirely by focusing only on task success rate — meaning agents cannot reliably operate in human-centric digital ecosystems like WeChat or major Chinese super-apps. - How it does it: An external post-processing wrapper transforms raw agent action trajectories via B-spline curve smoothing or data-driven human trajectory matching, while injecting fake micro-interactions and sampling human-like tap durations to close the distributional gap to real touch dynamics. - The good: Raw LMM agents are detectable at 99.5%–100% XGBoost accuracy, and the best humanization (offline History Matching) drops this to 94.5% while maintaining reasonable task utility — empirically confirming the imitability-utility Pareto frontier the paper formalizes. - The missing: The detectors evaluated are SVM and XGBoost trained on a fixed 24-feature set; no adaptive adversarial detector (one that knows humanization is being applied) is tested, leaving the question of robust detection open. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on GUI agents, mobile security, or behavioral biometrics, as it opens a new evaluation axis that is practically unavoidable for agent deployment; skip if you need deployment-ready techniques, since the best strategies still leave agents detectable above 90% accuracy. Problem & Motivation: LMM-based GUI agents (UI-TARS, MobileAgent-E, AgentCPM, AutoGLM) automate mobile tasks by injecting synthetic touch events. Digital platforms — whose revenue depends on human attention and engagement — deploy behavioral biometric detection to block non-human input patterns, causing service denials and account restrictions (illustrated by the Doubao Mobile Assistant / WeChat incident in late 2025). The core behavioral gap is that agents produce mechanically perfect linear trajectories, near-zero tap durations (~0 ms versus human 50–100 ms Gaussian), and inference-latency-driven action intervals (ui-tars clusters at 5–10 s, mobile-agent-e at 50–80 s) that trivially distinguish them from humans. Existing agent research optimizes Task Success Rate with no accounting for detection survivability, leaving agents architecturally unfit for adversarial digital ecosystems. Key Idea: The key insight is that agent detectability is a distributional divergence problem: the JS divergence between human and agent touch-event distributions is maximized (log 2) for raw agents because the distributions have disjoint support. Any transformation that injects variance — even simple B-spline smoothing — strictly reduces JS divergence and therefore strictly reduces maximum detector efficacy (Theorem 2). The practically superior strategy, History Matching, replaces synthetic trajectories with real human trajectories affinely warped to the required endpoint, asymptotically collapsing the divergence to zero (Theorem 3), at the cost of requiring an offline human trajectory database. Methodology: The interaction is modeled as a MinMax game: detector D_Θ maximizes cross-entropy distinguishability (Eq. 3), agent G_Φ minimizes detection probability subject to a task utility constraint λ·R_task (Eq. 4). The paper adopts an External Wrapper H that post-processes raw actions a_raw → a_human before OS injection, leaving the LMM policy unmodified. Four strategies are implemented: (1) B-spline Noise — generates a curved swipe trajectory via control points scattered around a linear chord using B-spline basis functions; (2) History Matching — retrieves a human trajectory with similar displacement/direction from a database, applies affine rotation-and-scale transformation (Eq. 6) to align it; (3) Fake Actions — injects micro-interactions (slight scrolls, hovers) during LMM inference latency gaps, targeting the action interval distribution; (4) Longer Presses — samples tap durations from a Gaussian fitted to human data. Detectors are hierarchical: rule-based checks (swipe accuracy, time interval accuracy, tap duration accuracy) form the first layer; SVM and XGBoost trained on 24 features (velocity percentiles, acceleration percentiles, deviation-from-line metrics, path efficiency, mean resultant length, coordinates, duration) form the second. Information gain analysis identifies maxDev (IG=0.6726) and meanResultantLength (IG=0.6581) as the most discriminative features, and startX/endX coordinates as the least (IG~0.40). Data collection runs on a single device (Xiaomi Mi Max 2, MIUI 11.0.2.0) across 21 apps in 5 task clusters. Human data covers 4 demographic sub-populations; agent data covers UI-TARS, MobileAgent-E (GPT-4o and Claude-3.5-Sonnet), AgentCPM, and AutoGLM. Total events: ~37.8 M human, ~243 M agent. Key Results: Baseline (raw agent) detectability: XGBoost achieves 0.995–1.000 accuracy across all 5 task clusters; SVM achieves 0.979–0.988. This confirms the detection problem is trivially solved without humanization. Best humanization (offline History Matching, Social Media cluster): SVM drops from 0.9817 → 0.9633; XGBoost from 1.0000 → 0.9450. Best online mode (real-time History Matching): SVM 0.7190, XGBoost 0.9450. No strategy approaches the 0.5 (random-guessing) threshold that would constitute passing the Turing Test on Screen. Fake Actions reduce interval-rule accuracy to ~0.52 (near random) but at severe utility cost: Trip Planning task success rate collapses from 0.75 to 0.15 in online mode with fake actions, because hard-coded fake gestures trigger unintended UI state transitions. Long Press effectively bypasses tap-duration heuristics, reducing detection accuracy from 0.9840 to 0.6133 in Cluster 1. A counter-intuitive finding: combining Fake Actions with trajectory humanization can increase overall ML detectability relative to trajectory humanization alone, because the injected fake gestures introduce a new predictable mechanical artifact that XGBoost exploits. Feature analysis shows path-shape metrics (maxDev, meanResultantLength) are easiest to humanize (drop to 0.55–0.64 with rotation-and-match), while endpoint precision (endY) and action interval remain resistant — adding coordinate noise risks missing UI tap targets, and fake interval injection degrades utility. Novelty Classification: NEW PROBLEM (GUI agent humanization as a formal adversarial objective), NEW BENCHMARK/DATASET (AHB with real mobile touch dynamics at scale), NEW FORMULATION (MinMax game grounding detection-evasion in GAN theory), NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (imitability-utility Pareto frontier; non-additive interaction between humanization strategies). The individual techniques (B-splines, SVM/XGBoost classification, GAN-style min-max) are standard; the novelty is framing and systematizing the humanization problem for LMM-based agents, which prior work did not address. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations exist across all combinations of four strategies, evaluated in both online (real-time execution) and offline (post-hoc modification) modes across 5 task clusters — this is a thorough combinatorial evaluation. No error bars or confidence intervals are reported for classification accuracy or task success rates; with the reported human sample sizes (644–5050 taps per sub-population), variance could be material. The paper justifies using SVM/XGBoost over LSTMs/Transformers on the grounds that raw agents exhibit such extreme distributional separation that simple models saturate at 100%, making the interpretable features more valuable for identifying humanization targets. Three formal theorems are proved (Appendix C) with complete derivations grounded in GAN theory. Code and data are publicly released at GitHub (github.com/Gebro13/Passing-the-Turing-Test-on-Screen-Agent-Humanization-Benchmark) and HuggingFace. Preprint only — not peer-reviewed. All experiments run on a single device model, which limits generalizability across Android hardware and OS versions. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: All humanization wrappers run on a standard Android device; history matching requires a pre-built trajectory database but no GPU at inference time. - Efficient: B-spline smoothing is real-time capable; offline History Matching introduces lookup latency that the paper acknowledges can cause agents to miss transient UI events (popup windows), degrading success rate. - Effective: Humanization substantially reduces detection from ~100% toward ~72–94% SVM/XGBoost accuracy, but no strategy reaches the 50% random-guessing threshold that would constitute a passed Turing Test on Screen; the problem is meaningfully reduced, not solved. - Scalable: Evaluated on one device, 21 apps, ~5 LMM agents; behavior on different OS versions, screen sizes, or against detectors that adapt to known humanization strategies is untested and unaddressed. The work delivers a dataset, benchmark harness, and humanization wrapper code as software artifacts. Strengths: - Formalizing humanization as a constrained MinMax problem is the right abstraction; the three theorems provide non-trivial theoretical grounding that distinguishes this from purely empirical benchmarking work. - The imitability-utility Pareto frontier finding is empirically concrete and practically consequential: it demonstrates that the naive "plug-and-play" assumption for humanization strategies is false, with interaction effects that increase detectability. - The dataset is unusually large for behavioral biometrics work (~280M motion events across humans and agents), collected under controlled conditions on real hardware, enabling reproducible comparison. - Information gain analysis of the 24 features provides actionable guidance on which dimensions are easiest and hardest to humanize, grounding future work in empirical feature difficulty. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly limit the study to MotionEvents (touch trajectory dynamics), deferring SensorEvents (gyroscope, magnetometer) to future work on the grounds that sensor simulation requires kernel-level signal injection beyond algorithm policy. They acknowledge the imitability-utility Pareto frontier tension directly, noting that fake action injection in complex tasks causes severe success rate degradation. They discuss the online latency cost of History Matching and advocate for end-to-end LMM-integrated humanization as a superior future direction. The ethical implications of anti-bot bypass are addressed with a Red Team philosophy argument and a commitment to restrictive licensing. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No adaptive detector baseline: all detectors are trained on data that does not include humanized agents. A detector trained jointly on humanized and raw data would likely recover substantial accuracy, and the paper does not bound this adaptive arms-race scenario. - Single device is a significant constraint for an Android benchmark: iOS, different screen sizes, and different Android OEM touch sampling rates all affect raw feature distributions in ways the paper does not characterize. - Human sample sizes are modest (644–5050 taps per sub-population for some groups), and demographic sub-populations are defined by age/gender without validation that they span the relevant behavioral variance. - Task success rate is measured against a fixed task suite; the utility loss from humanization under more complex multi-step tasks (beyond the 5 clusters tested) is extrapolated but not measured. - The claim that SVM/XGBoost are sufficient baselines is well-argued but not verified by including at least one LSTM or Transformer detector result, which would let readers confirm the saturation claim quantitatively. Bottom Line: A practitioner building LMM-based GUI agents for real-world deployment — or a security researcher studying behavioral bot detection — gains a concrete new evaluation framework, a large public dataset, and the first systematic empirical picture of the imitability-utility trade-off; the theoretical apparatus is clean and the failure modes documented are practically important, but the benchmark's single-device scope and absence of adaptive detector evaluation mean the conclusions should be treated as a solid starting point rather than definitive upper bounds on what detection can achieve.
Recommended 2604.09574 PDF
#52 — Innovative (72)

STTID: High-Performance Sparse Tensor-Train Interpolative Decomposition

arcb.csc.ncsu.edu · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (75) strong — first working TT decomposition for sparse tensors via PRRLU skeleton selection is a genuine methodological advance. Rigor (60) reduced by single-run timings, no 6D+ testing, and missing comparison with concurrent Zhang et al. preprint. Non-Obviousness (75) — the skeleton-preservation insight is principled, not obvious. Completeness (70) decent with acknowledged fill-in issues. Resilience (60) — unresolved fill-in and dimensionality ceiling weaken claims. Impact (80) — public CUDA code, only working method for target regime. Weighted: 0.15*75 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.30*80 = 72.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces STTID, a sparse Tensor-Train Interpolative Decomposition algorithm that decomposes large-scale sparse tensors directly in COO format while preserving sparsity in the output TT-cores, with a hybrid CPU-GPU implementation achieving 77.9×–550.3× GPU speedups over CPU. - Why it's important: Existing TT decomposition methods either destroy sparsity in TT-cores (TT-SVD) or fail to converge on sparse tensors (TT-cross), making large sparse tensors in quantum simulation, knowledge graphs, and PDEs intractable — STTID is the first method that handles them at all. - How it does it: STTID replaces the SVD/cross-decomposition backbone with a sparse partial rank-revealing LU (PRRLU) that selects actual rows and columns (skeleton indices) from the data, so TT-cores are built from entries of the original sparse tensor and structurally inherit its sparsity, augmented by selective data separation and hash-table-based Gaussian elimination to manage fill-ins. - The good: STTID is the only method among five evaluated that successfully decomposes 4D/5D tensors with up to 27,843 nonzeros (Rnd5/Rnd6) and achieves exact decomposition on knowledge-graph tensors, while all four baselines fail via OOM errors or divergence. - The missing: Fill-in during PRRLU is a structural problem the authors acknowledge but do not solve — for JF17K3, fill-in remains substantial, and no block-based or fill-reducing pivoting strategy is implemented, leaving scalability at higher tensor orders or denser structures uncharacterized. - Read it or not: Researchers working on high-dimensional sparse tensor decomposition for quantum simulation, knowledge graphs, or scientific computing should read this; those needing TT decomposition of dense or only moderately sparse tensors can skip it. Problem & Motivation: High-dimensional sparse tensors arise in quantum chemistry and simulation, recommendation systems, knowledge graphs, and solution of high-dimensional PDEs. Tensor-Train (TT) decomposition offers a compact representation that tames the curse of dimensionality, but two classes of existing TT algorithms fail on sparse inputs. TT-SVD computes TT-cores via sequential SVD on matricized tensor slices, producing dense orthogonal cores that discard sparsity and require memory proportional to the full tensor size — infeasible for large sparse inputs. TT-cross algorithms, designed for partially observable tensor functions, perform back-and-forth sweeps updating skeleton indices from sparse tensor slices; since slices of a sparse tensor contain mostly zeros, the skeleton updates receive negligible signal, preventing convergence or causing singular matrix inversions. This leaves no functioning TT algorithm for the large sparse tensors that routinely arise in real applications. Beyond decomposition, downstream computations (tensor reconstruction, contraction, completion) benefit substantially from sparse TT-cores, which existing methods cannot produce reliably. Key Idea: The central insight is to use interpolative decomposition (ID) rather than SVD or cross decomposition as the TT backbone: PRRLU selects actual rows and columns of each matricized tensor slice as skeleton indices, and the resulting TT-core is literally a subset of the columns of the sparse input matrix. Because the TT-core entries are chosen from nonzero entries of the original tensor (not computed as linear combinations that fill in zeros), sparsity is architecturally preserved without regularization tuning. COO format enables O(nnz) reshaping via coordinate recomputation, making this practical. Methodology: TT-ID sweeps from mode 1 to d-1. At iteration k: (1) reshape the residual tensor T into matrix M (in COO format) with shape (n_k r_{k-1}, N/(n_k r_{k-1})); (2) apply PRRLU to M to find rank-r skeleton row/column indices (I_k, J_k) and permutation arrays perm_r, perm_c; (3) extract column skeleton C = M(:, J_k) and cross matrix X_k = M(I_k, J_k); (4) reshape C into 3D TT-core G_k; (5) set residual R = M(I_k, :) for the next iteration. The TCI form of the TT (Equation 2) stores the decomposition using only skeleton indices and input tensor entries — no explicit matrix inverses are stored, since G_k X_k^{-1} can be evaluated efficiently via LU factors. SPRRLU (Algorithm 2) proceeds in three phases per iteration s: Phase 1 finds the pivot entry (maximum absolute value in submatrix Ms) in O(M.nnz) via COO traversal; Phase 2 performs row/column pivoting by swapping index entries in COO arrays; Phase 3 applies outer-product Gaussian elimination, updating nonzero entries of the separated submatrix Mt using hash table Mt_H for O(1) lookup and insertion. Three algorithmic optimizations address the O(u.nnz × v.nnz × M.nnz) complexity of naive sparse PRRLU: (1) Selective data separation partitions M into Ms (needing update) and already-processed entries concurrent with Phase 2, reducing Phase 3 complexity to O(u.nnz × v.nnz × Mt.nnz). (2) Hash table-based Gaussian elimination replaces the dense perm_c array (up to 465 GB for mode-500 5th-order tensors) with a hash map, reducing storage and lookup to O(1) expected cost. (3) Hybrid CPU-GPU implementation offloads PRRLU to NVIDIA GPUs: Phase 1 uses cuBLAS cublasIdamax_64; Phase 2 parallelizes permutation across CUDA threads; Phase 3 uses cuco::static_map on-device hash tables with kernel fusion combining Phases 2-3 and shared memory to localize atomic operations. Critical design decisions: (i) COO over CSR/CSC for reshaping — well-justified: dimension-agnostic pivoting requires O(nnz) traversal, impossible with pointer-based formats. (ii) PRRLU over RRQR for skeleton selection — justified on two grounds: PRRLU provides both row and column pivots in one pass; RRQR introduces dense orthogonal factors incompatible with sparse storage. (iii) cuco::static_map for on-device hash table (linear probing, fixed capacity) over multi_map/dynamic_map — justified by throughput benchmarks; dynamic_map currently lacks GPU-bulk APIs. Key Results: Quality comparison (Fig. 3) on 6 random tensors (4D/5D, power-law nonzero distribution, 91–27,843 NNZs) and 8 real-world tensors (knowledge graphs + FROSTT: up to 6.3M NNZs). Baselines: TT-SVD, TT-SVD-lasso (l1-regularized TT-SVD from TensorLy), TT-cross-qr (baseline TT-cross), TT-cross-lu (improved TT-cross variant). STTID consistently produces the sparsest TT-cores (core density orders of magnitude below TT-SVD) at comparable reconstruction error. TT-SVD-lasso achieves up to 2 orders of magnitude higher sparsity than TT-SVD on Rnd3/Rnd4 but is input-dependent and fails on Rnd1/Rnd2. Both TT-cross methods produce sparse TT-cores when they converge but fail on Rnd3–Rnd6 via singular matrix inversion (TT-cross-qr) or non-convergence (TT-cross-lu). STTID is the sole functional method for Rnd5 (100,100,100,100; 12,729 NNZs) and Rnd6 (50,50,50,50,50; 27,843 NNZs). Exact decomposition (E_r ~ 10^{-14}–10^{-16}) is achieved on all four knowledge-graph tensors. Performance (Fig. 4) on 2 large random + 8 real-world tensors: Dense TT-ID on CPU requires 9298.2s (Rnd5) and 11725.4s (Rnd6); STTID CPU: 15.3s and 18.6s (607.7× and 630.4× faster). GPU vs. CPU STTID: 77.9×–169.9× on A5000; 194.3×–550.3× on H100. PRRLU accounts for >95% of total runtime. Ablation on Wiki3/JF17K3 (Fig. 5): data separation reduces runtime by >50% on CPU; hash tables add 9.4× and 50.0× over data separation; GPU H100 adds 29.4×–70.1× over optimized CPU; kernel fusion + shared memory adds ~10%. Downstream: Sparse TT reconstruction achieves 12.1× (Rnd5) and 29.8× (Rnd6) speedup over dense reconstruction using STTID's sparse TCI-form TT-cores. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The adaptation of interpolative decomposition (PRRLU) to the TT setting for preserving sparsity in output TT-cores is genuinely novel — prior TT-ID work (e.g., TT-cross) exists but was not designed for sparse tensors and does not produce sparse output. The COO-native sparse PRRLU with selective data separation and hash-table Gaussian elimination, combined with the hybrid CPU-GPU implementation, constitutes a new algorithmic system. The sparsity preservation via skeleton selection (architectural, not regularization-based) is a substantive methodological advance over TT-SVD-lasso. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation study is thorough: each of four optimization stages is timed independently on two representative tensors, allowing unambiguous attribution of speedup. Reconstruction error metric (relative Frobenius norm) is standard. No runtime variability, error bars, or multiple-run statistics are reported — GPU timings appear to be single-run. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/tensorworld/STTID.git, enabling reproduction. Evaluation uses FROSTT (community standard) and knowledge-graph tensors from prior published work. Missing data points in Fig. 3 are explicitly flagged and explained (Section VII-B). The paper does not compare against a recent closely related preprint (Zhang et al., arXiv:2503.18921, cited as [105]) on interpolative decompositions for sparse tensors — this is an important omission that reviewers at IPDPS will likely flag. The paper is submitted to IPDPS 2026 (peer-review pending); results are not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability: SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs on commodity hardware (AMD Threadripper with 503 GB RAM for CPU; NVIDIA A5000 and H100 for GPU). The hash-table approach specifically solves the 465 GB dense permutation-vector problem that would otherwise make large-mode tensors infeasible on any hardware. - Efficient: Memory scales with nnz rather than full tensor size; STTID CPU is 607–630× faster than dense TT-ID at the tested scale. The primary cost is PRRLU (>95% of runtime), and fill-ins during Gaussian elimination can increase nnz substantially, as observed for JF17K3. - Effective: STTID produces sparse TT-cores with reconstruction errors within a small factor of TT-SVD (the theoretically optimal approximation) on random tensors, and achieves exact decomposition on knowledge graphs. It handles all tested tensors where baselines fail entirely. - Scalable: Tested on tensors up to 5D with up to 6.3M nonzeros. Fill-in growth is data-dependent and not fully controlled (JF17K3 shows sustained high nnz counts during decomposition, Fig. 6). Behavior at 10D or with 60M+ nonzeros is untested and unknown; the claim of scalability to larger tensors is not empirically supported beyond the tested range. The paper delivers a software artifact (C++/CUDA codebase at GitHub) alongside the algorithmic contribution. Strengths: - Skeleton-based sparsity preservation is principled: TT-cores built from selected tensor entries are architecturally sparse without regularization parameters to tune, making robustness independent of data statistics. - COO-native PRRLU with dimension-agnostic pivoting is a clean algorithmic insight that enables O(nnz) reshaping — the asymptotic advantage over CSR/CSC (O(nnz²)) is substantial and experimentally confirmed. - The ablation study quantifies each optimization layer precisely, providing a reproducible engineering blueprint: data separation (>50% reduction), hash tables (9.4–50×), GPU parallelism (29–70×), kernel fusion (~10%). - STTID is the only demonstrated method for decomposing the tested large sparse tensors — the experimental proof of exclusive feasibility is the strongest possible argument for the contribution's practical necessity. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge that fill-in during PRRLU is a persistent structural problem — JF17K3 shows sustained high nonzero counts throughout decomposition (Fig. 6) that data separation does not fully mitigate. They acknowledge CC-common as a boundary case where high nonzero density causes STTID CPU to underperform dense TT-ID. They note that cuco::static_map's concurrency limitation (no simultaneous find-and-update for existing key-value pairs) required a workaround using fetch_add, and that dynamic_map (which would handle capacity growth) currently lacks GPU-bulk API support. Future work on block-based strategies is proposed but not implemented. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Single-run timing: all reported runtimes appear to be single measurements with no standard deviation. GPU performance is sensitive to memory layout and thermal state; multi-run statistics are standard and their absence is a reproducibility concern. - Dimensionality ceiling: all tested tensors are at most 5D. TT decomposition is primarily motivated for 6D–20D settings (e.g., high-dimensional PDEs, quantum circuits); STTID's fill-in behavior and convergence properties at d ≥ 6 are entirely uncharacterized. - Proxy metric only: the evaluation uses reconstruction error as quality metric. For the stated applications (quantum simulation, knowledge-graph completion, tensor completion), downstream task performance on sparse STTID TT-cores vs. baselines is never measured — reconstruction error is a proxy. - No comparison with Zhang et al. [105] (arXiv:2503.18921, March 2025): this concurrent preprint on interpolative decompositions for sparse tensors is cited in related work but not experimentally compared. Given its direct relevance, the omission is significant. - Fill-in characterization is missing: the paper observes that fill-in is data-dependent but provides no analytical or empirical characterization of which tensor structures are fill-in-friendly, making it impossible to predict when STTID will be practical without running it. Bottom Line: Sparse TT decomposition has been an open problem, and STTID delivers the first practical solution with a working GPU implementation and public code — practitioners dealing with large sparse multidimensional data should evaluate it, though the unresolved fill-in problem and lack of testing beyond 5D leave open questions about its limits.
Search Results
#53 — Innovative (72)

Do diacritics matter? evaluating the impact of arabic diacritics on tokenization and llm benchmarks

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (78) is strong — McNemar significance testing, nine models spanning Arabic-centric/multilingual/proprietary, standard community benchmarks (reducing Goodhart risk), public code+data, cost-disclosed. Novelty (65) is a new empirical finding rather than new method — but the contradiction of the standard strip-diacritics preprocessing default is genuinely actionable. Non-Obviousness (72) is solid: the mechanistic link from tokenizer vocabulary → fertility → Jaccard divergence → accuracy drop, plus the counterintuitive wild>undiac result. Completeness (72) and Resilience (68) are good with honest limitations, though the full* condition confounds diacritizer errors (~11%) with diacritization density, and zero-shot-only narrows the claim. Impact (70) — no deployed artifact but clear preprocessing guidance for Arabic NLP practitioners. Weighted: 0.20*65+0.25*78+0.15*72+0.15*72+0.15*68+0.10*70 = 72.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 72 Resilience 68 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents the first systematic empirical study of how three levels of Arabic diacritization (undiacritized, naturally occurring "wild," and fully diacritized) affect tokenization behavior and zero-shot benchmark accuracy across nine LLMs and three Arabic benchmarks. - Why it's important: Arabic NLP pipelines commonly strip diacritics as preprocessing, but children's literature, religious texts, and educational materials use heavy diacritization — if LLMs degrade on those domains, the practical gap between benchmark scores and real-world performance is larger than currently understood. - How it does it: The authors construct three diacritization variants of each benchmark (ArabicMMHU, ArabCulture, AraTrust) using rule-based removal and a BERT-based automatic diacritizer, then measure tokenization fertility/Jaccard similarity and zero-shot accuracy across all nine models under each variant. - The good: Full diacritization consistently and significantly degrades accuracy across nearly all models and benchmarks (McNemar p < 0.05), while wild diacritics are largely benign — and retaining them sometimes beats removing them, directly contradicting the standard strip-diacritics preprocessing practice. - The missing: The study is zero-shot only; whether the degradation persists under few-shot prompting or fine-tuning is untested, and the automatic diacritizer introduces its own errors (88.9% word-level accuracy) that conflate model sensitivity with input noise. - Read it or not: Arabic NLP practitioners and anyone building or evaluating LLMs for high-diacritization domains (education, religious text, children's content) should read this — it surfaces a concrete, previously unmeasured failure mode with direct preprocessing implications. Problem & Motivation: Arabic text spans a continuum of diacritization: children's books and religious texts are heavily diacritized (>80% of lines), while news and user-generated content are nearly diacritic-free (<15% of lines). Standard Arabic NLP pipelines strip diacritics during preprocessing on the assumption that they increase tokenizer fragmentation and introduce noise. LLM benchmarks for Arabic (ArabicMMHU, ArabCulture, AraTrust) are evaluated on naturally occurring text, leaving open the question of how sensitive these models actually are to the degree of diacritization — particularly for the heavily diacritized domains where Arabic is widely read. No prior work had measured this systematically across multiple LLMs. Key Idea: The core insight is that tokenizer vocabulary composition determines whether diacritics cause catastrophic fragmentation or graceful degradation: tokenizers with few diacritic-aware vocabulary entries (Jais, Jais-2) fragment fully diacritized text to near character-level granularity, while models with richer Arabic vocabularies (ALLaM, Fanar) degrade more gracefully. This tokenization instability propagates directly into representational divergence (measured via layer-wise cosine similarity) and downstream benchmark accuracy drops. Methodology: The pipeline has three components. First, three diacritization variants of each benchmark sample are constructed: (i) undiac — diacritics removed via CAMeL Tools `dediac_ar`; (ii) wild — original benchmark text; (iii) full — automatic diacritization using the Elgamal et al. (2024) BERT-based morphological diacritizer at 88.9% word-level accuracy. Second, tokenization analysis is performed on the Wild2MaxDiacs dataset across nine tokenizers, measuring fertility (subwords per word), token density (characters per subword), percentage of words with equal subword counts across diacritization pairs, and Jaccard similarity of subword sets. Third, zero-shot benchmark evaluation uses the LM Evaluation Harness (open-weight models) and OpenAI Batch API (GPT-4o), running 0-shot multiple-choice with likelihood-based answer selection. Statistical significance is assessed via McNemar's test (p < 0.05). Critical design decisions: (1) using likelihood-based rather than generation-based evaluation — well-justified for open-weight consistency; (2) relying on automatic diacritization for full rather than human-annotated text — pragmatic but introduces confounding noise; (3) limiting evaluation to zero-shot — limits generalizability but is explicitly acknowledged. Key Results: Across all nine models and three benchmarks, model average accuracy on wild text is 62.8% (ArabicMMHU), 43.4% (ArabCulture), and 81.9% (AraTrust). Full diacritization degrades these to 56.2%, 41.8%, and 72.0% respectively — statistically significant drops in the majority of model-benchmark pairs. Removing diacritics (undiac) produces small, mostly non-significant changes versus wild. GPT-4o is the most robust, dropping only from 87.2% to 86.5% macro average under full diacritization, while open-weight Arabic-centric models suffer more. Jais Chat (13B) is the most sensitive: 14.4-point drop on ArabicMMHU, 9.7 on ArabCulture, 26.2 on AraTrust under wild→full. Tokenizer fertility rises monotonically from undiac to wild to full across all nine models; for Jais-2, full diacritization pushes #chars/subword to 0.9, reflecting near-character-level tokenization. Jaccard similarity between undiac and full tokenizations averages 0.01–0.13 across models, indicating almost entirely non-overlapping subword segmentations. Layer-wise cosine similarity shows that undiac-full pairs have lower embedding similarity at layer 0 than undiac-wild pairs across all models, but similarity converges to ~1.0 in middle layers — suggesting models partially recover representations internally despite surface-level tokenization chaos. Baselines are current (2024–2025 models); the evaluation suite covers Arabic-centric, multilingual, and proprietary models, which is appropriately broad. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The diacritization–tokenization–performance link was theorized but never measured systematically across LLMs. The contribution is the empirical quantification of that link: the specific magnitude of accuracy degradation by model, benchmark, and diacritization level. The methodology (construct variants, measure tokenization then performance) is a straightforward combination of known evaluation techniques; what is genuinely novel is the finding that wild diacritics are benign or beneficial, contradicting the received preprocessing wisdom, and that full diacritization causes measurable performance collapse in Arabic-centric models. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations of component contributions are needed given the observational design. Statistical significance is properly assessed via McNemar's test throughout. No error bars on tokenization metrics are reported (deterministic), but accuracy comparisons use significance testing. The study is zero-shot, so variance across seeds is not applicable for open-weight models (greedy decoding); GPT-4o uses a fixed seed (12345). Code and data are released at https://github.com/mbzuai-nlp/do_diacritics_matter. One methodological concern: the full* condition uses automatic diacritization at 88.9% word-level accuracy, meaning roughly 1 in 9 words is incorrectly diacritized — some measured performance degradation may reflect input errors rather than model sensitivity per se. The authors acknowledge this. Benchmarks used are standard community benchmarks (ArabicMMHU, ArabCulture, AraTrust), not author-constructed, which reduces Goodhart's Law risk. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: All open-weight experiments ran on a single NVIDIA A100 (80GB) in at most 24 GPU hours; commercially accessible hardware. - Efficient: GPT-4o evaluation used OpenAI Batch API at under $15 USD total; open-weight inference is standard LM Evaluation Harness — no overhead beyond normal inference. - Effective: The study measures what it claims — the effect of diacritization on tokenization and zero-shot accuracy — with appropriate controls and significance testing; the finding is well-supported within its zero-shot scope. - Scalable: The study covers models up to 13B parameters; whether findings generalize to larger models (70B+) or instruction-tuned variants with different tokenizers is not tested and is an explicit limitation. The paper delivers no software artifact beyond the evaluation scripts and diacritization pipeline; the primary output is the empirical findings. Strengths: - Directly contradicts the standard preprocessing assumption (strip diacritics) with quantified evidence across nine models and three benchmarks — actionable and non-obvious. - Connects tokenization-level analysis (fertility, Jaccard, layer-wise cosine similarity) to downstream performance, providing a mechanistic account of why performance degrades rather than merely reporting that it does. - Coverage across Arabic-centric, multilingual, and proprietary models avoids cherry-picking and reveals architecture/tokenizer-specific sensitivities (Jais fragility vs. ALLaM robustness). - Statistical testing (McNemar) with explicit significance thresholds for all pairwise comparisons is more rigorous than most evaluation papers in this space. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors clearly state four limitations: zero-shot evaluation only (few-shot/fine-tuning not assessed); automatic diacritization introduces errors that may inflate observed degradation; resource constraints limited model and benchmark coverage; the analysis focuses on MSA, leaving dialectal Arabic unexplored. These are confronted directly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The full* condition confounds two variables — higher diacritization density and diacritizer errors — making it impossible to attribute the full observed degradation to diacritization per se versus input corruption. A human-diacritized gold subset, even small, would have resolved this. The study evaluates only multiple-choice benchmarks; generative tasks (summarization, translation, QA with open answers) may show different sensitivity profiles. The claim that retaining wild diacritics is preferable to stripping them rests on small and largely non-significant accuracy differences (undiac vs. wild); the practical recommendation should be stated more cautiously given the effect sizes. Finally, no analysis of whether fine-tuning on diacritized data could recover performance is included — the most actionable remediation question is left entirely open. Bottom Line: This paper delivers a concrete, well-executed measurement that should change preprocessing defaults for Arabic LLM development — practitioners building or evaluating Arabic models for diacritization-heavy domains will find specific, actionable numbers here that are not available elsewhere.
Recommended
#54 — Innovative (72)

DR$^{3}$-Eval: Towards Realistic and Reproducible Deep Research Evaluation (21 ▲ )

Qianqian Xie, Qingheng Xiong, He Zhu, Tiantian Xia, Xueming Han, Fanyu Meng, Jiakai Wang, Zhiqi Bai, Chengkang Jiang, Zhaohui Wang, Yubin Guo, Yuqing Wen, Jiayang Mao, Zijie Zhang, Shihao Li, Yanghai Wang, Yuxiang Ren, Junlan Feng, Jiaheng Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (82) is the strongest dimension — bootstrap significance testing, three-run stability, three alternative judge LLMs, four-expert human correlation at r=0.78, and manually verified gold insights collectively set a high bar for benchmark papers. Novelty (70) good — reverse query construction and three-tier Supportive/Distractor/Noise taxonomy are principled innovations over prior DRA benchmarks. Non-Obviousness (70) — the empirical finding that hallucination dominates retrieval failure is a concrete, actionable diagnostic. Completeness (70) and Resilience (65) decent but weakened by only 100 tasks sourced from students (no expert workflows), DR³-Agent co-designed with the benchmark, and lenient FA prompt potentially understating error rates. Impact (70) — public code/data, but 5–7 minutes per task limits rapid iteration. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*82 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*70 = 72.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 82 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DR³-Eval, a multimodal benchmark of 100 tasks for evaluating Deep Research Agents (DRAs) on realistic, reproducible multi-file report generation, pairing authentic user-provided documents with per-task static sandbox corpora that simulate open-web complexity at controlled signal-to-noise ratios. - Why it's important: Existing DRA benchmarks are either non-reproducible (live-web search produces temporally volatile results) or unrealistically clean (text-only corpora omitting multimodal content and confounding noise), leaving practitioners unable to reliably measure or compare agent capabilities on tasks resembling actual research workflows. - How it does it: Queries are reverse-constructed from pre-verified evidential documents — the answer path is determined first, then the query is synthesized to require joint reasoning over those sources — eliminating answerability ambiguity while preserving realistic task complexity; sandbox corpora categorize documents as Supportive, Distractor, or Noise at five controlled context lengths (32k–512k tokens). - The good: A 10,000-iteration bootstrap analysis confirms statistically significant score separation between the top two models (Wilcoxon p=0.0046), and the automated LLM-judge scores correlate with four-expert human judgment at r=0.78/ρ=0.73 (pairwise agreement 0.89), approaching inter-human agreement of r=0.83/ρ=0.76. - The missing: With only 100 tasks sourced exclusively from undergraduate and graduate students, sub-domain coverage is thin (fewer than 10 tasks per of 13 sub-domains), and expert-level research workflows (medical, legal, financial) are untested. - Read it or not: Researchers building or evaluating deep research agents should read this paper for its reproducible evaluation protocol and its empirically grounded finding that hallucination — not retrieval failure — is the dominant bottleneck for current frontier LLMs in long-horizon research tasks. Problem & Motivation Deep Research Agents must autonomously plan, retrieve from noisy heterogeneous web sources, parse multimodal content (text, images, video, audio, spreadsheets), and synthesize citation-grounded long-form reports. Evaluating this capability reliably is unsolved. Live-web benchmarks (e.g., DeepResearch Bench) produce temporally unstable scores because web content changes between runs. Offline text-only benchmarks (DRBench, DeepResearchGym) omit multimodal user-provided files and the three-tier noise structure (genuinely useful evidence vs. thematically related but misleading distractors vs. random noise) that characterizes real research environments. Open-ended queries in prior work often have multiple plausible answer paths or are directly solvable via single-step web search, making it impossible to assess an agent's multi-hop synthesis capability. No prior benchmark simultaneously satisfies realism (authentic user files, realistic noise), controllability (static, verifiable sandbox), and multimodality across diverse domains. Key Idea The core technical insight is reverse query construction: instead of posing a query and hoping relevant evidence exists somewhere on the web, the benchmark assembles the evidential documents first (user files + verified supporting web pages), then uses an LLM to synthesize a query that naturally requires all those sources for a complete answer without directly exposing the target keywords. This guarantees every task has a unique, verifiable solution path rooted in the sandbox, while the query remains natural-sounding and implicitly guiding rather than explicitly hinting. The divergent-convergent keyword generation (broad exploration → signal/noise classification) then populates the sandbox with documents that are semantically adjacent but informationally misleading, making retrieval strategy genuinely consequential. Methodology Benchmark construction proceeds in five stages. Stage 1: 280 candidate multimodal document sets collected from paid student volunteers (50 English, 50 Chinese, covering Technology/Economy/Humanities across 13 sub-domains), with two-stage PII sanitization. Stage 2: Gemini-2.5-Pro performs divergent-convergent keyword generation producing 10 candidate keywords per task, classified into signal keywords (guide toward the answer) and noise keywords (thematically plausible but misleading). Stage 3: Up to 100 web pages retrieved per keyword, crawled, cleaned, and categorized as Supportive (manually verified as necessary evidence), Distractor (outdated or one-sided), or Noise; five sandbox sizes built by proportionally adding distractors and noise to always include the complete supportive set. Stage 4: Reverse query synthesis conditioned on evidential documents and signal keywords, enforcing that the answer requires joint reasoning over user files and web evidence. Stage 5: Four-filter QC funnel (Implicit Guidance, Synthesis Necessity via leave-one-out, Insight Novelty, Interpretive Unambiguity) reduces 280 candidates to 100 tasks (35.7% pass rate). Evaluation uses five metrics across two dimensions. Information-seeking: IR_UF and IR_SC (fraction of manually extracted gold insights from user files and sandbox respectively that are fully covered in the generated report, scored 0/0.5/1 by GPT-5.1); Citation Coverage (CC = |D_req ∩ D_cited| / |D_req|). Report-generation: Factual Accuracy (FA = mean entailment score over all claim-source pairs in the report, verified by GPT-5.1 for text and Gemini-2.5-Pro for multimodal); Instruction Following (IF = binary checklist compliance); Depth Quality (DQ = LLM-as-judge score on analytical substance against a predefined rubric). The DR³-Agent used in experiments is a hierarchical multi-agent system: a Main Agent running Plan-Act-Observe with integrated perception tools for video/audio; a RAG sub-agent using ReAct-style iterative dense retrieval (text-embedding-3-small) over the sandbox; and a File Reader sub-agent for page-level document parsing. Key Results Eight models were evaluated (GPT-4.1, Claude Sonnet 4, Gemini-2.5-Pro, GLM-4.6, GLM-4.7, Qwen3-235B-A22B, Qwen3-30B-A3B, Qwen3-32B) through the DR³-Agent wrapper. Claude Sonnet 4 achieves the highest average at 512k context (65.6), followed by GLM-4.7 (48.3) — a large gap. At 64k context GLM-4.7 (70.3) and Claude Sonnet 4 (70.7) are near-tied, indicating GLM-4.7 degrades severely with longer contexts. All models show monotonic score decline as sandbox size grows from 64k to 512k, with IR_SC and CC showing the steepest drops. A critical cross-metric finding: Qwen3-235B-A22B achieves IF=73.5 (64k) but FA=49.4 (64k), demonstrating that format compliance is decoupled from factual grounding. Error attribution across 100 reports per model reveals hallucination as the dominant failure mode for Gemini-2.5-Pro (~65% of errors), Claude Sonnet 4 (~71%), and Qwen3 models (~72–77%), while retrieval error dominates for GLM-4.7 (~48%). Sandbox-vs-live-web comparison on an English subset shows overall performance differences of −0.3 (Qwen3-235B) and +0.7 (Gemini-2.5-Pro), with particularly high citation-coverage consistency, validating the sandbox as a reliable substitute. DR³-Agent outperforms DeerFlow (when given the same RAG component) on fragmented user-file integration. Judge model ranking stability is high: alternative judges (Gemini-2.5-Pro, Qwen-Max) produce at most one rank swap from GPT-5.1, mean Spearman ρ=0.924. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine novelty lies in the combination of (a) real multimodal user-provided files as task input, (b) per-task controlled three-tier sandbox corpora with systematic signal-to-noise scaling, and (c) reverse construction to guarantee unambiguous verifiability. The five-metric evaluation framework assembles existing ideas (nugget recall from CRAG, checklist-based IF, LLM-as-judge) rather than contributing fundamentally new evaluation methodology. The diagnostic finding that hallucination dominates over retrieval failure across frontier models in a controlled multimodal research setting is a new empirical result with implications for DRA development priorities. Rigor & Reproducibility Code and data are publicly released at https://github.com/NJU-LINK/DR3-Eval. All gold insights are manually verified; citation ground truth is manually annotated. Repeated runs for three models (Claude Sonnet 4, GLM-4.7, GLM-4.6) yield standard deviations of 0.83, 0.85, and 1.33 respectively, indicating stable scoring. Bootstrap significance (10,000 iterations, Wilcoxon p=0.0046) validates model ranking at the top. Judge consistency is tested across three LLMs. Human correlation is measured on 30 sampled reports reviewed by four independent experts. Claim extraction for FA achieves precision 0.924 and recall 0.960 against human annotations. One methodological concern: the FA verification prompt is explicitly designed to prefer false positives over false negatives ("lenient standard, only flag obvious errors"), which may systematically understate factual error rates and is potentially in tension with the paper's conclusion that hallucination is the dominant failure mode — the observed FA scores may be inflated relative to stricter standards. The 100-task scale is small; per-sub-domain breakdowns (fewer than 10 tasks each) are too sparse for reliable sub-domain conclusions. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — a built benchmark and evaluation pipeline evaluated by deployment against multiple LLMs. - Practical: The benchmark runs entirely offline after corpus construction, with API costs of approximately $0.30–$1.00 per inference task and $0.10 per evaluation task on commercially available LLM APIs. - Efficient: Inference takes 300–400 seconds per task (5–7 minutes), evaluation adds 90 seconds; the full pipeline is feasible for research labs but not for rapid iteration — 100 tasks at 512k context takes several hours end-to-end. - Effective: The benchmark produces statistically validated model rankings, surfaces a meaningful IF/FA decoupling diagnostic, and sandbox-vs-web validation confirms ecological validity for evidence-chain alignment. - Scalable: The benchmark is currently 100 tasks; the 512k-token sandbox (averaging 465.5 web pages) already strains current frontier context windows, and the paper does not demonstrate scaling the task count or corpus size beyond these settings. The paper delivers a software artifact (benchmark dataset, evaluation scripts, DR³-Agent code) via public GitHub. Strengths - The reverse-construction methodology is a principled solution to the answerability ambiguity problem in DRA evaluation, and the four-filter QC funnel is more rigorous than most benchmark construction pipelines. - The three-tier document taxonomy (Supportive/Distractor/Noise) with controlled proportional scaling is more principled than prior work that distinguishes only relevant/irrelevant, enabling systematic analysis of how signal-to-noise ratio affects performance. - The empirical finding that hallucination dominates over retrieval failure for most frontier models is a concrete, actionable diagnostic: it redirects development attention from retrieval pipelines toward faithfulness constraints and evidence-grounded generation. - Evaluation robustness is unusually well-validated for a benchmark paper: three runs per model, three alternative judge LLMs, human correlation on 30 sampled reports, and bootstrap significance testing are all reported. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note that the sandbox is a static snapshot (potentially becoming outdated), that longer contexts degrade performance across all models (a current LLM constraint), and that performance varies significantly across domains. They acknowledge the benchmark covers only three broad topic areas and note the dependence on Gemini-2.5-Pro for keyword generation. The paper confronts these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive, though the implications for generalizability are not fully explored. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All user files were sourced from undergraduate and graduate students rather than domain experts; expert research workflows (medical literature review, legal discovery, scientific hypothesis generation) likely differ in information density, cross-source contradiction patterns, and required synthesis depth — the benchmark's task difficulty may not reflect real professional DRA use cases. - The DR³-Agent was co-designed with the benchmark it is used to evaluate, creating a risk that design choices in both were mutually optimized; results for the agent should be treated cautiously as performance upper-bounds may be inflated. - The paper evaluates base LLMs through the DR³-Agent wrapper rather than end-to-end DRA products (OpenAI Deep Research, Gemini Deep Research, Perplexity Pro Research), despite framing motivation around those systems — making it hard to know how commercial DRA products would actually score. - The lenient FA prompt ("prefer false positives") produces measured FA scores in the 80–87% range even for models with known hallucination problems, which appears inconsistent with the error attribution showing hallucination as the dominant failure mode; the two metrics are measuring different populations (FA validates claims the model chose to cite, while error attribution counts all errors including uncited fabrications). Bottom Line This preprint (not yet peer-reviewed) delivers the most reproducible and methodologically grounded DRA benchmark to date for multimodal report-generation; researchers actively evaluating or building deep research agents should read it, but should be cautious about the 100-task scale and the leniently calibrated factual-accuracy metric when interpreting absolute numbers.
Recommended 2604.14683 PDF
#55 — Innovative (72)

Toward Autonomous Long-Horizon Engineering for ML Research (23 ▲ )

Guoxin Chen, Jie Chen, Lei Chen, Jiale Zhao, Fanzhe Meng, Wayne Xin Zhao, Ruihua Song, Cheng Chen, Ji-Rong Wen, Kai Jia · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Impact (80) strong — GitHub release, concrete cost-per-task numbers, 2× PaperBench improvement over baselines at lower cost, competitive on MLE-Bench Lite. Novelty (68) — File-as-Bus protocol is a genuinely new architectural principle for multi-agent coordination, though hierarchical decomposition itself extends prior work. Rigor (62) held back by no error bars on 20-task runs, factorially incomplete ablation (hierarchy vs. File-as-Bus not cleanly separated), GPT-5.4 grader dependency, two frontier-only backbones. Non-Obviousness (75) — the 31.82-point ablation identifying state continuity (not interaction rounds) as the bottleneck is a sharp, actionable insight. Completeness (68) and Resilience (65) limited by missing time-budget sensitivity and absence of variance estimates. Weighted: 0.15*68 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*65 + 0.30*80 ≈ 72.
systems Novelty 68 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 68 Resilience 65 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AiScientist, a multi-agent system for autonomous long-horizon ML research engineering that combines hierarchical orchestration with a File-as-Bus shared workspace protocol for durable state continuity across hours-long implementation-and-debugging loops. - Why it's important: Existing autonomous agents fail at multi-day ML engineering tasks because project state (paper analyses, code, experiment logs) degrades across conversational handoffs, preventing coherent progress; the best prior agent achieves only 21% on PaperBench's replication rubric versus 41% for human ML PhDs. - How it does it: The system separates "thin control" (a lightweight orchestrator that reasons from concise summaries and a compact workspace map) from "thick state" (durable structured files that downstream agents repeatedly re-ground on via targeted reads), using a permission-scoped filesystem as the coordination substrate rather than conversational context. - The good: Removing File-as-Bus causes a 31.82-point drop in MLE-Bench Lite Any Medal%, empirically demonstrating that durable state continuity — not additional interaction rounds — is the primary bottleneck in long-horizon ML engineering performance. - The missing: All experiments use a fixed 24-hour/one-H20-GPU budget with only two backbone LLMs (Gemini-3-Flash, GLM-5); no sensitivity analysis on performance versus time budget and no evaluation with weaker or open-weight models leaves the generality of gains unknown. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems researchers and anyone designing multi-agent engineering pipelines — the File-as-Bus insight is concrete, the ablations are quantitatively persuasive, and the benchmark setup provides a reproducible evaluation protocol. Problem & Motivation: Automating the full loop of ML research engineering — turning a paper into a runnable, validated reproduction by autonomously handling environment setup, implementation, experimentation, debugging, and iterative refinement over hours or days — remains unsolved. The core failure mode is state fragmentation: critical intermediate context (paper analyses, plans, code decisions, execution logs) is either lost across agent invocations or must be re-derived from scratch each round. Delayed and confounded experimental feedback compounds this: failures may stem from interpretation errors, infrastructure issues, or implementation bugs simultaneously, and only surface after runs complete. Prior multi-agent systems rely primarily on conversational handoffs for coordination, yielding progressively lossy representations of accumulated project state. The gap is visible in rigorous evaluation: even the best reported agent achieves 21% on PaperBench's replication rubric, against 41% for human ML PhDs under a 48-hour budget. Key Idea: Long-horizon ML engineering performance is a joint optimization over orchestration structure and state continuity. The core insight — once understood, sufficient to reconstruct the approach — is that the shared filesystem should serve as the primary coordination substrate ("File-as-Bus") rather than conversational context: agents re-ground on durable artifacts that persist across invocations, keeping control thin (lightweight orchestrator operates on a compact workspace map plus concise summaries) while state stays thick (structured paper analyses, prioritized task lists, implementation logs, and experiment records accumulate as files that any later agent can read on demand). Methodology: AiScientist uses a three-tier hierarchy. Tier-0 Orchestrator receives a stage-level control context and a compact workspace map m_t = M(W_t), a textual index of major artifact regions, and selects the next action from its native tool set or the Tier-1 specialist pool; it does not carry the full workspace in its active context. Tier-1 Specialists (Paper Comprehension, Prioritization, Implementation, Experimentation, Generic Helper) are re-initialized per invocation: each receives the orchestrator's directive and the workspace map, reads task-relevant artifacts on demand, executes its local loop potentially spawning Tier-2 subagents for focused leaf tasks, and returns a concise summary plus workspace updates (Δ W_t). Tier-2 subagents handle structure extraction, environment setup, and similar bounded subtasks without spawning further depth. The workspace partitions state into three role-aligned regions: paper_analysis/ (structured paper understanding, target metrics, implementation ambiguities), submission/ (runnable reproduction repo with source code, config, and reproduce.sh), and agent/ (prioritized_task.md, plan.md, impl_log.md, exp_log.md). Permission scoping enforces that each specialist writes only to required regions; shared logs are append-only and iteration-structured. The evidence-driven loop proceeds as: establish runnable scaffold, then iteratively implement, run, diagnose from durable execution traces, patch, and re-validate — all within the fixed 24-hour/one-H20-GPU budget. Three critical design decisions: (1) progressive disclosure via workspace map rather than full-workspace injection — well-justified by context length constraints and empirically ablated; (2) append-only logs with iteration structure — prevents cross-agent interference and preserves traceability; (3) Agent-as-Tool delegation — makes specialist invocation a native decision within the orchestrator's action space, not a mandatory protocol. Key Results: On PaperBench (20-task from-scratch paper replication benchmark), AiScientist scores 30.52 average with Gemini-3-Flash (+9.92 over the best matched baseline IterativeAgent at 20.60) and 33.73 with GLM-5 (+11.15 over IterativeAgent at 22.37), at costs of $15.67/task versus $27.44 for IterativeAgent under Gemini-3-Flash. AiScientist still trails the reported human baseline of approximately 41%. On MLE-Bench Lite (controlled evaluation, Any Medal% primary metric), AiScientist achieves 81.82% under both backbones, compared to LoongFlow 77.27% and AIDE 45.45%/40.91% — improvements of 4.55–18.18 points over the best matched baselines. Ablation: removing File-as-Bus drops PaperBench average by 6.41 points and MLE-Bench Lite Any Medal% by 31.82 points; the much larger drop on MLE-Bench Lite confirms File-as-Bus is especially critical for iterative refinement, not just initial scaffolding. Comparison to a simpler non-hierarchical agent baseline without File-as-Bus shows AiScientist still outperforms by 4.74 PaperBench points and 9.09 Above-Median points on MLE-Bench Lite, indicating hierarchical orchestration contributes independently of state continuity. In a case study on MLE-Bench Lite's Detecting Insults task, the system ran 74 experiment cycles over 23 hours, raising validation AUC from 0.903 to 0.982. Against official MLE-Bench leaderboard entries (unmatched comparisons), 81.82 Any Medal% exceeds the highest published result of 75.76%. No error bars or significance tests are reported across these results. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The File-as-Bus coordination protocol and its operationalization of "thin control over thick state" via workspace maps and permission-scoped artifact regions is a genuinely novel architectural contribution to multi-agent engineering systems. The empirical finding that durable state continuity — not additional interaction rounds — is the primary bottleneck (evidenced by IterativeAgent underperforming AiScientist despite having more interaction than BasicAgent) is a non-obvious and directly actionable result. The hierarchical multi-agent decomposition extends existing paradigms (CAMEL, MetaGPT, ChatDev) rather than inventing new ones; the combination applied to long-horizon ML research engineering is the novel instantiation. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and quantitatively informative: File-as-Bus removal and comparison to simpler agent organizations both show clear, large-magnitude impacts.
