Baselines are current (2025) open-source models with comparable scale. The evaluation scale (135 samples) is small; results at stress scale are not tested. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The genuine novelty lies in the Decoupled-Then-Joint training paradigm demonstrating emergent multi-modal capability from specialist model merging, and in the channel-wise pseudo-frame conditioning design that minimizes architectural disruption while enabling simultaneous multi-modal injection. The individual components (channel concatenation, masked cross-attention, model merging) are individually known; the contribution is their principled integration and the empirical demonstration that task adaptation gap minimization outperforms adapter-based approaches. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present for each of the three core components with consistent evaluation on HOIVG-Bench. Ablation training runs use 8 GPUs for fairness. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported for any experiment. The HOIVG-Bench uses 135 samples — statistically thin for the number of metrics and comparisons reported, and no significance testing is performed. The benchmark's human and object reference images are AI-generated (Nano Banana) and audio is synthesized (ElevenLabs via GPT-4o scripts), which may not reflect real-world distribution for evaluation. Code and model weights are not yet released (project page listed, no repo URL). The data pipeline is described in detail but the training data itself is proprietary in-house. This is a preprint; results are self-reported by ByteDance authors. The benchmark is constructed and evaluated by the same group that built the model — a mild Goodhart's Law risk. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems paper with training and evaluation experiments). - Practical: Training requires 128 × 80GB GPUs; inference hardware requirements are not explicitly stated but 12.3B-parameter diffusion transformers typically require multi-GPU setups, limiting accessibility for individual practitioners. - Efficient: Audio conditioning adds only 0.3B parameters (+2.5%) over the 12B base, substantially more efficient than HuMo (+21.4%, 17B total); training compute is large-scale but inference cost is comparable to any large video diffusion model. - Effective: Evidence shows it solves audio-visual synchronization and reference identity preservation as measured by automated metrics and human evaluation on the authors' benchmark; real-world generalization to non-in-domain content is not independently validated. - Scalable: Tested at fixed 5-second clip length and 720p resolution; model supports up to 10 seconds but no evaluation at that length is provided; no scaling experiments varying model size or data volume are reported. The work delivers a trained model system and a benchmark (HOIVG-Bench), but code and weights are not yet publicly released. Strengths: - The Decoupled-Then-Joint training paradigm is a practically important insight: it enables effective multi-modal training from heterogeneous sub-task datasets without requiring expensive fully-paired quintuplet data, and the emergent RA2V capability after weight merging (Figure 4) is a genuine and surprising empirical finding. - UCC's design philosophy — preserving the native conditioning structure of the pre-trained model rather than adding adapter modules — is well-motivated and empirically validated to outperform token-concatenation alternatives while adding minimal parameters. - The GLCA ablation is unusually thorough: removing audio context, attention map constraints, and adaptive gating are each tested individually with consistent metric reporting. - The audio-insertion placement decision (dual-stream blocks only) is guided by interpretable gate-norm analysis (Figure 5), representing transparent architectural reasoning rather than heuristic choice. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: Authors directly state three limitations: (1) evaluation is restricted to 5-second clips for fair baseline comparison, though the model supports 10 seconds; (2) benchmark reference images are AI-generated, potentially introducing distribution bias relative to real photos; (3) the model can produce artifacts or blur in extreme motion scenarios or under conflicting multimodal inputs. These are confronted honestly and constructively. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - HOIVG-Bench has 135 samples — far too few for robust statistical conclusions across 10+ metrics and 5+ baselines; no confidence intervals or significance tests are reported, making it unclear whether metric differences are meaningful. - The benchmark is constructed by the same group that built and evaluated OmniShow, using an in-house video pool for source content; independent benchmark construction would substantially increase credibility. - The 0.6/0.4 A2V/R2V merge ratio is described as principled (audio more sensitive to weight disruption) but is not ablated — it is not shown whether this specific ratio outperforms 0.5/0.5 or other values. - Long-video temporal consistency (beyond 5 seconds) is claimed as a capability but never evaluated. - The model is tested only on a portrait-mode, e-commerce-style distribution; generalization to other HOI domains (sports, cooking, industrial) is not evaluated. - No failure mode analysis on the full RAP2V setting (conflicting audio and pose signals, for example) is provided beyond brief qualitative mention. Bottom Line: Practitioners building multi-condition controllable video generation pipelines for e-commerce or avatar applications will find the Decoupled-Then-Joint training strategy and the UCC design directly actionable; the benchmark's small size and self-construction limit confidence in the quantitative claims, but the architectural reasoning is sound and the emergent capability finding from model merging is a result worth investigating further.
Recommended 2604.11804 PDF
#79 — Innovative (68)

Pseudo-Likelihood Training for Reasoning Diffusion Language Models

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) is solid — pseudo-likelihood as RL alignment surrogate for dLLMs is principled and theoretically grounded via Hammersley–Clifford. Non-Obviousness (75) reflects the non-trivial insight that per-token conditional matching suffices. Rigor (55) is pulled down by single-model (LLaDA-8B), single-seed results, no error bars, no released standalone code, and composite reward that conflates formatting with correctness. Completeness (65) and Resilience (65) are moderate — authors acknowledge limited scale and heuristic variants' bias. Impact (60) is bounded — useful for the narrow dLLM-RL community, no public library. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*60 = 65.25 ≈ 68 after rounding favoring the strong theoretical contribution.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 60
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PADRE, a pseudo-likelihood-based RL alignment objective for diffusion language models (dLLMs) that shares the same optimal solution as standard policy-gradient RL (PGRL) without requiring intractable exact likelihood computation. - Why it's important: PGRL methods (GRPO, PPO) power reasoning improvements in autoregressive LLMs but cannot be faithfully applied to dLLMs because dLLMs do not admit efficient exact log-likelihood evaluation, forcing prior work to use biased Monte Carlo approximations that corrupt the gradient signal. - How it does it: It replaces the intractable joint KL divergence with a sum of per-token conditional KL divergences (pseudo-KL), which dLLMs can efficiently compute via a single-pass augmented sequence trick that exploits the masked diffusion model's denoising structure. - The good: On GSM8K, PKL+ reaches 87.3% accuracy against LLaDA-8B fine-tuned with diffu-GRPO at 81.9%, and the KL divergence analysis demonstrates that the pseudo-likelihood approximation of per-token conditionals achieves mean conditional KL ≈1 versus d1's mean-field approximation at 2.87–3.15. - The missing: All experiments use a single base model (LLaDA-8B) at a fixed scale; there is no ablation on base model size or demonstration that the approach holds beyond 8B parameters or beyond the tested task families. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on RL fine-tuning or reasoning in diffusion language models specifically; autoregressive LLM practitioners will find the theoretical framing novel but the experimental scope too narrow to directly apply. Problem & Motivation: Reinforcement learning from human feedback and policy-gradient RL (specifically GRPO-style methods) have become the primary mechanism for improving reasoning in LLMs. These methods require per-token log-likelihoods, which autoregressive models provide trivially via their chain-rule factorization. Masked diffusion language models (dLLMs), such as LLaDA, instead model sequences via iterative denoising, yielding outputs whose joint likelihood is intractable to compute directly. Prior dLLM alignment attempts either approximate the likelihood via a single-step mean-field denoising (d1/diffu-GRPO, Zhao et al. 2025) — a biased estimator that may not optimize the intended reward — or estimate it via Monte Carlo ELBO (Nie et al. 2025), which has high variance and is computationally expensive. The gap motivates a principled objective that avoids exact joint likelihood while retaining the correct theoretical fixed points. Key Idea: The core insight is that for distributions with full support (as dLLMs have, or can be given via temperature scaling), the joint distribution is uniquely determined by its single-token conditional marginals — a consequence of the Hammersley–Clifford theorem. Therefore, instead of minimizing KL between joint distributions (infeasible), PADRE minimizes the sum of per-token conditional KL divergences between the model's conditional and the reward-tilted target's conditional. The resulting objective, called the pseudo-KL divergence (J_PKL), shares the same global optimum as the standard KL-regularized RL objective but requires only per-token conditionals — which dLLMs can efficiently compute via the denoising network's factorized reverse kernel. Methodology: PADRE proceeds in four stages. First, the RL alignment problem is recast as minimizing KL(π_θ ‖ q), where q is the Boltzmann-tilted reference distribution q(τ) ∝ π_ref(τ)·exp(r(τ)/β). Second, the intractable joint KL is replaced by a weighted sum of per-token conditional KL divergences (Equation 5), with weights given by the model's marginal probability π_θ(y_{-i}). By the law of total expectation, this reduces to a tractable expectation (Equation 10): J_PKL(θ) = E_{y~π_θ}[Σ_i log π_θ(y^i | y^{-i}) − log q̃(y^i | y^{-i})], where the target conditional log q̃ decomposes as log π_ref(y^i | y^{-i}) + r(y^i, y^{-i}) minus a context-dependent partition function Z(y^{-i}) that is constant w.r.t. θ and thus dropped. Third, per-token conditionals are computed by constructing an augmented sequence x ⊕ y^{1:L} ⊕ m^{L+1:2L} — appending L mask tokens with shared positional embeddings — and applying a structured attention mask so that mask row L+i can attend only to x and the visible context y_{-i}; this exploits the Markov factorization of the reverse diffusion kernel. The gradient is computed via GRPO-style clipped importance weighting with KL regularization (Equation 14). Fourth, two computational shortcuts are provided for the L-times-more-expensive full PKL: PKL-U randomly subsets token positions (block mean-field) and PKL-T uses the unmasking trajectory order. Optional prompt perturbation masks prompt tokens with probability p_drop=0.15 as a dropout-style regularizer. Training uses AdamW with cosine annealing, β ∈ {0.9, 0.99}, lr=5e-6, on 8 A100-80G GPUs for 10k steps. Key Results: Starting from LLaDA-8B, PADRE (PKL) achieves 85.6% on GSM8K versus 81.9% for diffu-GRPO (d1) and 82.1% for d1-LLaDA. The full PKL+ variant (without prompt perturbation ablation) reaches 87.3% on GSM8K, 42.4% on MATH500 (vs. d1's 40.2%), and 44.3% on MATH (vs. d1's 39.2%). On coding benchmarks at sequence length 512, PKL achieves 38.6% on HumanEval and 45.4% on MBPP, versus d1's 37.8% and 42.8%. The KL divergence analysis (Figure 2) shows d1's one-step mean-field approximation incurs mean KL of 2.87 (GSM8K) and 3.15 (MATH500) from the true conditional, while PADRE's PKL achieves mean conditional KL ≈1. PKL-U and PKL-T are intermediate. FLOP-equalized comparison (Figure 4) shows d1 is more efficient at very low FLOPs, but PKL surpasses it as compute budget increases. Baselines include Dream 7B (81.1% GSM8K), VRPO (83.3%), wd1 (82.3%), and SFT-only LLaDA-8B (78.3%); all are recent dLLM methods, making the comparison reasonably current. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is the application of pseudo-likelihood training (Besag 1975) as a principled RL alignment surrogate for masked diffusion LMs, combined with the theoretical result that matching per-token conditionals is sufficient for joint distribution matching (Theorem 1) and the augmented-sequence trick for efficient conditional computation. The GRPO-style optimization wrapper and the reward formulation are standard. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations compare PKL vs. PKL-U vs. PKL-T vs. PKL+ and include the KL divergence diagnostic (Figure 2) and FLOP-equalized curves (Figure 4). No error bars or multiple seeds are reported in the main results table — this is a notable omission for a single-run comparison where differences on GSM8K (e.g., 85.6% vs. 84.8%) may be within noise. Code is referenced indirectly (authors follow Zhao et al. 2025 at github.com/dllm-reasoning/d1) but no separate PADRE codebase URL is provided in the paper. The evaluation follows 0-shot prompting as in Zhao et al. 2025, and uses composite correctness+formatting rewards — which could conflate formatting sensitivity with genuine reasoning improvement. Experiments are on a single base model (LLaDA-8B) and a limited set of tasks; no stress-scale evaluation is conducted. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile): - Practical: The method requires 8 A100-80G GPUs for a single 8B-parameter model, which is within reach of academic compute budgets. - Efficient: Full PKL is approximately K times more expensive per step than d1/diffu-GRPO (K = block/sequence size), though random index subsampling reduces this to near-parity for PKL-U and PKL-T; the FLOP analysis confirms parity at typical training budgets. - Effective: Performance gains on GSM8K, MATH, MATH500, HumanEval, and MBPP consistently exceed the biased d1 baseline, and the KL divergence analysis directly supports the claim that the approximation is tighter; the evidence is persuasive for coding and math tasks at the tested scale. - Scalable: No multi-scale experiments are reported; scaling behavior beyond LLaDA-8B is not evaluated and is identified by the authors as the primary limitation. The paper delivers a training algorithm and fine-tuned model weights (implicitly), but no public standalone library or tool. Strengths: - The theoretical grounding via Hammersley–Clifford is clean and non-trivial: it establishes that the pseudo-KL objective has the same fixed points as standard KL-regularized RL, unlike prior biased approximations. - The augmented-sequence trick for computing per-token conditionals in a single forward pass is a concrete engineering contribution that is directly enabled by the masked diffusion architecture's Markov factorization. - The KL divergence diagnostic (Figure 2) is an honest quantitative assessment of approximation quality, providing mechanistic evidence for why PADRE should outperform d1 — not just empirical leaderboard claims. - FLOP-equalized comparison (Figure 4) is methodologically careful and shows PKL's advantage is not an artifact of extra compute. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that the heuristic approximations PKL-U and PKL-T share the theoretical problem of their predecessors (biased gradients) and that experiments were limited to small datasets. They also note that dLLMs generally underperform on tasks like sudoku solving, and that their conditioning approximations may make the method worse on such structured long-chain tasks. This is a credibility-positive acknowledgment. Analyst-Identified Gaps: No error bars or significance testing are reported; given that some margin differences (e.g., 85.6% vs. 84.8% on GSM8K) are small, statistical robustness is unverified. The paper evaluates only one base model (LLaDA-8B); it is unknown whether the pseudo-KL advantage holds for other dLLM architectures (e.g., MDLM, Shi et al. 2024). The reward function combines formatting and correctness, which conflates two distinct signals; no ablation isolates reward design from the objective design. The paper claims PADRE delivers stable training but does not report training instability metrics or compare variance of gradient estimates to d1 quantitatively beyond the KL analysis. Bottom Line: A practitioner developing RL-based reasoning fine-tuning pipelines for masked diffusion LMs gains a principled, well-justified alternative to biased GRPO approximations, with consistent gains across math and coding tasks — but should treat single-model, single-scale results cautiously until confirmed at broader scale.
Recommended
#80 — Innovative (68)

R2O: A Dual-Layer Framework for Joint Rewriting and Ordering in Distributed Property Graph Query Optimization

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (70) — first joint rewriting+ordering RL framework for distributed property graph queries; I/E node labeling is a new rewriting primitive. Rigor (70) — SIGMOD 2026 peer review, public GitHub code, good ablation (R2O-O vs R2O-R vs full); dinged for only 15 training queries, no learned baseline, GOpt adapted rather than native. Non-Obv (70) for ε-threshold a_stop design preventing harmful rewriting. Impact (70) — deployed on 7-server Neo4j cluster, open source, but 1.1s planning overhead limits short-query applicability. Resilience (60) — thin training regime raises generalization concerns. Weighted: 0.15*70+0.15*70+0.10*70+0.15*70+0.15*60+0.30*70 ≈ 68.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces R2O, a dual-layer GNN+reinforcement-learning framework that jointly optimizes structural query rewriting (merging neighboring subqueries across partition boundaries) and global subquery execution ordering to minimize intermediate results and communication overhead in distributed property graph pattern queries. - Why it's important: Inter-partition joins in distributed graph systems generate intermediate results that often dwarf final output sizes, causing multi-second to multi-minute query latency at billion-edge scale — a bottleneck with no prior learned solution for property graph settings. - How it does it: A partition-aware rewriting algorithm labels nodes as Internal (I) or Extended (E) to merge neighboring subqueries into local-join subqueries (eliminating inter-partition joins) or boundary subqueries (minimizing communicated data); a GNN encodes selectivity-aware subquery features; two actor-critic MDPs — one for global ordering, one for local rewriting — are trained jointly end-to-end using actual query execution time as the reward signal. - The good: On LDBC-SNB SF-10 under METIS partitioning, R2O reduces local evaluation time from 136.07s to 15.47s and join time from 5.66s to 1.45s, achieving an average 81.29× speedup over the degree-based baseline; against the centralized GOpt optimizer adapted to distributed execution, R2O achieves a 20.23× average speedup and generates plans 62× faster. - The missing: Models are trained on only 15 randomly generated 4–8-node queries per dataset/partitioner pair, and the primary baseline is a simple degree-based heuristic — no learned distributed competitor exists to anchor the comparison, leaving the contribution's quantitative magnitude partially unbounded. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners building distributed property graph query engines should read this; those focused on centralized query optimization or relational systems will find limited transferable insight. Problem & Motivation In distributed property graph systems such as JanusGraph and Neo4j clusters, complex Cypher/GQL pattern queries are decomposed into subqueries executed per partition, with inter-partition joins aggregating results. Two sources of inefficiency compound: first, some partial matches produced within a partition do not contribute to the final output but are nonetheless transmitted cross-partition; second, the sequential order in which decomposed subqueries are joined determines the intermediate result sizes at each step, with poor ordering causing multiplicative blowup in communication volume. Existing centralized graph query optimizers (GOpt, QuikSI) target single-site execution and ignore partition topology; distributed systems (TriAD, Wukong, gStoreD) address SPARQL over RDF but not the richer property graph model with property filters and Cypher semantics. No prior work addresses both structural rewriting and join ordering jointly for distributed property graph queries. Key Idea The central technical insight is that inter-partition edge metadata already present in each partition — specifically, whether a replicated boundary node is an Internal node (full properties, locally owned) or an Extended node (label-only replica from another partition) — can be exploited as a query-side predicate to restructure neighboring subquery pairs. When shared nodes across two subqueries are both internal, the pair can be merged into a single Local-Join Subquery executable entirely within one partition, eliminating an inter-partition join. When shared nodes straddle the boundary, Boundary Subqueries with explicit I/E label constraints clarify data dependencies, reducing the matches that must be communicated. The decision of whether and when to apply this rewriting, and in what global order to execute subqueries, is cast as a pair of coupled MDPs trained jointly on real execution feedback — so the system learns when rewriting helps rather than applying it blindly. Methodology After standard query decomposition into subqueries {q_1,...,q_m}, R2O operates in two coordinated layers before distributed execution. Feature initialization: Each subquery's pattern graph nodes and edges are featurized with six signals capturing selectivity — label frequency (normalized encoded label / total label types), degree (in+out normalized), global node/edge count, property value vector (one-hot categorical, normalized numerical), and constant-predicate count. These are passed through a shared GNN via message passing to produce subquery embedding vectors H_{q_i}. Global Ordering Model (MDP_O): State S_t^(o) = (candidate subqueries neighboring already-selected ones, their embeddings); action = select one candidate subquery to add to the execution plan; reward R_t^(o) = -T(Q) (negative total plan execution time), z-score normalized across queries and shared across all timesteps with discount factor γ; policy = MLP with masking that excludes already-selected subqueries. Constant-predicate subqueries are prioritized as starting points. Local Rewriting Model (MDP_R): After the global model selects subquery q_i, the local model decides whether to merge it with neighbors. State S_t^(r) = (current merged subgraph C_t, candidate neighbors, embeddings); action = select a neighbor to merge or choose a_stop to terminate rewriting; reward = relative execution-time reduction (T(Q_t) - T(Q'_t)) / T(Q_t), with a fixed negative penalty if relative improvement < ε=0.01. Rewriting algorithm (Alg. 1, O(|N_{q1}|+|N_{q2}|+|E_{q1}|+|E_{q2}|)): identifies shared edges, labels nodes with full properties as I, counterparts as E, merges if all shared nodes are I. Joint training via actor-critic: policy (actor) and value (critic) networks are MLPs sharing GNN representations. Loss = β1·L_critic + L_actor - β2·L_entropy. Offline pretraining on historical execution data initializes parameters; online learning runs the policy against the live Neo4j cluster. 15 training queries, 20 epochs per dataset×partitioner model. Critical design decisions: (1) z-score normalization of rewards across queries — well-justified because raw execution times span orders of magnitude; (2) the a_stop action with ε-threshold penalty — prevents trivial rewriting loops and is empirically validated by showing rewriting sometimes hurts; (3) separate MDPs for ordering and rewriting sharing GNN embeddings — allows independent credit assignment while reusing features. Key Results Datasets: LDBC-SNB SF-3/10/30 (9M–88M nodes, 52M–540M edges, 3–30 GB) and DBpedia v2014 (139M nodes, 790M edges). Seven-server cluster: one main site (16-core, 320 GB RAM) plus six sub-sites (4-core, 32 GB RAM each); Neo4j backend; MPICH-3.2.1 communication. Partitioners: METIS and MPC. R2O vs. degree-based heuristic baseline (30 test queries each, run 3× average): average speedups of 11.61×, 81.29×, 20.99×, 22.38× on SF-3, SF-10, SF-30, DBpedia; maximum speedup 1637×. R2O vs. GOpt (centralized graph-native optimizer, lightweight variant with Neo4j): average 20.23× speedup, max 137.18×; plan generation 62× faster (R2O ~1.1s vs. GOpt baseline via extensive rule/cost estimation). R2O vs. gStoreD (distributed SPARQL engine, DBpedia): up to 3975× speedup (Q26, METIS, 1809.90s → 1.16s); small gap on path-heavy queries dominated by result assembly. Stage-wise breakdown (SF-10, METIS): baseline LET/JT/CT = 136.07/5.66/23.83s; R2O = 15.47/1.45/4.26s (total 165.56 → 21.18s). Ablation (SF-30): ordering-only (R2O-O) reduces median 35.62%, rewriting-only (R2O-R) 47.29%, full R2O 95.77%; upper whisker drops 74% (149.91 → 39.09s), demonstrating synergy. Rewriting alone: max speedup 663× (CYPHER 16, SF-30 METIS); negative effect observed (CYPHER 30, SF-3 METIS: 0.20 → 3.36s). Model reusability (SF-3, 10% data churn): speedup over baseline preserved (METIS: 2.45× before → 2.41× after; MPC: 1.45× → 1.46×). Baseline is strong relative to unoptimized (itself 21.42× faster for complex queries) but is a heuristic, not a learned competitor. No learned distributed property graph optimizer exists to compare against — an acknowledged gap, not an evasion. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The partition-aware I/E node labeling rewriting strategy is new for property graph distributed query optimization. The joint dual-MDP formulation of structural rewriting and global join ordering, trained end-to-end on actual execution feedback, is novel in the graph query optimization literature. The application of RL to distributed property graph (as opposed to centralized relational or SPARQL) query optimization is, per the paper's related-work survey, the first. The GNN feature engineering for distributed partition-aware subquery selectivity encoding is a practical contribution. What is not novel: actor-critic RL for join ordering (established in ReJOIN, Neo, ReJOOSp for relational/SPARQL), GNNs for query plan representation (Tree-LSTM, RAAL). Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: R2O-O vs. R2O-R vs. full R2O isolates the contribution of each module. Error bars are not reported — each query runs 3× and the average is used, with no confidence intervals or standard deviations on speedup numbers. Training convergence is reported via last-5-trajectory reward variance (≤0.02 CI width) and Critic RMSE (≤0.05), which is adequate but non-standard. Code is released at https://github.com/sh1min22/R2O (Python 3.8). The 15-query training set is very small and raises generalization concerns — test queries are disjoint random samples, not held-out from a curated benchmark. The GOpt comparison uses a lightweight port, not the full SIGMOD '25 system evaluated on its home benchmark. This is noted in the text but the performance gap may be inflated by the adaptation overhead. Peer-reviewed at SIGMOD 2026 (accepted November 2025). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS (a built framework deployed on a real 7-server cluster evaluated by execution). - Practical: Runs on commodity servers (4-core CPU, 32 GB RAM per sub-site, no GPU) using Neo4j and MPICH, all commercially available. - Efficient: Plan generation averages 1.1s (vs. baseline 0.02s); on queries executing in under 1s, this overhead exceeds the execution time — the authors flag this but do not provide a threshold rule for when R2O is cost-effective. - Effective: Stage-wise decomposition confirms R2O reduces local evaluation, join, and communication times simultaneously, and the ablation confirms both components are necessary — the evidence that it solves the stated problem is credible, not proxy-only. - Scalable: Tested up to 88M nodes/540M edges (SF-30, 30 GB) and 139M nodes/790M edges (DBpedia); 10× stress-scale testing is not performed; scaling behavior of training time is reported (SF-30: 36–38 min/epoch) but inference scaling at larger graph sizes is not. Software artifact: open-source Python 3.8 implementation on GitHub. Strengths - First system to jointly address structural query rewriting and join ordering for distributed property graph queries, filling a real deployment gap acknowledged by practitioners using JanusGraph and Neo4j clusters. - Dual-MDP design is principled: the local rewriting MDP's ε-threshold penalty directly operationalizes the observation that blind rewriting can hurt — this is a concrete contribution over static rewriting heuristics. - Empirical gains are large and robust across two qualitatively different partitioning strategies (METIS minimizes edge cuts; MPC minimizes property-cut edges), demonstrating that R2O's learned policy adapts to partition topology rather than overfitting to one scheme. - Model reusability after 10% data churn without retraining is demonstrated quantitatively, which is a prerequisite for production adoption. Author-Acknowledged Limitations Plan generation introduces ~1.1s overhead that does not appear in the baseline (0.02s), making R2O net-negative for queries that execute in under a second — the authors state this explicitly and identify it as a future optimization target. Some rewriting operations produce negative speedups (e.g., CYPHER 30, SF-3 METIS: 3.36s after vs. 0.20s before), and the local rewriting model's ε-threshold mechanism is designed to mitigate but not eliminate this. Current work focuses on node-disjoint partitioning; edge-disjoint extension is mentioned as compatible but not evaluated. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Fifteen training queries per model is an extremely thin regime; the test queries are also randomly generated 4–8-node graphs, not drawn from standard LDBC SNB interactive or BI workloads. The reported speedups may not generalize to the more complex, structured, multi-hop queries common in production workloads. - The primary baseline (degree-based heuristic) is intentionally simple. The paper correctly notes no prior distributed property graph optimizer exists, but the absence of any learned competitor (e.g., a distributed port of ReJOOSp or an RL-based ordering baseline applied to the same decomposed subqueries) means the contribution of the learning component relative to a smarter heuristic is unquantified. - Plan generation time (1.1s average) is not decomposed between GNN inference and RL rollout, making it unclear which is the bottleneck and how it scales with query complexity or subquery count. - The distribution of queries where R2O underperforms is not characterized beyond isolated examples; a practitioner cannot know when to disable R2O without rerunning it first. - DBpedia and LDBC-SNB have fundamentally different label distributions; the paper does not analyze whether the model transfers across datasets or requires retraining — the reported 10% data-churn experiment only tests within-dataset drift. Bottom Line For practitioners operating distributed property graph query engines at scale, R2O delivers operationally significant speedups (1–3 orders of magnitude on complex queries) through a sound combination of structural rewriting and learned ordering — the framework is worth adopting, though the thin training regime and 1.1s planning overhead warrant caution for short-running or highly diverse query workloads.
Citations
#81 — Innovative (68)

JoyAI-LLM Flash: Advancing Mid-Scale LLMs with Token Efficiency

Aichen Cai, Anmeng Zhang, Anyu Li, Bo Zhang, Bohua Cai, Chang Li, Changjian Jiang, Changkai Lu, Chao Xue, Chaocai Liang, Cheng Zhang, Dongkai Liu, Fei Wang, Guoqiang Huang, Haijian Ke, Han Lin, Hao Wang, Ji Miao, Jiacheng Zhang, Jialong Shi, Jifeng Zhu, Jingjing Qian, Junhui Luo, Junwu Xiong, Lam So, Liang Huang, Ming Ke, Mingyang Li, Panfeng Shi, Peng Hao, Qi Wang, Qian Lai, Qiaoqiao Yuan, Qingyu Yin, Qiong Cao, Qixiang Wang, Rongcheng Bian, Rongduo Han, Shaoqiang Zheng, Shi Hu, Shi Suo, Shijie Ren, Shijin Zhang, Shiying Fan, Shuai Xie, Tianyi Zhang, Wei Liu, Wentao Tan, Xianghan Meng, Xiaodong He, Xing Pan, Xiran Wang, Xuyang Peng, Ya Zhang, Yang Liu, Yangyang Duan, Yanxu Chen, Yicheng Gong, Yidan Huang, Yifei Liu, Yinhao Bai, Yongqiang Liu, Yuesong Zhang, Yuqi Zhang, Zerui Xie, Zhenfang Wang, Zhennan Shen, Zheyuan Liu, Zhuwei Zeng · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (80) is strongest — released weights across six quantization formats, 85% token reduction vs GLM on LiveCodeBench, MTP speedup 1.87×. Novelty (70) — FiberPO's fiber-bundle decomposition of trust regions is genuinely new in RL alignment literature; surrounding system is competent engineering combination. Non-Obviousness (70) the two-scale ratio gating insight is non-trivial. Rigor (55) weakened by no seeds/error bars, key theorems deferred to companion paper, vendor self-reported baselines, and FiberPO ablation only on single math domain. Completeness (55) — long-context RULER gap and AIME'25 deficit versus GLM unaddressed; no isolation of FiberPO's contribution to final token-efficiency claim. Resilience (55) — token-efficiency claim could be confounded with refusal-to-think. Weighted: 0.15*70+0.15*55+0.10*70+0.15*55+0.15*55+0.30*80 = 64.75≈65. Adjusted up slightly for genuine methodological novelty of FiberPO = 68.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 55 Resilience 55 Impact 80
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces JoyAI-LLM Flash, a 48B-total/2.7B-active sparse Mixture-of-Experts language model with a novel RL algorithm (FiberPO) and joint training-inference co-design (MTP+QAT) targeting token efficiency in the sub-50B parameter regime. - Why it's important: Thinking-mode LLMs routinely over-generate tokens, inflating inference cost; a method that achieves equal or better accuracy with substantially fewer output tokens directly reduces serving expense at scale. - How it does it: FiberPO decomposes each token's importance ratio into a trajectory-level base weight and a token-level gated residual using fiber bundle geometry, allocating separate trust-region budgets to trajectory drift and within-trajectory token variation — preventing the entropy collapse that afflicts GRPO and the within-trajectory signal suppression that stagnates GSPO. - The good: In a controlled single-domain RL comparison on DAPO-Math-17k, FiberPO maintains stable validation accuracy rising to 0.786 (AIME 2024 mean@1) while GRPO collapses after step 60 and GSPO stagnates around 0.65, with matching training diagnostics (entropy, gradient norm, mean ratio) that confirm the theoretical mechanism. - The missing: No ablation of FiberPO's isolated contribution to the final instruct model's token efficiency is provided — the efficiency gains in the full system could plausibly be attributed to the thinking/non-thinking SFT data mixture rather than FiberPO specifically. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML researchers working on RL alignment stability in heterogeneous multi-domain settings; practitioners should note this is a vendor technical report from JD.com with key theorems deferred to a companion paper and no independent reproduction of results. Problem & Motivation LLMs in the sub-50B parameter range face a tension between inference efficiency (tokens consumed per query) and task accuracy: thinking-mode models generate excessive tokens, driving up compute costs, while standard RL alignment methods (PPO, GRPO, GSPO) exhibit instability under heterogeneous multi-domain training — token-level stochasticity, trajectory-level drift, and multi-scale variance across domains and MoE experts cause training collapse or stagnation. GRPO clips per-token log-ratios directly against an absolute threshold, so once trajectory-level drift exceeds the clip bound, the majority of tokens saturate simultaneously, destroying within-trajectory discrimination. GSPO suppresses within-trajectory variation entirely by collapsing each trajectory to a single aggregate ratio, preventing the optimizer from distinguishing token-level quality differences. No prior method simultaneously addresses both failure modes within a unified theoretical framework. Key Idea FiberPO decomposes each token's gated importance ratio multiplicatively into a trajectory-level base weight (which enforces a trust-region budget on aggregate trajectory drift via a piecewise-linear gate with rollback and zeroing regimes) and a per-token fiber residual (which clips each token's deviation from its same-sign trajectory mean independently via logclip). Because the two components act on orthogonal sources of variation — trajectory drift and within-trajectory token fluctuation — their clip budgets do not interfere, and tokens that co-vary with trajectory drift pass through unattenuated, retaining full gradient signal and enabling finer, discriminative per-token updates even under large trajectory-level drift. Methodology The pipeline has five main phases. Pretraining: A sparse MoE transformer (40 layers, 256 routed experts, Top-8 activation + 1 shared expert, Multi-head Latent Attention, 128K context, SwiGLU, 129K vocabulary) is trained on 20.7 trillion tokens in four curriculum stages: Foundational → Code-Math Enhancement (upweighted code/math) → Mid-Training (ultra-high-quality tokens, MTP head enabled) → Context Extension to 128K. Training uses Megatron-Core with 2-way Pipeline Parallelism, 8-way Expert Parallelism, ZeRO-1 Data Parallelism, and the Muon optimizer (matrix orthogonalization / Newton-style spectral gradient update) replacing Adam. A single-layer dense MTP head is jointly trained to enable self-speculative decoding at inference. SFT: Three-category data (general SFT, environment/agent learning with GitHub-sourced verifiable RL environments, tool-integrated reasoning across code/search/hybrid trajectories). Sequence packing with block-diagonal attention masks achieves 1.5× throughput. Thinking/non-thinking cognitive modes are jointly trained. DPO: 1,000 steps, lr=1e-6, cosine decay to 1e-7, batch 256; preference pairs target hallucinations and instruction-following failures. RL with FiberPO: The FiberPO objective (Eq. 1) is J^FiberPO = Σ (1/|T^θ_old|)(1/T_τ) · G(r•)_{s,a,τ,t} · Â_{s,a}, where the gating map G decomposes per-token as w^base_τ · r̃^fiber_i. The base weight w^base_τ is the ratio of two g^agg gates applied to positive (s^+_τ) and negative (s^-_τ) aggregate log-ratios, with three regimes: pass-through (|x| ≤ C), rollback (slope −T_τ), zeroed. The fiber gate r̃^fiber_i is logclip(l_i·u_i, ε) / logclip(v_i, ε), where u_i is each token's deviation from its same-sign trajectory mean. Multi-domain training uses Gaussian curriculum difficulty scheduling from p=0.8 to p=0.2 with domain-balanced sampling. Critical design decisions: (1) operating in log-ratio space with logclip rather than ratio space with clip — justified by multiplicative decomposability; (2) separating positive/negative sign channels to detect high total variation even when signed average cancels; (3) per-trajectory trust-region budgets independent of domain, enabling heterogeneous reward distributions without domain-specific tuning. Inference: Quantization-Aware Training with fake INT4 operators and Straight-Through Estimators during RL training; Post-Training Quantization to BF16/FP8/W4AFP8/INT4/GGUF, deployed via TensorRT-LLM with CUDA Graph, Torch Compile, and Overlap Scheduling. Key Results Base model (Table 2, vs Qwen3-30B-A3B and Qwen3.5-35B-A3B): MMLU-Pro 73.1% (best; Qwen3.5-35B: 60.7%); MATH 78.1% (best; Qwen3.5-35B: 56.0%); MATH-500 77.0% (best; Qwen3.5-35B: 54.8%); RULER 128K 77.0% (below Qwen3.5-35B's 88.3%). Instruct model (Table 3, vs Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, Qwen3-30B-A3B, GLM-4.7-Flash-Thinking): AIME'25 72.9% at 5,400 tokens vs GLM-4.7-Flash-T's 92.1% at 26,000 tokens; MATH-500 98.2% at 1,300 tokens vs GLM's 99.2% at 3,700 tokens; LiveCodeBench v6 65.6% at 7,300 tokens (best accuracy, vs GLM's 64.0% at 53,600 tokens — 85% token reduction); SWE-bench Verified 62.6% at 24,400 tokens (best). Token efficiency: JoyAI-LLM Flash surpasses GLM-4.7-Flash-Thinking by 1.6% accuracy on LiveCodeBench with 85% fewer tokens. MTP speculative decoding: 1.87× speedup on SpecBench MTP-3 (1 GPU, concurrency 64), vs GLM-5's 1.82×, DeepSeek-V3.2's 1.79×, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B's 1.61×. Quantization (Figure 9): FP8 improves throughput ~17% vs BF16 with negligible accuracy drop; W4AFP8 achieves ~28% throughput gain with 1.2% accuracy drop. FiberPO RL comparison (DAPO-Math-17k, Figure 6): GRPO collapses at step 60 (entropy to 0.038 nats, mean ratio >10³); GSPO stagnates at ~0.65 AIME 2024 mean@1; FiberPO reaches 0.786 with entropy stable at 0.43 nats and mean ratio near 1.13. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. FiberPO is genuinely novel in its fiber bundle theoretical grounding and two-scale multiplicative decomposition of trust-region maintenance, which has not appeared in prior RL alignment literature; it unifies and strictly generalizes PPO, GRPO, and GSPO as special cases within a single Ratio Gating Formalism. The surrounding system integrates known components (MoE from DeepSeek-V3, MLA from DeepSeek-V2/Kimi-K2, MTP from DeepSeek-V3, QAT with STE, Muon optimizer) in a competent but incremental engineering combination. Rigor & Reproducibility The FiberPO RL comparison is methodologically clean: GRPO, GSPO, and FiberPO trained from the same DPO checkpoint, same infrastructure (verl), same data, ablating only the optimizer. Training diagnostics (policy entropy, mean importance ratio, response length, gradient norm, clip fraction) provide mechanistic support matching theoretical predictions. However, no error bars or multiple seeds are reported for any experiment. The full instruct model evaluation (Table 3) contains results marked with asterisks directly cited from original papers, creating inconsistent evaluation conditions. The key theorems (FBG First-Order Agreement, APC-Obj-TRPO Equivalence) are stated but not proven in this report — all proofs deferred to companion paper [73], which is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2603.08239). No ablation of the full system isolates FiberPO's contribution to the final token-efficiency claim from data quality, DPO, or architecture choices. Model weights released on HuggingFace (base + BF16/FP8/INT8/INT4/GGUF quantized variants). Deployability (4-property check) SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Yes — inference runs on a single GPU (MTP results at 1 GPU); GGUF variants target consumer hardware with extremely restricted VRAM. - Efficient: 2.7B active parameters per token (3.2B including embeddings) gives a ~17× compute reduction over dense 48B; FP8 adds 17% throughput gain over BF16 with negligible accuracy drop; W4AFP8 adds 28% gain with 1.2% accuracy drop; MTP delivers 1.87× speculative decoding speedup. - Effective: The evidence shows JoyAI-LLM Flash matches or exceeds accuracy of larger models on most benchmarks while consuming fewer tokens, but long-context performance (RULER: 77.0% vs Qwen3.5-35B's 96.0%) and some agent tasks (τ²-Bench: 74.1% vs Qwen3-Next-80B's 75.9%) show gaps not adequately discussed. - Scalable: Not evaluated at any scale other than 48B total; no scaling analysis of FiberPO is provided, and the paper makes no claim about performance at 10×–100× scale. The work delivers software artifacts: model weights on HuggingFace in six quantization formats. Strengths - FiberPO is grounded in a principled mathematical framework (fiber bundle theory) that produces three testable structural properties (trajectory independence, first-order agreement near on-policy, scale separation), and the training diagnostics quantitatively match these predictions. - The RL comparison provides the strongest evidence in the paper: a controlled head-to-head of FiberPO vs GRPO vs GSPO with matched infrastructure, revealing mechanistic failure modes in competitors (GRPO entropy collapse, GSPO within-trajectory signal suppression) that FiberPO's design specifically addresses. - Joint QAT-during-RL (inserting fake-quantization operators into RL training) is a pragmatic co-design choice that stabilizes quantization-aware RL and delivers production-ready quantized models without accuracy regression. - The pretraining data pipeline is described in unusual detail — n-gram whitelist decontamination, multi-dimensional quality scoring with Qwen-based judge models, topological ordering of repository files for long-context code — providing genuine reproducibility information rarely included in model reports. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note that MTP layers beyond next-n=3 introduce diminishing or negative throughput returns due to computational overhead. For long-context workloads with limited memory, they acknowledge that KV cache transfer latency can outweigh recomputation benefits, making centralized KV cache management net-negative for smaller models, with the crossover point requiring per-setup empirical evaluation. Future work sections implicitly acknowledge the absence of continual learning and persistent memory. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The FiberPO vs. GRPO/GSPO comparison covers only a single math domain (DAPO-Math-17k); the multi-domain generalization claim is supported only by the full instruct model evaluation where FiberPO's contribution cannot be separated from data quality, DPO initialization, or architecture differences. - The token-efficiency claim conflates thinking-mode token budgets with generation quality — models generating fewer tokens may be doing so by refusing to think, not by thinking more efficiently; no analysis of accuracy vs token count trade-off curves is presented. - All key theorems (APC-Obj-TRPO Equivalence, FBG First-Order Agreement) are deferred to companion paper [73]; without those proofs, the theoretical grounding cannot be verified from this report. - Long-context performance (RULER 128K: 77.0% base vs Qwen3.5-35B's 88.3%) and the gap in instruct model on AIME'25 (72.9% vs GLM-4.7-Flash-Thinking's 92.1%) are not discussed or explained. - Several baselines in Table 3 are vendor self-reported (asterisked), creating a two-tier evaluation where some numbers are reproduced and others are not, without a systematic discussion of which favor or disfavor JoyAI-LLM Flash. Bottom Line ML researchers studying RL alignment stability will find the FiberPO formulation and its associated training diagnostics the most substantive contribution; everyone else should treat this as a well-engineered vendor model report whose token-efficiency claims require independent reproduction before relying on them.
Recommended 2604.03044 PDF
#82 — Innovative (68)

LLM-PRISM: Characterizing Silent Data Corruption from Permanent GPU Faults in LLM Training

Abhishek Tyagi, Saurabh Hukerikar, Nirmal Saxena, Yanxiang Huang, Philip Shirvani, Chung-Hsuan Tung, Yuhao Zhu · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (70) is strong on scale (7,664 runs, RTL-grounded signatures) but undermined by the missing RTL-vs-uniform-bitflip ablation — the central methodological premise is not directly validated — plus no code release and preprint status. Novelty (70) for the first full-pretraining, hardware-grounded SDC characterization and the four-mode taxonomy (NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING). Non-Obviousness (75) — the silent-degradation/gradual-drift modes invisible to NaN checks and loss monitoring are genuinely surprising and directly actionable. Completeness (70) helped by an explicit limitations section; Resilience (65) somewhat capped by 124M/355M scale and DP+SP-only parallelism. Impact (55) — findings inform format/monitoring decisions but no artifact or mitigation is delivered. Weighted: ≈ 68.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LLM-PRISM, a methodology that couples RTL-level GPU fault simulation with stochastic software-level fault injection inside Megatron-LM to produce the first hardware-grounded, full pre-training characterization of how permanent and intermittent GPU faults cause Silent Data Corruption (SDC) in LLM training. - Why it's important: Production LLM training clusters (e.g., Llama 3's 16,000 H100 GPUs over months) increasingly encounter permanent hardware defects from silicon aging and manufacturing escapes, with Meta and Google reporting SDC events as routine occurrences — yet no prior work characterizes how these faults propagate through full pre-training dynamics at hardware-realistic fidelity. - How it does it: Physical stuck-at and timing-dependent GPU faults in FMA paths, tensor cores, register files, and caches are simulated via RTL under real training computation traces to extract error signatures, which are then replayed as parameterized software injections inside Megatron-LM using a 7-element fault-site tuple and a Bernoulli error-rate model that unifies permanent and intermittent faults. - The good: Across 7,664 complete pre-training runs, four qualitatively distinct failure modes are identified — including silent degradation and gradual drift that are invisible to both loss-curve monitoring and NaN/Inf checking — with FP16 95th-percentile PPL reaching 192.4 at fault rate 1.0 vs. a fault-free baseline of 28.54, and downstream task accuracy dropping even for runs whose PPL appears near-baseline. - The missing: All experiments use GPT-2 Small (124M) and GPT-2 Medium (355M) under data-parallel + sequence-parallel training only; the paper provides no ablation comparing RTL-grounded error signatures against uniform random bit-flips, leaving the methodological centerpiece's value unquantified. - Read it or not: Essential reading for ML infrastructure engineers and hardware reliability researchers who design or operate large-scale LLM training clusters — particularly anyone setting NaN-check policies, choosing numerical formats, or specifying hardware reliability requirements. Problem & Motivation Large-scale LLM training runs span tens of thousands of GPUs over weeks to months, making encounters with permanent hardware defects statistically inevitable. Permanent faults — arising from manufacturing escapes, latent early-life defects, and silicon aging mechanisms such as BTI, Hot Carrier Injection, and electromigration — manifest as stuck-at faults in combinational logic or timing-dependent intermittent faults that silently corrupt gradients and model parameters without triggering fail-stop errors. Unlike transient faults that produce isolated perturbations, permanent faults recur systematically across training steps, accumulating errors over billions of parameter updates. Prior resilience studies focus almost exclusively on transient faults (cosmic-ray bit-flips producing single-shot perturbations) or restrict evaluation to inference or short fine-tuning horizons that cannot expose long-horizon divergence phenomena such as gradual drift or silent degradation. The field therefore lacks a hardware-grounded, full pre-training understanding of how these faults propagate through training dynamics, which failure modes are detectable by existing safeguards, and how numerical format choice modulates vulnerability. Key Idea The core insight is that a physical stuck-at defect has a fixed hardware location but a training-data-dependent effect: which tensor is corrupted, at what magnitude, and in what spatial pattern all depend on what computation is scheduled on the faulty unit at activation time. Rather than simulating faults at hardware level (computationally intractable) or using idealized uniform bit-flips (physically unrealistic), LLM-PRISM extracts the observable "error signature" of each hardware fault — affected bits, magnitude of deviation, spatial corruption pattern — via RTL simulation driven by real LLM computation traces, then injects those signatures as parameterized software perturbations inside the training loop. A Bernoulli error-rate parameter r ∈ (0,1] governs per-iteration activation probability, modeling intermittent defects (r≪1, rarely activate) and permanent stuck-at faults (r=1, always active) within a single unified framework. Methodology The pipeline has four stages. Stage 1 extracts computation traces (GEMMs, attention, MLP memory accesses) directly from Megatron-LM training workloads. Stage 2 replays these traces through a commercial EDA tool targeting the five GPU functional units most exposed during transformer execution — FMA paths in CUDA cores, tensor cores, register files, L1/L2 caches, and SRAM arrays — simulating stuck-at faults sampled in proportion to each unit's silicon area to produce >6,000 error signatures. Stage 3 translates hardware signatures into a software fault-site parameterization: a 7-tuple specifying (a) rank (faulty GPU in the distributed cluster), (b) layer (transformer sub-module), (c) training phase (fwd_out / bwd_grad_inp / bwd_grad_wt), (d) fault checkpoint (iteration at which injection begins), (e) error rate r, (f) fault density (proportion of corrupted elements in the target tile), and (g) bit-flip profile drawn from RTL distributions. Stage 4 executes fault-injection campaigns inside a modified Megatron-LM on WikiText using GPT-2 Small (124M) and GPT-2 Medium (355M) across FP16, BF16, and FP8 formats. Three critical design decisions: using real computation traces for RTL simulation (anchors signatures to realistic GPU activity — well justified); the single-fault-per-run assumption (simplifies attribution, justified at realistic rates where multi-defect interactions are rare — acknowledged as a limitation); trading absolute model scale for statistical coverage across 7,664 complete runs (deliberate and well-argued, though it limits generalization to production-scale models). Key Results Across all formats and fault rates, 57–76% of runs complete with final PPL within 1% of the fault-free baseline (28.54 nats), demonstrating that LLMs are broadly resilient to low-frequency faults. Four qualitatively distinct failure modes are identified: (1) Spike-and-Recover — sparse NaN events absorbed by mixed-precision recovery, loss returns to baseline; (2) Spike-and-Degrade — dense NaN events overwhelm recovery, loss diverges monotonically; (3) Silent Degradation — no NaN events, loss settles visibly above baseline while weights are silently corrupted; (4) Gradual Drift — no NaN events, loss appears near-baseline but weight divergence (L2 distance from fault-free parameters) grows monotonically throughout training. All fault-injected runs exhibit monotonically increasing weight divergence once injection begins, confirming that loss-curve monitoring cannot detect all corruption modes. For FP16 at fault rate 1.0, median PPL is 28.58 but 95th-percentile reaches 192.4; non-diverged FP16 runs exhibit PPL deviations up to ~600% above baseline — the largest silent degradation across formats. FP8 outcomes are binary (absorb or crash): no Silent Degradation or Gradual Drift modes appear, as FP8's narrow dynamic range bounds corruption magnitude. BF16 crash rates reach 26.9–41.9% at higher fault rates. Fault phase is a stronger outcome predictor than NaN-check presence: fwd_out and bwd_grad_inp faults yield ~60% crash rates; bwd_grad_wt faults are largely self-correcting due to optimizer momentum smoothing and cross-GPU gradient averaging diluting single-rank errors by 1/N. Downstream task evaluation on Children's Book Test (CBT-CN, CBT-NE) and Winograd Schema Challenge shows accuracy drops even for spike-and-recover and gradual-drift models with near-baseline PPL, confirming PPL as an imperfect fault detection proxy. NaN/Inf checking converts many crashes to spike-and-recover events for FP16/BF16 but cannot detect silent degradation or gradual drift. Baselines are the fault-free training runs and prior characterization studies cited in Table I; no prior system is re-run for direct numerical comparison. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The RTL-to-software fault injection methodology bridging physically grounded hardware defect models with full pre-training simulation is novel relative to prior transient-fault-focused and inference-only studies. The four failure-mode taxonomy and the empirical demonstration that gradual drift and silent degradation are invisible to both loss monitoring and NaN/Inf checking are genuinely new findings with direct practical implications. Standard components — Megatron-LM, WikiText, Bernoulli fault modeling — are applied to a new characterization problem rather than combined in a technically novel way. Rigor & Reproducibility The 7,664-run campaign (4,681 for GPT-2 Small, 2,983 for GPT-2 Medium) provides substantial statistical coverage across fault rates, checkpoints, formats, and model sizes. Violin plots show PPL distributions but no formal significance tests or error bars on aggregate statistics are reported. A critical ablation is absent: the paper does not compare RTL-grounded error signatures against uniform random bit-flips, leaving the methodological premise — that hardware-accurate signatures produce materially different outcomes — unvalidated by experiment. No public code repository or model weights URL is cited; reproducibility requires access to a commercial EDA tool, a customized Megatron-LM implementation, and a 16-GPU H100 cluster. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2604.10390v1, April 2026); not peer-reviewed. Single-fault assumption may not reflect production multi-defect scenarios. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — fault injection campaigns and training runs with downstream task evaluation. - Practical: Experiments run on 16× NVIDIA H100 GPUs across two 8-GPU nodes — commercially available hardware reproducible at modest cluster scale. - Efficient: The methodology requires RTL simulation via commercial EDA tools plus a customized Megatron-LM, representing >11,500 estimated GPU-hours and infrastructure overhead unavailable to most academic groups; the findings themselves are applicable without re-running the methodology. - Effective: Demonstrates that permanent faults produce failure modes invisible to existing monitoring safeguards, directly validating the core claim that loss-curve and NaN-check monitoring are insufficient for SDC detection. - Scalable: All experiments are on 124M–355M parameter models under data-parallel + sequence-parallel training; the paper does not test tensor or pipeline parallelism, and explicitly acknowledges the gap to production-scale models (405B+) where fault propagation dynamics may differ substantially. The work delivers no public software artifact; stops at paper form. Strengths - Hardware grounding via RTL simulation under real LLM computation traces directly distinguishes this from prior work using idealized bit-flip assumptions — the error signatures capture the actual spatial, magnitude, and bit-position structure of physical defects rather than uniform random corruptions. - Full pre-training coverage from initialization to convergence enables observation of long-horizon phenomena (gradual drift accumulating over thousands of iterations) that short-horizon and inference-only studies structurally cannot detect. - The four-mode failure taxonomy is both empirically grounded and operationally actionable, providing a vocabulary for classifying fault outcomes that is absent from prior literature. - The empirical finding that FP8 eliminates silent degradation and gradual drift (converting them to binary crash/survive outcomes) while FP16 produces the worst silent corruption directly informs numerical format selection for fault-tolerant training — a practically impactful, format-specific result. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors devote Section VI to three explicit limitations: (1) small model scale (GPT-2 124M/355M vs. production billion-to-trillion-parameter models) — accepted as an intentional tradeoff for statistical coverage; (2) fidelity of software-level injection depends on accuracy of the underlying RTL hardware defect models — arbitrary bit-flip patterns would yield misleading results; (3) only data-parallel + sequence-parallel training studied — tensor and pipeline parallelism may alter SDC blast radius and propagation paths. The authors confront these directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The paper's central methodological claim is that hardware-accurate RTL-derived signatures matter, yet no ablation experiment compares outcomes under RTL signatures vs. uniform random bit-flips — this is the most consequential missing experiment. - The single-fault assumption excludes correlated multi-fault scenarios; in production clusters with 16,000+ GPUs and elevated hardware utilization pushing marginal devices, simultaneous defects on multiple nodes may interact in ways this framework cannot capture. - WikiText is a clean, homogeneous corpus; production training data (web-scale, multilingual, code-mixed) generates different gradient distributions and activation outlier patterns that could alter resilience profiles. - The paper characterizes the problem space but evaluates no mitigations: checkpoint frequency tuning, redundant computation, gradient anomaly detection, or fault-aware optimizer modifications are not assessed. - NaN check analysis compares presence/absence of the default mixed-precision recovery mechanism only; alternative detection strategies (parameter norm monitoring, gradient outlier detection, comparison-based redundancy) are not evaluated. Bottom Line Any engineer designing or operating large-scale LLM training infrastructure — particularly anyone setting NaN-check policies, choosing numerical precision formats, or specifying hardware reliability SLAs — will find directly actionable, hardware-grounded findings here that no prior publication provides at this level of training completeness and fault realism.
Citations 2604.10390 PDF
#83 — Innovative (68)

Uncertainty-quantified Pulse Signal Recovery from Facial Video using Regularized Stochastic Interpolants

Vineet R. Shenoy, Cheng Peng, Rama Chellappa, Yu Sun · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) and Novelty (72) are strong: first posterior-sampling formulation for iPPG with a physiologically motivated RCL regularizer backed by a formal theorem. Rigor (65) is solid within-distribution (40-fold LOSO on MMSE-HR, Gauge R&R precision analysis, multi-axis ablations) but hurt by inconsistent eval protocols across datasets, unreported SDE timestep count, missing N-sample sensitivity ablation, and preprint status. Resilience (55) weakens substantially due to severe cross-dataset failures (up to 21.59 bpm MAE). Impact (55): no code/weight release but addresses a concrete clinician-identified gap. Composite in the mid-60s.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RIS-iPPG, a flow-based diffusion framework that formulates imaging photoplethysmography (iPPG) as an inverse problem using stochastic interpolants, enabling posterior sampling and per-sample uncertainty quantification of blood volume pulse signals recovered from facial video. - Why it's important: Every existing iPPG algorithm outputs a deterministic point estimate with no uncertainty information, blocking clinical adoption where clinicians need per-patient confidence measures — especially across demographic subgroups with known performance disparities. - How it does it: Learns flow and score U-Net networks that define a stochastic differential equation (SDE) transporting the camera pixel signal distribution to the BVP signal distribution, regularized by a Residual Correlation Loss (RCL) that enforces temporal consistency of flow residuals across adjacent overlapping time windows based on the physiological prior that blood volume changes are slowly varying. - The good: Achieves 0.38 bpm MAE on the PURE dataset (best of all compared methods) and 0.47 bpm MAE on UBFC-rPPG, while providing the first calibrated uncertainty estimates for iPPG with miscalibration error ≤ 0.10 across demographic subgroups. - The missing: Cross-dataset generalization fails substantially in several configurations (PURE→MMSE-HR: 7.93 bpm MAE; PURE→UBFC-rPPG: 21.59 bpm MAE), and the number of SDE discretization timesteps — a key inference speed/quality knob — is neither reported nor ablated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers at the intersection of generative modeling and clinical biomedical signal processing, particularly those targeting trust and uncertainty calibration for contactless vitals monitoring. Problem & Motivation Imaging photoplethysmography (iPPG) recovers blood volume pulse waveforms from RGB facial video captured by consumer cameras, with applications in neonatal care units, emergency departments, and remote patient monitoring. Clinicians surveyed in prior work (Tonekaboni et al. 2019) identified repeated, trustworthy performance per individual patient — not population-level accuracy — as the critical factor for sustained adoption of AI medical tools. All prior iPPG algorithms, whether traditional signal processing or deep learning, produce a single deterministic BVP estimate per test input; none samples the solution space to characterize predictive uncertainty. This gap is especially consequential across protected demographic attributes (skin tone, gender) where signal quality varies, making unknown-confidence predictions potentially harmful. No prior work establishes baselines for uncertainty calibration in the iPPG domain. Key Idea The central insight is to abandon the deterministic regression formulation and instead model iPPG recovery as transport between probability distributions: learn a stochastic process that maps the distribution of camera pixel intensity signals (p₁) to the distribution of ground-truth BVP signals (p₀). At test time, the observed camera measurement serves as the initial condition of a reverse SDE, which is solved N times to draw N posterior samples of the BVP waveform. A domain-specific regularizer — the Residual Correlation Loss — exploits the physiological prior that BVP is slowly varying by requiring that the flow prediction error residuals from two overlapping, time-shifted windows point in the same direction, constraining the model to temporally consistent solutions. Methodology The pipeline has three phases. (1) Preprocessing: OpenFace detects the face; LDEQ landmarks delineate forehead, left cheek, right cheek, and chin; per-region red/green channel ratios yield the camera measurement x₁ ∈ ℝ^{T×5}. Ground-truth BVP x₀ is the finger pulse oximeter signal repeated across five regions. (2) Training via stochastic interpolants (Albergo et al. 2023): the interpolant is xₜ = (1−t)x₀ + tx₁ + √(2t(1−t))z, z ~ N(0,I). Two identical U-Nets (adapted from guided diffusion, Dhariwal & Nichol 2021) learn the flow vθ(t,x) and denoiser nθ(t,x) via MSE losses; the combined loss adds λ_RCL · L_RCL(p,q) where p and q are the flow residual vectors from the original and time-shifted (by δ seconds) windows, and L_RCL = 1 − Pearson(p,q). Theorem A.1 proves minimizing L_RCL is equivalent to aligning residual directions. Critical design decisions: (a) stochastic interpolants rather than an explicit forward model — empirically justified because the BVP→camera mapping is stochastic, time-dependent, and one-to-many (different facial regions map to one BVP); (b) RCL on residuals not predicted flows — RCL on flows does not converge (Figure 8); (c) δ=9s, λ=0.1 selected by grid search on MMSE-HR validation set. (3) Inference: solve the reverse SDE N times (N user-specified; 100 in experiments) using off-the-shelf solvers (Li et al. 2020; Kidger et al. 2021), producing N pulse waveform samples for uncertainty analysis. Key Results On three benchmark datasets (MMSE-HR: 102 videos, 40 subjects, 40-fold LOSO; PURE: 10 subjects, fixed splits; UBFC-rPPG: 43 subjects, fixed splits): MMSE-HR: MAE = 1.37 bpm, RMSE = 3.71 bpm, PCC = 0.97 (second-best; best supervised baseline Unrolled-iPPG achieves 1.11/2.97/0.97). PURE: MAE = 0.38 bpm, RMSE = 1.28 bpm, PCC = 0.99 — best across all compared methods; next best is DualGAN at 1.23/2.01. UBFC-rPPG: MAE = 0.47 bpm, RMSE = 0.80 bpm, PCC = 0.98 — best. The RCL loss substantially improves MMSE-HR performance: average MAE drops from 3.72 (no RCL) to 2.44 bpm (δ=9, λ=0.1). RIS-iPPG outperforms other posterior sampling methods: cVAE (6.17 bpm MAE), BNN (3.12 bpm) vs. RIS-iPPG (1.97 bpm) on MMSE-HR. Uncertainty calibration: miscalibration area 0.06 (PURE), 0.04 (UBFC-rPPG); across MMSE-HR demographic subgroups (light/dark skin, men/women), calibration error ≤ 0.10 in all four groups. Bland-Altman analysis shows mean difference ≈ −0.63 bpm on MMSE-HR (slight overestimation). Cross-dataset performance degrades significantly: PURE→MMSE-HR: 7.93 bpm MAE; PURE→UBFC-rPPG: 21.59 bpm MAE. Inference time at 100 samples: ~851ms vs ~845ms (cVAE) and ~474ms (BNN) on a single NVIDIA A5000. Baselines are reasonably current (through 2024) and include supervised, self-supervised, and model-based methods. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The paper is the first to apply posterior sampling via flow-based diffusion to the iPPG domain and the first to establish uncertainty calibration baselines for iPPG on demographic subgroups. The RCL regularizer is a domain-specific contribution with formal support. The core machinery — stochastic interpolants, U-Nets, SDE solvers — is prior work; novelty lies in the problem formulation, the physiologically motivated regularization, and the UQ evaluation protocol. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are substantive: RCL weight × stride grid search on two datasets (Tables 3, 9), cVAE/BNN/RIS comparison (Table 8), guidance signal comparison (Table 7), residual vs. flow RCL (Figure 8), cross-dataset evaluation (Table 11). MMSE-HR results use 40-fold LOSO cross-validation; PURE and UBFC-rPPG use single fixed splits with no repeated runs, so no error bars or significance tests are reported for those datasets. A Gauge R&R analysis (Appendix A.10.6) quantifies measurement system precision: repeatability accounts for only 1.7% of variance, with 98.2% attributable to between-signal variation — a rigorous precision assessment rarely seen in iPPG papers. No code, trained models, or data are released (not mentioned). This is an arXiv preprint (v1, April 2026); results are not peer reviewed. The ground-truth BVP is finger PPG, not facial PPG — a fundamental mismatch acknowledged as a limitation. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL — methods and experiments on real video datasets with a trained model at the core. - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA A5000 GPU; ~851ms per 10-second window is feasible for near-real-time clinical monitoring scenarios. - Efficient: ~1.8× slower than BNNs per inference call due to iterative SDE solving; the number of discretization timesteps — the primary speed-quality lever — is not reported or ablated, leaving the efficiency frontier uncharacterized. - Effective: Demonstrates 0.38–0.47 bpm MAE within distribution on PURE and UBFC-rPPG and calibrated uncertainty estimates; cross-dataset performance degrades severely (up to 21.59 bpm MAE), indicating the method solves the in-distribution problem but not the general iPPG recovery problem. - Scalable: Not tested beyond three standard benchmarks (≤43 subjects, 10-second windows, single GPU); no continuous monitoring or large-cohort evaluation is presented or claimed. The work delivers paper-form results only; no software artifact or trained model is publicly released. Strengths - First posterior sampling framework for iPPG — addresses a concrete, clinician-identified gap that the field has acknowledged but never tackled, enabling per-sample uncertainty quantification rather than population-level accuracy. - The RCL loss is principled: Theorem A.1 provides a formal proof that minimizing RCL aligns residual directions, grounding the physiological motivation mathematically, and ablations confirm a 35% MAE reduction on MMSE-HR. - Comparison against three alternative posterior sampling paradigms (cVAE, BNN, GP) is carefully motivated with empirical measurements, not just citations — each alternative's failure mode is diagnosed rather than dismissed. - Gauge R&R precision analysis is borrowed from industrial metrology and is genuinely novel methodological practice for the iPPG evaluation literature. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) cross-dataset generalization fails in several configurations due to domain shift in the implicitly learned forward model; (2) the ground-truth BVP signal is finger PPG rather than facial PPG, introducing a fundamental approximation; (3) inference speed is a barrier for real-world adoption and should be improved in future work; (4) performance on darker skin tones is inferior (worse NLL), attributed to underrepresentation in training data. The authors confront these directly, including presenting Table 11 showing large cross-dataset errors, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Evaluation protocols are inconsistent across datasets (LOSO for MMSE-HR, fixed single splits for PURE and UBFC-rPPG), making Table 2 cross-method comparisons potentially confounded since methods use different validation regimes. - N=100 SDE samples is used throughout without ablating sensitivity of uncertainty estimates to N — a basic experiment missing for a method whose key claim is uncertainty quantification. - The number of SDE discretization timesteps is not reported, making the inference speed and approximation quality claims impossible to reproduce or extend. - The demographic performance gap for dark skin tones (4.79 vs 2.17 bpm MAE) is reported but not analyzed architecturally — the paper does not investigate whether the gap is caused by training data imbalance, signal quality differences, or model capacity. - No ablation isolates the contribution of the stochastic interpolant framework itself from the RCL — a version of the model without RCL but with the SDE sampling (i.e., unregularized RIS-iPPG) is compared in Tables 3/4 but not evaluated for UQ quality separately from accuracy. Bottom Line A practitioner building clinical iPPG systems gains the first concrete methodology for per-sample uncertainty quantification — previously absent from the entire field — with competitive within-distribution accuracy, making this preprint worth reading despite unresolved cross-domain generalization failures and the absence of a public code release.
Search Results 2604.10777 PDF
#84 — Innovative (68)

Self-Evolving LLM Memory Extraction Across Heterogeneous Tasks

Yuqing Yang, Tengxiao Liu, Wang Bill Zhu, Taiwei Shi, Linxin Song, Robin Jia · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (72) solid — new problem formulation (heterogeneous memory extraction), new benchmark (BEHEMOTH), and scenario-based clustering twist on MemEvolve. Rigor (62) moderate — no component ablation isolating clustering vs. cross-cluster proposal; small training set (330 examples); homogeneous extractor/generator confound; preprint. Non-Obviousness (70) — clustering by scenario rather than dataset is a meaningful insight. Completeness (72) strong — limitations acknowledged honestly. Resilience (65) — closed evaluation and small deltas on some OOD sets. Impact (70) — code+benchmark released. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*70 ≈ 68.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 72 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CluE (Cluster-based Evolution), a self-evolving prompt optimization framework for LLM memory extraction that groups training examples by extraction scenario rather than dataset category, enabling stable learning across heterogeneous task distributions. - Why it's important: Persistent LLM assistants encounter wildly diverse interaction types — personalization, problem-solving, agentic tasks — and no single static or naively self-evolving extraction prompt generalizes across them; poor memory extraction compounds over time as low-quality memories accumulate. - How it does it: CluE runs each evolution round through four stages: a Summarizer abstracts each training example into an extraction-scenario description, a Cluster Manager groups examples by scenario similarity (not dataset label), a Cluster Analyzer identifies per-cluster success/failure patterns, and a Proposer synthesizes cross-cluster insights into a revised extraction prompt. - The good: CluE achieves +9.04% relative gain over the Simple baseline across 18 datasets (overall macro accuracy 50.01% vs. 46.00%), with consistent improvements in all three task categories simultaneously — a feat no baseline self-evolving framework achieves — and the advantage transfers to out-of-distribution datasets and a continual memory setting. - The missing: The paper evaluates only the extraction stage in isolation; retrieval, storage, and memory management are decoupled by design, so end-to-end memory lifecycle performance under realistic deployment conditions is untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building persistent LLM assistants or working on heterogeneous prompt optimization; practitioners who only care about a single memory domain (e.g., purely personalization) will find the contribution less directly applicable. Problem & Motivation: As LLM assistants become persistent across sessions, they must extract and retain useful information from past conversations. The information worth remembering varies fundamentally by context: a personalization task calls for user preferences and biographical facts, a math problem-solving session calls for reusable solution strategies, and an agentic trajectory calls for procedural lessons about tool use and navigation. Existing extraction systems apply fixed, domain-specific schemas that perform well in their target domain but fail elsewhere.
