No benchmark previously measured memory extraction generalizability across all three interaction types under a unified protocol, so the scale of this cross-domain failure was unquantified. Self-evolving frameworks that auto-tune extraction prompts from feedback exist, but they assume homogeneous task distributions; under heterogeneous distributions their updates either overfit to recent examples (GEPA) or wash out fine-grained patterns by averaging across dissimilar tasks (MemEvolve, ACE). Key Idea: The core insight is that memory extraction scenarios cut across dataset and task-category boundaries — a long agentic trajectory requiring procedural knowledge extraction shares more structure with a multi-step math session than with a short personalization dialogue — so clustering examples by extraction scenario rather than by dataset origin allows a self-evolving system to learn scenario-specific strategies while synthesizing shared cross-scenario principles, avoiding both the overfitting and the signal dilution that afflict prior methods. Methodology: The paper formalizes single-step memory extraction as: given source conversation c, an extraction model LLM_e conditioned on prompt P produces memory string m = LLM_e(P, c); a generation model LLM_g then uses m to answer target query q; reward R(ŷ) ∈ [0,1] evaluates the answer. This isolates extraction quality from retrieval and management. BEHEMOTH is constructed by repurposing 18 existing datasets into this triplet format (c, q, R), organized into personalization (5 datasets), problem-solving (7 datasets), and agentic (6 datasets) categories; Qwen3-32B serves as LLM_g throughout. Three held-out datasets (LongMemEval, GPQA-Diamond, ToolBench) serve as out-of-distribution test sets. Evaluation uses Macro Accuracy (equal-weighted mean across datasets) and Relative Gain (geometric mean of per-dataset improvement ratios over a Simple baseline). CluE operates in rounds, each with four steps: (1) Summarization — a Summarizer LLM reads each training log and produces a scenario description abstracting away surface content; (2) Clustering — a Cluster Manager assigns examples to clusters by scenario, allowed to create/merge/split clusters across rounds; (3) Cluster-based Analysis — a Cluster Analyzer inspects logs within each cluster, identifying success patterns, failure patterns, and targeted recommendations; (4) Cross-cluster Proposal — a Proposer reads all cluster analyses, identifies cross-domain principles, resolves conflicts, and outputs a revised prompt with a structured memory taxonomy and scenario-specific guidelines. Critical design decisions: (a) clustering by extraction scenario rather than dataset is the central mechanism that breaks the heterogeneity problem — justified by ablation showing MemEvolve with same batch size but no clustering underperforms CluE substantially; (b) per-cluster rather than global analysis prevents conflicting signals from canceling, justified by qualitative analysis showing MemEvolve's evolved prompt devolves to known failure modes; (c) cross-cluster synthesis in the Proposer step allows domain-agnostic principles to emerge — the final CluE prompt contains both a structured memory taxonomy and general extraction guidelines absent from all baselines. Key Results: On in-distribution test sets with Qwen3-32B evolving from the Simple seed, CluE achieves 50.01% macro accuracy (+9.04% relative gain over Simple), versus MemEvolve at 47.08% (+2.11%), GEPA at 47.52% (+5.06%), and ACE at 45.91% (+3.56%). Critically, all three baseline frameworks exhibit category-level regressions: GEPA gains +14.08% on agentic but loses −2.16% on personalization; MemEvolve gains +10.76% on personalization but loses −3.25% on agentic; CluE is the only method with positive relative gains in all three categories simultaneously (+12.34%, +8.39%, +7.22%). On the three out-of-distribution datasets, CluE achieves 63.07 on LongMemEval (vs. 56.82 for second-best MemEvolve), does not drop below the Simple prompt on GPQA-Diamond or ToolBench, whereas GEPA degrades on ToolBench and ACE degrades on GPQA-Diamond. Evolving from the stronger Survey seed prompt, CluE achieves +6.54% overall relative gain, the only method with positive gains; GEPA finds no improvement and returns the seed unchanged; ACE and MemEvolve produce overall negative gains (−1.44%, −0.74%). On Gemini-3-Flash as extraction backend, CluE again achieves the highest relative gain (+3.40%) while ACE (−3.93%) and MemEvolve (−0.29%) regress. In the continual memory setting (Game of 24 and AlfWorld), CluE maintains its advantage over MemEvolve (50.83 vs. 43.33 on Game of 24; 67.25 vs. 62.57 on AlfWorld). Efficiency: CluE requires ~5.5h wall time and 221 optimization LLM calls, versus MemEvolve's 5.0h and 30 calls — a moderate overhead for substantially better results. Novelty Classification: NEW PROBLEM + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD. The heterogeneous memory extraction problem formulation and the BEHEMOTH benchmark are genuinely new: no prior work evaluated memory extraction generalization across all three task types under a unified protocol. The CluE method's scenario-based clustering within a self-evolving prompt optimization loop is a meaningful extension of the MemEvolve analyzer-proposer architecture, not merely a parameter tweak; the key novelty is that the clustering axis (extraction scenario, not dataset/category) is learned rather than given, and the cross-cluster synthesis step is new. The underlying components — LLM-based prompt optimization, tournament-based evolution (from MemEvolve), utility-driven reward — are existing ideas. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are implicitly provided through the comprehensive comparison against four baselines across 18 datasets, three categories, two seed prompts, two extraction backends, and a continual setting. However, there is no explicit ablation removing individual CluE components (e.g., ablating clustering alone or cross-cluster proposal alone) to isolate which stage drives gains — this is the main methodological gap. Error bars are reported for methods run multiple times due to small test sets (three runs averaged where N < 200), but not uniformly across all experiments. Code and data are publicly released at https://github.com/ayyyq/heterogeneous-memory-extraction. The benchmark construction pipeline is parameterized by dataset and LLM_g choice, making it extensible. One methodological note: all experiments use Qwen3-32B as both the extraction and generation model, meaning the extraction model optimizes prompts for a generation model with identical capabilities — real deployments might use different models, and this homogeneity could inflate perceived gains. This is partially addressed by the Gemini-3-Flash cross-backend experiment (Appendix B.1). This is a preprint under review; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: CluE uses off-the-shelf LLM APIs (Qwen3-32B, Gemini-3-Flash) and runs on standard infrastructure; the evolved prompt is just a text string requiring no model weights or specialized hardware to deploy. - Efficient: Evolution costs ~5.5h wall time and 221 optimization LLM calls (roughly 7× more calls than MemEvolve's 30, but similar wall time at ~5.0h due to parallelism); inference is a single prompt prepended to each conversation — negligible marginal cost. - Effective: The downstream utility-driven metric (not LLM-as-judge) provides direct evidence that better extracted memories improve answer quality; the +9.04% relative gain is measured on held-out test examples from all 18 datasets, and out-of-distribution gains on three unseen datasets corroborate generalization. - Scalable: Not tested beyond the 18-dataset, 330-training-example regime; the paper acknowledges that real deployments have greater task diversity and longer interaction histories, but provides no experiment at that scale — scalability is claimed, not measured. The work delivers a software artifact (code + benchmark data at the GitHub repo above) and an evolved text prompt that is immediately usable as a drop-in extraction instruction. Strengths: - The problem formulation is well-motivated and the BEHEMOTH benchmark fills a real gap: 18 datasets, three categories, out-of-distribution test split, and a utility-driven (non-LLM-judge) reward make it a credible evaluation platform for future memory extraction research. - The central empirical finding — that every prior self-evolving framework exhibits category-level regressions under heterogeneous distributions, trading off gains on one category against losses on another — is clearly demonstrated and directly motivates CluE's design. - CluE's cluster evolution analysis (Appendix B.3) provides interpretable mechanistic insight: the system starts with 7 clusters, consolidates to 4 by merging scenarios that share extraction patterns, and the Proposer maps these 4 clusters to a 5-category memory taxonomy by decoupling mixed clusters — a qualitative validation that the learned structure is semantically coherent. - The stronger-seed experiment (Table 4) is a genuinely useful stress test: CluE is the only method that improves over a Survey seed prompt without damaging its existing strengths, demonstrating robustness to a more structured initialization. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note two scope boundaries: (1) BEHEMOTH covers 18 datasets but real deployments have more complex scenarios, greater task diversity, and longer interaction histories; constructing more realistic benchmarks is listed as future work. (2) The paper addresses only memory extraction (the first stage of the memory lifecycle); generalizing to the full storage, retrieval, and management pipeline is an open challenge. These are confronted directly and honestly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No component-level ablation: the paper never removes clustering alone or the cross-cluster proposal step alone to show which contributes more; the comparison to MemEvolve (same architecture, no clustering) serves as a partial ablation but conflates the clustering axis with the scenario-summarization step. - The homogeneous LLM setup (same model as extractor and generator) is a real confound: the extraction model optimizes prompts that benefit a model with identical internal knowledge, which may not transfer to dissimilar generator/extractor pairs beyond the Gemini cross-backend test. - Training set size is very small (20 examples per dataset, 330 total), and the evolved prompt is evaluated primarily on test sets drawn from the same 18 source datasets. The three out-of-distribution datasets (LongMemEval, GPQA-Diamond, ToolBench) are valuable but still limited in diversity; the claim of broad generalizability is plausible but not fully established. - The continual memory experiment (§6.2) selects only two tasks (Game of 24 and AlfWorld) where CluE already outperforms MemEvolve in single-step evaluation, which is cherry-picked; a full BEHEMOTH-wide continual evaluation would be more convincing. - The utility-driven reward is a downstream accuracy proxy and may not capture all dimensions of memory quality (e.g., privacy, conciseness, hallucination in the extracted memory string itself). Bottom Line: This is a well-executed, problem-first paper that establishes a needed benchmark and identifies a real failure mode in existing self-evolving memory systems; practitioners building persistent assistants across heterogeneous task types will find both the BEHEMOTH benchmark and the CluE framework directly useful, though the absence of component ablations and the small training scale leave the precise source of gains somewhat underspecified.
Recommended 2604.11610 PDF
#85 — Innovative (68)

Safeguarding Language Models via Self-Destruct Trapdoor

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (80) is high—genuinely original use of BF16 overflow as kill switch. Non-Obviousness (80) similarly high. Rigor (50) drags hard: 100-prompt single-seed eval, no CIs, unexplained Falcon3 anomaly, no quantization eval. Completeness (55), Resilience (55) limited by detectability-by-inspection and quantization gap. Impact (70) helped by code release. Composite: 0.20*80 + 0.25*50 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*70 ≈ 62-64. Adjusted up slightly for distinctive primitive.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 55 Resilience 55 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Self-Destruct, a weight-level defense that embeds a hardware-overflow trapdoor into a single attention head of a pre-trained LM, causing inference to halt deterministically when harmful activations are detected while leaving benign behavior intact. - Why it's important: Existing LM defenses either add inference overhead (external monitors, secondary LM calls) or are bypassed by gradient-based jailbreaks; a defense with zero inference overhead that is inherently gradient-resistant addresses a core gap in deployment-grade safety. - How it does it: It constructs a weight perturbation W' via SVD null-space isolation — W' is invisible to benign activations but amplifies harmful activations to near the BF16 overflow boundary, so the Q·Kᵀ dot product exceeds the representable maximum, propagates NaN through all subsequent layers, and halts auto-regressive generation. - The good: Gradient-based adaptive attacks (GCG) fail from the first optimization step because backpropagation through NaN values yields undefined gradients, confirming robustness against the strongest known white-box jailbreaks. - The missing: The 100-prompt-per-benchmark evaluation with a single fixed seed and no confidence intervals makes it impossible to assess statistical reliability of the reported ASR reductions, and there is no evaluation on quantized (INT4/INT8) models that dominate real deployment. - Read it or not: Practitioners building production LM safety systems should read this — the zero-overhead, gradient-resistant profile is genuinely differentiated — but should note the quantization gap before deployment. Problem & Motivation: Language models deployed in production generate harmful content — malware code, instruction-override responses, biased text — when subjected to adversarial prompts ("jailbreaks"). Existing defenses fall into three camps: alignment training (computationally expensive, bypassed by sophisticated prompts), external monitors (add inference latency, require auxiliary models), and interpretability-guided steering (immature, limited scope). A critical property of modern LM inference frameworks — BF16/FP16 floating-point overflow — has been entirely overlooked as a defensive resource. When a BF16 computation exceeds the format's maximum (~3.39×10³⁸), the result is clamped to Inf; subsequent softmax and sampling operations over NaN logits raise a runtime error, halting generation at token 1. This hardware-level kill switch, if activated selectively on harmful inputs, would require no auxiliary compute and could be embedded directly in the model's weights. Key Idea: The core insight is that a targeted weight perturbation W' can be constructed so that it lies in the approximate null space of benign hidden-state activations H (making it invisible during normal inference) while simultaneously maximally amplifying harmful activations H' (pushing the Q·Kᵀ attention dot product above the BF16 overflow threshold). The null space provides a "safe channel" through which W' travels to harmful inputs but not benign ones. Methodology: The method modifies a single attention head's query and key projection matrices (Ŵ_q, Ŵ_k) via a three-step SVD-based construction. Step 1 (null-space isolation): compute SVD of the benign activation matrix H ∈ ℝ^(N×d); extract right null-space basis V₀ ∈ ℝ^(d×(d−r)) such that HV₀ ≈ 0. Step 2 (targeted subspace extraction): project harmful activations H' into the null space to get Z = H'V₀; compute SVD of Z and extract the top δ right singular vectors V₁^(δ) — these are null-space directions that maximize activation on H' inputs. Step 3 (head injection): form W' = D_factor × V₀ × V₁^(δ) ∈ ℝ^(d×δ) and add it to both Ŵ_q and Ŵ_k of a single attention head. At inference, benign h: q+q' ≈ q, k+k' ≈ k (W' suppressed by null space); harmful h': ‖q+q'‖ ≈ √Ω, ‖k+k'‖ ≈ √Ω, so (q+q')(k+k')ᵀ ≈ Ω → Overflow. A clustering phase (k-means on cosine similarity with n=8 groups) diversifies coverage across the H' distribution. A destruction factor D_factor is calibrated via grid search on a held-out validation split to maximize Δ = RefusalRate(H') − RefusalRate(H). Critical design decisions: (1) targeting the Q·Kᵀ dot product rather than post-softmax values, since softmax normalizes moderate increases and overflow must precede it — well-justified; (2) scaling both q and k to √Ω individually so each stays in-range while their product exceeds Ω — explicitly motivated; (3) modifying only one head to minimize benign disruption — empirically validated but not formally bounded. Key Results: Evaluated across 10 models from 5 families (Falcon3 3B/7B, Llama-3.2 1B/3B, Mistral-7B-v0.3, Qwen2.5 3B/7B/14B, Gemma2 2B/9B; all BF16) against 4 jailbreak benchmarks (MalwareGen, PromptInject, PAIR, AutoDAN) and 4 QA capability benchmarks (PIQA, OpenBookQA, SIQA, ARC), using 100 randomly sampled prompts per benchmark. On MalwareGen, Self-Destruct reduces ASR from 12→1 (Qwen2.5-3B), 39→1 (Qwen2.5-7B), 27→2 (Qwen2.5-14B), 67→4 (Mistral-7B). On AutoDAN, near-zero ASR across most Qwen2.5 and Gemma2 models. PromptInject is the hardest: Falcon3-7B drops only from 10→7, and some models remain in the 10–20 range. Baselines compared: Perplexity Filtering, Self-Defense, SelfDefenseInput, SmoothLLM — all single-LM defenses. Self-Destruct achieves the lowest ASR on 9 of 10 models across most benchmarks. QA accuracy is largely preserved: Qwen2.5-14B and Falcon3-3B show ≤1% change; notable exceptions are Qwen2.5-7B (avg −5.5%), Mistral-7B (avg −3.2%), and Qwen2.5-3B (elevated benign refusal on PIQA at 11%). A surprising anomaly: Falcon3-7B QA improves by +17.8% average under Self-Destruct. Fine-tuning resistance: NaN gradients in every batch regardless of optimizer (SGD, AdamW), batch size (1–10), or training data (harmful or harmless) — model becomes untrainable. GCG white-box attack: optimization diverges immediately (NaN/negative probability gradients). Cross-vendor: verified identical behavior on Apple MPS, vLLM with Triton-fused kernels, EAGER/SDPA/FLEX attention backends. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel contribution is using hardware floating-point overflow as a selective, weight-embedded kill switch for LM safety — no prior work has done this. The null-space injection technique adapts ideas from backdoor/watermarking literature (Shan et al. 2020; Xu et al. 2024a) but reframes them defensively and ties activation to a hardware-level termination rather than classification output. The clustering and destruction-factor calibration are standard engineering around the core SVD insight. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations over hyperparameters (layer, head, δ, n clusters, destruction factor) are presented via scatter plots (Figure 4) with interpretable observations (layer selection more critical than head choice; δ=1, n=8 robust across models). No error bars or multiple-seed runs are reported — all experiments use a single fixed seed with 100-prompt subsamples per benchmark, which is a meaningful concern given that ASR differences of 2–5 percentage points (e.g., Gemma2-9B) may not be statistically reliable at this sample size. The anomalous +17.8% QA improvement on Falcon3-7B is not analyzed. Code and configuration files are publicly released at https://github.com/shaharkatz3/LLM-Self-Destruct-Trapdoor with documented random seeds and environment details. Training data is from a third-party dataset (Arditi et al. 2024), distinct from evaluation benchmarks — no Goodhart's Law concern on the primary defense task. The ASR judge is a fine-tuned Llama-2-13B (HarmBench), supplemented by Gemma-3-27B in the appendix, with high rank correlation between judges (a credibility positive). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on standard NVIDIA GPUs (A100, L40s, RTX 3090, A5000, A6000) and Apple silicon MPS; compatible with vLLM and HuggingFace Transformers; requires only ~760 calibration samples. - Efficient: Zero additional inference overhead — the defense activates simultaneously with content generation, unlike SmoothLLM or Self-Defense which require additional LM forward passes; calibration grid search is the only upfront cost. - Effective: Convincingly blocks MalwareGen and AutoDAN for most models, but PromptInject resistance is inconsistent and a few models (Mistral-7B, Qwen2.5 small/mid) still allow 6–20% ASR; defense works by halting generation at token 1, so partial harmful output is impossible once triggered. - Scalable: Tested up to 14B parameters; behavior not evaluated at 70B+ scale; no evaluation on quantized (INT4/INT8/INT32) models, which are the dominant real-world deployment format, leaving a material gap. The work delivers a software artifact (code, config files, scripts) that can be applied to any BF16 HuggingFace-compatible model. Strengths: - The hardware-overflow kill switch is a genuinely original defensive primitive: it is zero-overhead, operates at the earliest possible point in generation (token 1), and is not a learned classifier subject to distribution shift. - Resistance to gradient-based adaptive attacks is a strong and non-obvious result — the NaN gradient pathology means the standard arms-race of "generate adversarial suffix against defended model" fails by construction. - The null-space construction provides a principled theoretical account of why benign inputs are unaffected, rather than relying on empirical tuning alone. - Generalization to bias mitigation (Δ=1 on BBQ) and model fingerprinting demonstrates that the mechanism is a general concept-level switch, not a brittle alignment hack. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors candidly state: (1) BF16/FP32 only — INT8/INT32/INT64 and quantization-aware training not evaluated; (2) an informed adversary with model access could detect the unusually large weights (threshold ~1×10⁵) and zero them out; (3) training data is limited to Arditi et al. (2024), covering a narrow distribution of harmful prompts; (4) only 100 prompts per benchmark subsampled due to cost of external LM-as-judge; (5) transparency concern — an embedded hidden kill switch may undermine user trust. They note these limitations directly and with appropriate specificity, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: A critical reviewer would flag: (1) 100-sample evaluation with a single fixed seed produces no confidence intervals — the reported ASR differences of 1–5 points on some models are not statistically distinguishable from noise, and the +17.8% QA improvement on Falcon3-7B is unexplained and possibly artifactual at this sample size; (2) no evaluation on quantized models despite these being the dominant production deployment format (INT4 via GPTQ/AWQ); (3) the comparison set deliberately excludes training-based defenses (RLHF, DPO) — reasonable on the inference-overhead framing but means practitioners cannot judge Self-Destruct against the primary production safety baseline; (4) the benign refusal elevation on Qwen2.5-3B (11% on PIQA) and Qwen2.5-7B (19% on PIQA, 22% SIQA) is a real accuracy-safety tradeoff that the paper does not adequately analyze or explain; (5) the white-box inspectability attack (zero out large weights) is described qualitatively but not experimentally evaluated — it would require only one line of code for a model owner and is a significant practical concern for open-weight deployments. Bottom Line: Practitioners building zero-overhead, gradient-resistant LM safety layers on BF16 open-weight models will find this directly actionable; the method's core vulnerability — detectability by weight inspection and inapplicability to quantized models — must be resolved before it can serve as a primary production defense.
Recommended
#86 — Innovative (68)

DARE: Diffusion Large Language Models Alignment and Reinforcement Executor

Jingyi Yang, Yuxian Jiang, Xuhao Hu, Shuang Cheng, Biqing Qi, Jing Shao · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (60) — framework integration plus empirical finding that no algorithm universally dominates. Rigor (55) weakened by single-epoch, no error bars, no comparison to original implementations. Non-Obviousness (65) — decoupled attention backends is a nice insight. Completeness (60) — BDLM RL results absent. Resilience (55). Impact (85) — released open-source framework fills a real fragmentation gap. Composite ≈ 0.15*60+0.15*55+0.10*65+0.15*60+0.15*55+0.30*85≈66.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 85
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DARE, an open-source unified framework for post-training (SFT, PEFT, preference optimization, and seven RL algorithms) and evaluating diffusion large language models across both masked and block diffusion model families. - Why it's important: The dLLM post-training ecosystem is so fragmented — each paper ships its own model fork, rollout engine, and eval scripts — that cross-paper algorithm comparisons conflate implementation differences with algorithmic differences, making it impossible to know which RL objective actually works better. - How it does it: DARE decouples the shared outer execution skeleton (rollout orchestration, reward dispatch, log-probability recomputation, checkpointing) from model- or algorithm-specific hooks, so algorithms plug in as small overrides rather than full pipeline forks, and further decouples rollout attention backends from actor-update attention backends to exploit each path's distinct bottleneck. - The good: Running seven previously fragmented RL algorithms under identical infrastructure on two dLLM backbones reveals that no single algorithm universally dominates — an empirical finding that would be masked by mismatched paper-specific implementations — while also delivering ~4× end-to-end RL pipeline speedup for MDLMs and >14× for BDLMs. - The missing: The algorithm comparison tables report no error bars or multiple seeds (all trained for a single epoch), and RL results are shown only for MDLM backbones (LLaDA, Dream), not for the BDLMs also supported by the framework. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone actively doing post-training research on diffusion language models; practitioners working only on autoregressive models can skip it. Problem & Motivation: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) — spanning masked diffusion models (LLaDA, Dream, LLaDA-MoE) and block diffusion models (SDAR, LLaDA2.0, LLaDA2.1) — have matured to the point where the bottleneck has shifted from architecture design to post-training infrastructure. The current ecosystem is acutely fragmented: RL methods such as VRPO, D1, Coupled-GRPO, MDPO, CJ-GRPO, SPG, and BGPO each live in their own repository with incompatible rollout implementations, reward interfaces, and benchmark scripts. This creates three concrete problems for researchers: new method integration requires re-implementing surrounding infrastructure, cross-paper comparisons are unreliable because algorithmic differences are entangled with execution differences, and the engineering barrier to studying or extending prior work is high. The fragmentation cannot be solved by reusing LLM RL frameworks (e.g., verl, Open-R1) because dLLMs require diffusion-aware forward/reverse processes, denoising-state likelihood surrogates, and model-family-specific rollout backends incompatible with left-to-right autoregressive assumptions. Key Idea: The core insight is that most dLLM post-training algorithms share the same outer execution structure — rollout generation, reward computation, forward-process recomputation, log-probability recomputation, advantage estimation, and actor update — and differ only in a small number of well-defined hooks: the forward corruption process, the trajectory construction rule, and the likelihood estimator or surrogate bound. By keeping the outer skeleton shared and restricting customization to these hooks, DARE transforms the unit of comparison from "paper plus custom codebase" to "algorithm inside a shared executor." A second key insight is that rollout and training benefit from different attention backends (KV-caching favors rollout; variable-length packing favors training), so decoupling these paths yields substantial practical speedups without sacrificing generality. Methodology: DARE is built on verl for distributed training and OpenCompass for evaluation, augmented with dLLM-specific execution layers. The framework is structured around three abstractions: Workers (rollout, actor, reward model or verifier, optional reference policy and critic), DataFlow (how prompts, responses, reward signals, perturbed trajectories, and log-probability signals move between workers), and Workflow (the outer optimization loop covering reverse-process rollout, reward computation, forward-process recomputation, RL-objective estimation, and actor update). This abstraction unifies SFT, PEFT, VRPO, D1, Coupled-GRPO, MDPO, CJ-GRPO, SPG, BGPO, and EBPO under a single codebase. For MDLMs, training uses flash_attn_varlen_func with sequence packing to eliminate padding overhead, while rollout uses Fast-dLLM combined with flash_attn_with_kvcache to exploit KV-cache reuse across denoising steps; this backend split is a deliberate design choice justified by the distinct bottlenecks of each path. For BDLMs, rollout is accelerated via LMDeploy or SGLang, and training uses FlexAttention to express semi-autoregressive block constraints with compiler-driven kernel optimization. Critical design decisions: (1) keeping the outer skeleton shared and exposing only algorithm-aware hooks — well-justified as it enables fair comparison; (2) decoupling rollout from training attention backends — well-justified empirically in Figure 2; (3) integrating evaluation as part of the same framework rather than a downstream afterthought — justified by the common failure mode of post-training and benchmark scripts diverging independently. Key Results: On training-side optimization, switching MDLM SFT from eager execution to flash_attn_varlen_func reduces per-iteration latency from approximately 22.1s to 10.8s (~2.0× speedup). On rollout-side, Fast-dLLM combined with flash_attn_with_kvcache reduces rollout latency from ~161.6s to ~73.4s (~2.2× speedup), and the decoupled rollout/training path yields ~4× end-to-end RL pipeline speedup for MDLMs; BDLMs achieve >14× RL pipeline acceleration. Baseline reproduction is reported for LLaDA-8B-Instruct, Dream-7B-Instruct, SDAR-8B-Chat, SDAR-30B-A3B, LLaDA2.0-mini, and LLaDA2.1-mini across MMLU, MMLU-Pro, HellaSwag, ARC-C, GPQA, GSM8K, MATH, AIME, Olympiad, HumanEval, and MBPP. In RL algorithm comparisons on LLaDA-8B-Instruct, CJ-GRPO reaches 85.6 on GSM8K (vs. baseline 76.5) and Coupled-GRPO reaches 41.0 on MATH (vs. baseline 34.6); on Dream-7B-Instruct, CJ-GRPO reaches 85.7 on GSM8K (vs. baseline 77.2). The central empirical finding is that no single algorithm dominates uniformly: algorithm rankings change across tasks (math vs. code vs. planning) and across backbones (LLaDA vs. Dream). Additionally, d1 and Coupled-GRPO show consistently stable training reward curves, while ELBO-based methods (SPG, BGPO) exhibit more instability attributed to Monte Carlo variance in the likelihood surrogate. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL and NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The framework architecture itself (shared executor with algorithm plug-in hooks, decoupled rollout/training backends) is a genuine engineering contribution not previously assembled for dLLMs. The finding that ELBO-based RL methods are systematically more unstable than D1/Coupled-GRPO under controlled conditions is a concrete empirical contribution. The individual RL algorithms integrated into DARE are all prior work; DARE's novelty is the integration and the controlled comparison it enables. Rigor & Reproducibility: Code is publicly available at https://github.com/yjyddq/DARE. Unified hyperparameters are reported for all RL experiments (rollout group size 8, block length 32, KL disabled, Monte Carlo sampling number 16 for applicable methods). However, the algorithm comparison tables report single runs with no error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple seeds, which is a meaningful concern for a paper whose central claim is enabling reliable comparative evaluation. All experiments train for a single epoch. The paper does not compare DARE-reproduced numbers against the original paper-specific implementations to verify fidelity of reproduction. The instability observations from training curves (Figure 3) are qualitative and not quantified statistically. Deployability: SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs on commercially available GPU hardware; specific GPU types are not stated, but the supported model sizes (7B–30B) are within standard multi-GPU setups. - Efficient: ~4× end-to-end RL speedup for MDLMs and >14× for BDLMs relative to baseline (eager/unoptimized) execution within the same framework; no comparison to external baselines like paper-specific codebases. - Effective: Reproduced baseline numbers are reported and the framework produces improvements above baseline across all tested RL algorithms, demonstrating the pipeline functions correctly; however, no independent verification against original paper implementations is provided. - Scalable: Tested only on 7B–8B models (and one 30B sparse MoE); no experiments at larger scales and no scaling claims are made. The paper delivers a software artifact (open-source framework with training, RL, and evaluation pipelines). Strengths: - Unifies seven previously incompatible dLLM RL codebases (sourced from D1, Coupled-GRPO, Open-R1 forks, verl forks, and closed-source implementations) under a single executor, making controlled algorithm comparison possible for the first time in this domain. - The decoupled rollout/training attention backend strategy is a non-obvious engineering insight that yields large concrete speedups (4× MDLM, 14× BDLM) without requiring algorithm-specific changes. - Coverage of both MDLM and BDLM families in a single framework addresses a genuine structural gap — these paradigms have meaningfully different rollout and training requirements that no prior unified tool addressed. - The empirical finding that algorithm robustness varies substantially across model families (SPG is much weaker on Dream than LLaDA for math and code) is a concrete, actionable insight that would be invisible without a unified executor. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that DARE does not propose a new optimization objective and is explicitly a systems substrate, not a method. Future work sections acknowledge that vision/omni dLLMs will require integration, that new estimators and stability techniques should be absorbed, and that more comprehensive efficiency ablations and deployment-oriented evaluation backends are needed. The authors do not directly confront the absence of error bars or the single-epoch training protocol. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The algorithm comparison tables (Tables 3–5) contain no error bars, standard deviations, or multiple seeds, which undermines confidence in fine-grained ranking claims — a paper positioning itself as enabling reliable comparison should apply higher statistical standards to its own results. RL algorithm comparisons are presented only for MDLM backbones (LLaDA, Dream); the BDLMs (SDAR, LLaDA2.0, LLaDA2.1) receive baseline benchmark tables but no RL algorithm comparisons, leaving the BDLM RL capability of DARE empirically unvalidated beyond training curves. No numbers from original paper-specific implementations are provided alongside DARE-reproduced numbers, so it is unverifiable whether DARE's reproductions match the originals. The >14× BDLM speedup claim lacks the same detailed ablation provided for MDLMs. All models tested are in the 7B–8B range (one 30B sparse MoE for baseline only); the framework's behavior at larger scales is untested. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or comparing dLLM post-training pipelines gains direct, practical value from DARE — it eliminates weeks of infrastructure work and provides the only public venue for controlled RL algorithm comparisons across dLLM families — but the statistical rigor of the comparative experiments should be treated with appropriate skepticism until multi-seed results are reported.
Recommended 2604.04215 PDF
#87 — Innovative (68)

From $P(y|x)$ to $P(y)$: Investigating Reinforcement Learning in Pre-train Space (23 ▲ )

Yuqiao Tan, Minzheng Wang, Bo Liu, Zichen Liu, Tian Liang, Shizhu He, Jun Zhao, Kang Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) — pre-train space formalization and NSR/PSR asymmetry is conceptually fresh with a gradient-alignment theorem + empirical check. Rigor (60) — no error bars, margin over Dr.GRPO is 0.5 pts, gradient alignment validated on one model/dataset. Non-Obviousness (75) — PSR collapse vs NSR effectiveness is non-trivial. Completeness (65) — math-only training. Resilience (60) — small gains could be within noise. Impact (65). Composite ≈ 67.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Pre-train Space Reinforcement Learning (PreRL) and Dual Space RL (DSRL), a two-phase training strategy that first applies Negative Sample Reinforcement while optimizing the marginal distribution P(y) (input question masked) before transitioning to standard post-train GRPO over P(y|x). - Why it's important: Standard RLVR (e.g., GRPO, PPO) is bounded by trajectories already latent in the base model's conditional distribution, and no prior work had applied online, reward-driven RL directly to P(y) — the space where pre-training encodes broad reasoning capacity. - How it does it: The input question x is masked during policy gradient updates so the model optimizes P(y) rather than P(y|x); shared model parameters ensure that reducing probability mass on incorrect trajectories in the marginal space simultaneously improves the conditional, as theoretically justified and empirically validated via gradient alignment. - The good: On Qwen3-8B, DSRL achieves an Avg@32 of 58.47 across six math benchmarks versus 57.00 for GRPO and 56.56 for DAPO, with a 56.15 on AIME24 compared to GRPO's 54.06, and OOD gains of +4.32 on MMLU-Pro and +2.52 on GPQA-Diamond. - The missing: All training and evaluation uses mathematics tasks only (MATH-7500 training set, AMC/AIME/Minerva/OlympiadBench evaluation), leaving generalization to non-math reasoning unestablished, and no experiments exceed 8B parameters. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners developing RLVR training pipelines for reasoning models should read it for the actionable PSR/NSR asymmetry insight and the pre-train space formalization, though gains over the strongest baselines are modest and narrow in domain. Problem & Motivation: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) trains LLMs by maximizing reward over the conditional distribution P(y|x), which constrains optimization to trajectories already present in the base model's output distribution. Prior work (Yue et al., 2025) has argued that RLVR cannot incentivize reasoning capacity beyond what the base model already encodes. Pre-train space optimization — directly targeting the marginal distribution P(y) — offers broader exploratory capacity by encoding reasoning ability into model parameters without conditioning on specific inputs. However, conventional pre-training relies on static corpora with passive next-token prediction objectives, creating a distribution shift between pre-trained knowledge and the task distribution encountered during post-training. No prior method had applied online, reward-driven RL directly to P(y) with verifiable task rewards. Key Idea: The central trick is input masking during the policy gradient update: by removing the conditioning on question x when computing the gradient (∇_θ log π_θ(y_t|x̸, y_{<t})), the model optimizes the marginal distribution P(y) rather than P(y|x). The justification relies on shared parameters — since the same θ governs both P(y) and P(y|x), a first-order Taylor expansion shows that one PreRL step updates log π(y|x) by η·R(y)·⟨∇log π(y), ∇log π(y|x)⟩ + O(η²). When gradient alignment is non-negative (empirically confirmed on 100% of 400 rollouts from AMC23 with average dot product +9.2 and positive cosine similarity), improving P(y) reliably improves P(y|x). The further asymmetric insight — that Negative Sample Reinforcement (NSR) works effectively in pre-train space while Positive Sample Reinforcement (PSR) collapses — drives the full DSRL design. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. First, the paper formalizes pre-train versus post-train space optimization as two distinct MDP objectives over the same policy parameters (Definitions 1–2), and provides both the gradient alignment theorem and empirical validation on Qwen3-4B with 400 AMC23 rollouts. Second, NSR-PreRL runs for S ∈ [10, 25] steps: at each step, GRPO samples G=8 responses per question; responses with negative advantage (A_i < 0) are used to compute the pre-train space gradient with x masked, suppressing incorrect trajectories. PSR (positive advantage samples) is excluded because maximizing P(y) on self-generated correct responses accumulates probability mass on on-policy trajectories and causes collapse. Third, DSRL applies Policy Reincarnation: at step S the model is re-initialized to the NSR-PreRL checkpoint and standard GRPO over P(y|x) resumes with all samples. The joint objective (Eq. 6) encodes this transition via indicator functions I[s≤S] (mask x) and I[s>S ∨ R(y)<0] (NSR constraint). Training uses Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B on MATH (7,500 problems), batch size 128, 8 rollouts per prompt, LR 1e-6, 6 epochs, max 8,192 response tokens. Three design decisions: (1) NSR-only in warmup phase — well-justified by the PSR collapse experiment; (2) hard transition at step S rather than mixing — motivated by preventing over-exploration interference with fine-grained conditional optimization; (3) optimal S ∈ [10, 25] — validated by ablation showing an inverted-U curve, with too few steps giving insufficient exploration and too many producing excessively long outputs. Key Results: On Qwen3-4B across six benchmarks (MATH500, AMC23, AIME24, AIME25, Minerva, OlympiadBench), DSRL achieves Avg@32 of 57.54 versus GRPO 55.79, Dr.GRPO 57.04, DAPO 55.82, PPO 56.15, RLOO 55.14. On Qwen3-8B, DSRL achieves 58.47 versus GRPO 57.00, Dr.GRPO 56.16, DAPO 56.56, PPO 57.34. The most substantial gains are on AIME24: +4.69 for 4B (51.15 vs 46.46) and +2.09 for 8B (56.15 vs 54.06). Pass@K curves (K from 1 to 256) consistently favor DSRL over GRPO for both model sizes. OOD generalization on Qwen3-4B: GPQA-Diamond +3.79, MMLU-Pro +5.37 versus GRPO; Qwen3-8B: GPQA-Diamond +2.52, MMLU-Pro +4.32. NSR-PreRL alone achieves 86% of GRPO's final accuracy in 3× fewer steps and increases transition thoughts 14.89× and reflection thoughts 6.54× versus the vanilla model. The ablation showing NSR-RL warmup (post-train space, same negative samples but conditioned on x) scores only 54.38 versus DSRL's 57.54 is the key control confirming that the pre-train space — not just negative sampling — drives improvement. Baselines (GRPO, PPO, RLOO, Reinforce++, Dr.GRPO, DAPO) are current and competitive. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The formalization of pre-train space as a distinct RL optimization target for LLMs with verifiable rewards is novel, as is the gradient alignment theorem connecting P(y) optimization to P(y|x) improvement. The PSR-vs-NSR asymmetry in pre-train space is a new empirical finding with a mechanistic explanation. The Policy Reincarnation strategy itself is borrowed from Agarwal et al. (2022); DSRL's novelty is specifically the NSR-PreRL warmup phase and the pre/post-train space decomposition framework. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present for warmup duration (Figure 7, inverted-U over S∈{5,10,15,20,25,30}) and warmup strategy (Table 3, NSR-RL vs NSR-PreRL). Gradient alignment is empirically validated on 400 rollouts from AMC23 using Qwen3-4B, showing 100% non-negative dot products. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported in the main results tables, which is a concern given that the margin over Dr.GRPO for Qwen3-4B is only 0.50 points average. Avg@32 (Pass@1 averaged over 32 rollouts) and n=300 samples for Pass@K reduce variance but do not substitute for significance testing. Code is stated to be publicly available (hyperlink in abstract). The gradient alignment validation relies on a single model and a single dataset (AMC23), and it is not confirmed to hold across architectures or non-math domains. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B using the open-source verl framework on standard GPU hardware, with no architectural modification required — only a masking change to the policy gradient computation. - Efficient: The NSR-PreRL warmup requires only 10–25 gradient steps, adding negligible overhead to the overall DSRL training run, which otherwise matches GRPO compute. - Effective: DSRL demonstrably improves Avg@32 over all baselines on math reasoning benchmarks and shows OOD gains on GPQA-Diamond, MMLU-Pro, BBH, and HumanEval, supporting that it solves more than a proxy for the stated problem. - Scalable: Tested only at 4B and 8B scales; the claim that pre-train space optimization expands the reasoning ceiling is not validated at 70B+ or at broader task diversity; scale behavior is claimed but not measured. The work delivers a trained-model artifact (checkpoints implied by code release) and a reusable training recipe, not just a paper-form result. Strengths: - The gradient alignment proof with direct empirical validation (gradient dot products, cosine similarity histograms, per-token log-probability comparisons) provides a principled and falsifiable justification for treating P(y) optimization as a surrogate for P(y|x) — this is more rigorous than most "pre-training as initialization" arguments. - The PSR-collapse vs NSR-effectiveness asymmetry (Table 3, Figure 3a) is a concrete, reproducible finding that clarifies why symmetric use of PreRL fails and directly motivates the DSRL design. - The ablation in Table 3 isolating the pre-train space from mere negative sampling (NSR-RL warmup at 54.38 vs DSRL at 57.54 vs GRPO at 55.79) cleanly attributes the gain to the optimization space, not just the sample selection. - OOD generalization across four out-of-domain benchmarks, including code (HumanEval +2.44 for 8B), reduces the concern that gains reflect narrow math benchmark overfitting. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge three limitations: (1) PSR-PreRL collapses on on-policy data and requires high-quality off-policy demonstrations to work, leaving positive sample integration in pre-train space as an open problem. (2) NSR-PreRL eventually generates excessively long outputs that destabilize continuous training, necessitating the transition back to standard RL. (3) The optimal warmup window S ∈ [10, 25] exhibits an inverted-U pattern, making the transition point a sensitive hyperparameter. The authors confront these directly and use them to motivate DSRL's design — a credibility-positive approach. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - All training and evaluation is restricted to mathematics (MATH-7500 training, AMC/AIME/Minerva/OlympiadBench/MATH500 evaluation). The OOD benchmarks (GPQA-Diamond, MMLU-Pro, BBH, HumanEval) are held-out evaluation sets, not training targets — whether PreRL's pre-train space insight transfers to code reasoning, scientific QA, or instruction following is untested. - No experiments exceed Qwen3-8B; whether gradient alignment holds and NSR-PreRL provides proportional benefit at 32B or 70B+ scale is entirely unaddressed. - The gradient alignment premise is validated on a single model (Qwen3-4B) and a single dataset (AMC23); its universality across model families and non-math domains is assumed rather than demonstrated. - Main result tables lack error bars or significance tests. The 0.50-point Avg@32 advantage of DSRL over Dr.GRPO (the closest competitor) on Qwen3-4B could plausibly be within noise given the evaluation variance at these scales. - The comparison to Policy Reincarnation precursors (Liang et al., 2025; Agarwal et al., 2022) is positional rather than ablative — no experiment tests whether vanilla warm-starting from a standard GRPO checkpoint of equivalent compute would match DSRL's performance. Bottom Line: This paper delivers a principled and actionable decomposition of LLM RL training into pre-train and post-train optimization spaces, with credible theoretical grounding and a clear empirical signal — practitioners building RLVR pipelines for math reasoning should adopt NSR-PreRL warmup, but should verify the gradient alignment assumption holds on their target domain before assuming the gains transfer.
Recommended 2604.14142 PDF
#88 — Innovative (68)

Exploration and Exploitation Errors Are Measurable for Language Model Agents (21 ▲ )

Jaden Park, Jungtaek Kim, Jongwon Jeong, Robert D. Nowak, Kangwook Lee, Yong Jae Lee · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (80) and Non-Obviousness (75) are the strengths — a policy-agnostic, graph-theoretically grounded decomposition of exploration vs. exploitation error from trajectories alone is a genuinely new formulation with classical Tarjan/Panaite-Pelc grounding. Rigor (60) is mixed: the headline R²=0.947 is computed over only 13 aggregate model points with no CIs, 3 seeds per config, no budget sensitivity ablation, and trajectory-dependent normalization the authors acknowledge undermines cross-model magnitude comparisons. Completeness (65) is helped by honest limitation acknowledgment. Resilience (55) — the scale gap between ≤64-cell grids and the real-world agent tasks motivating the work is unaddressed. Impact (60) — code released, useful diagnostic lens, but narrow in current form. Weighted: 0.20*80+0.25*60+0.15*75+0.15*65+0.15*55+0.10*60 = 65.5 → 66, nudged to 68 for the striking cross-model finding.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 55 Impact 60
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a policy-agnostic framework with a graph-theoretic error metric that quantifies exploration and exploitation failures in LM agents from observed action trajectories alone, using procedurally generated partially observable 2D grid environments with unknown task DAGs. - Why it's important: Current LM agent evaluation relies on binary task success rates, which cannot distinguish whether failures stem from poor exploration (missing task-critical discoveries), poor exploitation (failing to act on known information), or both — a distinction essential for targeted agent improvement. - How it does it: It defines a "stale score" accumulating cyclomatic number increments, edge reuse beyond budget 2, and node reuse beyond budget 2 within no-progress trajectory segments, then attributes each stale-score increase to exploration error, exploitation error, or both based on the agent's current map/task state — grounded in classical graph exploration theory (Tarjan, Panaite & Pelc). - The good: Log exploration error shows an R²=0.947 negative linear correlation with task success rate across 13 frontier LMs, strongly validating the metric's discriminative power. - The missing: All environments are semantics-free 2D grids with ≤8-node DAGs, so the R²=0.947 finding rests on 13 aggregate data points with no within-model confidence intervals, and no evidence is provided that findings transfer to real-world agent tasks at the scales motivating the work. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers who evaluate or build LM agents and want a principled behavioral metric beyond success rate; less relevant for those focused on real-world deployment where semantic reasoning dominates. Problem & Motivation: LM agents deployed in AI coding, workflow automation, and embodied AI must navigate partially observable environments to discover task-relevant entities and then exploit that knowledge to complete multi-step dependent tasks. While classical RL formalizes exploration and exploitation through internal policy or value functions, LM agents are black boxes whose internal strategies are inaccessible. Existing agent evaluation methods (ALFWorld, SWE-bench, Terminal-Bench) measure only task success, and more fine-grained metrics like stepwise alignment with reference trajectories implicitly assume a fixed optimal strategy. No prior framework quantitatively separates exploration failures from exploitation failures in LM agents using behavior alone, leaving practitioners unable to diagnose or target the correct failure mode. Key Idea: The core insight is to detect structurally redundant actions within no-progress trajectory segments — not by comparing to a reference trajectory, but by checking whether the agent's traversal exceeds graph-theoretically justified budgets: the cyclomatic number of the traversed subgraph (counting closed loops), edge reuse beyond 2 (the tight bound for optimal online undirected graph exploration from Tarjan 1972 and Panaite & Pelc 1999), and node reuse beyond 2. Any action that fails to make progress toward targets AND increases this stale score is classified as an error, attributed to exploration, exploitation, or both based on whether the agent currently has pending actionable tasks and unobserved cells. Methodology: The pipeline has three integrated components. First, environments are procedurally generated: partially observable 2D grid maps with traversable cell density 0.1–0.4 (controlling exploration demand) and corridor widths 1–3 cells, paired with symbolic task DAGs whose nodes use random 4-character alphanumeric identifiers (e.g., "D7UX") to eliminate semantic priors. DAGs have 4, 6, or 8 nodes (small/medium/large) with AND/OR prerequisite structure sampled from categorical distributions. Budget B = 3|O| where |O| is the number of traversable cells. Second, at each timestep the agent state is classified into four cases (Table 1): Case 1 — no pending tasks, explore; Case 2 — goal is pending, exploit; Case 3 — pending tasks exist, map fully explored, exploit; Case 4 — pending tasks and unobserved cells both exist, either action is valid. The target set T(t) — cells the agent should move toward — is derived from this case classification. Third, the stale score S_t = c_t + e_t + n_t is tracked within each no-progress segment (reset on each progress event), and err(t)=1 when Gain(t→t+1)=0 and S_t > S_{t-1}, with attribution to exploration/exploitation per Table 1. Critical design decisions: (a) symbolic node names are well-justified and effectively isolate raw navigation from pretrained knowledge; (b) the budget-of-2 constants for edges and nodes are grounded in classical graph exploration theory and well-justified; (c) no-progress segment scoping prevents stale-score accumulation from penalizing later correct behavior, though this also makes normalized error values trajectory-dependent. Key Results: Across 13 frontier LMs (GPT-4.1, GPT-4.1 mini/nano, GPT-5.4, GPT-5.4 mini/nano, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Gemini 3 Flash, Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite, Claude Opus 4.6, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Haiku 4.5, GPT-OSS-120B), log exploration error vs. success rate shows R²=0.947 negative linear correlation; log exploitation error vs. success rate shows only R²=0.006 — exploration failure is the dominant driver of task failure. Top models (Claude Opus 4.6, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Gemini 3 Flash) achieve ~100% success with low exploration error; weaker models (GPT-4.1 nano, GPT-5.4 nano) fail frequently with high exploration error. Harness engineering (injecting a rule-based structured memory summary with no new information) improves Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite from 51.9% to 88.9% success (exploration error: 0.172→0.030) and GPT-4.1 from 63.0% to 92.6% (exploration error: 0.297→0.053), with step counts also reduced. Exploration-focused prompts achieve highest success rate (80% for GPT-4.1 vs. 63% baseline); exploitation-focused prompts reduce exploitation error. Semantic information reintroduction (pasta-cooking task variant) produces opposite effects in different models: GPT-4.1 success improves ~3× with lower exploration error (semantic priors guide exploration), while Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite shows ~33% higher exploration error but 6× lower exploitation error (semantic priors bias toward premature exploitation). At R²=0.947, note this is a regression over 13 aggregate model-level data points, not per-episode statistics. Main experiment: 216 episodes per model (9 map configs × 3 seeds × 8 prompt modes). Semantic experiment: 80 episodes per model (4 maps × 5 seeds × 2 prompt modes × 2 semantic variants). Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK/EVALUATION FRAMEWORK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The policy-agnostic error decomposition grounded in classical graph exploration theory applied to LM agent behavioral evaluation is genuinely novel — no prior work formalized exploration and exploitation errors from trajectories without reference policies. The environments combine known elements (grid worlds, task DAGs) but their specific configuration for controlled LM evaluation is a new formulation. Prompt ablations and harness engineering experiments are competent execution of known ideas. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover four prompt variants and harness engineering; semantic experiments test both symbolic and semantic variants. Three random seeds per configuration at temperature 0 are used — a minimal variance control that the authors explicitly acknowledge as insufficient for clean trends in some experiments (Appendix G shows weak exploitation-error vs. exploitation-demand correlation). The headline R²=0.947 is computed over 13 aggregate data points with no confidence intervals or bootstrap tests reported. Cross-model error magnitude comparison is problematic because normalized error is trajectory-dependent (path choices that diverge early yield different denominators), a limitation the authors acknowledge directly. Code is publicly released (hyperlink in abstract). No trained models or datasets beyond the procedural generator are released. The small grid scale (≤64 cells, ≤8 DAG nodes) is the primary reproducibility strength — any group with API access can replicate experiments at low cost. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs entirely on standard LM API access with no special hardware; environments are generated in Python and require no GPU. - Efficient: 216 API calls per model for the main experiment; cost scales linearly with model pricing and episode count, feasible for research groups. - Effective: The R²=0.947 correlation validates the metric as a predictive indicator of success; harness engineering experiments confirm that reducing exploration error per the metric translates to real success-rate gains. - Scalable: Tested on grids up to ~64 cells and DAGs up to 8 nodes — substantially smaller than the coding/workflow tasks motivating the work; the authors claim extendability to richer environments but present no experiments beyond this scale. The work delivers a software artifact (evaluation code, procedural generator, prompts) stopping short of a trained model or reusable library in the traditional sense. Strengths: - The graph-theoretic grounding (cyclomatic complexity, Tarjan/Panaite-Pelc budget constants) gives the error metric a principled formal basis absent from ad-hoc heuristic error counts in prior work. - R²=0.947 between log exploration error and success rate across 13 models is a striking and concrete validation that the metric captures something real and cross-model predictive. - The symbolic node-naming design choice cleanly controls for semantic priors, enabling a controlled behavioral measurement that could not be achieved with real-world task names. - The finding that semantic information shifts different models' behavior in qualitatively opposite directions (exploration-boosting for GPT-4.1, exploitation-biasing for Gemini Flash Lite) is a specific, counter-intuitive result with implications for harness design in practice. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly confront three limitations: (1) symbolic abstraction removes semantic information present in all real deployments, making the framework a controlled test suite rather than a real-world proxy; (2) normalized error values are trajectory-dependent and should be treated as behavioral summaries rather than absolute quality measures for cross-model comparison; (3) per-run variance is high for some experiments and 3 seeds may be insufficient. This directness is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The R²=0.947 derives from 13 model-level aggregate points with no reported confidence intervals or statistical tests; it is compelling but not statistically rigorous at this sample size. - The step budget B=3|O| is a key hyperparameter directly controlling task feasibility; its sensitivity is never ablated — different budget values would change which actions are flagged as errors and potentially alter model rankings. - Maximum environment scale (8-node DAGs, ~64-cell grids) is far below the hundreds-of-step coding or workflow tasks motivating the work; the paper never demonstrates the metric behaves sensibly at larger scales. - The dramatic harness improvement (51.9%→88.9%) with no new information — only reorganized context — implies LMs fail at basic trajectory state tracking from raw history, which is itself an important finding the paper does not fully analyze relative to context-window-length or memory-augmentation baselines. - In Case 4 (pending tasks and unobserved cells both exist), errors increment both exploration and exploitation counters simultaneously, which prevents clean per-action attribution and somewhat dilutes the separation the paper claims as its contribution. Bottom Line: Researchers evaluating or improving LM agents will find the policy-agnostic metric and the R²=0.947 exploration-error/success finding a genuinely useful diagnostic lens beyond task success rate, but the small environment scale and absence of statistical rigor on the headline result mean findings should be treated as a promising framework requiring validation at realistic scales rather than established benchmark results.
Recommended 2604.13151 PDF
#89 — Innovative (67)

Reasoning Resides in Layers: Restoring Temporal Reasoning in Video-Language Models with Layer-Selective Merging

Zihang Fu, Haonan Wang, Jian Kang, Kenji Kawaguchi, Jiaying Wu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) and Completeness (75) lead — the interventional layer-masking gives causal rather than correlational evidence and limitations are explicitly confronted in an appendix. Novelty (70) is a principled constrained formulation over known components. Rigor (65) is hurt by the asymmetric best-of-6 MERIT vs. mean RANDOM-K comparison and unablated λ. Resilience (60) and Impact (50) reflect the 4–8B scale ceiling and lack of retraining-baseline benchmarking. Weighted composite ≈ 67.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 60 Impact 50
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MERIT, a training-free, layer-selective model merging framework that restores temporal reasoning in video-language models by using CMA-ES evolutionary search to identify which self-attention layers to interpolate from a paired text-only LLM backbone. - Why it's important: Multimodal adaptation systematically degrades the temporal reasoning abilities LLMs acquire during language-only pretraining, leaving deployed VLMs with strong perceptual competence but weak causal/temporal inference—a problem that retraining addresses expensively and uniform merging addresses unreliably. - How it does it: MERIT parameterizes layer-wise merging as a continuous gating vector over self-attention layers, then optimizes a constrained objective—maximizing temporal reasoning accuracy while penalizing temporal perception degradation—via black-box CMA-ES over a small held-in evaluation sample. - The good: TR gains of +23.9% (LongVA-7B), +10.8% (InternVL3-8B), and +3.8% (Qwen3-VL-4B) on Video-MME generalize to four held-out benchmarks not used during search, and interventional layer masking provides causal evidence that selected layers are disproportionately responsible for temporal reasoning. - The missing: MERIT is evaluated only at 4B–8B scale, and no comparison against DPO-based or instruction-tuned retraining is provided at matched compute—the training-free efficiency claim is asserted but not quantified against the training-based alternative. - Read it or not: Worth reading for VLM researchers and practitioners working on model merging or temporal reasoning, primarily for the constrained optimization formulation and the mechanistic interventional analysis; less essential for those outside video-language modeling. Problem & Motivation When LLMs are adapted into video-language models by attaching visual encoders, the resulting VLMs regularly lose the temporal reasoning abilities of the original text backbone. This degradation appears in two forms: (1) VLMs fail on text-only temporal logic questions that the source LLM answers correctly, and (2) even when VLMs correctly identify salient visual entities, they fail to infer causal-temporal structure from event sequences. The root cause is a mismatch in adaptation objectives: multimodal training pipelines provide strong supervision for spatial perception and object alignment but weak supervision for temporal abstraction and causal inference. Existing recovery approaches are either expensive (DPO-based retraining) or coarse (uniform full-model merging that trades perceptual competence for reasoning gains unpredictably). The specific gap closed is the lack of a targeted, training-free method that recovers reasoning at specific network locations without degrading perception. Key Idea Temporal reasoning in VLMs is not uniformly distributed across layers but resides disproportionately in a localized subset of self-attention layers; replacing those specific layers' parameters with corresponding parameters from the text backbone—identified by optimizing a constrained objective over a small labeled evaluation sample—recovers temporal reasoning while leaving perception-critical layers intact. This stands in contrast to the implicit assumption of full-model merging that the language backbone is a homogeneous block. Methodology MERIT defines layer-wise merging recipes as a continuous gating vector g ∈ [0,1]^L, one gate per self-attention layer. At evaluation, gates are thresholded (≥0.5 → VLM-dominant, <0.5 → LLM-dominant). At each layer, parameters are interpolated: θ_ℓ = α·θ^M_ℓ + (1−α)·θ^N_ℓ (VLM-dominant) or (1−α)·θ^M_ℓ + α·θ^N_ℓ (LLM-dominant), where α ∈ {1.0, 0.9, 0.8, 0.7, 0.6, 0.5} controls interpolation strength. Only self-attention parameters are merged; feed-forward layers remain unchanged. The search objective is F(g) = Acc_TR(g) − λ·D_TP(g), where D_TP(g) = max(0, Acc^base_TP − Acc_TP(g)) penalizes perceptual degradation. The objective is non-differentiable (discrete model evaluations), so CMA-ES is used with population size λ_pop = 4 + ⌊3 ln L⌋, capped at 1,600 evaluations. The search runs independently for each α value; the final recipe is the best-scoring across all runs. The search uses 55 TP examples and 177 TR examples from Video-MME. Three critical design decisions: (1) self-attention-only merging (not FFN) reduces search space and is motivated by prior work on layer-wise reasoning localization—justified; (2) continuous gating with discrete thresholding enables CMA-ES to explore continuously while evaluating concrete models—well-justified for non-differentiable objectives; (3) α is searched discretely rather than jointly optimized with g—a tractability compromise the authors acknowledge as a limitation. Key Results On Video-MME (Table 1), MERIT achieves TR accuracy of 49.7% vs. 40.1% base for LongVA-7B (+23.9%), 57.6% vs. 52.0% for InternVL3-8B (+10.8%), and 46.9% vs. 45.2% for Qwen3-VL-4B (+3.8%), while temporal perception is preserved or improved in all three cases. On four held-out benchmarks (Table 2)—LongVideoBench, LVBench, MMBench-Video, Video-Holmes—MERIT consistently outperforms the base VLM, with Video-Holmes overall accuracy gains of +24.3% (LongVA-7B), +1.9% (InternVL3-8B), and +4.4% (Qwen3-VL-4B). Both ALL-LAYER (uniform full merge) and RANDOM-K (random layer selection matching MERIT's count, mean±std over 5 runs) underperform MERIT on TR in all three backbones, confirming that layer identity matters beyond layer count. The interventional masking experiment (LVBench) shows that at κ=0.4, masking MERIT-selected layers causes Reasoning to drop 35.1% vs. 16.8% Overall and 2.7% Others for LongVA-7B; for InternVL3-8B at κ=0, Reasoning drops 58.2% vs. 35.2% for non-selected layers. Baselines are recent (LongVA-7B, InternVL3-8B, Qwen3-VL-4B, all 2024–2025). No retraining-based baseline is included. Scale is limited to 4B–8B models. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The core novelty is formulating VLM temporal reasoning recovery as a layer-wise, perception-constrained, training-free optimization problem solved via evolutionary search—specifically the combination of an explicit TP degradation penalty in the merging objective and CMA-ES over self-attention gating vectors, applied to the video-language temporal reasoning problem. Individual components (model merging, CMA-ES, self-attention localization) are established; the principled constrained formulation and its application to targeted capability recovery in VLMs are the genuine contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility The MERIT vs. ALL-LAYER vs. RANDOM-K comparison across three VLMs and five benchmarks functions as an ablation of the selectivity claim. RANDOM-K reports mean±std over 5 runs; MERIT runs once per α (6 runs) and selects the best, introducing a selection effect not discussed—the reported MERIT numbers are not directly comparable to RANDOM-K means. The penalty weight λ is not ablated; no sensitivity analysis is reported. The interventional masking analysis provides causal (not merely correlational) evidence, which is methodologically strong. The search set (232 Video-MME samples) is distinct from evaluation benchmarks, mitigating direct overfitting. No code repository or model weights are mentioned. MMBench-Video and Video-Holmes use GPT-4-Turbo and GPT-4-0125 as LLM judges, introducing vendor-dependent evaluation variance. This is a preprint under review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (with SYSTEMS elements—a built merging pipeline using mergekit-evolve). - Practical: Runs on standard 7B–8B VLMs on commercially available GPU hardware; the search requires ~1,600 forward passes on 232 examples, feasible on a single GPU node, though absolute GPU-hour cost is not stated. - Efficient: The merged model has identical inference cost to the base VLM; search achieves 2–3× speedup through caching of visual encoder outputs and duplicate recipe detection. The final recipe requires no additional parameters or inference overhead. - Effective: TR gains transfer to four held-out benchmarks not used during search, and frame-level attribution shows MERIT grounds decisions in temporally distributed causal evidence rather than salient local frames—providing mechanistic corroboration beyond benchmark accuracy. - Scalable: Tested only on 4B–8B models; behavior at 13B+ or 70B+ scale is not evaluated. Search cost scales with model depth (L self-attention layers), but the paper does not analyze this scaling. Whether the layer-selectivity phenomenon holds in larger models with more redundant reasoning representations is unknown. The work delivers a pipeline built on mergekit-evolve; no standalone library, trained model weights, or dataset is released. Strengths - The constrained optimization objective explicitly balances TR recovery against TP preservation—a principled formulation that prevents the perceptual collapse seen in naive uniform merging and directly operationalizes the paper's central claim. - The interventional layer masking experiment (§4.3) provides causal evidence: masking MERIT-selected layers degrades Reasoning substantially faster than Overall or non-selected layers, moving beyond correlational benchmark comparisons. - Generalization to four held-out benchmarks (LongVideoBench, LVBench, MMBench-Video, Video-Holmes) not seen during search credibly demonstrates recovery of general reasoning behavior rather than benchmark-specific fitting. - Frame-level gradient-activation attribution reveals that MERIT shifts grounding from locally salient but causally irrelevant frames toward temporally distributed, causally informative evidence—providing interpretable mechanistic support for the accuracy gains. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state three limitations in Appendix A: (1) MERIT depends on benchmark-defined evaluation signals for TR and TP, so discovered recipes may be tied to the specific operationalization of these tasks, limiting applicability where supervision is weaker or noisier; (2) separately optimizing α from g is a tractability compromise—jointly optimizing both would yield a richer search space but is computationally harder; (3) optimal recipes are model-specific and do not transfer across architectures, making the approach non-reusable without re-running search for each new VLM. The authors confront these directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No comparison against DPO-based retraining or instruction-tuned baselines at matched GPU budgets: the training-free efficiency advantage is asserted without quantifying TR recovery per unit compute against the alternative. - MERIT selects the best recipe across 6 α runs; RANDOM-K reports the mean across 5 runs. This asymmetry inflates MERIT's apparent advantage—a best-of-6 MERIT should be compared against best-of-5 RANDOM-K, not mean RANDOM-K. - The penalty weight λ is entirely unablated; it is unclear whether TR gains are sensitive to λ or whether λ=0 (unconstrained TR maximization) would recover equally without perception harm. - All tested VLMs are 4B–8B; the layer-selectivity hypothesis is untested at 30B+ scale where temporal reasoning may be more redundantly distributed across layers. - Video-Holmes absolute accuracy is low (15%–37%), and the GPT-4-0125 LLM judge introduces additional variance; it is unclear whether differences at this performance level are statistically reliable. Bottom Line Practitioners working on video-language understanding or model merging will find MERIT's constrained layer-selective formulation and its supporting interventional evidence a genuine contribution, but the absence of retraining baselines, incomplete ablations, and evaluation limited to sub-10B models leave the method's full operating envelope incompletely characterized.
Recommended 2604.11399 PDF
#90 — Innovative (67)

CLEAR: Context Augmentation from Contrastive Learning of Experience via Agentic Reflection

Linbo Liu, Guande Wu, Han Ding, Yawei Wang, Qiang Zhou, Yuzhe Lu, Zhichao Xu, Huan Song, Panpan Xu, Lin Lee Cheong · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (70) is the main driver: three-run averaging, clear ablations isolating contrastive SFT vs. RL, public code, and a CAM-transferability experiment that partially addresses circularity; however, using Claude-Sonnet-4 as both trajectory source and GRPO reward source during training is an unacknowledged Goodhart risk, and WebShop-40k is a self-constructed easier variant. Novelty (65) is a clean new method combining contrastive agentic reflection with SFT+RL for a learned context generator, though the three-phase paradigm itself is standard. Non-Obviousness (65), Completeness (70), and Resilience (60) are solid; Impact (70) benefits from released code and demonstrated gains on proprietary execution models. Only two relatively shallow benchmarks (no SWE-bench/GAIA/WebArena) cap the composite. Composite ≈ 67.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CLEAR, a framework that trains a lightweight Context Augmentation Model (CAM) via contrastive agentic reflection, SFT, and GRPO-based RL to generate task-specific instructional context prepended to an LLM execution agent's prompt at inference time. - Why it's important: Many deployed LLM agents use proprietary models whose weights cannot be modified, so improving agent performance requires context engineering rather than fine-tuning the execution model itself — but static retrieval of past context imposes reasoning burden on the agent to adapt it to new tasks. - How it does it: A reflection agent analyzes multiple rollouts of the same task contrastively (comparing successes and failures), distills actionable instructions as SFT training data for the CAM, then RL further optimizes the CAM using task-completion rewards from a live execution agent as the signal. - The good: On AppWorld TEST-N, CLEAR raises Task Goal Completion from 72.62% (baseline) to 81.15% and Scenario Goal Completion from 52.38% to 66.67%, outperforming both RAG and ACE (which uses benchmark-specific prompts), with ablations confirming each of the three training phases contributes meaningfully. - The missing: WebShop evaluation uses a self-constructed 40k-item subset that the paper acknowledges is likely easier than the original benchmark, making cross-paper comparisons unreliable; evaluation is limited to two benchmarks with no coverage of code-heavy or long-horizon tasks like SWE-bench. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners building agentic pipelines on top of proprietary LLMs who want a principled alternative to RAG-based context engineering; can be skipped if your interest is in modifying the execution model directly. Problem & Motivation: LLM agents operating in sequential decision-making environments (tool-calling, web shopping, multi-app automation) rely on static parametric knowledge that may be outdated or insufficient for task-specific details. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) partially addresses this but requires the agent to reason over retrieved passages that were written in the context of past tasks, not the current one — an adaptation burden that degrades performance, especially when the underlying execution model is a weaker LLM. Prompt-optimization approaches like ACE and Dynamic Cheatsheet learn reusable heuristics from past experience but produce static, task-class-level guidance rather than context tailored to a specific new task instance.
