The problem compounds when the execution agent is a closed, proprietary model (GPT, Claude, Gemini) whose weights cannot be updated, making inference-time context injection the only available lever. Key Idea: Instead of retrieving or statically maintaining reusable prompts, CLEAR trains a small generative model (the CAM) to directly synthesize the most useful instructional context for a given new task query, where that synthesis capability is learned from contrastive analysis of multiple execution trajectories for the same task — extracting what differentiates successful from failed attempts — and then further refined by RL using live execution-agent rewards as the training signal. Methodology: CLEAR operates in three phases. Phase 0 (agentic reflection): for each training task q_i, the execution agent π^E is run m=6 times to collect a grouped replay buffer Γ_i of trajectories with associated rewards. A reflection agent π^R (Claude-Sonnet-4 with shell tool access for trajectory inspection) performs contrastive analysis over these grouped rollouts and produces a task-specific instruction c_i, yielding SFT pairs (q_i, c_i). To address small dataset size, C(6,3)=20 subsets of 3 trajectories per task are sampled, expanding the dataset 20×. Phase 1 (SFT): a Qwen/Qwen3-32B model is full-parameter fine-tuned on these pairs for 5 epochs on 8 NVIDIA B200 GPUs using LlamaFactory. Phase 2 (RL): the SFT-initialized CAM is further optimized via GRPO (implemented with Verl), where for each training query q, the CAM generates context c, the frozen execution agent π^E runs on q⊕c in the live benchmark environment, and the resulting task-completion reward is used to update only the CAM's parameters. The objective is max_θ E[R(τ)] where τ is the trajectory of π^E conditioned on the CAM-generated context. At inference, the CAM generates c_new for each new task, which is prepended to the task description before the execution agent acts. Critical design decisions: (1) separating the CAM from the execution agent — justified as necessary for compatibility with proprietary models; (2) contrastive multi-trajectory reflection rather than single-trajectory reflection — justified empirically via ablation; (3) SFT warm-up before RL — justified as necessary to avoid RL instability from a cold start. Key Results: On AppWorld TEST-N with Claude-Sonnet-4 as execution agent: CLEAR achieves 81.15% TGC (avg over 3 runs, ±2.48) and 66.67% SGC (±4.49), compared to baseline 72.62%/52.38%, RAG 86.31%/54.67%, and ACE 74.40%/58.93%. Pass@3 for CLEAR is 91.67% TGC and 82.14% SGC, above ACE's 85.71%/73.21%. RAG achieves higher TGC avg than ACE (86.31 vs 74.40) but lower SGC (54.67 vs 58.93), suggesting RAG retrieval is noisy on harder scenarios. On WebShop-40k with Claude-Sonnet-4: CLEAR averages 0.7406 reward (±0.0044) vs baseline 0.6799 and RAG 0.7252. Ablation (Table 3) shows that removing RL drops TGC from 81.15 to 74.21 and SGC from 66.67 to 50.60; removing contrastive learning (using single trajectory for SFT) drops TGC to 68.65; using untuned Qwen3-32B directly gives 67.40/48.81. CAM transferability experiment: when DeepSeek-V3.1 replaces Claude-Sonnet-4 as execution agent at test time (CAM trained on Claude trajectories), CLEAR still outperforms all baselines with 42.95% TGC vs 41.27% baseline, and RAG and ACE both underperform the baseline in this setting. Latency overhead is 1.21s per CAM invocation and 13.8s total additional task time — modest relative to performance gains. Baselines are reasonably current (RAG with BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5, ACE with equivalent LLM). The WebShop-40k benchmark is a self-constructed reduced version, explicitly noted as potentially easier than the original. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty lies in (1) training a dedicated, lightweight generative context augmentation model rather than retrieving from a static knowledge base, and (2) the three-phase pipeline combining contrastive agentic reflection for SFT data generation with downstream RL using live execution rewards. The SFT+RL paradigm itself is standard (following InstructGPT/DeepSeek-R1 practice); the contrastive reflection mechanism for SFT data generation in the agentic trajectory setting is the primary technical contribution. A concurrent work (Asawa et al., 2026) designs a similar RL pipeline without the contrastive SFT stage; CLEAR differs by including all three phases. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation study (Table 3, Appendix A) isolates the contribution of contrastive learning, SFT, and RL — all three are shown necessary with non-trivial deltas. Results are averaged over 3 independent runs with standard deviations reported for Avg metrics (Pass@3 uses a single run per method, no error bars). No significance tests are reported. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/awslabs/CLEAR. Hyperparameters for SFT and RL are fully specified in Appendix E. One methodological concern: the RL training signal is obtained by running Claude-Sonnet-4 as the execution agent on AppWorld during GRPO updates — the same model family used for trajectory collection and reflection — creating a feedback loop where the CAM is optimized specifically to complement Claude's reasoning patterns. The CAM transferability experiment partially addresses this, but the training-time circularity is unacknowledged. The WebShop-40k variant is constructed by the authors and not independently validated, and ACE is excluded from WebShop comparison because it lacks benchmark-specific prompts for that dataset, making the WebShop results a two-way rather than three-way comparison. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: CAM runs as a vLLM-served Qwen3-32B model on 8 NVIDIA B200 GPUs; the 32B scale requires high-end hardware, though smaller CAM variants are not explored. - Efficient: CAM inference adds 1.21s latency per task invocation (≈69.5 tokens/s throughput), totaling ~13.8s overhead per episode on AppWorld — acceptable for non-interactive workflows but potentially limiting for real-time applications. - Effective: On AppWorld and WebShop-40k the method demonstrably improves task completion over RAG and ACE baselines using the same execution agent; the evidence is credible given 3-run averaging and ablations. - Scalable: Tested only on two benchmarks with task sets numbering in the hundreds; behavior at 10×–100× task diversity, longer horizons, or more complex tool ecosystems (e.g., SWE-bench) is not evaluated and not claimed. The work delivers a code repository and trained CAM weights are implicitly available via the public repo. Strengths: - The CAM-as-generative-context paradigm is a clean separation of concerns: the small CAM is trainable while the large proprietary execution model is frozen, making the approach broadly deployable without model access. - Contrastive multi-trajectory reflection is empirically well-motivated: the ablation shows it adds +5.56 TGC points over SFT without contrastive learning, which is a non-trivial and specific contribution. - CAM transferability across execution agents (Claude-trained CAM deployed with DeepSeek) is a notable result that strengthens claims of generality — particularly the finding that RAG and ACE actually hurt performance with weaker execution agents while CLEAR helps. - Full hyperparameter specification and public code release support reproducibility. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that WebShop-40k is likely easier than the original WebShop benchmark due to reduced search space, and that ACE is excluded from the WebShop comparison due to the absence of benchmark-specific prompts for that dataset. They acknowledge the CLEAR framework requires training a CAM (unlike training-free ACE), but frame this as acceptable overhead. No discussion of failure modes of the reflection agent, potential reward hacking in the RL phase, or behavior on tasks outside the training distribution is provided. The authors confront these limitations briefly but do not investigate them empirically. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The RL training loop uses Claude-Sonnet-4 as both the trajectory generator and the reward source during GRPO updates, creating a potential Goodhart's Law situation where the CAM learns to generate context that exploits Claude's specific reasoning patterns rather than genuinely useful task guidance. The transferability experiment tests a different execution model at test time only, not during training. - Only two benchmarks are evaluated, both relatively shallow agentic tasks (multi-app API calls, e-commerce navigation). Harder agentic benchmarks like SWE-bench, GAIA, or WebArena are absent, making it unclear whether the approach scales to tasks requiring deeper reasoning. - The combinatorial data augmentation (C(6,3)=20 subsets) is a dataset inflation technique that introduces dependencies between training examples — this may inflate the effective dataset size without proportionally increasing information content, and its effect on CAM generalization is not isolated. - The paper does not compare against Dynamic Cheatsheet (Suzgun et al., 2025) or GEPA (Agrawal et al., 2025) on the same benchmarks, leaving two named competitors uncontested experimentally. - No analysis of what the CAM actually generates — whether it produces meaningfully different context across tasks or degenerates toward a fixed high-performing instruction for each benchmark — is provided. Bottom Line: CLEAR delivers a practically motivated and well-ablated method for improving black-box LLM agents via learned context generation, and practitioners building proprietary-LLM pipelines who cannot fine-tune the execution model should read it; the narrow benchmark scope and unacknowledged RL circularity temper confidence that the gains will hold on harder, more diverse agent tasks.
Recommended 2604.07487 PDF
#91 — Innovative (67)

Maximal Balanced Quasi-Clique Enumeration in Signed Graphs

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (80) strongest — NEW PROBLEM + NEW FORMULATION: first QC model for signed graphs grounded in structural balance, with NP-hardness proof. Non-Obv (75) for the signed-path candidate set construction and two-parameter γ1/γ2 design. Rigor (65) dinged — 3/6 datasets artificially signed (violating balance assumptions), no code release, no memory reporting. Completeness (60) and Resilience (60) weakened by absent comparison to (α,k)-clique/(ε,φ)-clique alternatives and missing recall in the case study. Impact (55) — Python implementation not released. Weighted: 0.20*80+0.25*65+0.15*75+0.15*60+0.15*60+0.10*55 ≈ 67.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the Maximal Balanced (γ1,γ2)-Quasi-Clique (MBQC) model for signed graphs — the first quasi-clique formulation grounded in structural balance theory — and delivers a branch-and-bound enumeration algorithm (MBQCEnum*) with four layers of signed-graph-specific pruning. - Why it's important: Real-world networks in social, biological, and financial domains carry both positive and negative edges, but all existing quasi-clique models ignore edge signs, leaving analysts without a principled tool to discover dense subgraphs that simultaneously reflect intra-group cohesion and inter-group antagonism. - How it does it: A balanced quasi-clique is defined as a connected subgraph 2-partitionable such that each node meets separate minimum positive-degree fraction γ1 within its partition and negative-degree fraction γ2 to the opposite partition; enumeration exploits the structural balance property that co-partition nodes are reachable only via positive signed paths, restricting the candidate search space and enabling degree-based, upper/lower-bound-based, and critical-node pruning rules. - The good: MBQCEnum* completes MBQC enumeration on all six real-world datasets up to 1.63M nodes and 22.3M edges — achieving up to 10× speedup over the unpruned base algorithm — while the baseline (which decouples positive and negative edge mining) fails to complete on all but the smallest dataset. - The missing: Three of six benchmark datasets (DBLP, Youtube, Pokec) are artificially signed by randomly relabeling 30% of unsigned edges as negative, violating the structural balance assumptions the algorithm exploits; the authors do not discuss how this distortion affects either the structure of found MBQCs or algorithm performance. - Read it or not: Researchers building cohesive subgraph mining pipelines for signed social, biological, or political networks should read this; those focused on unsigned graphs or other signed primitives (cores, trusses) can safely skip it. Problem & Motivation Many real-world networks carry both positive edges (friendship, activation, trust) and negative edges (enmity, inhibition, distrust), making signed graphs the natural data model for social networks, protein interaction networks, and financial networks. Quasi-clique (QC) models that relax the strict completeness requirement of cliques are widely used to find cohesive subgraphs in unsigned graphs, but no QC model exists for signed graphs. The closest alternatives — signed cliques, balanced cliques, (p,n)-cores, (α,k)-cliques — either require complete connectivity (too strict for noisy data) or ignore quasi-completeness (too coarse). The structural balance theory of signed graphs predicts that stable signed networks partition into two opposing groups with dense positive intra-group edges and negative inter-group edges; existing QC models applied naively to signed graphs discard this semantically meaningful structural property. The gap is: finding dense, noise-tolerant signed subgraphs that respect both quasi-completeness and balance structure simultaneously. Key Idea The core technical insight is to unify γ-quasi-clique density with structural balance by parameterizing intra-group and inter-group density separately: a balanced (γ1,γ2)-quasi-clique requires each node to have positive degree ≥ γ1 fraction of same-partition nodes and negative degree ≥ γ2 fraction of opposite-partition nodes. This two-parameter formulation independently controls cohesion and antagonism, reducing to the balanced clique when γ1 = γ2 = 1. A non-obvious structural property follows from balance theory: any two nodes in the same partition of a BQC are connected only through positive signed paths, and cross-partition nodes only through negative paths. This lets the algorithm pre-compute per-node positive candidate sets PC(u) and negative candidate sets NC(u) via L-hop signed path traversal (where L is bounded by BQC diameter), drastically restricting which nodes can co-occupy the same partition during enumeration. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. Pre-processing: reduce the input signed graph to its maximum (⌈γ1(θ-1)⌉, ⌈γ2θ⌉)-signed core by iteratively removing nodes with insufficient positive or negative degree; compute PC(u) and NC(u) for all nodes via Algorithm 2, a BFS-style L-hop signed path expansion where positive path neighbors go to PC and negative path neighbors go to NC, with O(nδD) total complexity (n nodes, δ degeneracy, D max degree). Enumeration: MBQCEnum (Algorithm 1) follows the classical set-enumeration search tree framework for non-hereditary subgraphs. Each branch B = ⟨X, C, D⟩ maintains partial partition sets X = {XL, XR}, candidate sets C = {CL, CR}, and exclusion sets D = {DL, DR}. Two branching rules expand X by adding a node from CL into XL (same-partition candidate) or from CR into XR (opposite-partition candidate), with C and D updated via PC/NC intersections to maintain balance consistency. Nodes are processed in degeneracy order. Worst-case complexity O(nδD·2^{δD}). Optimization (MBQCEnum*): four pruning layers applied iteratively. (1) Degree-based Type I pruning removes candidates from C that cannot satisfy degree constraints in any valid super-MBQC (Theorems 4.2, 4.6); Type II pruning terminates branches where nodes in X already fail degree requirements (Theorems 4.3, 4.7). (2) Bound-based pruning computes upper bounds UXL, UXR (max additional nodes that can join each partition, derived from minimum positive/negative degrees of current X nodes over C) and lower bounds LXL, LXR (min required to satisfy degree constraints); prunes when L > U (Theorems 4.4, 4.5). (3) Critical node pruning: if a node u ∈ XL is critical — its degree constraint is satisfied exactly only if all remaining eligible neighbors are added — those neighbors are force-included in X, collapsing the corresponding search subtree (Theorem 4.9). (4) Size threshold-based pre-pruning reduces the graph to a signed core before enumeration; a look-ahead check (Algorithm 5) tests whether G[XL∪CL, XR∪CR] already forms a valid BQC and stops early expansion if so. Post-processing: filter non-maximal BQCs using a polynomial-time set-containment query. Key Results Six real-world datasets span three orders of magnitude: SignedPPI (3.3K nodes, 20K edges), Referendum (10.8K, 251K), Epinions (131.5K, 711K), DBLP (317.1K, 1.05M), Youtube (1.13M, 2.98M), Pokec (1.63M, 22.3M). DBLP, Youtube, and Pokec are SNAP unsigned graphs with 30% of edges artificially relabeled as negative. Parameters γ1, γ2 range from 0.6–0.9 and θ from 4–15 depending on dataset. The baseline (independent maximal QC mining on G+ then quasi-biclique extension on G−) fails to complete on Referendum, Epinions, DBLP, Youtube, and Pokec at most parameter settings within the 24-hour time limit; MBQCEnum and MBQCEnum complete all cases. MBQCEnum is consistently 1–2 orders of magnitude faster than MBQCEnum, with up to 10× speedup (Exp-3 scalability). Ablation (Tables 3–4, DBLP default settings): degree-only baseline 409.52s; +look-ahead 405.53s (1.01×); +upper-bound 148.54s (2.76×); +lower-bound 40.87s (10.02×); +critical 38.78s (10.56×). Removing lower-bound pruning alone causes 4.71× slowdown on DBLP and 3.72× on Youtube — the single most impactful optimization. The baseline, even when it completes, cannot guarantee correctness due to BQC's non-hereditary property. Protein complex case study (Exp-7): MBQC outperforms maximal quasi-clique, balanced clique, and clique in average precision of top-20 complexes on SignedPPI, with MBQC precision peaking around γ = 0.8 (Fig. 15); exact precision values are readable from figures but not tabulated in text. Results are averages of 10 runs; no error bars reported. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM + NEW FORMULATION + NEW METHOD. The MBQC model is genuinely the first quasi-clique formulation for signed graphs integrating structural balance theory — not a straightforward combination of existing methods, as the non-hereditary structure of BQC invalidates all prior unsigned QC pruning techniques. The NP-hardness proof (via reduction from maximal balanced clique enumeration) is a new formal result. The positive/negative candidate set construction via signed path traversal and the four-layer pruning strategy tailored to the two-partition balance constraint are each non-obvious algorithmic contributions specific to this setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Algorithm correctness is formally proved: any MBQC in G is found exactly once (proved by degeneracy-order exclusion argument). All pruning theorems have formal proofs in Appendix A with full derivations. Ablation is conducted in two complementary ways (incremental addition, all-but-one), cleanly isolating each pruning layer. The NP-hardness argument is a clean polynomial reduction. No error bars or significance tests accompany the 10-run averaged runtimes. No code repository is mentioned in the paper. The three artificially signed datasets (DBLP, Youtube, Pokec) introduce a methodological concern: random negative edge assignment does not respect structural balance, potentially making balance-guided candidate set computation either artificially easier (random negatives create few balanced subgraphs) or artificially harder (random negatives violate path-based candidate validity assumptions). This is unacknowledged. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. All experiments run on a single 3.6GHz Intel Core machine with 128GB RAM. - Practical: Yes — the largest tested graph (Pokec, 1.63M nodes) completes within the 24-hour limit on commodity server hardware with no specialized infrastructure. - Efficient: MBQCEnum* completes DBLP (317K nodes) in 38.78s and Youtube (1.13M nodes) in 350.48s at default settings; memory consumption is not reported, which is a gap given that PC/NC precomputation requires O(nδD) space that may be substantial for dense graphs. - Effective: MBQC demonstrably outperforms clique, quasi-clique, and balanced clique models in protein complex precision on a natural signed dataset; the polarized community detection use case is motivated qualitatively but not evaluated quantitatively. - Scalable: Tested on subsamples from 20% to 100% of Youtube and Pokec (up to 1.63M nodes, ~22M edges); runtime grows log-linearly with both node count and edge count in Fig. 12, with acknowledged degradation on very dense graphs where degree-based pruning loses effectiveness. The paper delivers a Python implementation used in experiments but does not release code publicly. Strengths - The MBQC model cleanly unifies two well-studied frameworks (quasi-cliques and structural balance) into a principled definition that handles both the density relaxation and the sign semantics of real-world signed networks — the formulation is elegant and the two-parameter design is well-motivated. - The derivation of positive/negative candidate sets from signed path traversal is a technically non-trivial adaptation of balance theory to restrict the search space; it directly confronts the non-hereditary challenge unique to BQC and enables all subsequent pruning to be meaningful. - The bound-based pruning rules (upper and lower bounds on partition growth) are formally derived with full proofs and shown in ablation to account for the single largest efficiency gain — this is not just heuristic pruning but provably correct early termination. - Scalability to million-node networks with no prior comparable method available is a concrete empirical advance for practitioners working on large signed graphs. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The worst-case time complexity of MBQCEnum* remains O(nδD·2^{δD}), identical to the unpruned base algorithm — the optimizations provide empirical but not worst-case improvement. Very dense networks may incur higher cost because more candidate substructures are generated and degree-based pruning becomes less effective (acknowledged in Section 5.2). Parallelization is explicitly left as future work. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Three of six datasets are artificially signed by randomly relabeling 30% of edges as negative, a common approximation in signed graph literature but one that structurally violates the balance assumptions the algorithm exploits; the authors do not discuss whether MBQC outputs on these datasets are semantically meaningful or how random signing affects the algorithm's signed-path candidate pruning. - Memory usage is entirely absent from the evaluation; for Pokec-scale graphs, the O(nδD) candidate set precomputation could require tens of gigabytes, and the 128GB server may be necessary rather than sufficient — this is a hidden deployability constraint. - The protein complex case study reports only precision of top-20 complexes, omitting recall and F1; it is unknown whether MBQC's higher precision is offset by missing a significant fraction of true complexes. - No experimental comparison is made against the (α,k)-clique, (ε,φ)-clique, or antagonistic k-plex models discussed in related work, leaving the MBQC model's relative merits against alternative signed cohesive subgraph formulations entirely unquantified. - Parameter selection guidance is absent: Exp-5 shows MBQC count is non-monotone in γ1 (higher γ1 can increase output on DBLP by splitting larger patterns), leaving practitioners without principled guidance for tuning the three parameters in novel applications. Bottom Line This is a technically rigorous, well-motivated paper that fills a genuine formulation gap and should be read by anyone building cohesive subgraph mining tools for signed networks, though the reliance on artificially signed benchmarks for the scalability demonstration and the missing memory analysis leave non-trivial open questions about real-world applicability.
Citations
#92 — Innovative (67)

FORGE:Fine-grained Multimodal Evaluation for Manufacturing Scenarios (82 ▲ )

Xiangru Jian, Hao Xu, Wei Pang, Xinjian Zhao, Chengyu Tao, Qixin Zhang, Xikun Zhang, Chao Zhang, Guanzhi Deng, Alex Xue, Juan Du, Tianshu Yu, Garth Tarr, Linqi Song, Qiuzhuang Sun, Dacheng Tao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (70) reflects a genuinely new multimodal manufacturing benchmark plus a non-obvious empirical finding that domain knowledge, not visual grounding, is the MLLM bottleneck. Non-Obviousness (70) is driven by the structured bottleneck decomposition isolating grounding vs. knowledge with dedicated probes. Practical Impact (70) benefits from released code and dataset on HuggingFace/GitHub. Rigor (65) is pulled down by single-run results with no error bars and the SurfInsp synthetic-defect Goodhart risk, partially offset by the credible scenario-disjoint SFT protocol. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) are solid but limited by the unvalidated synthetic defects and narrow fastener domain. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*65+0.15*70+0.15*65+0.15*60+0.10*70 ≈ 67.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FORGE, a multimodal benchmark of ~12,972 real-world samples combining 2D images and 3D point clouds across three manufacturing tasks (workpiece verification, structural surface inspection, and assembly verification), used to evaluate 18 state-of-the-art MLLMs and demonstrate that FORGE annotations can train compact models to approach frontier performance. - Why it's important: Manufacturing automation increasingly needs MLLMs that can reason about fine-grained domain semantics (e.g., distinguishing nut M16 from M20), but no prior benchmark combines multimodal real-world data, model-number-level granularity, and systematic evaluation of this reasoning gap. - How it does it: The benchmark renders 3D point clouds as three-view (front/side/top) orthogonal projections so general-purpose MLLMs can process 3D geometry without native point-cloud encoders, then evaluates models under zero-shot, reference-conditioned, and in-context-demonstration settings. - The good: A structured bottleneck analysis — separating visual grounding ability from domain knowledge using isolated probing tasks — empirically proves that near-ceiling grounding accuracy (Gemini-3-Flash: 98.9% single-image, 84.3% cross-image) coexists with far-below-ceiling task accuracy, pinning the failure on domain knowledge rather than perception. - The missing: SurfInsp defect data is synthetically generated via morphology-based simulation rather than captured from actual factory defects, raising questions about whether benchmark conclusions transfer to real inspection lines. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or evaluating MLLMs for industrial applications — the bottleneck decomposition methodology is directly reusable for diagnosing perception-vs-reasoning failure modes in other domains. Problem & Motivation: Manufacturing systems increasingly rely on automated reasoning over heterogeneous sensor data — images, 3D scans, assembly specifications — to sort materials, detect defects, and verify assemblies. Traditional computer vision models handle perception (anomaly localization, pose estimation) but cannot execute the higher-level reasoning needed for autonomous decision-making in mixed-model production lines. MLLMs offer a path to bridge this gap, but existing evaluations either use simulated/CAD data, assess only coarse workpiece-type recognition (not model-number-level discrimination), or lack multimodal (image + point cloud) coverage. There is no benchmark that imposes the fine-grained domain-semantic demands of real factory environments on general-purpose MLLMs. Key Idea: The central insight is that 3D point clouds can be projected to three orthogonal 2D views (front, side, top) and fed to MLLMs as standard image inputs, sidestepping the lack of native 3D encoders in general-purpose models. Paired with tasks that require distinguishing model numbers (e.g., nuts M10–M20) rather than just workpiece categories, FORGE creates a systematic separation between perceptual capability and manufacturing domain knowledge — allowing causal attribution of model failures. Methodology: FORGE is constructed in three stages. (1) Data collection: 14 workpiece categories spanning 90 distinct model numbers are scanned with a 3D scanner (0.02mm precision), yielding 585 raw point cloud samples; separately, ~3,000 images are captured with a 50-megapixel camera. For WorkVeri and AssyVeri, batch samples are synthesized by stitching 4–5 individual point clouds via CloudCompare with random orientations; for SurfInsp, four defect types (Crack, Deformation, Dent, Cut) are simulated via morphology-based algorithms and non-rigid deformation, with 20 rotational augmentations per sample, producing ~30,000 total samples. (2) Task design: three MCQ tasks — WorkVeri (identify out-of-spec workpiece in a batch), SurfInsp (classify surface defect type), AssyVeri (identify assembly-rule violations) — with 3, 14, and 4 scenarios respectively. All tasks use exact-match accuracy against a random-guess weighted baseline. (3) Evaluation protocol: three settings — Zero-Shot (test image only), Reference-Conditioned (add images of correct assemblies/surfaces), and In-Context Demonstration (add solved examples as multi-turn dialogue). Eighteen MLLMs spanning open (Gemma-3-27B, InternVL3-78B, Llama-4-Maverick, Kimi-K2.5, Qwen3-VL-235B, GLM-4.6V, and others) and closed (GPT-5, GPT-5.2, O3, Gemini-3-Flash, Claude-4.5-Opus, Seed-1.6) families are evaluated. A bottleneck analysis isolates visual grounding (letter-coordinate mapping on labeled part images) from domain knowledge (missing-part identification given explicit assembly specs). Key Results: On WorkVeri (3V zero-shot), Kimi-K2.5 achieves 78.26% and Gemini-3-Flash achieves 69.56% against a 25% random baseline; most open-source models hover near chance. On AssyVeri (ICD, image), Kimi-K2.5 reaches 80.82%, with Gemini-3-Flash around 73.72%. SurfInsp is the hardest task: the best ICD result is ~47.12% (3V), barely above the 20% random baseline, with most models near or at chance on Deformation and Dent subtypes. Model-number-level tasks are consistently harder than workpiece-level tasks across all 18 models. Ref-Cond and ICD settings improve image-modality performance on WorkVeri and AssyVeri but degrade three-view performance — adding reference images introduces spatial confusion in 3V context. The bottleneck analysis shows Gemini-3-Flash achieves 98.9% on single-image grounding and top-4 models achieve 74.9–90.7% on missing-part detection when specs are explicit, confirming domain knowledge is the binding constraint. SFT of Qwen2.5-VL-3B on FORGE training splits yields 90.8% relative improvement on WorkVeri (held-out NUTS scenario, from 28.2% to 53.8% zero-shot) and 27.1% on AssyVeri (held-out CNC scenario), outperforming all open-source reference models. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The benchmark itself (real-world multimodal manufacturing data at model-number granularity) is genuinely novel relative to prior work. The empirical finding — that visual grounding is not the bottleneck for MLLM failures in manufacturing; domain knowledge is — is a non-obvious, methodologically grounded result that contradicts the common assumption that perception is the limiting factor. Rigor & Reproducibility: The bottleneck analysis is a methodological strength: it uses dedicated probing tasks (grounding-only, domain-knowledge-only) to causally separate failure modes rather than relying on aggregate accuracy. Ablations across zero-shot/ref-cond/ICD settings reveal setting-specific behavior. No error bars or significance tests are reported; all results are single-run. Code is released at https://github.com/AI4Manufacturing/FORGE, the dataset at https://huggingface.co/datasets/AI4Manufacturing/forge, and a project page at https://ai4manufacturing.github.io/forge-web/. A concern: SurfInsp ground-truth defects are synthetically simulated — the benchmark is developed on and evaluated against this synthetic defect set, raising a Goodhart's Law risk where SURFINSP scores may not predict real inspection performance. The SFT evaluation uses scenario-disjoint splits (train on MES/CHS, evaluate on CNC/NUTS), which is a credible out-of-distribution protocol. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (dataset + evaluation system, with fine-tuning experiments). - Practical: Evaluation runs on commercially available cloud APIs (GPT-5, Gemini, Claude) and open-weight models deployable on standard multi-GPU servers; the 3B SFT model runs on modest hardware. - Efficient: FORGE inference uses standard MLLM APIs with no specialized 3D infrastructure; the multi-view projection strategy adds preprocessing cost but no model modification. - Effective: The benchmark reveals concrete, reproducible capability gaps — best models stay below 80% on WorkVeri and below 50% on SurfInsp — and the SFT experiment closes part of the gap on held-out scenarios, but absolute task accuracy remains low for deployment. - Scalable: The dataset covers 14 workpiece categories and 90 model numbers; industrial deployments span thousands of SKUs, and the benchmark does not test or claim generalization at that scale. The work delivers a publicly released dataset and benchmark (software artifact + data), not a trained production model. Strengths: - The three-pronged bottleneck decomposition (visual grounding, domain knowledge, 3D text serialization) is methodologically rigorous and produces clear, actionable conclusions rather than aggregate leaderboard numbers. - Inclusion of both 2D image and 3D point cloud modalities with explicit model-number-level labels fills a genuine gap versus all five prior benchmarks surveyed (MMAD, MME-Industry, DesignQA, FailureSensorIQ, EngDesign). - The out-of-distribution SFT evaluation protocol (training on one scenario, testing on a held-out scenario) provides credible evidence that FORGE annotations encode transferable domain knowledge, not just task-specific patterns. - The counter-intuitive finding that Ref-Cond and ICD hurt 3V performance (spatial confusion from multi-image context) is a concrete, reproducible insight useful for prompt engineering practitioners. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that SurfInsp defects are synthetically generated and explicitly flag this as a limitation (Section 3.1). They also acknowledge that general-purpose MLLMs lack native 3D encoders, motivating the multi-view projection workaround, but do not claim this fully substitutes for native 3D reasoning. The SFT results are described as "preliminary evidence" rather than a definitive solution. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) SurfInsp synthetic defects are a significant concern: the morphology-based simulation may not capture the statistical distribution of real factory defects (e.g., machining burrs, weld spatter, corrosion), so the near-random SURFINSP scores may reflect domain shift from simulation rather than a fundamental MLLM limitation — the paper does not include a real-defect validation. (2) The dataset covers only metallic fasteners and screws; generalization to other manufacturing domains (PCBs, textiles, composites) is not evaluated or discussed. (3) The 3V projection strategy is validated as better than text serialization but not compared against specialized 3D-language models (e.g., PointLLM) — the claim that general MLLMs are the right architecture for this domain is assumed, not tested. (4) SFT results use Qwen2.5-VL-3B only; whether the findings scale to larger open-source models is untested. Bottom Line: Practitioners building MLLMs for manufacturing quality control or material handling will find FORGE's diagnostic methodology and the domain-knowledge-as-bottleneck finding directly actionable, making this a worthwhile read despite the synthetic-defect limitation in SurfInsp.
Recommended 2604.07413 PDF
#93 — Innovative (67)

The Past Is Not Past: Memory-Enhanced Dynamic Reward Shaping (21 ▲ )

Yang Liu, Enxi Wang, Yufei Gao, Weixin Zhang, Bo Wang, Zhiyuan Zeng, Yikai Zhang, Yining Zheng, Xipeng Qiu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) is the highest dimension — reusing layer-wise logits as behavioral fingerprints is a genuinely clever, non-obvious mechanism. Novelty (70) reflects the new integration and Theorem 1's formal guarantee. Rigor (65) limited by single-seed evaluation on small benchmarks (AIME24 has 24 problems), no confidence intervals, different hyperparameters across model scales without ablation, and relevant baselines (EEPO, DSDR) omitted. Resilience (60) — the headline AIME24 pass@1 gains are high-variance and unconfirmed statistically. Completeness (65): narrow limitations section that ignores single-domain scope. Impact (65): open code, low overhead, but math-only evaluation. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*65+0.15*75+0.15*65+0.15*60+0.10*65 ≈ 67.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MEDS, a memory-enhanced dynamic reward shaping framework that penalizes LLM rollouts assigned to frequently-recurring error clusters identified by HDBSCAN clustering of per-prompt layer-wise logit histories across training. - Why it's important: On-policy RL for LLMs routinely collapses into repetitive wrong reasoning trajectories that waste training signal and entrench errors; standard entropy regularization operates only on the current batch distribution and cannot suppress cross-rollout error recurrence. - How it does it: Layer-wise logits at the final answer token from the latter half of transformer layers are concatenated into a behavioral fingerprint, stored per-prompt across all rollouts, clustered via HDBSCAN, and rollouts in larger clusters receive a log-scaled penalty subtracted from the task reward — all reusing already-computed forward-pass tensors. - The good: Consistent pass@1 and pass@128 gains across all 15 model×benchmark combinations (3 models × 5 benchmarks), with the largest single gain being AIME24 pass@128 for Qwen3-8B increasing from 70.81 to 82.67 (~17% relative improvement). - The missing: Evaluation is restricted entirely to mathematical reasoning; no experiments on code generation, instruction following, or any non-math RLVR domain, leaving transferability of the method unestablished. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on exploration and diversity in LLM RL training, as it offers a computationally cheap, theoretically grounded mechanism to combat error collapse backed by consistent multi-scale empirical evidence. Problem & Motivation During on-policy RL training for LLMs (RLVR), as optimization progresses the policy concentrates probability mass on a narrow set of reasoning paths. When those paths contain structural errors, repeated sampling reinforces the same mistake because the reward signal evaluates only final correctness, not whether the specific failure mode has appeared before. Entropy regularization randomizes token choices under the current distribution but does not track whether the same logical error has appeared across prior training steps. The result is sample-inefficient training: many rollouts fall into the same erroneous reasoning cluster, providing redundant gradient signal. Prior work addresses batch-level diversity (DAPO asymmetric clipping, entropy advantage) or uses coarse historical outcome frequencies (UCB-style) but lacks fine-grained cross-step reasoning trajectory tracking without expensive external models or auxiliary networks. Key Idea The core insight is that the layer-wise logits of a transformer at the final answer token position constitute a lightweight fingerprint of the model's internal reasoning state: responses sharing the same structural error pattern activate similar computational circuits and produce similar logit vectors across the latter transformer layers. Clustering these logit vectors across the entire per-prompt rollout history via HDBSCAN identifies recurrent failure modes without any additional model, and penalizing rollouts in large clusters directly discourages reproduction of the same failure trajectory. Methodology MEDS adds a clustering-based penalty term to any RLVR training objective (demonstrated on GRPO/DAPO). The pipeline has three stages. (1) Logic Feature Extraction: For a sampled response ȳ, extract the scalar logit l^(n)_{y} at the first token of the final answer y across layers n = N/2, …, N, and concatenate to produce f(ȳ) ∈ ℝ^{N/2}. This reuses already-computed forward-pass activations; the design choice of using only the last N/2 layers is empirically validated — last-14-layer direct aggregation achieves 61.2% LLM-annotation correlation vs. 45.2% for a degenerate single-cluster baseline. (2) Memory-based Clustering: Maintain per-prompt error memory G_x accumulating all historical rollout feature vectors. Apply HDBSCAN (min cluster size 2, min samples 1, Euclidean distance) to G_x each training step, producing K density-based clusters plus a noise cluster; K is determined dynamically. The min-cluster-size-2 setting ensures genuine repetition before any penalty is assessed. (3) Cluster-based Reward Shaping: For response ȳ in cluster C_k, compute penalty = min(α·log(|C_k|+1), β), giving a penalty proportional to log cluster size capped at β. The shaped reward is r̃(x,ȳ) = r(x,ȳ) − penalty. Hyperparameters α=0.1, β=0.2 for 1.7B/7B models; α=0.02, β=0.04 for 8B. The theoretical justification (Theorem 1, proved via Chebyshev's rearrangement inequality in Appendix B) shows that adding a repetition penalty λ·c(y) yields an updated policy q₂ satisfying J(q₂) ≥ J(q₁) under KL-regularized optimization, providing a formal guarantee that the shaped reward is non-decreasing in expected task return. Training uses 512 prompt-level batch size, 16 rollouts per prompt, on H200 hardware; wall-clock overhead vs. DAPO is ~9% (9.73 vs. 8.95 min per 100 steps on 7B). Key Results Table 1 reports pass@1 and pass@128 across five benchmarks (AIME24, AMC23, MATH500, Minerva, OlympiadBench) for three base models. MEDS achieves the top average in all three model rows. Representative results: Qwen3-1.7B average pass@1 52.49 vs. DAPO 46.0 (+6.49); Qwen3-8B average pass@128 84.61 vs. DAPO 80.24 (+4.37). Largest absolute gain: AIME24 pass@128 for Qwen3-8B, 82.67 vs. DAPO 70.81 (+11.86 absolute, ~17% relative). The pass@128 gains are consistently larger than pass@1 gains, consistent with the claim that MEDS improves sampling diversity. The Top-1 Eigen Ratio (fraction of logit covariance explained by the dominant principal component) is lower for MEDS than DAPO at all late training steps, indicating more distributed exploration in representation space. LLM-judge (Claude-Haiku-4.5) Within-Step and Across-Step diversity scores also consistently favor MEDS. Baselines (GRPO, DAPO, GRPO+Entropy Advantage) are current and representative of the RLVR field. Testing scale: 1.7B–8B; no experiments above 8B. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The combination of (a) reusing layer-wise logits as behavioral fingerprints, (b) accumulating cross-rollout per-prompt error memory, and (c) HDBSCAN cluster-size penalty for reward shaping is genuinely novel. Individual components (logit analysis for interpretability, HDBSCAN, reward shaping) are known; their integration for history-aware error diversity in LLM RL training is new. Theorem 1 adds a formal guarantee not present in prior diversity reward shaping methods. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations over five logit aggregation strategies (Table 2) directly link clustering quality (LLM-annotation correlation) to downstream performance, identifying last-14-layer direct aggregation as optimal. Cross-model validation across three architectures/scales reduces cherry-picking risk. Critical concern: evaluation uses a single fixed seed (seed=0) with no confidence intervals or significance tests. AIME24 contains only 24 problems, so pass@1 variance is ~±4 points per problem; the 10.19-point gain for Qwen3-1.7B is non-trivial but unconfirmed statistically. No sensitivity analysis of the penalty hyperparameters α and β is reported, and these differ across model scales without ablation of the difference. The LLM-annotation correlation study uses Claude-Haiku-4.5 as ground truth, introducing a proprietary model dependency into the evaluation chain. Code is released at github.com/Linxi000/MEDS. This is a preprint; not peer-reviewed. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Training runs on H200 GPUs at 1.7B–8B scale; the ~9% wall-clock overhead over DAPO is commercially feasible, and logit reuse requires no additional forward passes. - Efficient: Per-prompt memory stores b×t feature vectors of size N/2 (≈1,600 vectors at 16 rollouts × 100 steps); this is manageable in CPU RAM. HDBSCAN clustering cost at this scale is not profiled but is unlikely to bottleneck GPU-bound training. - Effective: Evidence is positive across all 15 model×benchmark combinations for both pass@1 and pass@128, and the diversity metrics (Eigen Ratio, LLM-judge scores) provide mechanistic corroboration beyond just accuracy numbers. - Scalable: Tested only up to 8B parameters. The authors suggest benefits scale with base model capability based on a three-point trend (1.7B < 7B ≈ 8B), but no formal scaling experiment is conducted; at 70B+, per-prompt HDBSCAN over growing rollout histories and the memory footprint of N/2-dimensional logit vectors (where N can be 80+ layers) may become non-trivial. The paper does not analyze this. The work delivers open-source code but no trained model weights. Strengths - The logit-reuse mechanism is computationally clever: it extracts meaningful behavioral fingerprints from existing forward-pass outputs at near-zero overhead, unlike methods requiring external models for diversity evaluation. - Theorem 1 with a complete proof (Appendix B, using Chebyshev's rearrangement inequality) provides principled theoretical grounding for what would otherwise be a heuristic penalty. - Table 2 directly connects clustering quality (measured by LLM-annotation correlation) to downstream performance, making a causal argument rather than just reporting empirical improvements. - Consistent improvements across all 15 model×benchmark evaluation cells reduce the risk of domain- or scale-specific overfitting. Author-Acknowledged Limitations Section 6 acknowledges that the logit aggregation strategies explored are simple and that more sophisticated aggregation functions might improve performance further. This is a narrow, specific acknowledgment. The authors do not discuss the single-domain restriction, the lack of error bars, scale limitations above 8B, or the hyperparameter tuning dependency across model sizes. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Evaluation is entirely on mathematical reasoning; it is unknown whether the method transfers to code generation, instruction following, or multimodal RLVR where error patterns may not cluster as cleanly in logit space. - Single-seed evaluation on AIME24 (24 problems) makes pass@1 estimates high-variance; the 10.19-point gain for Qwen3-1.7B is visually large but lacks statistical confirmation. - The claim that "benefits scale with base model capability" rests on three model sizes without formal scaling analysis; no experiments above 8B are reported. - The 61.2% logit-to-LLM-annotation correlation implies ~39% of clustering decisions diverge from semantic ground truth; the paper does not analyze whether this noise floor limits achievable improvement. - Per-model hyperparameter tuning (different α/β for 8B vs. others) is not ablated; it is unclear whether the 8B gains stem from the MEDS mechanism or from better-tuned penalty coefficients. - Cited anti-collapse methods EEPO and DSDR are not included as baselines despite being directly relevant; only GRPO, DAPO, and GRPO+Entropy Advantage are compared. Bottom Line A practitioner training math-reasoning LLMs in the 1B–10B range will find MEDS a low-overhead, consistently beneficial augmentation to existing RLVR pipelines, though the single-domain, single-seed, sub-10B evaluation scope means extrapolation to larger models or different task types requires independent verification.
Recommended 2604.11297 PDF
#94 — Innovative (66)

VisionFoundry: Teaching VLMs Visual Perception with Synthetic Images (6 ▲ )

Guanyu Zhou, Yida Yin, Wenhao Chai, Shengbang Tong, Xingyu Fu, Zhuang Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (60) — end-to-end LLM→T2I→VLM-verifier pipeline with 'visual determinism' framing is a useful systems composition, but ProVision, LOVA3, SpatialVLM are direct precedents. Rigor (65) — meaningful ablations (verification necessity, strict-control synthetic-vs-natural, data-size, task transfer), 4-run averages, verifier audit with Cohen's κ, but no error bars in main tables, only 140 audit samples, and Goodhart risk since task selection was motivated by MMVP/CV-Bench failures that are then used as the primary evaluation. Non-Obviousness (62), Completeness (70), Resilience (62) — proprietary GPT-5.2/Gemini-3-Pro dependency is a real reproducibility barrier not confronted. Impact (72) — dataset and pipeline released, consistent cross-architecture gains. 0.20·60+0.25·65+0.15·62+0.15·70+0.15·62+0.10·72 = 64.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 70 Resilience 62 Impact 72
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces VisionFoundry, a fully automated three-stage pipeline that generates task-targeted synthetic VQA training data from a single task keyword using an LLM (GPT-5.2), a T2I model (Gemini-2.5-Flash-Image), and a VLM verifier (Gemini-3-Pro), and uses it to construct VisionFoundry-10K, a 10k-sample synthetic dataset across 10 visual perception tasks. - Why it's important: Vision-language models have persistent, well-documented weaknesses in low-level visual perception (spatial reasoning, depth ordering, viewpoint recognition) that natural image-text corpora fail to address systematically, limiting downstream reliability in spatially-grounded applications. - How it does it: The "visual determinism" principle encodes the answer-determining fact directly into the T2I generation prompt so that the correct answer is visually unambiguous in the synthesized image, followed by VLM-based binary alignment verification to filter misaligned samples before training. - The good: Consistent +7% on MMVP and +10% on CV-Bench-3D across three diverse VLM architectures (3B–11B parameters), with monotonically increasing gains as data volume scales from 500 to 10k samples. - The missing: The pipeline depends entirely on proprietary frontier models (GPT-5.2, Gemini-3-Pro) whose outputs are not fully reproducible, and the verifier quality audit covers only 140 samples from a single production trajectory — too thin to establish pipeline reliability at scale. - Read it or not: Worth reading for VLM researchers working on targeted post-training or synthetic data curation pipelines; less relevant for those focused on reasoning-heavy or language-grounded multimodal tasks. Problem & Motivation: Vision-language models systematically underperform on low-level visual perception tasks — orientation and direction, depth ordering, viewpoint recognition, spatial relationships, relative distance — as quantified by diagnostic benchmarks such as MMVP, CV-Bench, and RealWorldQA. The root cause is that large natural image-text corpora do not cover the combinatorial range of visual configurations required for robust perception: web-scraped images are not controlled for viewpoint, object arrangement, or attribute combinations relevant to these skills. Expensive human annotation or curated retrieval pipelines are the traditional remedy, but both are slow and domain-limited. The gap to close is: how to generate abundant, task-targeted visual supervision without reference images or human labelers. Key Idea: Visual determinism — embedding the answer-determining visual fact explicitly into the T2I generation prompt at synthesis time, so that the resulting image is semantically forced to contain the evidence needed to answer the question. This breaks the common failure mode in synthetic multimodal data where generated images are visually underspecified or inconsistent with the stated QA pair. Combined with a downstream VLM verifier that checks alignment before any sample enters training, this creates a closed-loop quality control mechanism that requires no human in the loop. Methodology: VisionFoundry operates in three sequential stages. Stage 1 (VQA triplet generation): given a task keyword (e.g., "Depth Order"), an adaptive concept pool is constructed by sampling object, attribute, scene, style, and task-specific entities from a Cartesian product of predefined dimensions. GPT-5.2 receives a task configuration and the sampled entity, and jointly generates a question, a deterministic short answer, and a detailed T2I prompt that explicitly encodes the answer-determining visual fact. The LLM is constrained to produce only visually verifiable properties and ambiguity-free answers. Stage 2 (image synthesis): Gemini-2.5-Flash-Image synthesizes an image conditioned on the T2I prompt. If the first generation fails verification, a minimal edit instruction is issued as a fallback; if that also fails, the entire sample is discarded and a new entity is sampled. The T2I model is treated as a black box. Stage 3 (alignment verification): the QA pair is converted to a declarative visual statement (e.g., "The red cube is to the left of the blue sphere") by a lightweight LLM call; Gemini-3-Pro then performs binary (YES/NO) image-statement alignment judgment. Only confirmed samples are retained. No human correction loop exists — false accepts remain in the final dataset. Critical design decisions: (1) visual determinism justified by eliminating language-prior shortcuts; (2) binary filtering with intentional automation bias justified by the goal of studying a fully automated pipeline rather than a curated one; (3) entity pool Cartesian sampling justified by systematic combinatorial coverage. Key Results: On Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct trained for one epoch on VisionFoundry-10K (10k samples), visual perception benchmark gains are: MMVP_pair 35.3→42.0 (+6.7pp), MMVP_single 64.3→68.3 (+4.0pp), CV-Bench-2D 67.3→72.4 (+5.1pp), CV-Bench-3D 66.0→76.5 (+10.5pp), RealWorldQA 65.0→66.9 (+1.9pp). Results replicate across Llama-3.2-11B-Vision-Instruct and MiMo-VL-7B-SFT (Table 1, 13 benchmarks, 15 metrics). General-purpose benchmarks are heterogeneous: some improve (MMBench, MathVista), others show small regressions (BLINK 48.1→47.9 for Qwen, OCRBench slight drop consistent with zero OCR supervision in the dataset). MiMo shows an anomalous MMBench jump (50.5→81.6) that the authors flag as unusual and attribute speculatively to visual perception compensating for weak non-thinking-mode logical ability — this result warrants independent scrutiny. Data-size scaling is monotonically increasing on visual perception benchmarks from 500 to 10k samples (Figure 4). A 2k synthetic + 2k natural mixture outperforms 4k natural-only on all visual perception benchmarks. Verifier quality: 92.1% accuracy, 99.0% precision, 90.8% recall, F1=94.7%, Cohen's κ=0.794 on an audited trajectory of 140 samples. The strict-control ablation (Appendix G) isolates the image-source effect: caption-conditioned synthetic images outperform natural images with identical synthetic QA by +0.2 to +5.3pp across five benchmarks, with the largest gap on CV-Bench-3D (+2.7pp under strict control). Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The closed-loop pipeline composing LLM → T2I → VLM verifier from a single task keyword with no reference images or human annotation is a meaningful systems contribution; the "visual determinism" framing is a clean, reusable design principle. Individual components (synthetic T2I data for VLMs, LLM-generated QA, VLM-as-judge filtering) have direct precedents (ProVision, LOVA3, ALLaVA, SpatialVLM), so the contribution lies primarily in the end-to-end automation and the task-targeted targeting strategy rather than any single algorithmic novelty. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and meaningful: verification necessity (Appendix F, matched 1k budget), synthetic vs. natural image source (Appendix G, two controlled variants including strict-control), data-size sensitivity (Figure 4, five subset sizes), epoch sensitivity (Figure 6), task-wise transfer (Figure 7). Results are averaged over four independent runs. Error bars are not shown in the main tables — only mean scores. The verifier quality audit covers 140 samples from a single production trajectory; Cohen's κ = 0.794 is indicative but the authors correctly note it should not be universalized. Code, dataset, and a project page are listed in the paper header as clickable links (URLs not extractable from this PDF rendering). A reproducibility concern: the pipeline uses GPT-5.2 and Gemini-3-Pro — both are proprietary, API-gated, and subject to version drift, making exact replication contingent on continued access to specific model versions. Tasks are explicitly selected based on known benchmark failure modes of VLMs, which creates a mild Goodhart's Law risk: the improvements on MMVP and CV-Bench are on the same benchmark family that motivated task selection, rather than on fully held-out distributions. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile): - Practical: Finetuning runs on standard GPU clusters (Princeton's Neuronic cluster); inference requires API access to GPT-5.2 and Gemini-3-Pro for pipeline operation, limiting plug-and-play use to organizations with frontier API access. - Efficient: Training is lightweight — one epoch on 10k samples with batch size 128; pipeline API costs are not disclosed, but generating 10k samples requires roughly 30k LLM calls (triplet generation + verification) at frontier-model rates. - Effective: Strong evidence for visual perception tasks; general-purpose benchmark effects are real but heterogeneous, with OCRBench showing consistent small regressions attributable to the absence of OCR-specific training signal. - Scalable: Tested from 500 to 10k samples — a 20x range — with monotonic improvement; no experiments beyond 10k samples and no tested model sizes beyond 11B parameters, so behavior at 100k samples or 70B+ models is claimed but unevaluated. The work delivers a dataset (VisionFoundry-10K), a described pipeline with published prompts (Appendix B), and code/data links, stopping short of a packaged library. Strengths: - The visual determinism principle is a crisp, transferable design insight: encoding the answer-determining fact in the T2I prompt at generation time is a clean solution to the ambiguity failure mode in synthetic multimodal data, and the strict-control ablation (Appendix G) provides credible evidence that synthetic images deliver additional value beyond synthetic QA alone. - The verifier audit with a confusion matrix and Cohen's κ is more transparent than most synthetic-data papers, which typically report only downstream task accuracy without examining pipeline internals. - Consistent gains across three architecturally diverse VLMs (3B, 7B, 11B, different training regimes) reduces the risk that results are idiosyncratic to one model family. - The controlled mix-vs.-natural experiment (Figure 5) cleanly demonstrates that verifier-filtered synthetic data provides complementary signal not easily replicated by adding more natural images. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that VisionFoundry-10K targets visual perception tasks and leave open whether the approach extends to complex visual reasoning requiring longer inference chains or compositional reasoning. They acknowledge heterogeneous effects on general-purpose benchmarks and explain OCRBench regression as expected given the absence of OCR training signal. They note that the verifier audit is post hoc only and does not introduce a human correction loop, meaning false accepts persist in the dataset. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The pipeline's dependence on GPT-5.2 and Gemini-3-Pro creates a practical reproducibility barrier not acknowledged in the paper — any replication requires the same API models, and version drift or access restrictions would invalidate comparisons. The verifier audit of 140 samples from one trajectory is insufficient to characterize pipeline reliability across tasks and entity distributions; error rates may vary substantially by task. The MiMo MMBench anomaly (50.5→81.6) is flagged but not explained or controlled for, and the speculative explanation (visual perception compensating for logical weakness in non-thinking mode) is not tested. No direct comparison against other synthetic VQA generation pipelines (ProVision, LOVA3) on the same target tasks is provided — the baselines are natural image training, not competing synthetic pipelines. The task selection based on existing benchmark failures risks circular evaluation: improvements measured on MMVP and CV-Bench are on benchmarks that motivated the task choices. Bottom Line: A practitioner building VLM post-training pipelines gains a concrete, well-ablated framework showing that 10k verifier-filtered synthetic samples provide meaningful, reproducible gains on visual perception without degrading general capabilities — but the proprietary API dependencies and narrow verifier audit mean adoption requires revalidation rather than direct replication.
Recommended 2604.09531 PDF
#95 — Innovative (66)

TRACE: An Experiential Framework for Coherent Multi-hop Knowledge Graph Question Answering

Yingxu Wang, Jiaxin Huang, Mengzhu Wang, Nan Yin · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) for the dual-memory (local narrative + cross-trajectory priors) architecture is genuine. Rigor (65) has clean component and backbone ablations and sensitivity sweeps on k, L, ζ, but no error bars, no code release, and a preprint status. Non-Obviousness (70) is supported by the non-additive ablation interaction. Completeness (70) covers most bases but no controlled GPT-4.1-backbone comparison against RwT. Resilience (60) is weakened by backbone conflation — the architectural-vs-model contribution is not cleanly separated. Impact (55) is tempered by the GPT-4.1 dependency and absent software artifact. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*65+0.15*70+0.15*70+0.15*60+0.10*55 ≈ 66.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TRACE, a framework for multi-hop Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA) that combines dynamic natural-language context generation, reusable exploration priors distilled from failed trajectories, and dual-feedback re-ranking to produce coherent, non-redundant reasoning paths. - Why it's important: Existing LLM+KG reasoning methods treat each hop in isolation, causing semantic drift and redundant graph exploration, which degrades accuracy on compositional multi-hop queries that require sustained goal alignment across 3–4 relation traversals. - How it does it: At each step, the accumulated relation sequence is translated into a natural-language narrative (Dynamic Context Generation) that conditions both candidate retrieval and a two-stage re-ranker that further consults abstracted failure patterns (exploration priors) extracted from terminated trajectories during the same inference run. - The good: TRACE achieves 91.6 Hits@1 / 81.7 F1 on WebQSP and 76.9 Hits@1 / 72.9 F1 on CWQ against a field of 20+ baselines, while simultaneously consuming fewer LLM tokens (8,782 vs. 10,680 for the closest competitor RwT on WebQSP) and fewer LLM calls (14.2 vs. 15.1). - The missing: The method relies on GPT-4.1 as its backbone for all four LLM roles (context generation, retrieval, re-ranking, summarization), making cost and latency comparisons against fine-tuned or lighter systems structurally unfair; no wall-clock latency figures are reported. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on LLM-guided graph traversal or experiential/memory-augmented reasoning agents; practitioners wanting a drop-in KGQA component should note the GPT-4.1 dependency and manually designed prompt engineering. Problem & Motivation: Multi-hop KGQA requires an LLM to navigate a knowledge graph across several relation hops to answer natural-language questions (e.g., "What film featured Kim Richards and was produced by Ann Marie Sanerlin?"). The core failure modes of existing LLM+KG systems are: (1) each hop is decided without awareness of the accumulated reasoning context, causing the path to drift from the question's intent; (2) failed exploration trajectories are discarded rather than reused, so the model repeatedly tries unproductive relation patterns; (3) no unified mechanism combines step-local semantic context with globally accumulated exploration experience. These failures produce incoherent paths and inflate inference cost through redundant graph branching. Key Idea: The central insight is that multi-hop KG reasoning should maintain two complementary memory streams simultaneously: a step-local narrative that translates the growing relation sequence into natural language (keeping the current decision grounded in question semantics), and a cross-trajectory experiential prior that compresses patterns from all previously terminated (failed or exhausted) paths into reusable guidance. The dual-feedback re-ranker is the unification point where both streams jointly score candidate relations at each hop. Methodology: TRACE is formulated as sequential decision-making over KG K = (e_s, r, e_o) ⊆ E × R × E. The pipeline runs up to I=30 beam iterations with maximum path length L=4 hops. Three modules operate per iteration: (1) Dynamic Context Generation — the relation sequence R_q^(t) = (r_1,...,r_t) is passed to an LLM (GPT-4.1) as f_ctx(q, R_q^(t)) → C_q^(t), a natural-language narrative such as "Find the director's birthplace." This narrative conditions step-(t+1) retrieval. (2) Exploration Generalization — when a trajectory terminates (max depth or no valid expansion), f_sum(·) generates a trajectory-level description D_q of why it failed; periodically, f_gen(·) aggregates accumulated D_q summaries into abstracted exploration priors F that flag relation families to avoid (e.g., "cast-appearance paths rarely reach producer-role targets"). (3) Dual-Feedback Re-ranking — an LLM retriever proposes top-k candidate relations conditioned on C_q^(t) (local stage); a scoring function f_rank(q, R_q^(t), r, F) re-ranks them using both the relation sequence and exploration priors F (global stage). Relations with s(r) ≥ ζ=0.5 are retained to branch the beam. Critical design decisions: k=3 (WebQSP) / k=4 (CWQ) balances breadth vs. cost — well-justified by sensitivity ablation; L=4 is chosen empirically as performance peaks at 4 hops and degrades at 5; ζ=0.5 is the precision-recall trade-off point, also validated by sensitivity analysis. The objective is implicitly argmax over relevance score at the answer step; no formal loss function is trained. Key Results: On WebQSP (1,639 test, sampled to 1,000 for evaluation following prior work): TRACE achieves 91.6 Hits@1 and 81.7 F1, surpassing the next-best LLM+KG baseline RwT (87.0 / 79.7) and DualR (87.5 / 81.4). On CWQ (3,531 test, sampled to 1,000): 76.9 Hits@1 and 72.9 F1, surpassing DP (75.8 / 69.4) and RwT (72.4 / 66.7). Baselines span semantic parsing (KV-Mem, DECAF, NSM), retrieval-based (GraftNet, SR+NSM), standalone LLMs (ChatGPT, Llama3-8B), and LLM+KG methods (ToG, RoG, FiDeLis, GNN-RAG, DoG, DualR, DP, RwT). Ablation removes Dynamic Context Generation (−2.7 Hits@1 on WebQSP) and Exploration Generalization (−3.0 Hits@1 on WebQSP) independently, with both removed producing the largest drop (−4.1). Backbone ablation: TRACE with Llama2-13B scores 88.1 / 78.3 on WebQSP, still beating strong baselines, confirming architectural rather than purely model-driven gains. Efficiency: TRACE uses 8,782 tokens / 14.2 calls per question on WebQSP vs. RwT's 10,680 / 15.1, and worst-case token use is only ~1.5× average, demonstrating bounded rather than exponential cost growth. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The combination of step-local LLM-generated narrative grounding with cross-trajectory experiential prior abstraction in a unified re-ranker for KGQA is novel. Individual components (LLM-guided KG traversal, beam search over relations, trajectory summarization) exist in prior work; the contribution is their specific integration and the two-memory-stream architecture. It is not a new problem, dataset, or theoretical result. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and isolate contributions of both modules. Sensitivity analysis covers all three key hyperparameters (k, L, ζ) across both datasets with plotted curves. No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2604.11193, April 2026); no peer review. Code and model weights are not released (no repository URL mentioned). Evaluation follows prior work convention of sampling 1,000 test questions from each benchmark rather than evaluating the full test set — this is standard practice in the field but limits direct comparison to works that use the full set. All four LLM roles use GPT-4.1 (a closed commercial model), making full reproduction dependent on API access and subject to model version drift. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile): - Practical: Runs via GPT-4.1 API calls; requires no specialized hardware, but incurs non-trivial API costs (14.2 calls/question average). - Efficient: Uses fewer tokens and calls than the closest competitors (RwT, DoG), with worst-case cost bounded at ~1.5–1.7× average; however, absolute cost in dollars and wall-clock latency per question are not reported. - Effective: Achieves the highest reported Hits@1 and F1 on both WebQSP and CWQ benchmarks against 20+ baselines on the same evaluation protocol, indicating the method solves the stated problem at benchmark scale. - Scalable: Tested on standard KGQA benchmarks (WebQSP ~1.6K, CWQ ~3.5K test instances) against Freebase subgraphs; behavior on much larger KGs (Wikidata scale, 10^9+ triples) or questions requiring >4 hops is not evaluated and only claimed plausible. The work delivers a paper-form system; no library, CLI, or trained model artifact is released. Strengths: - The dual-memory architecture (step-local narrative + cross-trajectory experiential priors) is a coherent and non-obvious design that addresses two distinct failure modes simultaneously rather than patching one. - Efficiency gains alongside accuracy gains are a genuine contribution: most prior work trades compute for accuracy; TRACE achieves both, with the exploration prior mechanism providing a causal explanation for the token reduction. - Backbone robustness is demonstrated: TRACE with Llama2-13B still outperforms strong GPT-based baselines (RwT, FiDeLis), indicating the architecture contributes independently of model scale. - Ablations are cleanly designed and the two components show complementary (non-redundant) contributions, with the joint removal producing a larger drop than the sum of individual removals. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state three limitations directly: (1) prompts and exploration priors are manually designed, limiting adaptability to heterogeneous or noisy KGs; (2) multiple LLM components introduce non-trivial inference costs; (3) TRACE is evaluated only on KGQA and not extended to scientific discovery or multi-agent settings. These are confronted honestly without minimization. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) All comparisons involving TRACE use GPT-4.1, while several baselines (RwT, DP, DualR) use different backbone LLMs; the performance gap conflates architectural contribution with backbone advantage — a GPT-4.1-backbone version of RwT is not provided as a controlled baseline. (2) Evaluation uses 1,000 sampled questions rather than full test sets, which is standard but means the reported improvement over RwT (4.6 Hits@1 on WebQSP) could narrow or widen on the full set. (3) The exploration priors F are populated only from trajectories within the same inference run for the same question — it is unclear how many failed trajectories typically accumulate before priors become informative, and whether the priors generalize across questions or are question-local. (4) No latency measurements; the 14.2 calls/question figure does not translate to user-facing response time without per-call latency data. (5) The confidence threshold ζ as a fixed hyperparameter assumes all KGs have similar relation density; its sensitivity to schema-sparse or schema-rich graphs is unexamined. Bottom Line: TRACE is a technically credible LLM+KG reasoning framework with the best reported numbers on WebQSP and CWQ, and its dual-memory design addresses a real structural gap in prior work — but the GPT-4.1 backbone dependency, lack of code release, and absence of a controlled backbone comparison leave open whether the gain is architectural or model-driven, making it essential reading for KG reasoning researchers but requiring replication before practitioners adopt it.
Citations 2604.11193 PDF
#96 — Innovative (66)

ParSCo: Performance-Driven Par titioning and S cheduling Co-optimization Framework for Processor-based Emulation

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (65) in joint co-optimization formulation; O(1) TOB gain theorem (Non-Obv 70) is a non-trivial mathematical contribution. Rigor (70) — extensive ablations, hyperparameter sensitivity, public benchmark dataset; dinged for no public code, closed-source Sphinx evaluator, and single-run determinism vs hMetis best-of-5. Impact (65) — C++ framework unreleased, 9.1M nodes tested but 100× below rack-scale target. Resilience (60) — quality claims depend on closed evaluator. Weighted: 0.15*65+0.15*70+0.10*70+0.15*65+0.15*60+0.30*65 ≈ 66.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ParSCo, a co-optimization framework that jointly performs hypergraph partitioning and scheduling for processor-based emulation (PBE) compilation by incorporating topological order balancing (TOB) as a co-objective across every stage of the multi-level partitioning pipeline. - Why it's important: Functional verification of billion-gate VLSI designs depends on PBE emulation speed, which is determined by the time step (instruction cycles); treating partitioning and scheduling as independent sequential stages produces suboptimal time steps that directly lengthen verification cycles at a scale where software simulation is orders of magnitude too slow. - How it does it: The framework integrates a TOB metric (variance of node counts per topological order across processors) alongside cut size into coarsening, initial partitioning, FM-style refinement, and a new propagation-based scheduling algorithm, with an algebraic theorem reducing the TOB gain update cost from O(k) to O(1). - The good: On 22 Titan23 public benchmarks and 3 open-source chip designs (up to 9.1M nodes/9.3M nets), ParSCo achieves 22.5% average time step reduction, 72% TOB improvement, and 55% CPU time reduction versus the SOTA two-stage approach (hMetis/KaHyPar + list scheduling). - The missing: No experiment approaches rack-level PBE scale (~1B gates); the largest tested design (NVDLA, 9.1M nodes) is roughly 100× below full rack capacity, leaving production-scale scalability unvalidated by measurement. - Read it or not: EDA researchers and engineers building PBE compilation toolchains should read this; it delivers a rigorous algorithmic framework, a non-trivial O(1) gain theorem, and public benchmark data, though absence of public code limits direct reproducibility. Problem & Motivation Processor-based emulators (PBEs) compile VLSI designs — represented as directed acyclic hypergraphs (DAHs) of logic gates and nets — onto arrays of Boolean processors organized in a four-level hierarchy (processor, board, cluster, rack). Compilation involves two phases: partitioning the DAH into sub-graphs that fit each processor's gate capacity, then scheduling the order in which sub-graphs are emulated. The primary performance metric is the time step (number of instruction cycles). In the conventional two-stage flow, min-cut hypergraph partitioning runs first (ignoring scheduling), followed by topological order balancing (TOB) scheduling — the degree to which gates at each topological level are evenly distributed across processors. Because partitioning does not account for TOB, the scheduler receives a partition that may already structurally preclude a good time step, and the solution space is unnecessarily limited. No prior work has addressed DAH partitioning and scheduling as a joint co-optimization problem under PBE-specific constraints: fixed-node preassignment (IP modules bound to specific processors), BRAM grouping constraints, input/output scheduling constraints, and hierarchical multi-level architecture partitioning targets. Key Idea The core insight is that TOB — the variance of node counts per topological order level across all processor blocks — is the structural property that directly determines scheduling quality, and therefore a TOB metric should be embedded as a co-objective (alongside cut size) in every partitioning stage: coarsening, initial partitioning, and refinement. The secondary algorithmic key idea is a mathematical theorem (Theorem 2 and Corollary 1) proving that when a node changes topological order from m to n, the change in the aggregate TOB metric K(σ²) depends only on the current counts |P^m| and |P^n| and their running means — all of which are O(1)-accessible global state — reducing per-move gain calculation from O(k) (traversing all k partitions) to O(1). This makes the co-objective tractable at million-node scale. Methodology ParSCo implements a multi-level partitioning paradigm extended with scheduling awareness across five stages: 1. Preprocessing: Duplicate hyperedges are merged; ASAP and ALAP topological orders are computed via Kahn's algorithm and its reverse to derive each node's mobility (τ_l − τ_s), the scheduling slack. 2.
