TOB-aware Coarsening with Multiple Constraints: Extends prior TopoOrderPart coarsening by enforcing PBE scheduling constraints (input/BRAM/output nodes contract with their forced co-partition neighbors) and fixed-node general constraints (contracted fixed nodes propagate their block assignment). Connectivity-plus-TOB rating guides contraction. Parallel nets are deliberately not removed — identifying them at million-node scale costs ~8% runtime without offsetting partitioning benefit, an explicitly justified design decision. 3. Global Growing Initial Partitioning with Fixed Nodes: Modified Super-Far Root Growing where fixed nodes anchor block roots. Gain is decomposed into two separate data structures — k cutsize priority queues and k×|V| TOB gain arrays — avoiding hyperparameter instability from the combined gain formulation when fixed nodes are present. Nodes are assigned by first selecting maximum cutsize gain globally, then choosing the target block by maximum TOB gain (Algorithm 1). 4. TopoRefinement (Local Search): FM-style refinement with gain function gain_r(v) = ζΔ₂(v) + Σω(e), where Δ₂(v) is the TOB gain (Equation 8), ζ=2, and Σω(e) is incident net weight. Two strategies: boundary nodes refinement (locks moved nodes, sweeps only cut-edge nodes) and local refinement (propagates TOB gain updates to neighbors via the O(1) theorem). Contracted nodes caused by grouping/scheduling constraints are excluded from uncoarsening in this stage. 5. Partitioning-aware Static Scheduling: After final-level partitioning, nodes are iteratively moved within their mobility ranges [τ_s, τ_l] to minimize TOB using forward and backward BFS propagation (Algorithm 3). Each propagation adjusts predecessors'/successors' topological orders to maintain legality; propagations that violate constraints or mobility bounds are rolled back. The process terminates when all mobilities are zero or remaining propagations lock. The joint objective is Φ(H, Π_H) = Σ_e cut(e) + βK(σ²_τ), with β = 1/5 empirically set, where K(σ²_τ) = Σ_τ (σ²_τ / |P^τ|²) aggregates normalized variance across all topological orders. Key Results On the Titan23 public benchmark (22 designs, 92K–1,089K nodes, up to 923K nets, evaluated in an industrial PBE simulator [Sphinx]): - vs. list scheduling baseline: 22% time step reduction, 70% TOB improvement, 54% CPU time reduction. - vs. hMetis + scheduling (best of 5 runs): 9% time step reduction, 15% TOB improvement, 1.72× speedup. - vs. KaHyPar + scheduling: 13% time step reduction, 35% TOB improvement. - Table 5 confirms ParSCo's advantage holds even against hMetis average-of-5 (0.88 vs. 0.87 time step ratio, 0.30 vs. 0.45 TOB ratio). On 3 open-source chip benchmarks (XiangShan1: 2.6M nodes, XiangShan2: 3.3M, NVDLA: 9.1M): - vs. KaHyPar + scheduling: 23% time step reduction, 74% TOB improvement, 56% runtime improvement, 12.5× speedup. - vs. hMetis + scheduling: 12% time step reduction, 91% TOB improvement, 12.5× runtime speedup. Ablation (Figure 7, Titan23): Full ParSCo improves 15% over TopoOrderPart (conference version) + plain scheduling. All four novel components (TOB-aware coarsening, global growing initial partitioning, TopoRefinement, partitioning-aware scheduling) each contribute positively; using any single technique without the others degrades TOB. The NVDLA design (9.1M nodes) requires up to 8.58 GB peak memory for ParSCo vs. 12,450 GB for KaHyPar (Table 4 — appears to be a unit error in the paper; likely MB or the KaHyPar figure reflects OOM conditions). Hyperparameter sensitivity (Figure 9): stable across β ∈ [1/10, 1/2] and ζ ∈ [1, 2]; performance degrades at β=4, ζ=4. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The joint formulation of DAH partitioning and scheduling under PBE-specific constraints (fixed nodes, BRAM grouping, hierarchical architecture levels) using TOB as a shared co-objective across all pipeline stages is genuinely novel — no prior work addresses this combined problem. The O(1) TOB gain theorem is a non-trivial mathematical result. The individual building blocks (multi-level partitioning, FM refinement, ASAP/ALAP scheduling, TOB metric) are drawn from prior work; the novelty lies in their integration under the co-optimization objective and the constraint-aware algorithmic machinery required to make it work. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation covers 4 component variants across 22 benchmarks with clear quantification (Figure 7). Hyperparameter sensitivity is reported across a grid of β and ζ values (Figure 9). No error bars or statistical significance tests are provided for ParSCo's deterministic single-run results. The hMetis comparison uses best-of-5 (due to random seed sensitivity) while ParSCo is deterministic — a mild methodological asymmetry that the authors partially address by including an average-of-5 comparison in Table 5. The DAH benchmark dataset is publicly released (Google Drive). The C++ implementation is not publicly released, limiting full reproducibility. The primary metric (time step) is measured using the closed-source Sphinx industrial tool [34], making independent validation of this metric impossible without access to the same evaluator. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (C++ implementation evaluated on real VLSI benchmarks on commodity server hardware). - Practical: Runs on a single Linux workstation (Intel Xeon Gold 6248R, 256 GB RAM, Ubuntu 22.04) — commercially available hardware; the largest 9.1M-node NVDLA case requires approximately 8.58 GB peak memory for ParSCo. - Efficient: ParSCo is 1.72× faster than hMetis+scheduling on Titan23 and 12.5× faster than KaHyPar+scheduling on open-source chip benchmarks; coarsening dominates runtime (40.3%), followed by local refinement (21.5%) and TopoRefinement (26.6%). - Effective: Consistent time step and TOB improvements across 25 diverse benchmarks provide strong evidence; however, time step measurement depends on the closed-source Sphinx evaluator, limiting independent verification. - Scalable: Tested up to 9.1M nodes; the PBE rack architecture supports ~1.15B gates, leaving production scale unvalidated by measurement — the paper claims "promising scalability" based on open-source benchmark trends but provides no billion-gate experiment. The work delivers a C++ software framework (not publicly released) and a public DAH benchmark dataset. Strengths 1. The co-optimization formulation unifies cut size and TOB across all five pipeline stages (coarsening, initial partitioning, TopoRefinement, and scheduling) rather than patching scheduling information onto one stage — this is architecturally coherent and the ablation confirms each stage's contribution. 2. Theorem 2 and Corollary 1 reducing TOB gain update to O(1) are non-trivial, rigorously proven, and practically essential for million-scale tractability — without this, the joint objective would be a bottleneck. 3. The global growing initial partitioning with fixed nodes correctly identifies and solves a subtle instability problem caused by combining fixed-node constraints with the TOB gain hyperparameter, replacing a fragile unified gain with two cleanly separated data structures. 4. Testing on real open-source RISC-V processor (XiangShan) and DNN accelerator (NVDLA) designs at multi-million node scale bridges the gap between academic hypergraph benchmarks and industrial PBE workloads. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge: (i) parallel net removal during coarsening is skipped because identification cost (~8% runtime) is not offset by quality gain at large scale; (ii) fixed-node and grouping constraints are randomly assigned (0.01% fixed, 1% BRAM) rather than from real IP preassignment scenarios; (iii) true simultaneous partitioning and scheduling (not sequential with feedback) remains unexplored and is proposed as future work; (iv) performance degrades at extreme hyperparameter settings (β=4, ζ=4). The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Production-scale validation is absent: the largest test (9.1M nodes, NVDLA) is ~100× below full rack capacity (1.15B gates); claims of scalability remain extrapolated. - The C++ implementation is not released, making the framework itself non-reproducible by the community despite the benchmark data being public. - Fixed-node density in experiments (0.01%) may be unrealistically low — real IP preassignment can reach substantially higher densities, and the paper does not test constraint robustness across fixed-node density ranges. - The hMetis baseline uses a commercial tool's default parameters (hMetis v1.5.3) rather than an extensively tuned configuration; KaHyPar uses default settings — both may understate achievable two-stage quality. - Memory scaling is not characterized; Table 4 contains what appears to be a unit discrepancy for KaHyPar memory on large designs that is not explained. Bottom Line A practitioner building PBE compilation toolchains or researching scheduling-aware hypergraph partitioning gains concrete, non-trivial algorithms — particularly the O(1) TOB gain theorem and the fixed-node-aware initial partitioning — plus a public benchmark dataset, making this paper worth reading, though absence of public code and billion-gate validation are real limitations for industrial adoption.
Citations
#97 — Innovative (66)

SG-Serve: Efficient Model Serving for Subgraph-based Graph Representation Learning

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (60) — integration-level novelty; components (work-stealing, MPS, producer-consumer) are established. Rigor (70) — unusually clean four-level ablation, 3 models × 4 graphs × 2 GPU generations, synthetic Poisson traces acknowledged. Non-Obv (65) for the 5-step DAG abstraction unifying 16 SGRL models. Impact (70) — concrete Triton backend, large gains, but no public code repository. Resilience (60) — burst traffic and non-stationary access patterns unevaluated. Weighted: 0.15*60+0.15*70+0.10*65+0.15*65+0.15*60+0.30*70 ≈ 66.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SG-Serve, a specialized online serving system for subgraph-based graph representation learning (SGRL) models that reduces P99 tail latency by up to 13.1x and improves request throughput by up to 33.3x over existing synchronous batch processing systems. - Why it's important: SGRL models are supplanting classical GNNs in high-stakes online applications (recommendation, fraud detection) because of their accuracy advantages, but their inference workloads are highly irregular — P99 subgraph size is 27.4x–60.7x the median — causing severe tail latency spikes that existing ML serving frameworks cannot handle. - How it does it: SG-Serve decouples subgraph extraction and GPU inference via a producer-consumer architecture, parallelizes extraction by decomposing it into a fine-grained 5-step DAG scheduled with work-stealing across CPU threads, and batches GPU inference by subgraph workload (node+edge count) rather than request count, using dual GPU processes via Nvidia MPS to prevent HoL blocking. - The good: End-to-end throughput improvements exceed 10x in almost all tested configurations across 3 SGRL models and 4 graphs (up to 111M nodes, 1.8B edges), with ablations cleanly isolating each component's contribution. - The missing: All request traces are synthetic Poisson-process arrivals; no real production workload traces are used, leaving open whether the observed workload irregularity and performance gains transfer to real deployment traffic patterns. - Read it or not: Essential reading for ML systems researchers and practitioners building online serving infrastructure for SGRL models; researchers working on SGRL model design or non-graph ML serving will find the irregular-workload scheduling techniques conceptually interesting but only partially transferable. Problem & Motivation SGRL models require two sequential per-request stages: subgraph extraction from the data graph on CPU, followed by GNN inference on GPU. The key challenge is that extraction workload per request is governed by graph degree distributions, which follow power laws in real-world graphs, producing a long-tailed request size distribution. Profiling with the SEAL model on Papers100M shows that P99 requests are 27.4x and 60.7x the median in node and edge count respectively, and their running time is similarly skewed. Existing serving systems (PyG, DGL) address none of this: they use synchronous batch processing (SBP) — one CPU thread per request, a fixed batch count, and a barrier between extraction and inference — which causes (1) batch straggler effects where one heavy request delays all others in the batch, (2) head-of-line (HoL) blocking where heavy requests monopolize CPU threads and GPU resources, and (3) single-thread extraction bottlenecks for heavy requests. Classical GNN serving systems (DGI, GCNP) precompute node embeddings offline, which is infeasible for SGRL because the number of possible seed-node combinations is combinatorially large (e.g., 10^16 pairs for Papers100M's 10^8 nodes). Key Idea The central insight is that the two SGRL pipeline stages have different irregularity manifestations and require stage-specific mitigations: for CPU subgraph extraction, decompose the extraction algorithm for each request into a directed acyclic graph of fine-grained node-parallel tasks and schedule them across threads with work-stealing — parallelizing heavy requests without letting them starve light ones; for GPU model inference, define "workload" by subgraph size in nodes and edges (not request count) and use a skip-FIFO batching strategy that separates heavy subgraphs into standalone batches while grouping light ones, combined with dual-process GPU partitioning to prevent heavy-batch monopolization. Methodology SG-Serve has two main components linked by a producer-consumer shared queue. Subgraph Extraction Engine: A survey of 16 SGRL models reveals that all extraction algorithms decompose into five sequential steps: Expand (k-hop or PPR-based neighbor selection per seed), Merge (union or intersection of per-seed node sets), Induce (edge retrieval for merged nodes), Encode (structural label computation, e.g., shortest-distance BFS), and Combine (label aggregation across seeds). SG-Serve models this as a DAG of operators and exposes a programmable API where users implement node-level functions per operator; the system automatically decomposes each operator into segments of 128 nodes as independent tasks. Multiple requests' DAGs are co-scheduled on a CPU thread pool using work-stealing: each DAG is assigned a primary thread that executes its local queue; idle threads steal tasks in round-robin from other threads. This provides parallelism for heavy requests (their tasks spread across idle threads) while ensuring no single heavy request can monopolize all threads (each DAG is pinned to one primary thread). Model Inference Engine: Workload-aware batching (Algorithm 1) uses node limit Nb and edge limit Eb = λ·Nb (where λ is the observed average edge/node ratio) to bound batch workload. The dispatcher scans the subgraph queue in FIFO order, greedily adding light subgraphs to the current batch while skipping heavy ones that would violate limits; skipped heavy subgraphs are not starved as they advance to the head of the queue for the next batch cycle. Dual-process dispatching via Nvidia MPS partitions the GPU into two 50%-share processes; heavy-request batches are sent to one process while light ones go to the other, preventing monopolization. Nb is configured offline by profiling-based binary search. Hot node embeddings (high-degree nodes) are cached in GPU memory to reduce CPU–GPU transfer overhead. Implementation is ~6k lines of C++ deployed as a Triton backend. Shared-memory pinned buffers enable zero-copy GPU dispatch; separate CUDA streams per worker overlap data transfer and kernel computation. Key design decisions and their justification: (1) DAG abstraction covering 16 models — justified by exhaustive survey, though the Merge operator is not parallelized due to data dependency; (2) work-stealing over FIFO-parallel — justified by showing FIFO causes HoL at high throughput (Figure 16); (3) 2 MPS processes over more — justified empirically by showing 4+ processes degrades due to reduced kernel size and scheduler overhead (Figure 19). Key Results Experiments use SEAL, Shadow, and GraIL on four graphs: Orkut (3.1M nodes, 120M edges), Livejournal (4.8M, 68M), Friendster (65M, 1.8B), Papers100M (111M, 1.7B). Baselines are SBP (re-implemented in C++ on SG-Serve codebase), PC (producer-consumer only), and PC+E (adds parallelized extraction). Hardware: Nvidia T4 GPU (15GB), 24-core Intel Xeon 8259CL, 187GB RAM. At a fixed P99 latency of 80ms (Table 3), SG-Serve achieves 8.75x–33.3x higher throughput than SBP across all model–dataset combinations. At the same input throughput, SG-Serve reduces P50 latency by up to 19.3x and P99 by up to 13.1x. Ablation contributions are clearly quantified: PC alone reduces P99 vs. SBP by up to 4.76x by eliminating batch synchronization; PC+E adds 1.24x–3.60x over PC at low throughput (where extraction dominates); full SG-Serve (PC+E+I) adds 1.21x–3.92x over PC+E at high throughput (where inference dominates). Within inference, dual-process MPS reduces P99 by 1.38x–2.11x over single-process; workload-aware batching adds 1.25x–2.05x on top. Hot embedding caching reduces CPU–GPU communication latency by up to 5.48x and end-to-end latency by up to 2.29x. On A100 GPU, trends hold: SG-Serve reduces P99 by up to 10.7x vs. SBP, confirming generalizability across GPU generations. A counter-intuitive finding: FIFO-parallel scheduling outperforms task-stealing at low throughput (sufficient idle threads) but becomes worse than even single-threaded no-parallel at high throughput due to HoL monopolization — task-stealing is strictly better across all throughput regimes. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The contribution is an end-to-end serving system, not a new model or algorithm. The genuinely novel elements are: (a) the 5-step DAG abstraction that unifies 16 existing SGRL extraction algorithms into a single parallelizable framework, and (b) the co-design of workload-aware batching with skip-FIFO and dual-process GPU dispatching specifically motivated by SGRL's node/edge-count-driven irregularity. Individual components — work stealing, Nvidia MPS, producer-consumer pipelines — are established techniques; the novelty lies in their analysis-driven integration for this specific workload class. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough and structurally sound: each system variant (SBP, PC, PC+E, PC+E+I) isolates one design choice, and design-choice ablations (scheduling policy, MPS count, batching strategy, caching) are each evaluated independently. No error bars or confidence intervals are reported — standard practice in systems papers but notable for latency distributions. Experiments span 3 models × 4 datasets × 2 GPU generations. Request traces are synthetic (Poisson process), which is disclosed and cited as common practice for serving system evaluation; however, no sensitivity analysis to the arrival distribution is provided. The baseline SBP is re-implemented in C++ rather than using original Python PyG/DGL, which improves fairness within the paper but means the reported gains do not directly correspond to drop-in replacement speedups practitioners would observe. No public code repository is mentioned. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: Runs on a single commodity server (T4 or A100 GPU, 24-core CPU); no specialized hardware required beyond what SGRL deployment already demands. - Efficient: The dual-process MPS configuration reserves 50% of GPU resources for light requests, reducing peak GPU utilization when all requests are heavy; the paper argues this is acceptable because heavy requests are rare, and empirically GPU utilization is not significantly impacted (Fig. 19 shows 2 MPS is optimal). Hot embedding caching has a fixed GPU memory footprint proportional to high-degree node count. - Effective: Evidence shows SG-Serve solves the actual tail latency and throughput problems (P99, P50, and throughput all improve substantially), not merely proxy metrics, across diverse models and graphs. - Scalable: Tested on graphs up to 111M nodes and 1.8B edges and throughput up to ~10,000 RPS; multi-server scale-out is delegated to Triton's instance group mechanism and not analyzed in the paper — a gap for large-scale deployments. The paper delivers a working C++ software artifact (Triton backend), but the repository is not publicly released. Strengths - The 5-step DAG abstraction that subsumes 16 existing SGRL models — validated by surveying the literature — is a genuine generalization that removes the need for per-model serving engineering while exposing fine-grained parallelism automatically. - The empirical motivation is rigorous: the authors profile SBP's three failure modes (batch synchronization delay, extraction bottleneck, queuing spike) separately with concrete measurements before proposing solutions, grounding every design decision in observed data. - Ablation structure is unusually clean — each of the four main design choices (producer-consumer, parallel extraction, workload-aware batching, dual-process MPS) is isolated and its contribution quantified across multiple throughput regimes, revealing that different optimizations dominate at different load levels. - Generalizability is tested: three structurally distinct SGRL models, four graphs spanning two orders of magnitude in size, and two GPU generations all show consistent trends. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge that request traces are synthetic (Poisson process) and no real production SGRL serving traces are publicly available. They note that multi-GPU scale-out is handled by Triton's instance group and is not the focus of this work. The profiling-based batch configuration requires a per-deployment tuning step. The paper does not address dynamic graphs where the data graph changes during serving. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The C++ re-implementation of SBP for baseline comparison controls for language overhead but obscures the practical speedup a real team would observe when replacing PyG/DGL — the paper should report original Python baseline numbers alongside. - The Poisson arrival model is memoryless and does not capture burst traffic (flash sales, coordinated fraud attacks) where multiple heavy requests arrive simultaneously; workload-aware batching's skip-FIFO could starve skipped heavy requests under sustained burst conditions. - P999 latency is never reported; for fraud detection applications where the paper claims relevance, missing a single heavy fraudulent transaction due to P999 queuing failures could be costly. - Hot embedding caching assumes high-degree nodes are the hot set, but in personalized recommendation the access distribution shifts with user behavior over time; cache effectiveness under non-stationary query distributions is not evaluated. - The Encode step (which runs a graph algorithm like BFS for distance labeling) is the most algorithmically complex operator, yet no per-operator breakdown of the 5.17x extraction speedup is provided — it is unclear whether parallelism is evenly beneficial across all operators or concentrated in Encode. Bottom Line A practitioner building online inference pipelines for SGRL models (link prediction, fraud detection, recommendation) will find SG-Serve's design directly actionable and its performance gains substantial enough to justify adoption; those without an SGRL-specific deployment need will find the irregular-workload scheduling analysis instructive but the system itself inapplicable.
Citations
#98 — Innovative (66)

Accelerating Triangle-Connected Truss Community Search Across Heterogeneous Hardware

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (65) — triangle-type classification with formal order-independence (Theorem 1) and batch-merge convergence (Theorem 2) are genuine theoretical contributions. Non-Obv (70) for the insight that triangle-type determines index operation. Rigor (65) — targeted ablations but no public code, author-implemented CUDA MCETree baseline, and Python/PyTorch vs C++ language asymmetry inflates speedup numbers. Impact (70) — cross-hardware portability (NVIDIA + AMD) demonstrated, large speedups, but no repository. Resilience (60) limited by baseline implementation quality. Weighted: 0.15*65+0.15*65+0.10*70+0.15*60+0.15*60+0.30*70 ≈ 66.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TETree/TETruss, a tensor-based framework for constructing, searching, and maintaining Triangle-Connected k-Truss Community (k-TTC) indices on heterogeneous hardware accelerators (GPUs, TPUs, NPUs) via Tensor Computation Runtimes such as PyTorch. - Why it's important: Sequential and multi-core CPU algorithms for k-TTC index construction on billion-edge graphs require hours to build and seconds to search, making interactive community search at web scale infractically slow. - How it does it: Triangles are classified into internal, marginal, and external types — each with proven distinct roles in EquiTree construction — enabling all supernodes and superedges across all trussness values to be created in a single tensor batch, eliminating the costly per-k iteration, then a batch-based supernode merging strategy refines the index to a tree structure in parallel. - The good: TETree achieves a 336× average speedup over sequential SETree on index construction across 7 real-world graphs up to 261M edges, with cross-hardware portability validated on NVIDIA A100, RTX 3090, and AMD 7700XT GPUs. - The missing: No public code repository is cited, the CUDA MCETree baseline is author-implemented rather than drawn from an optimized production library, and the Python/PyTorch implementation language introduces uncontrolled overhead relative to C++ CPU baselines. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers building GPU-accelerated graph analytics or community search systems should read this; those focused on community detection algorithms or graph neural networks can skip it. Problem & Motivation Triangle-Connected k-Truss Community (k-TTC) is a cohesive subgraph model used in social network analysis, protein-interaction search, geo-social group discovery, and code graph analysis. Its tighter diameter bound over plain k-truss makes it preferable for attributed, size-constrained, or user-preference-driven search tasks. The practical problem is that for graphs with billions of edges — the Orkut graph has 117M edges and the Weibo graph 261M edges — the sequential EquiTree index construction takes hours and even the best parallel CPU method (MCETree) takes tens of minutes. The fundamental bottleneck is that existing CPU algorithms iterate over each trussness value k from k_max down to 3, examining k-triangles and building equivalence classes one k-level at a time. This iterative paradigm is antithetical to the batch-parallel execution model of GPU/TPU hardware accelerators, which are optimized for large uniform tensor operations rather than many small sequential kernel launches. Key Idea The core insight is that every triangle in a graph can be classified as internal (all three edges share the same trussness k), marginal (two edges share trussness k, one edge has higher trussness), or external (the lowest-trussness edge differs from the other two), and these three types play provably distinct, non-overlapping roles during EquiTree index construction (Properties 1–3, Theorem 1). Because the type of each triangle determines what index operation it contributes — internal triangles merge edges into the same supernode, external triangles create superedges between supernodes, marginal triangles do both — all triangles across all trussness values can be processed simultaneously in one batch using tailored tensor operations, replacing the sequential k-loop. Theorem 1 formally proves that the order in which triangles are processed does not affect the resulting EquiTree index, which is the mathematical guarantee that enables this single-pass construction. Methodology The framework has three algorithmic phases: index construction (Algorithms 1 and 2), k-TTC search (Algorithm 3), and index maintenance (Algorithm 4). The graph is stored in CSR format (tensors ptr and adj) and the index in COO format (tensors idx_src and idx_dst for superedges). Basic index construction (Alg. 1) parallelizes within each k-level but still iterates k from k_max to 3. For each k-class Φ_k, it computes k-triangles, runs tensor Link (union-find merge via min-labeling) and Compress (path compression to root) operations to build connected components representing k-truss equivalence classes (supernodes), then uses EdgeUnique to deduplicate and MergeNode to merge conflicting supernodes that violate the truss-precedence relation. Step complexity is O(Σ log(t_k)); work complexity is O(m^1.5). Advanced index construction (Alg. 2) eliminates the k-loop. All triangles are computed at once and split into internal (E_s^i, E_l^i, E_r^i), marginal (E_s^m, E_l^m, E_r^m), and external (E_s^e, E_l^e, E_r^e) edge sets. Internal and marginal triangles are Linked into supernodes; external and marginal triangles generate superedges. The refinement phase then batch-merges conflicting supernodes: it identifies maximal active supernodes (those with multiple higher-trussness neighbors), creates chain paths among their neighbors to enforce truss-precedence, applies Link/Compress in parallel, and iterates until all supernodes are static. Theorem 2 proves that after one round on maximal active supernodes, those supernodes become static and are never re-activated, guaranteeing convergence. In practice TETree requires far fewer iterations than TETree-Basic (e.g., LJ: 126 vs. 575; WB: 34 vs. 343). Creation phase step complexity is O(log(m)); total step complexity is O(log(m) + R·log(m_{I_T})). Three critical design decisions: (1) Triangle-type classification — well-justified by formal properties and the order-independence theorem. (2) Batch-based merging during refinement rather than layer-by-layer — an ablation (TETree-W/O-BM) shows up to 6.15× speedup from this strategy. (3) Optimized Parallel CC (OPCC) for search — a dedicated algorithm that updates CC labels along superedges and connects to root via Compress, outperforming label-propagation (LP) by 3.21× and two-fold (TF) traversal by 11.73× on average (Fig. 11). For index maintenance (Alg. 4): when edges are inserted or deleted, affected edges E_A are identified using trussness bounds, the subgraph G_A is extracted and trussnesses recomputed, affected supernodes are split from the existing index, new supernodes/superedges for G_A are built using Alg. 2 lines 2–8, and conflicting supernodes are merged. This avoids full index reconstruction for small edge updates. Implementation: Python with PyTorch 2.2.2 and torch-scatter 2.1.2, evaluated on Ubuntu 22.04 with CUDA 12.1. CPU baselines implemented in C++. Key Results Seven real-world graphs from SNAP/Network Repository: Facebook (88K edges), DBLP (1M), Youtube (3M), Catster (5.4M), LiveJournal (34M), Orkut (117M), Weibo (261M edges, 212M triangles). Index construction (Table 2): TETree on A100 achieves 336.26× average speedup over sequential SETree (CPU); 16.76× over TETree-Basic (GPU). On Weibo, TETree completes in 12.178s vs. SETree's >2 hours (could not complete). TETruss achieves 154.15× speedup over multi-core MCETree (CPU) and 19.59× over author-implemented CUDA MCETree. Even on CPUs only, TETruss achieves 2.42–7.18× over MCETree. MCETree runs out of memory on Orkut and Weibo; TETree handles these via batch processing. Search (Table 5): TETree achieves 108× average speedup over SETree; TETruss achieves 100× over SETruss. TETree searches Weibo in 16.251ms. TETree outperforms TETruss by 7.64× and 7.44× respectively, validating EquiTree's structural advantage. Cross-hardware (Table 4): TETree runs on A100, RTX 3090, and AMD 7700XT. A100 is fastest; 7700XT is slower than 3090 due to ROCm/PyTorch backend maturity but still significantly faster than CPU baselines. Parameter sensitivity (Figs. 9, 12): Construction time scales with triangle count (governed by average degree and k_max); search time decreases with increasing k as communities shrink. OPCC consistently outperforms LP and TF across all degree ranks and k values. Baselines are current: SETree and SETruss are the state-of-the-art sequential implementations from the original authors; MCETree is the published parallel CPU method, with a CUDA variant implemented by the authors for comparison. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The triangle-type classification (internal/marginal/external) with formal order-independence proofs that enable single-batch EquiTree construction is genuinely novel. The batch-based merging strategy with convergence guarantee is non-obvious. The OPCC-based k-TTC search algorithm tailored to tree-structured indices is a new contribution. Applying TCR-based tensor operations (PyTorch) to community search indices — rather than the DL tasks TCRs were designed for — extends prior TCR graph work (relational queries, pagerank, subgraph matching) to a new problem class. The underlying community model (k-TTC) and EquiTree index structure are prior work [47]. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and targeted: TETree-W/O-BM isolates batch merging (up to 6.15× impact), TETree-OPCC vs. TETree-LP vs. TETree-TF isolates the search strategy (Fig. 11). No error bars or confidence intervals are reported for timing results; for deterministic graph algorithms this is a minor concern, but variance from GPU scheduling is unaddressed. No public code URL is cited in the paper — reproducibility depends entirely on the algorithm descriptions and pseudocode, which are detailed. The CUDA MCETree baseline is author-implemented, introducing risk that it does not match a production-quality CUDA implementation. The Python/PyTorch implementation vs. C++ baselines introduces an uncontrolled language overhead; the paper does not attempt to quantify or control for this factor, which may inflate GPU speedup numbers relative to CPU. Seven diverse publicly available datasets are used, which is a reproducibility positive. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems paper — a built framework evaluated by deployment on real hardware). - Practical: Runs on commercially available NVIDIA A100 (80GB), RTX 3090 (24GB), and AMD 7700XT (16GB) GPUs; the Python/PyTorch implementation requires no hardware-specific code. - Efficient: TETree consumes 15–20GB peak GPU memory on large graphs (LJ, OK, WB) when processing 2×10^7 triangles per batch, substantially more than TETree-Basic; batch size is tunable to fit smaller GPUs at the cost of more iterations. - Effective: Two orders of magnitude speedup on both index construction and k-TTC search are demonstrated across all seven datasets; index maintenance outperforms full reconstruction for updates of up to 10^4 edges, though performance degrades toward reconstruction cost at larger update batches. - Scalable: Tested up to 261M edges and 212M triangles (Weibo) using multi-batch processing; the paper describes a pipeline strategy for graphs exceeding device memory but does not evaluate graphs beyond Weibo, leaving behavior at 10× scale (billions of edges) as a claim rather than a measurement. Software artifact: Python implementation; no public repository URL stated. Strengths - The triangle-type classification with formal proofs (Properties 1–3, Theorem 1 on order independence, Theorem 2 on batch merging convergence) is a clean, non-obvious theoretical foundation that directly unlocks the main engineering contribution; the theory and implementation are tightly coupled. - Cross-hardware portability via TCRs without hardware-specific code is practically significant: the same Python code runs on NVIDIA and AMD GPUs, with the backend abstraction insulating the application from hardware churn. - The ablation study is honest about component contributions: batch merging accounts for up to 6.15× speedup, and the OPCC search strategy is independently validated against two alternatives. - Index maintenance for dynamic graphs is a practically important and often neglected component of community search systems; the paper handles insertions and deletions with the same tensor machinery as construction. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: (1) the basic algorithm's k-iteration creates high step complexity and many small tensor operations, motivating the advanced algorithm; (2) TETree's single-batch creation consumes more GPU memory than TETree-Basic on large graphs, addressed by configurable batch sizes; (3) AMD 7700XT performance lags NVIDIA A100 due to physical hardware limitations and immature ROCm/PyTorch backend optimization. These are acknowledged directly and with technical specificity, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The CUDA MCETree baseline is author-implemented in native CUDA; if an expert-tuned production CUDA implementation exists or could be produced, the GPU speedup numbers relative to GPU baselines would be more credible. - Python/PyTorch implementation vs. C++ CPU baselines introduces a language-level performance disadvantage for CPUs that the paper does not control for or quantify; the true algorithmic speedup over CPU may be lower than the reported wall-clock speedup. - No public code release is announced, which significantly limits reproducibility for a systems paper with complex tensor orchestration. - The k-TTC search evaluation uses a fixed k=4 for the main comparison table (Table 5); k=4 is a low trussness that returns large communities — search performance at high k (small communities, potentially faster) or very low-degree vertices is analyzed parametrically but not in the headline comparison. - No ground-truth community benchmarks are used to validate search correctness beyond cross-checking against baseline outputs; quality of returned communities relative to known ground-truth is not assessed. - Memory consumption on Weibo (261M edges) at the default batch size is not reported explicitly, leaving unclear whether the A100's 80GB is required or whether the 3090's 24GB suffices without aggressive batch reduction. Bottom Line This is a well-grounded systems paper with genuine theoretical contributions — the triangle-type classification and batch merging convergence proofs are non-trivial — backed by large-scale experiments that credibly demonstrate two orders of magnitude speedup; practitioners building GPU-accelerated graph community search pipelines will find directly usable techniques, though the absence of public code and the Python-vs-C++ baseline asymmetry warrant caution before benchmarking against it.
Citations
#99 — Innovative (66)

OccuBench: Evaluating AI Agents on Real-World Professional Tasks via Language World Models (43 ▲ )

Xiaomeng Hu, Yinger Zhang, Fei Huang, Jianhong Tu, Yang Su, Lianghao Deng, Yuxuan Liu, Yantao Liu, Dayiheng Liu, Tsung-Yi Ho · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (75) drives the score — the LES abstraction makes 65 previously untestable professional domains evaluable, the benchmark and synthesis pipeline are released, and the E2>E1 fault-injection finding is directly useful for enterprise deployment decisions. Novelty (65) — using LLMs as configured environment simulators for benchmark construction at this breadth is new, though the underlying pieces are familiar. Rigor (55) is the limiter: simulator self-dependence (Gemini Flash both synthesizes and is evaluated), no CIs on small per-industry splits (4–19 scenarios), robustness measured on only 9/15 models, and no quantification of how many tasks flip solvability across simulators. Completeness (65) — limitations are honestly confronted. Resilience (60) — simulator-circularity concern and domain-data vs. domain-logic gap are real. Weighted: 0.15*65+0.15*55+0.10*60+0.15*65+0.15*60+0.30*75 = 64.75 → 65, rounded up to 66 for the practical reach of the LES abstraction.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OccuBench, a benchmark of 382 evaluation instances across 100 professional task scenarios, 10 industry categories, and 65 specialized domains that evaluates AI agents on real-world occupational tasks using LLM-driven Language Environment Simulators (LESs) in place of real system APIs. - Why it's important: The vast majority of high-value professional domains (healthcare triage, nuclear safety, customs processing) have no public APIs or sandbox environments, making agent evaluation there impossible with existing benchmarks — a critical blind spot for production deployment decisions. - How it does it: An LLM configured with a (system_prompt, tool_schema, initial_state, state_description) tuple acts as a stateful interactive simulator, generating plausible tool responses for any domain an LLM understands, enabling benchmark construction as a configuration problem rather than an engineering one. - The good: The cross-simulator pairwise ranking agreement of 85.7% between two capable simulators (Gemini Flash and Qwen 3.5 Plus) confirms that LES-based evaluation produces consistent relative model rankings when simulator quality is sufficient. - The missing: LESs simulate domain logic but not domain data — they evaluate whether agents follow correct decision-making procedures, not whether they can handle real numerical values, exact database records, or precise financial calculations, a gap the paper acknowledges but does not empirically quantify. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or evaluating tool-using agents, particularly those interested in professional/enterprise deployments where no real environment testbed exists; evaluators focused purely on output-quality benchmarks can treat this as complementary context. Problem & Motivation: AI agents are increasingly deployed in high-stakes occupational contexts — emergency department triage, nuclear reactor monitoring, customs import processing, greenhouse irrigation control — yet existing agent benchmarks are structurally limited to domains with public APIs or sandboxed environments. WebArena covers web browsing, OSWorld covers desktop operations, SWE-bench covers code repositories, TAU-bench covers retail and airline APIs. Healthcare, finance, legal, energy, manufacturing, and logistics are essentially unrepresented. This is not an edge case but the default state for the vast majority of economically valuable professional work. A secondary gap is that all existing benchmarks evaluate agents only on clean "happy path" environments, providing no systematic assessment of robustness to the transient faults (timeouts, truncated data, missing fields) that characterize real production systems. Key Idea: The core insight is that the environment itself can be simulated by an LLM. Given a structured configuration (system prompt encoding behavioral rules, tool schema defining the action space, initial state as a JSON object, and semantic state descriptions), a sufficiently capable LLM becomes a stateful interactive simulator capable of generating consistent tool responses for any domain it understands at the operational logic level. This transforms environment construction from a systems engineering problem into a configuration authoring problem, making it feasible to cover 65 specialized domains that would individually require months of real-environment integration work. Methodology: OccuBench is built on a four-component LES formalism: the transition function f_θ(s_t, a_t; c) maps agent tool calls to observations while maintaining implicit state through in-context conversation history. Benchmark construction uses a multi-agent synthesis pipeline powered by Gemini-3-Flash-Preview: it generates 16 non-overlapping reference sub-topics per scenario from professional reference documents, then produces environment configurations, task instructions, tool definitions, solution plans, and verification rubrics. Each task is executed multiple times with and without a reference plan; a majority-vote verifier assesses trajectories and a repair module fixes failures, filtering out trivially solvable (100% pass rate) and unsolvable (0% pass rate) instances. The final 382 instances average 5.5 tools and 16.2 tool calls per task. Environmental robustness is evaluated via controlled fault injection at four levels: E0 (clean), E1 (explicit errors: HTTP 500, TimeoutError, ConnectionRefused), E2 (implicit faults: truncated data, missing fields, stale values with no error signal), and E3 (mixed). Robustness is scored as R = min(CR_E1, CR_E2, CR_E3) / CR_E0. Evaluation uses two metrics: Completion Rate (CR) — fraction of 382 tasks where the trajectory passes rubric verification — and Robustness Score R. Design decisions: (1) task solvability verification is automated and well-justified as a quality gate; (2) difficulty calibration by selecting the sub-difficulty level with lowest autonomous success rate is reasonable but creates a dependency between benchmark difficulty and the synthesis simulator's capability; (3) fault injection appended to the LES system prompt is simple and controllable but may not replicate the spatial/temporal patterns of real production failures. Key Results: 15 frontier models across 8 families are evaluated (GPT-5.2, Gemini 3.1 Pro/Flash-Lite, Claude Opus/Sonnet 4/4.5/4.6, DeepSeek V3.2, Kimi K2.5, GLM-5, MiniMax M2.7, Qwen 3.5 Plus/Flash). Under clean conditions (E0): GPT-5.2 leads overall at 79.6%, Gemini 3.1 Pro second at 72.3%, Claude Opus 4.6 third at 71.5%. Open-source models Qwen 3.5 Plus (69.9%) and DeepSeek V3.2 (69.6%) outperform several Claude variants. No single model dominates: Gemini 3.1 Pro leads Education (84%) and Science (81%) but scores only 62% in Healthcare; Claude Opus 4.6 leads Transportation (77%) but scores 53% in Commerce. Under fault injection, average CR drops from 67.5% (E0) to 53.4% (E2), a 14.1-point decline under implicit faults. Implicit faults (E2, avg 53.4%) are harder than explicit (E1, avg 62.6%) — a counter-intuitive finding: 4 of 9 models perform worse under E2 than E3. Robustness ranking: Gemini 3.1 Pro and MiniMax M2.7 tie at R=0.87; GPT-5.2 at R=0.84; Kimi K2.5 worst at R=0.63. Reasoning effort ablation on GPT-5.2 shows a 27.5-point improvement from none (54.7%) to xhigh (82.2%). Cross-simulator analysis: GPT-5.2 ranks 1st as agent (79.6%) but produces worst simulation quality (avg agent score drops to 29.3% under GPT-5.2 as simulator vs. 67.9% under Gemini Flash), demonstrating that agent capability and simulator capability are dissociable. Pairwise ranking agreement between Gemini Flash and Qwen 3.5 Plus is 85.7%. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The LES concept of using a configured LLM as a domain-specific environment simulator is genuinely novel as applied to benchmark construction at this scale. The fault injection taxonomy (E0–E3 with transient, spaced faults) is a new empirical contribution. The cross-industry occupational scope (65 specialized domains, many previously untestable) is the primary differentiating novelty. The underlying techniques — LLM tool-response generation, rubric-based verification — combine existing ideas; the insight is their orchestration into a scalable evaluation pipeline. Rigor & Reproducibility: Code and data are stated as available (project page, GitHub, and data links shown on the title page, though URLs are not printed in the paper text). Ablations cover fault count and duration (Figure 4), reasoning effort levels (Figure 7), simulator quality (Table 4), and model size scaling (Figure 5). No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals are reported — given only 382 total instances and cross-industry splits of 4–19 scenarios each, category-level results are low-sample and conclusions about industry-specific capability profiles should be treated with caution. The synthesis pipeline uses Gemini-3-Flash-Preview as the default simulator, creating a potential circularity: tasks are verified as solvable under Gemini Flash, meaning task solvability is inherently simulator-relative. The paper acknowledges this and reports that switching simulators changes rankings, but does not quantify how many tasks become unsolvable under a different simulator. The robustness metric R uses the minimum across E1–E3, which is a conservative worst-case measure; this is a reasonable but debatable design choice that is not ablated. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — OccuBench is a built evaluation framework with benchmark data and a multi-agent synthesis pipeline. - Practical: The benchmark can run using any frontier LLM API as simulator; no specialized hardware required, though inference costs for evaluating 15 models × 382 tasks × 4 fault conditions are non-trivial. - Efficient: Evaluation cost scales linearly with the number of models, tasks, and fault conditions; the synthesis pipeline using Gemini Flash is cost-efficient relative to manual environment construction, though exact costs are not stated. - Effective: The 85.7% pairwise ranking agreement across two capable simulators provides reasonable evidence that the benchmark discriminates agent capability consistently, though simulator dependence remains a confound for absolute scores. - Scalable: The LES approach claims to scale to any domain an LLM understands; however, the practical upper limit on simulator fidelity — particularly for numerically precise or data-sensitive domains — is not tested beyond the 65 domains in the paper. The work delivers a software artifact (benchmark dataset + synthesis pipeline) with code and data stated as released. Strengths: - The LES formulation is a genuinely useful abstraction that recasts environment construction as configuration authoring, making the previously untestable majority of professional domains accessible to systematic agent evaluation. - The fault injection taxonomy (E0–E3) is well-designed: the distinction between implicit (E2) and explicit (E1) faults is theoretically motivated and produces a non-obvious empirical finding (E2 > E1 in difficulty), which is the paper's single most practically important result for deployment decisions. - Cross-industry occupational profiling reveals model specialization patterns (Gemini 3.1 Pro favors knowledge-intensive domains, Claude Opus 4.6 favors operational/stateful domains) that are invisible to single-domain benchmarks and have direct implications for enterprise model selection. - The simulator quality analysis (Section 6.6) honestly confronts a fundamental validity threat to the LES approach and provides quantitative evidence (85.7% pairwise agreement under capable simulators, failure mode taxonomy) that the approach is reliable when simulator quality is verified. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly confront two core limitations. First, simulation fidelity: LESs model domain logic, not domain data — they evaluate decision-making process correctness, not numerical precision or exact real-world data handling. For financially or medically precise calculations, LES-based evaluation should be complemented with real-environment testing. Second, simulator dependence: evaluation results are tied to the specific simulator used during data synthesis; tasks verified solvable under one LES may become unsolvable under another, and agent rankings shift. The authors recommend verifying simulator quality before drawing conclusions and re-verifying task solvability when switching simulators. Both limitations are stated directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The synthesis pipeline uses Gemini-3-Flash-Preview as the default LES for task generation and solvability verification, and Gemini models are also among the agents being evaluated — this creates a potential advantage for Gemini models whose implicit world model aligns with the synthesis simulator's logic, a bias that is not controlled for or quantified. Category-level results rest on small sample sizes (4 scenarios for Science, 7 for Healthcare) that make per-industry conclusions statistically fragile without confidence intervals. The robustness evaluation is conducted only on 9 of 15 models ("one per family"), so robustness rankings cannot be compared with clean-condition rankings for the other 6 models. The paper does not evaluate whether LES simulation quality degrades for domains that are less represented in LLM pre-training data — the claim that any domain an LLM understands is simulable is plausible but not stress-tested on niche industrial or regulatory domains. Bottom Line: A practitioner selecting or deploying AI agents for enterprise or professional work gains concretely useful findings here — particularly the E2 > E1 difficulty result and the occupational capability profiling — and the LES framework represents a durable contribution to the benchmark construction toolkit, despite the simulator dependence caveat that must be carefully managed in practice.
Recommended 2604.10866 PDF
#100 — Innovative (65)

SNEAK: Evaluating Strategic Communication and Information Leakage in Large Language Models

Adar Avsian, Larry Heck · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) for well-formalized signaling-game benchmark and decomposed Utility/Leakage. Rigor (65)—careful evaluator robustness, IAA reported, but single eval runs, best-of-3 human baseline inflates gap. Non-Obv (60). Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) limited by single-turn word-level scope. Impact (55)—dataset+scripts to be released, no model. Composite ~64-66.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SNEAK, a benchmark that measures whether LLMs can generate messages that signal knowledge of a secret word to an informed collaborator while remaining ambiguous to an uninformed adversary. - Why it's important: Multi-agent LLM deployments require selective information disclosure, but no existing benchmark measures whether models can control what they reveal — a gap that matters for privacy-preserving communication, tutoring, and strategic dialogue. - How it does it: Each benchmark instance gives a model a semantic category, candidate word set, and secret word; generated messages are scored by two simulated LLM agents (ally and chameleon) using constrained-decoding probabilities, yielding decomposed Utility and Leakage metrics combined multiplicatively into SoftScore. - The good: Human participants outperform all evaluated LLMs by up to 4× on SoftScore (59.22 vs. ≤34.87 for any model), providing a clear, credible upper-bound showing the capability gap is real and large. - The missing: The benchmark is single-turn and word-level only; it cannot measure whether models can sustain selective communication across multi-turn dialogue or under adversarial pressure, which is the practically relevant setting. - Read it or not: Worth reading for NLP researchers working on LLM evaluation, pragmatics, or multi-agent communication — it introduces a well-formalized problem and a reproducible evaluation framework that exposes a genuine and underappreciated capability gap. Problem & Motivation: Large language models deployed in multi-agent environments must often communicate asymmetrically — conveying enough information to collaborators who share context while withholding enough that uninformed observers cannot recover sensitive details. This trade-off arises concretely in pedagogical scaffolding (hint without revealing the answer), business consulting (demonstrate expertise without disclosing methodology), and privacy-sensitive AI systems. Existing LLM benchmarks evaluate what models know or can reason about, not what they choose to reveal. The absence of a controlled evaluation instrument for selective information sharing leaves this dimension entirely unmeasured. Key Idea: The core insight is to operationalize selective communication as a two-receiver signaling game with asymmetric information states, grounded formally in Crawford & Sobel's (1982) strategic information transmission model. By instantiating both the ally (informed collaborator) and the chameleon (uninformed adversary) as LLM agents that produce probability distributions over discrete answer choices via constrained decoding, the paper converts the inherently subjective notion of "informativeness vs. secrecy" into two objective, reproducible scalar scores. Methodology: Each benchmark instance is a triple (category c, word set W of 10–12 items, secret word w_s ∈ W). The message-generating model observes all three and produces a short natural-language message (≤5 words). Two evaluator agents then score it: the chameleon predicts P_cham(w | c, W, m) over candidate words (leakage = probability on w_s); the ally predicts P_ally(m | c, W, w_s, M) over a message set containing the generated message plus 5 WordNet-derived decoy messages (utility = probability on the generated message). Both are normalized relative to chance baselines and clipped to [0,1]. SoftScore = Utility × (1 − Leakage); BinaryScore is a strict binary success requiring the ally to rank the message first and the chameleon to not rank w_s first. The dataset draws 117 semantic categories (67 concrete, 50 abstract) from Banks & Connell (2023) category production norms, selecting the 12 most frequent exemplars per category, yielding 1,394 instances. Evaluation uses three independent evaluator models (Mixtral-8x7B, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct) averaged for robustness. Three inference-time scaling variants are tested per model: Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Self-Enhanced Test-Time Scaling (SETS), and a novel Recursive Message Refinement (RMR) that generates from the secret, then generates a second message conditioned on the first to induce indirection. Critical design decisions: (1) using constrained first-token logit decoding over labeled choices rather than free-form LLM-as-judge — well-justified to avoid subjectivity and positional biases; (2) WordNet-derived decoys derived from category-level attributes rather than secret-specific features — reasonable but evaluated via ablation; (3) taking the best-of-three human responses as the human baseline — this is an upper-bound approximation, not median human performance. Key Results: Across 1,394 instances, all modern LLMs achieve high Utility (63–99%) but substantially higher Leakage (50–75%) than humans (Utility=91.26, Leakage=33.81), producing SoftScores between 9.54 (Gemini 3.1 Pro) and 34.87 (Gemma-3-27B) vs. 59.22 for humans, and BinaryScores between 10.04 (Gemini 3.1 Pro) and 33.76 (Gemma-3-27B) vs. 54.70 for humans. RMR improves models modestly: Gemma-3-27B + RMR reaches BinaryScore=40.91. DeepSeek-V3.2 achieves near-ceiling Utility (98.52) but 75.42% Leakage, the highest leakage of any tested system. No LLM consistently attains the upper-left (high utility, low leakage) region of the utility–leakage Pareto frontier. Evaluator robustness is strong: Spearman correlations across evaluators range 0.9042–0.9680 for utility and 0.9547–0.9929 for leakage. Human–LLM evaluator agreement is 88.9% / 91.7% (ally, top-1 / strict majority) and 66.7% / 74.2% (chameleon). Abstract categories score higher (SoftScore 28.4 vs. 25.2) than concrete categories, and higher within-category semantic similarity correlates with lower utility (r = −0.43) and lower score (r = −0.28). Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The formalization of selective communication as a two-receiver signaling game with automated LLM evaluators is genuinely novel in the LLM evaluation literature, as is the decomposed Utility/Leakage measurement framework. The finding that LLMs exhibit a persistent utility–leakage coupling absent in humans is a new empirical result. The underlying components (party game inspiration, Gricean pragmatics, Rational Speech Acts) are well-established; the paper's contribution is in operationalizing them rigorously as a reproducible benchmark. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: decoy construction (WordNet vs. LLM-generated) changes scores by <6 utility and <1 leakage points, supporting robustness. Candidate and decoy set size sweeps (|W| ∈ {4,8,12,16,20}, |M| ∈ {1,3,5,7,9}) show predictable monotonic effects. Same-model evaluator bias is tested and found negligible (differences small and inconsistent in direction). Human study uses AMT with 95% approval rate, 5 independent annotators per evaluation item, and inter-annotator agreement reported (Fleiss' κ=0.73 ally, κ=0.27 chameleon). Error bars are not reported for LLM scores. The paper states dataset and evaluation scripts will be released upon publication but provides no URL. This is a preprint under review — results are not yet peer-reviewed. The human baseline reports best-of-three, not mean, which inflates the apparent human–LLM gap. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: The evaluation framework runs on instruction-tuned LLMs with standard constrained-decoding prompting; any practitioner with API access can replicate it on commercial hardware. - Efficient: Each benchmark instance requires one generation call plus six evaluation calls (three ally, three chameleon evaluators); at 1,394 instances, total compute is modest by current standards, though cost scales with the number of evaluator models used. - Effective: The benchmark measures a genuine communication capability gap — human vs. LLM differences are large and consistent across evaluators, categories, and scaling methods, suggesting it captures something real rather than an evaluation artifact. - Scalable: The benchmark is fixed at 1,394 instances across 117 categories; it has not been stress-tested at larger vocabulary sizes or richer linguistic contexts beyond short single-turn messages. The work delivers a dataset and evaluation scripts (upon publication), not a trained model or library. Strengths: - The decomposition into Utility and Leakage is principled, grounded in formal signaling theory, and produces interpretable, separable diagnostics — more informative than a single composite score. - The three-evaluator averaging with demonstrated robustness (Spearman ≥ 0.90) and explicit same-model bias testing is methodologically careful for an LLM-as-judge setup. - The human study validates both the task formulation and the automated evaluation framework through a non-trivial AMT experiment with inter-annotator agreement statistics. - The RMR inference-time variant (generate from secret, then generate a message from that) is a simple, novel prompting idea with measurable effect, providing a useful diagnostic on whether indirect reasoning helps. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note: (1) single-turn evaluation misses multi-turn dynamics, discussion, and deception; (2) LLM-based evaluators may not perfectly reflect human interpretation; (3) the dataset is derived from semantic category norms and may not represent real-world communication contexts with richer background knowledge. The authors frame SNEAK as an initial step. These are honest and direct admissions. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The human baseline is best-of-three, not mean — this systematically overstates the human advantage and should be reported alongside mean human performance for a fair comparison. No training signal is derived from SNEAK: the paper does not attempt to fine-tune any model toward better selective communication, leaving open whether the gap is fundamental or merely an instruction-following failure. The chameleon evaluator agreement with humans (r=0.39 for leakage) is only moderate, raising questions about whether the LLM chameleon accurately simulates human adversarial inference, especially for abstract categories. The benchmark uses prototypical, frequently-produced category members, meaning the hardest real-world cases (rare or ambiguous items) are underrepresented. Finally, no analysis addresses whether models that score higher on SNEAK generalize to related pragmatic tasks, leaving the construct validity of the benchmark partially unestablished. Bottom Line: Practitioners building multi-agent LLM systems that require privacy-aware or pedagogically-calibrated communication will find SNEAK directly relevant — it identifies a clear, reproducible capability gap and provides tooling to measure it, though the real-world significance of the single-turn word-game setting remains to be validated.
Recommended 2603.29846 PDF
#101 — Innovative (64)

Thinking Diffusion: Penalize and Guide Visual-Grounded Reasoning in Diffusion Multimodal Language Models

Keuntae Kim, Mingyu Kang, Yong Suk Choi · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (65) — PSP is a genuinely new inference-time mechanism specific to dMLLMs; VRG is CFG adapted, mechanically straightforward. Non-Obviousness (65) — the EAG/weak-grounding diagnosis is the real insight. Rigor (55) is limited by single-run greedy decoding, no error bars, no code release, preprint status, and only two CoT-fine-tuned dMLLMs evaluated. Completeness (60) misses non-CoT dMLLMs and open-ended generation tests. Resilience (60) — the 2-3% single-run gains are vulnerable to variance questions. Impact (70) — training-free, drop-in, 3× speedup is directly actionable, though no released artifact. Weighted ≈ 61, rounded to 64 reflecting the concrete efficiency win.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces two training-free inference-time methods — Position & Step Penalty (PSP) and Visual Reasoning Guidance (VRG) — that suppress premature answer generation and strengthen visual grounding in diffusion multimodal large language models (dMLLMs) during Chain-of-Thought reasoning. - Why it's important: dMLLMs offer faster parallel inference than autoregressive VLMs, but their CoT reasoning quality is degraded by failure modes that AR-designed methods cannot fix, limiting their practical viability for multimodal reasoning tasks. - How it does it: PSP applies a position- and timestep-weighted penalty to answer-position token confidence scores during early diffusion steps, forcing reasoning tokens to unmask first; VRG adapts classifier-free guidance to amplify the gap between visually-conditioned and unconditional logits at every timestep. - The good: PSP+VRG achieves 74.9% on MMBench at L=64/T=32, while the Low-conf baseline requires L=256/T=128 (4× more diffusion steps) to reach only 74.3% — simultaneously higher accuracy and over 3× faster inference. - The missing: All experiments cover only two dMLLMs (LaViDa ~7B, MMaDa 8B) that were already fine-tuned for CoT; whether the identified pathologies and fixes generalize to base dMLLMs or larger-scale models is untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and engineers working on diffusion language models or inference-time reasoning enhancement; practitioners using autoregressive VLMs can skip it. Problem & Motivation Diffusion multimodal large language models restore masked tokens in parallel across iterative timesteps, offering faster inference than autoregressive VLMs. When combined with Chain-of-Thought prompting, however, they exhibit two quantitatively documented failure modes. First, Early Answer Generation: dMLLMs commit to final answer tokens prematurely — in over 30% of cases with L=64 and T=32, the answer token appears before diffusion step 7/32, before sufficient reasoning has been established. Second, Weak Visual Grounding: visual prompt dependency measure (PDM) values for dMLLMs remain near-zero at early timesteps, unlike AR VLMs such as LLaVA-1.5 that show strong PDM from the first reasoning step. Existing CoT enhancement methods (DDCoT, CCoT, ICoT) were designed for sequential left-to-right generation and fail to improve dMLLMs because the parallel token-restoration mechanism decouples position from generation order. Key Idea The core insight is that the token confidence scores used by dMLLMs' remasking strategy — which determines which tokens to re-mask at each step — can be manipulated at inference time to enforce a desired reasoning order without retraining. Discounting confidence on answer-position tokens early in the diffusion process (PSP) delays their unmasking until reasoning tokens have been established; amplifying the log-ratio between visual-conditioned and unconditional output logits at every step (VRG) forces the model to attend to visual evidence throughout, not just at later timesteps. Methodology PSP operates on token confidence scores Cᵢⱼ at diffusion timestep i. Given relative diffusion progress τᵢ = i/K ∈ (0,1] and normalized sequence position rel(j) ∈ [0,1] (where 1.0 corresponds to the end of the response, where answer tokens appear), the penalty is: C̃ᵢⱼ = Cᵢⱼ · [1 − γ(1 − τᵢ)rel(j)]. At early timesteps (τᵢ small), tokens near the end of the sequence receive confidence scores close to zero, preventing their unmasking. As the diffusion proceeds (τᵢ → 1), the penalty vanishes. The penalty strength γ is fixed at 0.5. VRG extends Classifier-Free Guidance from image diffusion to the language modality. At each timestep, the model computes conditional logits (logits_c, given visual input v) and unconditional logits (logits_u, without v) in a second parallel forward pass. VRG combines them as: logits_vrg = logits_u + (s_vrg + 1) · (logits_c − logits_u), with s_vrg = 0.5. Token confidence scores are then computed via softmax of logits_vrg, and PSP's positional penalty is applied on top. The resulting penalized scores replace the base remasking strategy's confidence signal. Three design decisions warrant comment: (1) The positional weighting concentrating penalty on late-sequence tokens is well-motivated by the CoT format where rationale precedes answer — valid. (2) Fixing γ=0.5 and s_vrg=0.5 globally without per-dataset tuning is validated by a hyperparameter sensitivity sweep (Figure 5) showing robustness — adequate. (3) Greedy decoding throughout ensures strict reproducibility but may understate gains relative to sampling-based methods. Key Results On LaViDa (L=64/T=32): PSP+VRG reaches 48.4 on M³CoT (+2.6 over Low-conf 45.8), 74.9 on MMBench (+2.1), 72.8 on SQA-IMG (+1.8), 45.5 on V Bench (+2.6). On MMaDa: 36.4 on M³CoT (+2.7), 59.1 on MMBench (+3.1), 58.8 on SQA-IMG (+2.3), 37.7 on V Bench (+2.1). The critical efficiency comparison: LaViDa PSP+VRG at L=64/T=32 achieves 74.9 on MMBench, while Low-conf at L=256/T=128 achieves 74.3 — confirming the >3× speedup claim with higher accuracy. On MME, PSP+VRG reaches MME Total 570.0 vs Low-conf 545.0 and DDCoT 544.7. Inference time: PSP alone adds <1% overhead (4.03s vs 4.01s); VRG adds ~18% (4.73s); combined PSP+VRG is 4.65s — all well under the 4× step-reduction gain. Baselines include the strongest VLM CoT methods (DDCoT, CCoT) and three remasking strategies (Entropy, Margin, Low-conf); their consistent failure to outperform even Low-conf on dMLLMs is itself a substantive finding. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The quantitative characterization of Early Answer Generation and Weak Visual Grounding in dMLLMs using timestep-resolved PDM analysis is a genuinely new empirical contribution. PSP's position- and timestep-aware confidence penalization within the remasking loop has no direct precedent. VRG adapts CFG — a well-established image-generation technique — to the language reasoning setting in dMLLMs, which is novel in context though mechanically straightforward. The combination is not a trivial re-application of known ideas, as the interaction with discrete masked diffusion remasking is non-obvious. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations in Table 2 isolate PSP and VRG independently: PSP-only reaches 47.6 on M³CoT, VRG-only reaches 47.8, combined 48.4 vs baseline 45.8 — both components contribute. Table 3 shows PSP+VRG improves all three remasking strategies (Entropy, Margin, Low-conf), confirming plug-and-play generality. Figure 5 provides a hyperparameter sensitivity grid across γ ∈ {0, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7} and s_vrg ∈ {0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0}. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported — all results are single-run greedy decoding. Code is not released (not stated). This is an arXiv preprint (v1, April 7 2026) and has not been peer-reviewed. The PDM metric (Equation 1) is borrowed from prior peer-reviewed work [Favero et al., CVPR 2024], lending analytical credibility. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: PSP requires no additional forward passes and runs on standard GPU hardware used for dMLLM inference; VRG requires one additional unconditional forward pass per timestep, which is straightforward to implement. - Efficient: PSP adds negligible overhead (~0.5%); VRG adds ~18% per-step cost but allows 4× step reduction, yielding net speedup of >3×; combined PSP+VRG takes 4.65s vs 14.48s for Low-conf at 4× steps. - Effective: Consistent 2–3% absolute accuracy gains across 6 benchmarks, 2 models, and all tested L/T configurations; PDM measurements provide mechanistic corroboration that VRG actually increases visual grounding rather than just improving accuracy via a confound. - Scalable: Tested only on 7B–8B parameter models; no experiments at larger scale; training-free nature suggests straightforward scaling but this is unverified. The paper delivers no software artifact; PSP and VRG are described with sufficient algorithmic detail for re-implementation. Strengths - The timestep-resolved analysis of answer token generation distributions (Figure 3, showing >30% of samples generate answers before step 7/32) is a concrete, surprising finding that clearly motivates the work. - PSP is minimal and principled: a single multiplicative scalar applied to existing confidence scores, respecting both sequence position and diffusion progress, with only one hyperparameter. - The efficiency result — matching or exceeding 4× more diffusion steps while cutting inference cost by 3× — has direct practical value for anyone deploying dMLLMs. - Generalization across all three remasking strategies (Table 3) and two architecturally distinct dMLLMs (LaViDa, MMaDa) makes the contribution robust rather than model-specific. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note that setting s_vrg too large causes the model to over-rely on visual information and ignore text. They acknowledge in the background that diffusion CoT has not yet reached AR CoT reasoning performance levels. No explicit acknowledgment of the limited model-scale coverage or the absence of statistical significance testing. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Only two dMLLMs are evaluated, both fine-tuned specifically for CoT reasoning; it is unclear whether PSP+VRG helps or harms dMLLMs without CoT fine-tuning, or whether the pathologies exist in non-CoT dMLLMs at all. - No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals are reported; the 2–3% absolute accuracy gains, while consistent, are based on single greedy-decoding runs per configuration. - The PDM metric quantifies visual dependency as a proxy for visual grounding, but an increased PDM does not confirm that the additional visual attention targets the correct regions — the paper shows correlation between PDM increase and accuracy gain but not a causal mechanism. - All benchmarks are multiple-choice or structured perception tasks; PSP requires reliable answer token position detection, and its behavior on open-ended generation benchmarks (where the answer boundary is ambiguous) is not evaluated, despite a brief LLaVA-Bench COCO experiment that still uses a structured answer format. - VRG doubles forward passes per timestep; memory overhead is not analyzed, which matters when generation length L is large. Bottom Line A practitioner building dMLLM inference pipelines gains directly actionable knowledge — two drop-in, training-free modifications that recover 2–3% accuracy while cutting compute by 3× — making this paper worth reading for anyone in the diffusion language model space.
Citations 2604.05497 PDF
#102 — Innovative (64)

" Here, Let Me Help": An Empirical Study of User Interventions in Human–Web Agent Collaboration

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (78) drives positively — κ=0.85, appropriate statistics, strong within-subjects design. Novelty (65) — the functional taxonomy of interventions is a genuine empirical contribution but qualitative in nature. Non-Obviousness (65) — reason-type coupling is a non-obvious finding. Completeness (70) and Resilience (60) — authors directly confront limitations (single agent, young sample). Impact (50) — no released artifact; taxonomy is conceptual only. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*50 = 65.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 50
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a functional taxonomy of user interventions in human–web agent collaboration, derived from an in-lab study of 30 participants completing 12 realistic tasks across shopping, travel, and information-seeking domains. - Why it's important: Web agents still fail frequently in real-world conditions, and designing effective mixed-initiative systems requires understanding when, why, and how users intervene during agent execution — information that outcome-only success/failure metrics cannot provide. - How it does it: Qualitative axial coding of interaction logs, screen recordings, and user-submitted stop reasons (inter-rater κ = 0.85), mapped to a two-axis taxonomy of intervention reasons × intervention types, validated with Pearson correlations and two-way ANOVA. - The good: The finding that UI Misinterpretation is the dominant intervention trigger (127/309 instances, 53% in travel tasks), combined with the χ²(72) = 169.96 result that reason and type are non-independent, gives practitioners a principled, empirically grounded starting point for prioritizing agent improvements. - The missing: The study uses a single agent (browser-use + GPT-4o) on six pre-specified websites, so it cannot distinguish how much of the intervention pattern is attributable to GPT-4o's specific visual grounding failures versus structural properties of human–agent collaboration; no agent-architecture comparison is attempted. - Read it or not: Essential reading for HCI and AI researchers designing mixed-initiative web agent systems; practitioners building agent UX can mine the taxonomy directly, while ML-only agent researchers will find the framing peripheral. Problem & Motivation Web agents powered by large vision-language models can execute multi-step browser tasks from natural language instructions, but they remain error-prone in real-world deployments.
