Existing evaluation frameworks measure task success rates — binary outcome signals — which reveal that agents fail but not the moment-to-moment dynamics of why and how users step in. System designers building collaborative human–agent interfaces lack a principled account of intervention structure: whether users primarily re-prompt, directly manipulate the browser, or passively monitor, and which agent failure modes trigger which user responses. Without this, mixed-initiative system designs are ad hoc and potentially misaligned with actual user behavior during early adoption. Key Idea The central insight is that user interventions are not random corrections but structured responses to specific, classifiable agent failure modes, and that the type of intervention a user chooses is systematically predicted by the reason they intervene (χ²(72) = 169.96, p < .001, V = .26). Mapping this reason–type coupling gives designers a diagnostic tool: identify the failure mode, and you can anticipate the intervention strategy users will attempt and design affordances accordingly. Methodology The study employed a within-subjects, Latin-square counterbalanced design with 30 university-affiliated participants (ages 18–35, M = 24.5). Each participant completed 12 tasks drawn from 6 task designs across 3 domains (shopping, travel, information-seeking), instantiated on 6 live websites (Amazon, Coupang, Reddit, X, Korean Air, Booking). Tasks were structured as sequences of 4 activity subtypes — navigating, finding, gathering, transacting — derived from Broder's taxonomy of web queries. The agent backend was browser-use (Python library) with GPT-4o as the policy, running headless Playwright; a custom Gradio-based UI displayed agent actions in real time alongside a live browser view. Interaction logs captured mouse events, scrolls, keystrokes, clicks, and tab focus at fine granularity. Participants submitted stop reasons at intervention time; these textual reasons, combined with screen recordings, were coded by two independent coders using axial coding. A third coder resolved disagreements; κ = 0.85 was achieved. Post-task ratings used 7-point Likert scales for effectiveness (UMUX-Lite adapted), ease (SUS item), satisfaction (ISO 9241-11), and trust (Single Trust Question). Statistical analysis combined Pearson's r correlation across all measures and two-way ANOVA (task design × website) for usability perceptions. Critical design decisions: (1) fixed pre-specified websites to enable cross-participant comparison while preserving ecological validity — well-justified given the need to eliminate site-selection confounds; (2) in-lab versus in-the-wild to minimize device/network noise — justified by the need for comparable intervention data, though it constrains external validity; (3) single-agent architecture for all tasks — practical but limits generalizability. Key Results Across 360 task executions, 159 tasks (44.2%) completed without any intervention; 107 tasks (29.7%) required human intervention to complete (representing 40.2% of completed tasks); 41 tasks (11.4%) failed despite no intervention; and 53 tasks (14.7%) remained incomplete or failed even with intervention. A total of 309 explicit intervention instances were logged. The most frequent intervention reason was UI Misinterpretation (127/309 instances), with travel-domain tasks accounting for 104 of those (UI misinterpretation rate 53% in travel). Instruction Misinterpretation accounted for 43 instances and Incomplete Task Termination for 53 instances. For intervention types, Direct Substitution (n = 64), Guidance (n = 52), Instruction Restructuring (n = 61), and Tracking (n = 87, implicit) were most common. Subjective usability ratings were negatively correlated with behavioral intervention metrics: number of interventions vs. satisfaction r = −0.50, ease r = −0.53, trust r = −0.47, effectiveness r = −0.41 (all p < .001). Task design was the dominant driver of usability perceptions: effectiveness F(5,348) = 26.67, p < .001, η²p = .28; ease F(5,348) = 23.11, p < .001, η²p = .25; satisfaction F(5,348) = 17.07, p < .001, η²p = .20; trust F(5,348) = 20.62, p < .001, η²p = .23. Website effects were smaller but significant for some measures (satisfaction η²p = .14, effectiveness η²p = .22). Number of commands showed no reliable effect of task design (F(5,348) = 0.14, p = .87) but a website effect (F(3,348) = 3.43, p = .03) and significant interaction (F(25,348) = 17.25, p < .001, η²p = .55), indicating command behavior is shaped by site-specific UI structure more than task structure. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW FORMULATION. The functional taxonomy of intervention reasons and types is the primary contribution — no prior work had systematically characterized user interventions in human–web agent collaboration at this level of granularity. The distinction between explicit (agent-directed vs. interface-directed) and implicit intervention, and the empirical mapping of reason–type coupling, goes beyond existing benchmark-focused evaluations. It is a qualitative-empirical framing applied to a novel context rather than a new algorithmic method. Rigor & Reproducibility Inter-rater reliability is strong (κ = 0.85, verified by a third coder). Statistical analyses are appropriate: Pearson correlations for the usability/behavioral matrix, two-way ANOVA for task × website effects, chi-square for taxonomy independence tests. Error bars and significance thresholds are consistently reported. The study was IRB-approved. No code, dataset, or interaction logs are stated to be publicly released; the paper does not mention a repository URL. The within-subjects design with Latin-square counterbalancing is appropriate for controlling order effects. A methodological concern: GPT-4o's specific visual grounding limitations likely drive a substantial share of the UI Misinterpretation findings; no agent-baseline or agent-variation comparison isolates what is agent-specific versus task/domain-structural. Qualitative coding of implicit interventions relies on observable proxy signals (mouse movements, scrolling during agent execution) rather than think-aloud or eye-tracking, introducing potential miscategorization. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (human-subjects study with a deployed software system). - Practical: The study ran on standard dual-monitor desktop setups (1920×1080) using commercially available websites and a GPT-4o API-backed agent — no specialized hardware required. - Efficient: The browser-use + GPT-4o agent introduces per-step inference latency (not quantified), which itself drove a material fraction of interventions (Perceived Delay, n = 31); this cost is borne at inference time and is not optimized by the study. - Effective: The taxonomy demonstrably captures systematic patterns (reason–type non-independence confirmed statistically), and design implications are grounded in specific participant behaviors, not speculation. - Scalable: The study used 30 participants, 6 websites, and 3 domains — a reasonable CHI-scale study but not stress-tested across broader agent architectures, larger participant pools, higher-stakes domains (finance, healthcare), or more capable/different agents. No longitudinal or expert-user data are included. The paper delivers a conceptual taxonomy and empirical dataset; no software artifact or tool is released. Strengths - The reason–type coupling result (χ²(72) = 169.96, V = .26) is the study's most actionable finding: it means intervention type is not idiosyncratic but predictable from failure mode, giving system designers a concrete design target for each failure class. - The implicit intervention operationalization (tracking, cross-checking, forecasting) via mouse/scroll logs is methodologically creative, allowing continuous monitoring behavior to be coded without interrupting task flow. - Using live websites rather than sandboxed environments preserves ecological validity and surfaces real-world complexity (dynamic DOM, Cloudflare, site-specific UI patterns) that static benchmarks miss. - Task structure analysis (task design as dominant η² driver for usability perceptions, outweighing website effects for most measures) is a genuinely non-obvious finding that challenges the common assumption that UI site variance dominates user experience in web agent tasks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) young, university-recruited sample (ages 18–35) skewed toward novice web-agent users, limiting generalizability to expert users or older populations; (2) three domains (shopping, travel, information-seeking) do not cover higher-stakes areas like finance or healthcare; (3) single agent architecture (browser-use + GPT-4o) prevents attribution of findings to structural vs. agent-specific factors; (4) implicit intervention operationalization via mouse/scroll proxies is coarser than eye-tracking or think-aloud; (5) in-lab design constrains the full range of real-world collaborative contexts. The authors confront these limitations directly and substantively, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps The study does not compare intervention patterns across different agent backends (e.g., a weaker vs. stronger model, or a specialized vs. generalist agent), making it impossible to determine which findings are generalizable versus GPT-4o-specific. The 30-participant ceiling means cell counts in the reason × type cross-tabulation are small for several interaction types (e.g., Contextual Clarification n = 5, Cross-checking n = 6), potentially inflating Cramér's V. Implicit intervention counts rely on a broad proxy (any mouse event during agent execution = one implicit intervention instance), which conflates passive observation with active oversight — a user glancing at the screen is coded identically to a user deliberately cross-checking a specific UI element. No longitudinal component exists: early-adoption behaviors may differ substantially from experienced-user patterns, and the paper cannot speak to whether intervention frequency or type shifts with familiarity. Bottom Line A practitioner designing human–web agent collaboration interfaces will find the intervention taxonomy and reason–type coupling immediately usable for prioritizing where to invest in UI scaffolding, agent transparency cues, and task decomposition support — making this a high-value read for the HCI and agent-UX community specifically.
Citations
#103 — Innovative (64)

Pseudo-Unification: Entropy Probing Reveals Divergent Information Patterns in Unified Multimodal Models (36 ▲ )

Songlin Yang, Xianghao Kong, Anyi Rao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) — the RKHS Rényi-entropy probing of prompt-response coupling in UMMs is a genuine methodological contribution and the cross-architecture pseudo-unification finding is substantive. Non-Obviousness (75) — the modality-asymmetric encoding insight is non-trivial and falsifiable. Rigor (55) — synthetic validation of both probes is a strong credibility signal, but the kernel bandwidth σ is never specified or ablated, no error bars, no code release, scale/architecture confound only partially handled. Completeness (55) and Resilience (60) limited by the unmeasured causal link from entropy divergence to downstream task failure. Impact (50) — diagnostic-only, no artifact, no fix. Composite: 0.20*75 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*50 ≈ 61.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 55 Resilience 60 Impact 50
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces an information-theoretic probing framework using matrix-based Rényi entropy in reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHS) to diagnose "pseudo-unification" in unified multimodal models (UMMs), demonstrating that shared parameters do not produce shared information flow across text and image modalities. - Why it's important: UMMs are positioned as the central paradigm for joint text-image reasoning and generation, but whether they actually unify information processing internally — rather than merely co-locating modality-specific pipelines — has never been verified at the model-internal level, making architecture decisions effectively blind. - How it does it: It reformulates entropy and conditional entropy as non-parametric operations on Gram matrices of embedding sequences (Rényi entropy of kernel matrices), enabling comparison of high-dimensional, variable-length hidden-state sequences without density estimation. - The good: Across 10 architecturally diverse UMMs, 9 consistently exhibit higher conditional entropy for text generation than image generation, confirming pattern-split response as a structural, cross-architecture phenomenon rather than a model-specific artifact. - The missing: The probing framework is purely observational — it never links measured entropy divergence to downstream cross-modal task performance failures, so the causal claim that entropy divergence causes pseudo-unification is asserted but not measured. - Read it or not: Worth reading for multimodal systems researchers and UMM architects who want model-internal interpretability beyond benchmark scores; skippable for practitioners who only need deployment guidance, since no code is released and no fix is demonstrated. Problem & Motivation Unified multimodal models were designed to synergize LLM-like creative reasoning with fidelity-driven image synthesis by sharing a common parameter space and representation. In practice, this synergy fails: UMMs generate contextually rich, diverse text responses but produce deterministic, prompt-aligned images — the two modalities operate with fundamentally different generative logics despite shared weights. Existing probing methods either rely on task-specific benchmarks that cannot reveal why one model unifies better than another, or examine only prompt representations without tracking how inputs couple to outputs. The gap is the absence of any tool that inspects, at the model's internal level, whether text and image modalities share information flow rather than merely sharing a parameter matrix. Key Idea The core insight is that true unification requires consistency in information flow — not just shared parameters — and that this can be measured non-parametrically by treating embedding sequences as samples in kernel space: prompt entropy (the intrinsic isotropy and spread of representations) captures encoding quality, while the conditional entropy of responses given prompts captures whether generation is creative or fidelity-constrained. Both quantities are computable via matrix-based Rényi entropy on Gram matrices, bypassing the need for density estimation in the implicit, variable-length spaces of Transformer UMMs. Methodology The framework operates at two levels. At the prompt level, hidden-state embedding sequences are extracted layer-by-layer for text and image inputs; the α-order Rényi entropy (α = 1.01, approximating Shannon) is computed from the Gram matrix K_ij = exp(−‖z_i − z_j‖²/2σ²), normalized by trace: H_α(K) = (1/(1−α)) log(tr(A^α)). Layer-wise entropy trajectories reveal modality-specific compression and preservation patterns. At the response level, a conditional entropy proxy H̃(Z_r | Z_p) = H_α(K_joint) − H_α(K_pp) is computed from the block joint kernel matrix of prompt and response embeddings, measuring residual output uncertainty given the input. High values signal creative (LLM-like) generation; low values signal fidelity (diffusion-like) generation. Three design decisions: (1) choice of Rényi entropy with α = 1.01 — justified as numerically stable while approximating Shannon, validated on synthetic cluster experiments; (2) conditional entropy proxy via block kernel matrix — theoretically grounded as structural complexity of the joint space beyond the prompt, validated against synthetic alignment perturbations; (3) bandwidth parameter σ for the Gaussian kernel — critically, selection is not described or ablated. The framework is applied to 10 UMMs: BAGEL (14B), BAGEL-RecA (14B), Harmon (1.5B), Harmon-RecA (1.5B), JanusFlow (1.3B), Janus-Pro (7B), Janus-Pro (1B), Show-o (1.3B), Show-o2 (7B), OmniGen2 (7B), spanning all-in-one Transformer, Mixture-of-Transformers, and MLLM+Diffusion architectures. Data: T2I-CoReBench (1,080 text prompts with composition, deductive, inductive, abductive reasoning types) and MMBench (3,217 image prompts covering attribute, logical, relational, and perceptual tasks). Key Results Modality-Asymmetric Encoding: All 10 UMMs exhibit divergent layer-wise entropy trajectories for text versus image prompts. Large models (BAGEL 14B) show early "entropy collapse" in text representations (aggressive early-layer compression) while visual entropy plateaus at 5–6 vs. ~9–10 for images. Harmon shows asynchronous convergence — language encoded aggressively early, vision built up conservatively — converging to similar ranges in deep layers. Within each modality, entropy trajectories are nearly identical across prompt types (composition vs. deductive vs. abductive text; attribute vs. logical vs. relational images), confirming that encoding is governed by architecture and scale rather than semantic content. Table 1 quantifies: embedding entropy for text composition prompts ranges from ~1.05 (Show-o 1.3B) to ~6.84 (Janus-Pro 7B); image attribute entropy ranges from ~3.69 (Harmon 1.5B) to ~10.07 (BAGEL 14B). Pattern-Split Response: In 9 of 10 models, text-generation conditional entropy consistently exceeds image-generation conditional entropy across all layers, confirming a systematic creative-text vs. fidelity-image divergence. The lone exception is Harmon, where image conditional entropy exceeds text in early layers and the two converge by final layers — attributed to its masked autoencoder architecture that enforces contextual prediction for both modalities. OmniGen2 is excluded from response analysis as its image-response layer reuses the prompt-encoding layer, making layer-by-layer comparison undefined. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The method — non-parametric Rényi entropy probing of both prompt encoding and prompt-response coupling in implicit Transformer spaces — is a genuine contribution that extends information-theoretic probing from unimodal LLMs to joint multimodal systems. The empirical finding — that pseudo-unification is a systematic, architecture-independent failure across 9 of 10 tested UMMs, not a model-specific quirk — is substantive. Rényi entropy and kernel-based entropy estimation are established tools; the novelty lies in their joint application to cross-modal prompt-response dependencies in UMMs, which prior work had not operationalized. Rigor & Reproducibility Controlled synthetic validation is provided for both probing measures: entropy sensitivity is validated by showing monotonic increase with number of independent Gaussian clusters; the conditional entropy proxy is validated against synthetic alignment perturbations (identical, Gaussian-perturbed, and independent response sequences). These are credibility-positive practices absent from most probing papers. However, the bandwidth parameter σ for the Gaussian kernel — the most critical hyperparameter — is never described, discussed, or ablated; this is a significant reproducibility gap. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported on any entropy measurement. No code or data is released. The paper is an arXiv preprint (April 2026), not peer-reviewed. The motivating comparison between BAGEL (14B) and Harmon (1.5B) conflates architecture and scale; the paper partially addresses this by examining same-architecture variants at different scales but the Harmon exception interpretation relies on architecture alone without controlling for the order-of-magnitude scale difference. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: The framework requires only access to model hidden states, extractable on standard research GPUs without model modification. - Efficient: Gram matrix construction is O(n²) in sequence length, manageable for the embedding lengths used (hundreds to low thousands of tokens) but not described in terms of wall-clock cost or memory footprint. - Effective: The framework successfully distinguishes Harmon (genuinely unified) from 9 others (pseudo-unified) using internal signals not available from benchmark scores, validating diagnostic utility. - Scalable: Tested only on models up to 14B parameters; applicability to 70B+ frontier UMMs, video generation models, or streaming architectures is neither tested nor claimed. The work delivers no software artifact — it stops at paper form with no released implementation. Strengths - The RKHS reformulation of entropy for variable-length implicit embeddings is technically sound and fills a genuine methodological gap; prior probing work required explicit densities or fixed-length representations. - The consistency of pattern-split response across 9 architecturally diverse UMMs — spanning all-in-one Transformer, MoT, VQ-VAE, flow matching, and diffusion-based image generation — is a robust finding that is hard to dismiss as dataset or model artifact. - Synthetic validation of both probing measures (entropy and conditional entropy proxy) is provided and follows sound experimental design, lending credibility to the measurement apparatus. - The Harmon case study provides a concrete, falsifiable design hypothesis: aligning inductive biases to shared contextual prediction (not merely shared parameters or data) may be necessary for genuine unification. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note that adjusting prompt inputs to reshape measured entropy proved ineffective at changing learned model behavior, limiting actionability of the diagnostic. They frame the work as purely diagnostic and call for future work on pre-training objectives that enforce information consistency. The motivating figure's BAGEL vs. Harmon comparison is explicitly flagged as "unfair" due to scale differences — an unusual and credibility-positive disclosure. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The kernel bandwidth σ is the pivotal hyperparameter for Gaussian-kernel Rényi entropy: it controls the effective scale at which representational similarity is measured, and its omission from the paper makes replication non-trivial and results unverifiable. - No downstream correlation: the paper never validates that larger entropy divergence predicts worse performance on cross-modal tasks — the causal claim linking internal entropy divergence to behavioral pseudo-unification is supported by correlation, not intervention. - Model scale confound: Harmon (1.5B) is compared architecturally against models up to 14B; the claimed architectural explanation for Harmon's genuine unification may partly reflect scale effects not factored out. - The framework is purely observational with no intervention or training-time prescription — it diagnoses pseudo-unification but provides no empirical evidence for how to fix it beyond speculative future directions. - Only static, frozen models are probed; whether entropy trajectories change meaningfully during fine-tuning (RecA variants are included but differences are not prominently analyzed) is underdeveloped. Bottom Line A multimodal systems researcher or UMM architect gains a concrete, principled internal diagnostic that reveals what benchmark scores cannot — specifically, that current UMMs inherit modality-specific generative paradigms rather than sharing information flow — but must implement the framework from scratch (no code released) and cannot yet use it to guide training decisions backed by causal evidence.
Recommended 2604.10949 PDF
#104 — Innovative (64)

Memory Transfer Learning: How Memories are Transferred Across Domains in Coding Agents (24 ▲ )

Kangsan Kim, Minki Kang, Taeil Kim, Yanlai Yang, Mengye Ren, Sung Ju Hwang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (60) is the main drag — closed evaluation (memory pool and evaluation drawn from same six benchmarks), single-run Pass@3 without error bars on main table, judge not ablated, Pass@1 gains are notably smaller than Pass@3. Novelty (65) is a new empirical finding (abstraction governs cross-domain transferability) rather than new architecture. Non-Obviousness (72) — the abstraction-transferability result with converging t-SNE and formal sketch is non-trivial. Completeness (70) and Resilience (60) limited by Pass@1/Pass@3 discrepancy and closed task family. Impact (55) — no released library/memory pool announced. Weighted ≈ 64.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Memory Transfer Learning (MTL), a framework that improves coding agent performance by retrieving and applying memories generated from heterogeneous coding task domains rather than restricting memory to the same benchmark domain. - Why it's important: Real-world coding agents must handle diverse programming tasks (software engineering, ML research, competitive coding, scientific replication), yet existing self-evolving agent systems silently discard cross-domain experience that shares substantial infrastructure and procedural overlap. - How it does it: Offline inference generates memories from all benchmarks except the target in four abstraction formats (Trajectory, Workflow, Summary, Insight); at inference time, top-N memories are retrieved via cosine similarity of text embeddings and injected into the agent's system prompt. - The good: Insight-format MTL using only 431 memories outperforms AgentKB (5,899 memories) by +1.7% average Pass@3 across three benchmarks, demonstrating that compact, high-abstraction memories dominate quantity. - The missing: The memory pool is constructed offline from benchmark inference runs on the same set of six benchmarks used for evaluation, raising concern that the memory content is entangled with the evaluation distribution; no truly held-out benchmark tests generalization to a novel task family. - Read it or not: Researchers building or evaluating self-evolving coding agents should read this — it provides concrete, quantified design principles for cross-domain memory; practitioners deploying single-domain agents can likely skim Section 4.3 only. Problem & Motivation Memory-augmented self-evolving agents reuse past inference experience to improve future performance without additional training. The dominant assumption in the field is that memory should be restricted to the same task domain — an agent trained on SWE-Bench retrieves only SWE-Bench memories. This fails to exploit the substantial shared substrate across real-world coding tasks: Linux shell environments, Python package APIs, debugging workflows, and file-system conventions appear across repository-level bug fixing, ML model development, competitive programming, and scientific replication. The gap is that no prior work systematically investigated whether memories from heterogeneous domains transfer positively, under what conditions they help versus hurt, and what memory representation properties govern transferability. Key Idea The central insight is that high-level, abstract memory representations (Insights: task-agnostic behavioral guidelines) carry domain-invariant meta-knowledge — procedural discipline like "write a smoke test before submitting" — that transfers across coding domains, while low-level representations (Trajectories: raw command logs) carry domain-specific noise that causes negative transfer by anchoring agents to incompatible implementation details. Methodology The pipeline has two phases. Memory generation (offline): run a coding agent (mini-SWE-agent backbone, GPT-5-mini judge) across all benchmarks, collect inference trajectories, and distill each trajectory into one of four memory formats via LLM prompting — Trajectory (full command log with observations), Workflow (extracted reusable action sequence), Summary (task+experience narrative), or Insight (generalizable behavioral rule). Success and failure trajectories each get format-specific prompts. The memory pool for benchmark B_i excludes all memories derived from B_i itself. Memory retrieval (inference): embed the current task description using text-embedding-3-small, retrieve top-3 memories by cosine similarity from the pool, and prepend them to the agent system prompt. The evaluation metric is Pass@3 across 100 randomly sampled tasks per benchmark. No fine-tuning or gradient updates are involved — this is purely in-context transfer. Three critical design decisions: (1) using an LLM judge to label trajectory success/failure before memory generation (borrowed from prior work, justified but not ablated here); (2) fixed top-3 retrieval by embedding similarity (shown to outperform LLM reranking and adaptive rewriting in Table 7, counterintuitively); (3) memory format as the primary independent variable enabling the abstraction-transferability analysis. Key Results On six benchmarks (LiveCodeBenchv6, Aider-Polyglot, SWEBench-Verified, TerminalBench2, ReplicationBench, MLGym-Bench) with GPT-5-mini, MTL with Insight memories achieves average Pass@3 of 0.560 vs. 0.523 zero-shot baseline (+3.7%). Gains are uneven: MLGym-Bench +8.3%, TerminalBench2 +4.5%, SWEBench-Verified +4.0%, LiveCodeBenchv6 +2.0%; Aider-Polyglot shows 0% gain. Against self-evolving baselines on three benchmarks: ReasoningBank 0.601, AgentKB 0.613, MTL 0.630 (Pass@3). MTL uses 431 memories vs. AgentKB's 5,899. Cross-model transfer (Table 6, Pass@1): GPT-5-mini memories applied to GPT-5-mini score 0.543 avg vs. 0.515 zero-shot; GPT-5-mini memories applied to DeepSeek V3.2 score 0.501 vs. 0.486, confirming cross-model utility at lower magnitude. Task-agnostic Insights outperform task-specific Insights by +1.1% average (Table 4), isolating abstraction as the causal factor. Memory pool scaling: performance increases monotonically from 1/4 to full pool size; adding more source domains (up to 9) improves results, consistent with diversity hypothesis. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD. The finding that cross-domain memory transfer is net positive and that abstraction level predicts transferability is genuinely novel; the MTL framework itself is a straightforward extension of existing memory-augmented agent architectures (no new model architecture, loss function, or training procedure), applied to a previously unstudied cross-domain setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover memory format (Table 1), abstraction within format (Table 4), pool size (Figure 6 left), number of source domains (Figure 6 right), retrieval strategy (Table 7), and cross-model transfer (Table 6). Pass@3 with three runs is used for self-evolving comparisons (Table 2); single-run Pass@3 is used for the main Table 1 results, with no error bars or significance tests reported there, which limits confidence in small deltas (e.g., +1.0% to +2.0%). The formal model in Appendix C (Proposition 1) proves the abstraction-transfer tradeoff under two stated assumptions (bounded embedding norm, domain-specific component as noise for unseen tasks) but the proof is a one-proposition sketch, not a full theorem with tight bounds. Code and the project page are referenced (https://memorytransfer.github.io/) but no explicit statement about public code release or trained model availability is made in the main paper. The memory pool is constructed from the same six benchmarks used in evaluation — memories from benchmark B_i are excluded when evaluating on B_i, but the overall pool remains in-distribution with respect to the task family taxonomy, which is a mild form of benchmark leakage. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The full system runs on GPT-5-mini via API with OpenAI text-embedding-3-small for retrieval — commercially available and affordable at the evaluated scale (100 tasks × 6 benchmarks). - Efficient: Memory generation is an offline one-time cost; inference adds only an embedding lookup and three retrieved memory items to the system prompt, with negligible marginal compute overhead per query. - Effective: Pass@3 improvements are consistent across three independent models (GPT-5-mini, DeepSeek V3.2, Qwen3-Coder) and directionally positive on all but one benchmark (Aider-Polyglot), supporting the claim that cross-domain transfer is genuinely beneficial, though gains at Pass@1 are smaller (Table 8, +1.9% average for GPT-5-mini). - Scalable: Performance scales monotonically with pool size and domain count within the tested range; the paper does not test pools beyond ~431–5,899 memories or beyond 9 source domains, so behavior at 10× scale is a claim, not a measurement. The work delivers a methodology and empirical findings; no software library or released memory pool is announced. Strengths - The core empirical finding — that abstraction level, not memory format category, drives transfer effectiveness — is cleanly isolated through the task-specific vs. task-agnostic Insight comparison (Table 4) and t-SNE embedding analysis (Figure 4), providing converging evidence from two independent methodologies. - The negative transfer analysis (Section 4.4.1–4.4.2) is unusually candid for a self-reporting paper: three failure modes are named, case-studied with concrete trajectories, and connected to actionable mitigation directions. - The formal model (Appendix C) provides a first-principles grounding for the empirical abstraction-transfer correlation, elevating the contribution beyond pure empirical observation. - Demonstrating that 431 cross-domain memories outperform 5,899 mixed-domain memories (AgentKB) reframes the design space away from scale toward curation and abstraction. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) negative transfer remains a significant failure mode, with misapplied language-specific patterns and semantic distortion identified as primary causes; (2) static embedding-based retrieval does not generalize well to dynamic multi-step agentic settings, motivating future work on domain routing and step-wise retrieval; (3) cross-model transfer underperforms self-generated memories, suggesting model-specific biases in memory content. These are confronted directly and used to motivate concrete future directions, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The evaluation distribution is closed: all six benchmarks serve as both memory sources and evaluation targets (with leave-one-out exclusion). No truly out-of-distribution benchmark (e.g., a task family not seen during memory generation) is used to test whether MTL generalizes beyond its construction set. - Pass@3 as the primary metric is favorable to memory-augmented systems (more attempts benefit more from diverse memory hints); Pass@1 results in Appendix A show smaller gains (+1.9% for GPT-5-mini MTL-I vs. +3.7% for Pass@3), and this discrepancy is not discussed in the main text. - The LLM judge that labels trajectories as success/failure before memory generation is credited to prior work but not ablated — if the judge mislabels, negative memories could contaminate the pool; the sensitivity of MTL to judge accuracy is unstated. - All experiments use mini-SWE-agent as the backbone agent; no ablation tests whether the observed patterns hold for agents with substantially different reasoning architectures (e.g., longer-horizon planners, tool-use-heavy agents). Bottom Line Practitioners designing memory systems for coding agents gain actionable, well-evidenced design principles from this work — particularly the abstraction-transferability tradeoff — but should treat the absolute performance numbers with caution given the closed evaluation set and absence of significance testing on small deltas.
Recommended 2604.14004 PDF
#105 — Innovative (64)

Dive into Claude Code: The Design Space of Today's and Future AI Agent Systems (6 ▲ )

Jiacheng Liu, Xiaohan Zhao, Xinyi Shang, Zhiqiang Shen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Systems profile. Non-Obviousness (75) and Rigor (75) carry the score — explicit three-tier evidence scheme, file- and function-level citations, and adversarial honesty about CVEs and defense-in-depth failures are genuinely uncommon for architecture writing. Novelty (60) is moderate: first source-grounded characterization of Claude Code is a real documentation contribution but the underlying patterns are well-known. Completeness (70) and Resilience (70) benefit from clearly scoped limitations (feature-flag variability, static snapshot). Impact (50) reflects an analysis document plus repo rather than a deployable artifact. Weighted: 0.15*60+0.15*75+0.10*75+0.15*70+0.15*70+0.30*50 = 64.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 50
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a source-grounded architectural analysis of Claude Code (TypeScript v2.1.88, ~512K lines) that maps five human values through thirteen design principles to specific implementation decisions across seven subsystems, and contrasts the resulting design with OpenClaw across six dimensions to surface generalizable design-space lessons. - Why it's important: Practitioners building production AI agents face recurring architectural decisions — where reasoning lives, how safety is enforced, how context is managed, how subagents are orchestrated — without access to principled documentation of how deployed systems resolve these tensions; most agent literature either benchmarks task performance or describes frameworks at API level. - How it does it: Source-level reverse engineering of the publicly extractable npm package, tracing each subsystem's code paths to specific functions and files, supplemented by three-tier evidence: Tier A (official Anthropic documentation), Tier B (code-verified claims), and Tier C (reconstructed community analysis). - The good: Claims are tied to specific TypeScript files and functions (query.ts, permissions.ts, compact.ts, AgentTool.tsx), making the analysis reproducible and falsifiable against the published package — uncommon rigor for system architecture writing. - The missing: The analysis cannot confirm which feature flags are active in production builds, cannot measure runtime behavior or task performance, and is a static snapshot of one version; the gap between analyzed code and what deployed users experience is acknowledged but unbounded. - Read it or not: Essential for practitioners designing production AI coding agents who want a source-verified reference for how one leading system answers recurring questions about safety layering, context compaction, and subagent delegation. Problem & Motivation Production AI coding agents must resolve a recurring set of architectural questions that have no single correct answer: where reasoning lives relative to the harness, what default safety posture to adopt, how to manage a bounded context window across long tasks, how to delegate work to subagents, and how sessions persist across boundaries. These questions recur across any agent system, but existing literature either benchmarks task performance (SWE-Bench, HumanEval) or describes framework APIs (LangGraph, AutoGen) without examining how deployed systems actually implement them. Anthropic publishes user-facing Claude Code documentation but not architectural descriptions; community analysis has been shallow. The specific gap: agent builders lack a source-grounded reference that connects design philosophy to implementation. Key Idea The core architectural insight is that Claude Code consistently privileges model autonomy within a rich deterministic harness: an estimated 1.6% of the codebase constitutes AI decision logic, while 98.4% is operational infrastructure — permission gates, context compaction, tool routing, recovery logic, session persistence. This ratio is not accidental; it operationalizes the design principle that deterministic infrastructure creates the conditions under which a capable model can decide well, rather than scaffolding-side reasoning (explicit planners, state graphs) that constrains model choices. The corollary is that as models improve, the harness becomes the primary differentiator for reliability. Methodology The study is a software architecture case study applied to a production LLM-based agent. The evidence base is the Claude Code TypeScript package (v2.1.88, ~1,884 files, ~512K lines) obtained via public npm package extraction. The analytic procedure has three phases. First, a five-value framework (human decision authority, safety/security/privacy, reliable execution, capability amplification, contextual adaptability) is derived from official Anthropic documentation and empirical usage studies, then traced to thirteen design principles (Table 1) and finally to specific source files and functions. Second, six recurring design questions (where reasoning lives, how many execution engines, safety posture, binding resource constraint, extension surface structure, session persistence model) are answered by reference to code evidence. Third, the same framework is applied to OpenClaw — an independent open-source multi-channel personal assistant — across six dimensions (system scope, trust model, agent runtime, extension architecture, memory/context, multi-agent routing) to test generalizability. Critical design choices analyzed include: deny-first rule evaluation with seven permission modes and an ML-based auto-classifier (yoloClassifier.ts); a five-layer graduated compaction pipeline (budget reduction → snip → microcompact → context collapse → auto-compact) executing before every model call; isolated subagent boundaries with summary-only return to the parent; and append-only JSONL session transcripts. Claims are explicitly tiered: Tier B (code-verified) is treated as strongest evidence; Tier A (product documentation) establishes intent; Tier C (reconstructed) is hedged. Key Results No new benchmarks are run. The paper's findings are structural and analytical, grounded in code paths and cited third-party empirical work. Key cited measurements that anchor the analysis: users approve ~93% of permission prompts (Hughes, 2026), motivating the classifier-plus-sandboxing architecture over per-action approval; sandboxing reduced permission prompt frequency by ~84% (Dworken and Weller-Davies, 2025); auto-approve rates increase from ~20% at <50 sessions to ~40% at 750 sessions (McCain et al., 2026), documenting habituated trust trajectories that the architecture must account for autonomously. Long-term sustainability concerns are grounded in: Cursor adoption across 807 repositories showing code complexity +40.7% (p<0.001) with velocity gains dissipating by month three (He et al., 2025); a 304,000-commit audit finding ~25% of AI-introduced security issues persisting to latest revision (Liu et al., 2026). Security findings: CVE-2025-59536 (CVSS 8.7) and CVE-2026-21852 (CVSS 5.3) are pre-trust initialization vulnerabilities where extension loading (hooks, MCP servers) runs before the interactive trust dialog, creating a structurally privileged window outside the deny-first pipeline. Adversa.ai (2026) documented that commands with more than 50 subcommands fall back to a generic approval prompt, bypassing per-subcommand deny-rule checks because parsing latency caused UI freezes — demonstrating that the defense-in-depth architecture fails when layers share performance constraints. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (documentation artifact). The analysis is source-grounded architectural documentation of a production system at implementation depth — genuinely novel because no prior work has characterized Claude Code's internal structure at this level. The five-values-to-thirteen-principles framework is a modest conceptual contribution; the recursive design-question methodology and OpenClaw comparison are secondary. What is standard: the underlying architectural patterns (ReAct loop, RBAC permission models, layered compaction) are well-known; the contribution is their first principled documentation in a specific production system. Rigor & Reproducibility Evidence tiering is explicit and consistently applied — a credible practice that lets readers calibrate confidence by claim type. The source corpus (npm package v2.1.88) is publicly obtainable; the analysis GitHub repo is provided (https://github.com/VILA-Lab/Dive-into-Claude-Code). No ablations apply to an architectural study. Key reproducibility concerns: feature flags (TRANSCRIPT_CLASSIFIER, CONTEXT_COLLAPSE, CACHED_MICROCOMPACT) create build-time variability — in a build where TRANSCRIPT_CLASSIFIER is false, the entire auto-mode classifier is absent; different builds may be functionally different applications. The memoized context assembly functions (getSystemContext, getUserContext using lodash memoize) mean dynamic changes during a conversation may not be reflected immediately; lazy-loaded path-scoped rules provide only a partial counter-mechanism. The 1.6% decision-logic estimate is community-derived from extracted source, not an independently verified measurement, yet the paper uses it as a structurally significant architectural claim. The paper is an arXiv preprint and has not undergone peer review. Deployability Profile: SYSTEMS — architectural analysis of a deployed tool, evaluated by structural properties of the implementation, not experimental deployment. - Practical: Claude Code v2.1.88 runs on developer machines as a CLI; the analysis covers this deployed artifact without additional infrastructure requirements. - Efficient: The paper does not measure runtime cost; it characterizes token economics as a cross-cutting constraint and describes the five-layer compaction pipeline's cost-benefit ordering (cheaper layers first, model-generated summary as last resort), but no latency or throughput numbers are reported. - Effective: The analysis documents design choices but does not experimentally measure whether they achieve stated goals (safety, reliability, capability amplification); effectiveness claims rest on cited third-party empirical work targeting adjacent systems (Cursor, Copilot), not Claude Code directly. - Scalable: Horizon scaling is explicitly identified as an open question (Section 12.4); whether the harness's context management and session persistence remain sufficient as tasks extend from turns to multi-session programs is left unresolved and framed as "a directly measurable empirical question that source-level analysis cannot resolve." The paper delivers an analysis document and repository, not a software artifact. Strengths - Source-level falsifiability: Claims cite specific TypeScript files and functions with line-level precision (query.ts:365–453, sessionStorage.ts:247), making findings checkable against the published package — a standard virtually no prior agent architecture paper meets. - Adversarial honesty: The paper documents active CVEs, the Adversa.ai finding that >50-subcommand commands silently degrade safety coverage, and that the defense-in-depth independence assumption is structurally violated when layers share performance constraints — self-critical analysis uncommon in system documentation. - Value-tension grounding: Table 4 maps each tension (Authority×Safety, Safety×Capability, Adaptability×Safety) to concrete empirical evidence including approval fatigue rates, code-complexity measurements, and EEG neural-connectivity studies, giving the philosophical framework empirical weight. - External calibration via contrast: The OpenClaw comparison demonstrates that the architectural questions are real and recurring by showing they produce structurally inverted answers (per-action safety evaluation vs. perimeter identity control; agent-loop as center vs. gateway as center) under a different deployment context, elevating the analysis from description to design science. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors confront four epistemological constraints directly in Appendix B.3: (1) the analysis is a static snapshot of v2.1.88, and feature flags create build-time variability; (2) source code reveals structure and control flow but cannot confirm design intent, enabled production flags, runtime prevalence, or unshipped behavior; (3) findings describe one design point, not the full coding-agent design space; (4) the OpenClaw analysis reflects a specific development state and may not represent current capabilities. These are stated plainly without minimization — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The gap between analyzed code and deployed user experience is acknowledged but not bounded: the paper cannot state which feature flags are active for which user tiers in production, making it impossible to know how many of the analyzed subsystems (auto-mode classifier, context collapse, reactive compaction) are actually engaged for a typical user. - The long-term human capability lens (Section 2.4, 12.6) is the paper's most consequential evaluative frame — identifying that Claude Code provides limited mechanisms explicitly supporting long-term human understanding, codebase coherence, or developer pipeline health — but the paper declines to propose even candidate architectural remedies, leaving the most important open question without a research agenda. - Security coverage of the 27 hook event types and MCP server attack surface is necessarily incomplete from static analysis; the paper documents two CVE classes but cannot enumerate the combinatorial interaction surface between hooks, path-scoped rules, and the pre-trust initialization window. - The 1.6% decision-logic ratio is used as a signature architectural claim but its derivation (community source analysis) is not independently verified; the number could vary substantially depending on what counts as "decision logic" vs. infrastructure in a system where the model's outputs are parsed by harness code. Bottom Line Practitioners designing production AI coding agents — particularly those architecting safety systems, context compaction pipelines, or subagent orchestration — will find this the most source-grounded architectural reference currently available for a leading deployed system, and should read it as a concrete design-space map despite its inherent limitations as a static reverse-engineering snapshot of a rapidly evolving codebase.
Recommended 2604.14228 PDF
#106 — Innovative (64)

Lineage-based Reuse and Memory Management for Multi-backend ML Systems

sigmodrecord.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Impact (75) drives the score: integrated into Apache SystemDS with strong 2×–9.6× end-to-end speedups and a real 900MB-vs-30GB cache efficiency result. Novelty (60) — unified cross-backend lineage cache is a genuine architectural integration but individual components extend LIMA/HELIX/VISTA. Rigor (55) weakened by all three pipelines coming from the authors' own BIFOLD/SystemDS ecosystem, no error bars on timing, MAXPARALLELIZE not independently ablated, and the paper being an acknowledged shortened EDBT 2025 revision. Non-Obviousness (55), Completeness (60), and Resilience (55) are middling — TensorFlow/PyTorch applicability is claimed but never demonstrated, and failure recovery under node loss is not benchmarked. Weighted: 0.15·60 + 0.15·55 + 0.10·55 + 0.15·60 + 0.15·55 + 0.30·75 = 62.5 ≈ 63, rounded to 64 given the artifact release.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 75
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MEMPHIS, a holistic framework for multi-backend reuse and memory management in ML systems via a hierarchical lineage cache that spans CPU, GPU, and distributed (Spark) backends, integrated into Apache SystemDS. - Why it's important: Modern ML pipelines spanning heterogeneous backends exhibit high computational redundancy from hyper-parameter search, incremental modifications, and repeated forward passes, but existing reuse frameworks are backend-specific and cannot eliminate fine-grained cross-backend redundancy, wasting substantial compute and memory. - How it does it: MEMPHIS builds instruction-level lineage DAGs (keyed by opcode and input hashes) that map to backend-local cached objects, with tailored eviction policies for Spark's lazy evaluation and GPU pointer recycling, plus compiler rewrites for asynchronous prefetch, delayed caching, and workload-aware checkpoint placement. - The good: On end-to-end hyper-parameter tuning of cross-validated linear regression (HCV, up to 100GB inputs), MEMPHIS achieves up to 9.6x speedup over vanilla SystemDS and substantially outperforms LIMA and HELIX, with the 900MB MEMPHIS lineage cache achieving the same performance as a 30GB SystemDS cache. - The missing: All three end-to-end pipelines are evaluated within the authors' own SystemDS/BIFOLD ecosystem; no experiment verifies generalization to PyTorch-native, TensorFlow, or JAX workloads despite claims of broad applicability. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems researchers and infrastructure engineers who work on multi-backend or distributed ML pipelines; the full EDBT 2025 paper [69] should be consulted for complete experimental depth since this is an acknowledged abbreviated revision. Problem & Motivation ML pipelines increasingly span multiple backends — local CPU, GPU, distributed Spark, FPGAs — and exhibit high computational redundancy arising from incremental pipeline modifications, hyper-parameter search sweeps, data augmentation, and repeated forward passes in inference and training. This redundancy is both coarse-grained (repeated top-level operator chains) and fine-grained (repeated matrix operations inside primitives). Prior reuse frameworks address only one dimension: LIMA handles fine-grained CPU reuse via lineage tracing; HELIX handles coarse-grained Spark reuse; VISTA handles GPU-local feature reuse for transfer learning; Clipper handles prediction caching. None integrates all backends under a unified reuse abstraction. Meanwhile, GPU memory management for DNN workloads requires special handling of pointer recycling and defragmentation, and Spark's lazy evaluation creates dangling RDD references and broadcast variables that consume driver memory and interfere with cache eviction. The gap is a principled, application-agnostic, multi-backend framework that integrates lineage tracing, cache management, and compiler optimizations holistically. Key Idea The core insight is that a single hierarchical lineage cache — keyed by fine-grained instruction-level lineage DAGs (hashing opcodes plus data inputs with sub-DAG memoization) — can serve as a unified reuse abstraction across heterogeneous backends, delegating actual object storage and eviction to backend-specific policies (RDD wrappers for Spark, GPU pointer wrappers for CUDA) while presenting a common TRACE/REUSE/PUT/MAKE_SPACE API to the compiler and runtime. Methodology MEMPHIS has three layers. (1) Fine-grained lineage tracing: before each linear algebra instruction, TRACE incrementally builds a lineage DAG stored in a LineageMap hash map mapping variable names to lineage items (opcode + input items). Equality probes use non-recursive sub-DAG memoization with early abort. On cache hit, REUSE returns the cached object and skips execution; on miss, PUT inserts output. SERIALIZE/DESERIALIZE handle log-based persistence; RECOMPUTE replays full lineage to regenerate results. A compaction step replaces LineageMap entries with shared sub-DAG references to reduce memory footprint. (2) Hierarchical lineage cache: backend-local cached objects are wrapped as cache entries holding the actual data pointer plus metadata (compute costs, access counts, materialization status). For Spark, the cache probes before triggering job execution; on hit with an unmaterialized RDD, persist() is invoked lazily; for broadcast variables and dangling RDDs, MAKE_SPACE recursively traverses child RDDs and broadcasts, reclaiming stale objects via getRDDStorageInfo. Eviction for Spark uses a cost-size scoring function: argmin_{o∈Q} (r_h(o) + r_m(o) + r_j(o)) · c(o)/s(o) balancing hit/miss/job reuse statistics, compute cost, and estimated size. GPU eviction uses argmin_{o∈Q} T_n(o) + 1/h(o) + c(o) combining normalized last-access time, lineage DAG height (favoring reuse of input pipelines), and compute cost (recycling cheap ReLU before expensive Conv2d). GPU memory is organized in Live/Free pointer lists; recycling reuses Free-list entries of matching size to avoid cudaMalloc/cudaFree overhead; full defragmentation via device-to-host eviction is triggered only when allocation fails after exhausting free pointers. (3) Compiler and runtime integration: four rewrites are introduced — prefetch operators for asynchronous remote Spark/GPU job launch (returning futures), asynchronous broadcast operators for local-to-remote data transfer, evict instructions injected near detected loop patterns for GPU cleanup, and workload-aware RDD checkpoint placements at basic block boundaries and after shared iterative operators. A MAXPARALLELIZE operator ordering algorithm linearizes DAGs depth-first with Spark/GPU operator chains sorted by length (longer first) to maximize concurrent execution. A delay factor n defers materialization until the n-th cache hit, with automatic tuning based on execution frequency and loop-dependence analysis. Key Results Experiments run on an 8+1 node cluster (NVIDIA A40 GPUs 48GB, Hadoop 3.3, Spark 3.5, CUDA 10.2, 38GB Spark driver memory, 230GB executor memory). Reuse overhead: for inputs below 8MB, tracing adds 1.3x overhead and probing adds 2x with no benefit; for 8MB+ inputs, both overheads become negligible and reuse yields 1.1x–3x speedups. Cache efficiency: a 900MB MEMPHIS cache matches a 30GB SystemDS cache on a 2–10GB input workload with 40% reuse and 1M instructions. HCV (cross-validated linear regression, 5–100GB, 2.5K regularization parameters): MEMPHIS achieves up to 9.6x over Base, 2x over Base-A (async-only), and substantially outperforms LIMA and HELIX at 25GB+ where LIMA places intermediates in Spark and MEMPHIS reuses both local and distributed intermediates; HELIX achieves only local reuse. HBAND (Hyperband model selection, L2SVM + multi-class logistic regression, 5–20GB, 1.5K columns): MEMPHIS yields 2.6x–2.5x over Base, ~40% over HELIX and LIMA, reusing 4K RDDs, 2K Spark actions, 40K local matrices via delayed RDD caching. TLVIS (transfer learning, AlexNet/VGG16/ResNet18, CIFAR-10 and ImageNet 10K images): MEMPHIS achieves 2x on CIFAR-10 and 3x on ImageNet by reusing frozen feature extractions and recycling 30K–17.5K GPU pointers; VISTA runs out of memory on ImageNet while MEMPHIS succeeds. PyTorch with torch.compile suffers OOM after each model requiring manual empty_cache(). Baselines (LIMA 2021, HELIX 2021, CoorDL 2021, VISTA 2020, Clipper 2017, PyTorch 2019) are reasonably current for the domain. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The unified hierarchical lineage cache abstraction spanning fundamentally different execution models (eager CPU, lazy distributed Spark, asynchronous GPU) is a genuine architectural contribution; no prior work integrates all three backends under a single lineage-keyed cache. Individual components — fine-grained lineage tracing (from LIMA), cost-based Spark eviction (extending Cost&Size), GPU memory pools (extending standard CUDA practices) — are extensions of known ideas; novelty lies in their holistic integration with compiler optimizations that exploit cross-backend reuse potential jointly. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present: Base (no reuse), Base-A (async operators only, no fine-grained reuse), and MPH-NA (MEMPHIS without async) configurations isolate the contribution of asynchronous execution versus fine-grained reuse. No error bars or significance tests are reported for timing experiments — standard omission in systems papers but a gap when 9.6x is the headline claim. MEMPHIS is integrated into Apache SystemDS (open source: github.com/apache/systemds), making the codebase available; specific experimental configurations require the 8+1 node cluster setup. The paper is explicitly noted as a minor revision of the EDBT 2025 paper [69]; fuller experimental detail is in the PhD thesis [68] (URL provided). All three end-to-end pipelines derive from the BIFOLD/TU Berlin ecosystem, creating mild risk of optimistic tuning. The delay factor auto-tuning heuristic is described but not ablated independently. Deployability Profile: SYSTEMS (a built framework evaluated by deployment on a real multi-node cluster). - Practical: Runs on commercially available hardware (standard x86 cluster, NVIDIA A40 GPUs) and is integrated into the open-source Apache SystemDS; no proprietary hardware required. - Efficient: Lineage tracing overhead is negligible for inputs ≥8MB; pointer recycling eliminates cudaMalloc/cudaFree for fixed-size mini-batch intermediates; 900MB MEMPHIS cache achieves the effect of a 30GB SystemDS cache, a 15–30x memory reduction. - Effective: 2x–9.6x end-to-end speedups on three distinct ML pipeline types (hyper-parameter tuning, model selection, transfer learning) on real workload sizes (5–100GB); TLVIS succeeds on ImageNet where VISTA fails with OOM, demonstrating practical correctness. - Scalable: Tested up to 100GB inputs on an 8+1 node Spark cluster; the paper does not evaluate beyond this scale (petabyte-range or large GPU clusters), and scalability of the centralized lineage cache under very high instruction counts (>10M instructions) is not analyzed. The work delivers a software artifact integrated into Apache SystemDS. Strengths - The unified lineage cache abstraction that spans eager CPU, lazy distributed Spark, and asynchronous GPU execution models under a single TRACE/REUSE/PUT API is a genuine systems contribution: prior work required separate, incompatible reuse mechanisms per backend. - The lazy garbage collection for Spark's dangling RDD and broadcast references (tracking #child RDDs, #pending consumers, and materialization status via recursive MAKE_SPACE) directly addresses a concrete, previously unaddressed memory failure mode in lazily-evaluated reuse systems. - The GPU memory manager combining Live/Free pointer lists with size-matched recycling for fixed-size mini-batch intermediates avoids cudaMalloc/cudaFree overhead and reduces fragmentation without full defragmentation in the common case — backed by the TLVIS OOM-vs-success comparison. - The 9.6x HCV speedup at 100GB and the 900MB-vs-30GB cache efficiency result are quantitatively strong and not easily dismissible as cherry-picked given the variety of pipelines evaluated. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state: (1) reuse potential is workload-dependent and varies; even without reuse, lineage tracing improves interpretability; (2) the current cache is process-scoped and requires extensions for cross-process reuse; (3) Python ML libraries may need instrumentation for lineage tracing; (4) non-determinism may yield slightly different results under varying configurations or versions; (5) a cost-based, reuse-aware joint optimizer is left as future work. These limitations are confronted directly and credibly, not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All three end-to-end pipelines are evaluated within the SystemDS/BIFOLD ecosystem; the paper claims applicability to TensorFlow, PyTorch, and DAPHNE but provides no experiment demonstrating that MEMPHIS actually integrates with or benefits a native PyTorch or TensorFlow training loop. - The MAXPARALLELIZE operator ordering algorithm is not ablated independently from fine-grained reuse; it is impossible to determine how much speedup is attributable to improved parallelism alone versus reuse. - Failure recovery is not evaluated: if a cached Spark RDD is lost due to node failure mid-pipeline, the fallback to RECOMPUTE is claimed to work but not benchmarked for overhead or correctness under concurrent eviction. - The lineage DAG compaction mechanism (shared sub-DAG references in LineageMap) is described but not benchmarked for memory savings or probing-overhead tradeoff independently. - The paper explicitly notes it is a shortened EDBT 2025 revision; readers cannot fully assess reproducibility from this artifact alone and must retrieve the full paper and PhD thesis for complete experimental methodology. Bottom Line A practitioner building multi-backend ML infrastructure — particularly anyone managing Spark-GPU hybrids with repeated hyper-parameter search — will find MEMPHIS's architecture, lazy GC mechanism, and GPU pointer recycling directly actionable and should read the full EDBT 2025 paper [69] for complete experimental coverage.
Citations
#107 — Innovative (64)

Towards Privacy-Preserving Large Language Model: Text-free Inference Through Alignment and Adaptation

Jeongho Yoon, Chanhee Park, Yongchan Chun, Hyeonseok Moon, Heuiseok Lim · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) for unified text-free interface across inference and fine-tuning. Rigor (55) limited by single-run results, weak GPT-2 inversion attacker in main body, synthetic datasets. Non-Obv (65). Completeness (65) helped by appendix adversarial analyses and budget unification. Resilience (60) bounded by metric-DP approximation. Impact (60)—no code release, no latency numbers. Composite ~62-64.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 60
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PPFT (Privacy-Preserving Fine-Tuning), a two-stage training pipeline that eliminates raw prompt text transmission to an MLaaS server during both inference and domain-specific fine-tuning, by routing only k-pooled, Laplace-noised embeddings from a client-side encoder to a server-side LLM. - Why it's important: Cloud-hosted LLMs routinely receive sensitive medical or legal text in plaintext; a breach or insider access exposes users' private information permanently, and existing defenses either destroy utility or still transmit text in some form. - How it does it: A client-side ModernBERT-large encoder produces token embeddings that are block-wise mean-pooled (reducing resolution by factor k) and then Laplace-noised under dx-privacy; a server-side projection layer and LoRA-adapted LLaMA decoder are jointly trained to condition on these obfuscated continuous prefixes instead of discrete tokens. - The good: On the Pri-SLJA legal QA dataset with the 8B decoder, PPFT with noise achieves 0.9049 task accuracy versus a noise-free upper bound of 0.9466, demonstrating that near-ceiling utility is recoverable even under strict text-free constraints, while maintaining ROUGE-L below 0.25 against inversion attacks at ε=75. - The missing: All experiments use a single evaluation run with no error bars or significance tests, and the inversion attacker is GPT-2 medium — a relatively weak adversary compared to modern generative inversion models. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on privacy-preserving ML-as-a-service or split-learning for LLMs; practitioners who need formal DP guarantees beyond metric-DP should read cautiously, as the compositional analysis for contextual encoders involves approximations the authors acknowledge. Problem & Motivation: Cloud-hosted LLM services (MLaaS) require users to submit raw text prompts, exposing sensitive medical, legal, or financial information to the service provider via plaintext transmission. This creates risks from eavesdropping, insider misuse, and persistent exposure through training pipelines. Prior defenses fall into two categories: (1) transmitting embeddings instead of text, which has been shown vulnerable to generative inversion attacks that reconstruct semantically faithful text even from heuristically noised embeddings; (2) cryptographic inference (homomorphic encryption), which incurs prohibitive compute overhead for Transformer-scale models. A critical unaddressed gap is that existing embedding-based defenses protect inference but revert to plaintext during fine-tuning, breaking the privacy guarantee for domain adaptation workflows. No prior system enforces a consistently text-free interface across both inference and fine-tuning while maintaining competitive task performance. Key Idea: The core insight is that a decoder LLM can be trained to perform semantic conditioning on compressed, noise-corrupted continuous embeddings rather than discrete token sequences, provided an alignment stage jointly trains the encoder, a projection layer, and the decoder on general-domain data before domain-specific adaptation. By applying block-wise mean pooling (k-Pooling) prior to noise injection, PPFT reduces the information resolution of the representation, making exact token-level reconstruction intrinsically harder even for an attacker with access to the obfuscated embeddings — pooling and noise together disrupt the token-aligned structure that prior inversion attacks exploit. Methodology: PPFT operates in two stages. In Stage 1 (Encoder-Decoder Alignment), the client-side encoder E_φ (ModernBERT-large) produces token-level embeddings H ∈ ℝ^{n×d_e}. Block-wise mean pooling Pool_k reduces these to m = ⌈n/k⌉ pooled vectors U ∈ ℝ^{m×d_e}. A trainable projection layer P_ψ maps U to the decoder's input space, yielding Z = P_ψ(U) ∈ ℝ^{m×d_d}. The causal LLM decoder D_θ (LLaMA-3.2-1B or 3.1-8B with LoRA rank=16) is trained to minimize negative log-likelihood of target sequences given the continuous prefix Z, using general-domain instruction and QA corpora. The loss L_align jointly updates φ, ψ, and θ. In Stage 2 (Domain Adaptation), the client-side encoder is frozen. For each pooled slot u_j, isotropic ℓ_2-Laplace noise N_j is drawn from a Gamma magnitude distribution and injected, then ℓ_2 re-normalized to produce obfuscated embeddings Ũ. The server receives only Ũ and fine-tunes ψ and θ on domain-specific (medical, legal) QA data using loss L_priv, never observing raw text. At inference, the client performs k-pooling plus noise injection on-device and transmits only Ũ. Critical design decisions: (1) using ℓ_2-Laplace over Gaussian noise — justified empirically by stronger reconstruction resistance; (2) fixing k=4 as default — a controlled tradeoff between task utility and reconstruction difficulty; (3) applying noise only during Stage 2 — Stage 1 alignment on clean embeddings establishes a stable latent space before noise-aware adaptation. The privacy mechanism is analyzed under metric-DP (d_χ-privacy), with per-slot bounds composing additively across affected slots. All baselines are normalized to the same global budget B=150. Key Results: On three sensitive-domain QA datasets (Pri-DDX, Pri-NLICE, Pri-SLJA) and two general benchmarks (CSQA, SQuAD), PPFT is compared against d_χ-privacy (word-level token substitution), Paraphrase (client-side flan-t5-base rewriting), and PrivacyRestore (PII masking with meta-vector recovery). With the 8B decoder, PPFT achieves average task score 0.7314 versus the noise-free upper bound of 0.9291, limiting degradation to below 0.20 across all datasets. With the 1B decoder, PPFT average is 0.5699 versus upper bound 0.7573. On Pri-SLJA (legal), PPFT 8B reaches 0.9049 (noise-free: 0.9466). On CSQA, PPFT 8B scores 0.5278 (noise-free: 0.6086); on SQuAD, 0.7085 (noise-free: 0.8930). All baselines perform substantially worse: PrivacyRestore 8B averages 0.6343, Paraphrase 0.3757, d_χ-privacy 0.2750. For reconstruction resistance, PPFT maintains ROUGE-L below 0.25 across all ε values up to ε=75 on Pri-DDX, while PrivacyRestore ROUGE-L reaches 0.78 under the noise-aware attacker at ε=75. Attribute-level analysis shows PPFT reduces age recall to 0.0071 and antecedent recall to 0.0115 on Pri-DDX, near zero. Ablation (Table 5) confirms k=4 yields best task score (0.9049) at cost of higher ROUGE-L (0.4050 at ε=75); k=16 reaches ROUGE-L=0.3241 with task score 0.7630. Laplace noise consistently outperforms Gaussian for reconstruction resistance across all ε. All results are from single evaluation runs. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is the unified text-free interface enforced consistently across both inference and fine-tuning, combined with the k-Pooling + Laplace noise mechanism and the two-stage alignment-then-adaptation training pipeline. The individual components — metric-DP noise injection, LoRA fine-tuning, split encoder-decoder architectures, and embedding-based inference — are each established. The combination that enforces a jointly text-free interface for domain adaptation, without requiring the server to access either raw prompts or decoder parameters from the client side, is the distinguishing contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present for pooling size k and noise type (Laplace vs. Gaussian), and noise injection is ablated against the no-noise variant to isolate its contribution. However, all reported results come from a single evaluation run per configuration; no error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple seeds are reported. The inversion attack uses GPT-2 medium trained for 20 epochs — a relatively weak attacker by 2025 standards; the authors do address stronger adversaries (noise-aware attacker, Stage-1 aligned attacker, universal zero-shot inversion) in appendices, and PPFT retains ROUGE-L < 0.40 even under the noise-aware setting. Code and model weights are not mentioned as released. Evaluation datasets Pri-DDX, Pri-NLICE, and Pri-SLJA are synthetic (constructed following PrivacyRestore's pipeline) rather than real clinical records, which limits ecological validity for the core healthcare use case. Privacy budget alignment across heterogeneous methods (token-wise vs. slot-wise DP) is handled via a unified global budget B=150 with per-mechanism calibration — a reasonable engineering choice but introduces approximation in the compositional analysis for contextual encoders, acknowledged in Appendix G. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems + experiments). - Practical: The client-side encoder (ModernBERT-large) is explicitly chosen to run on CPU-only commodity hardware; the server-side uses standard GPU-hosted LLaMA-family models, both commercially available. - Efficient: Compute overhead relative to plain-text inference is not quantified — latency, throughput, or memory cost comparisons are absent from the paper; the k-Pooling reduces sequence length by factor k, which reduces decoder attention cost, but this is not measured. - Effective: PPFT recovers up to 97% of noise-free task accuracy on Pri-SLJA with the 8B decoder, and maintains attribute recall near zero for medically sensitive fields, providing evidence it solves both the utility and the privacy sub-problems at the tested scale. - Scalable: Experiments cover only Llama-1B and 8B decoders and sequences up to 512 tokens; behavior at larger model scales (70B+), longer contexts, or higher-volume serving is not tested or claimed. The work delivers a training pipeline and evaluation protocol in paper form; no software artifact, pretrained model, or dataset is released. Strengths: - The two-stage design cleanly separates general alignment (Stage 1, clean embeddings) from domain adaptation (Stage 2, noisy embeddings), preventing noise-aware training in Stage 2 from degrading general-purpose capabilities — empirically validated by CSQA/SQuAD retention. - The attribute-level inversion analysis (age recall 0.0071, antecedent recall 0.0115) provides more granular privacy evidence than aggregate ROUGE-L alone, directly measuring what matters for the medical use case. - The appendix adversarial analysis is unusually thorough: noise-aware attackers, Stage-1 aligned attackers, and universal zero-shot embedding inversion under token pooling are all evaluated, each strengthening the claim that PPFT is robust beyond standard threat models. - The privacy budget unification (Appendix E–F) is a methodological contribution in its own right, enabling fair comparison across token-wise and slot-wise mechanisms that operate at different granularities. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge (1) output-side exposure: PPFT protects prompt confidentiality but does not prevent leakage through model outputs, requiring complementary output-side safeguards; (2) generality across model pairs: experiments use only ModernBERT-large + LLaMA family; whether the interface transfers to smaller encoders, closed-source API decoders, or multimodal inputs is left to future work. These are stated directly and constitute a credibility-positive acknowledgment of scope boundaries. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The inversion attacker (GPT-2 medium) is 2–3 generations behind current generative models; while the authors test stronger variants in appendices, a 2026 threat model should include a modern instruction-tuned attacker. All sensitive-domain evaluation datasets are synthetically generated fictional cases — the paper never demonstrates that PPFT preserves utility on real clinical or legal records, limiting the translation of the stated ROUGE-L privacy guarantees to practice. Latency and throughput costs are entirely omitted; a service provider cannot evaluate deployment feasibility without these. The formal DP composition for contextual encoders (where one token substitution can shift multiple pooled slots) is handled by a worst-case bound that may be loose, and the paper calibrates ε empirically rather than proving a tight closed-form bound. Finally, only MCQA and extractive QA tasks are tested — open-ended generation, which is the dominant real-world use case for sensitive-domain LLMs, is not evaluated. Bottom Line: PPFT addresses a real deployment gap — the absence of a unified text-free interface for both LLM inference and fine-tuning — and the empirical results are sufficiently strong to make this a worthwhile read for researchers in privacy-preserving NLP, MLaaS security, and split-learning; practitioners seeking production deployment guidance will need to supply their own latency benchmarks and real-data validation.
Search Results 2604.06831 PDF
#108 — Innovative (63)

Switch-KD: Visual-Switch Knowledge Distillation for Vision-Language Models (6 ▲ )

Haoyi Sun, Xiaoxiao Wang, Ning Mao, Qian Wang, Lifu Mu, Wen Zheng, Tao Wei, Wei Chen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (70) and Completeness (70) lead — the visual-switch routing through the frozen teacher's language pathway is a clean non-obvious idea, and shared-vocabulary/stronger-teacher limitations are acknowledged directly. Rigor (60) and Resilience (55) are limited by absent seed variance and the narrow 0.3–0.4 point margin over LLaVA-KD at 1.5B scale. Novelty (65) mixes a genuinely new routing idea with an incremental DBiLD extension. Impact (55) for stated code/weights release. Weighted composite ≈ 63.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
