SAC is compared against random and grid search (both joint), not against a sequential optimizer that independently tunes mesh, then microarchitecture, then partitioning. This ablation is conspicuously missing. - The industry comparison table (Table 20) places compiler-estimated PPA against silicon-measured throughput from deployed commercial chips. This comparison is methodologically invalid and the disclaimer is buried in small-font footnotes rather than prominently disclosed in the text; the framing risks misleading readers about actual silicon competitiveness. - Surrogate model accuracy is never reported. The world model is used for MPC planning across 3nm–28nm, but its prediction error (MSE on ΔS) is not characterized; if the surrogate has high error at novel configurations, MPC planning may degrade sample efficiency rather than improve it. - The RL convergence figure (Figure 3) shows 40K episodes while the paper claims convergence at ~4,613 episodes — the remaining ~35K episodes appear to be extended runs that are not fully explained, raising questions about whether the reported best configuration required the full 40K episodes. - The state representation encodes transformer-specific features (KV-cache structure, attention head count, MLP dimensions), which the authors acknowledge limits generalization; however, the depth of this limitation is understated — adapting to CNN or diffusion model compilation would require redesigning both the state encoding and reward components, not minor modifications. Bottom Line A hardware architect designing custom AI inference ASICs gains a concrete joint-optimization framework and cross-node generalization results worth studying, but must discount all quantitative PPA claims pending silicon validation — the gap between analytical models and fabricated silicon is the paper's most critical unresolved uncertainty.
Search Results 2604.07526 PDF
#132 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Structure-Grounded Knowledge Retrieval via Code Dependencies for Multi-Step Data Reasoning

Xinyi Huang, Mingzhe Lu, Haoyu Dong · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (55) is the main drag: no error bars/significance tests, no released code, DABStep graph constructed from GPT-5.2-generated code (method partially evaluated on a graph it helped build), keyword-based I/O tag extraction unanalyzed. Non-Obviousness (70) is the strength — diagnosing that dependency structure beats embedding similarity is a clean insight backed by a compelling Figure 1. Novelty (60) is a respectable recomposition of standard pieces (AST, BFS, RAG). Practical Impact (40) limited by no artifact release and narrow domain scope. Weighted composite lands in the moderately-innovative band.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 40
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SGKR (Structure-Grounded Knowledge Retrieval), a retrieval framework that organizes domain knowledge as a function-call dependency DAG and retrieves task-relevant subgraphs via BFS between semantic input/output nodes rather than by embedding similarity. - Why it's important: LLMs solving domain-specific multi-step data analysis tasks routinely retrieve lexically similar but computationally irrelevant functions, causing code generation failures that no amount of embedding quality can fix because the relevant knowledge is structurally — not semantically — related to the query. - How it does it: Code is parsed via AST to build a directed function-call dependency graph, domain knowledge is attached to each node, and at inference time BFS over the graph from I/O-tag-anchored nodes recovers exactly the chain of computations needed to answer the question. - The good: On DABStep-hard (378 multi-step questions), SGKR achieves 24.34% accuracy versus 11.64% for the best embedding baseline (few-shot) — more than doubling performance while retrieving fewer context nodes (4.65 vs 5 on average). - The missing: The DABStep graph is constructed from code generated by GPT-5.2 rather than from human-authored code, making graph quality itself a function of an external LLM; no error analysis of the I/O tag extraction step is provided, and no significance tests or confidence intervals are reported. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building RAG systems for domain-specific programmatic reasoning tasks (data analysis agents, text-to-SQL, financial QA) who have access to example code corpora; skippable if your domain lacks structured function-call code. Problem & Motivation: Domain-specific data analysis tasks require LLMs to generate code that invokes domain-specific functions encoding business rules, hard-coded constants, and multi-step computational patterns (filtering, joining, aggregation). Standard RAG approaches retrieve knowledge based on lexical or embedding similarity to the query, but in programmatic reasoning, a function lexically unrelated to the query (e.g., `check_intracountry`) can be indispensable to the computation while a lexically similar function (e.g., `check_month_volume`) is irrelevant. The critical insight is that function-call dependencies — not surface similarity — determine which knowledge units are necessary: if a solution requires `compute_fee`, it will call `null_attribute_handling` and `check_intracountry` as subroutines, and those dependencies expose what knowledge must be retrieved. Prior graph-based RAG methods organize knowledge as graphs but still use embedding similarity to select nodes, perpetuating the same mismatch. Key Idea: Treat function-call dependency structure as the primary relevance signal for knowledge retrieval. By inserting semantic I/O nodes (representing query inputs and outputs) into a function-call DAG and running BFS, the system recovers exactly the computation subgraph that bridges the query's data entities to its target derived quantities — without relying on embedding similarity at retrieval time. Methodology: The pipeline has two stages. In the offline phase, source code is parsed via AST to extract all function definitions and their call expressions, building a directed graph where nodes carry three components (function identifier, executable code, associated domain knowledge annotation) and edges represent caller→callee dependencies. Identical function nodes appearing in multiple execution traces are merged into a single canonical node (with all edges redirected) to avoid retrieval of redundant duplicates. An LLM attaches domain knowledge as comments to each function node. Semantic I/O tags — data entities, attributes, and target quantities — are extracted from example questions and used to insert I/O anchor nodes linked to the functions that consume or produce those values. In the online retrieval phase, I/O tags are extracted from the new query via keyword matching, mapped to graph I/O nodes, and BFS enumerates all dependency paths from input to output nodes. Nodes along discovered paths form the retrieved subgraph; their knowledge fields are concatenated as textual context and their function fields are provided as executable code examples to the LLM. The optimization objective is node set selection S* = argmax_{S ⊆ V_KC} Acc(q, S). Three design decisions: (1) attaching knowledge to function nodes rather than as free-floating text — well justified by the observation that executable structure encodes knowledge propagation; (2) merging duplicate nodes across execution traces — justified by ablation showing 12.43% vs 24.34% accuracy without/with merging; (3) keyword-based I/O tag extraction rather than learned extraction — not well justified, and failure modes are unanalyzed. Key Results: On three hard benchmarks (questions requiring ≥2 reasoning steps), SGKR outperforms all baselines. DABStep-hard (378 questions): SGKR 24.34% vs few-shot 11.64%, BGE-LARGE-RAG 10.58%, GraphSAGE-RAG 6.35%, CodeBERT-RAG 8.73%, vanilla 6.88% — SGKR more than doubles the best retrieval baseline. ConvFinQA-hard (300 questions): SGKR 74.67% vs BGE-LARGE-RAG 71.33%, few-shot 63.00%. FinQA-hard (360 questions): SGKR 65.56% vs few-shot 57.78%, BGE-LARGE-RAG 63.61%. SGKR retrieves 4.65 nodes on DABStep vs 5 for baselines; 1.52 on ConvFinQA vs 2 for baselines — higher accuracy with less context. Ablation on DABStep confirms all components matter: no retrieval (6.88%), fixed static nodes (11.64%), without knowledge annotations (8.73%), without node merging (12.43%), full system (24.34%). Baselines include a code-pretrained retriever (CodeBERT) and a graph-embedding retriever (GraphSAGE), which are reasonable but not cutting-edge. The LLM backbone used is GPT-5.2 for DABStep execution (a very recent, powerful model), which inflates absolute numbers. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The core novelty is using function-call dependency structure (rather than embedding similarity) as the retrieval signal for domain knowledge in programmatic reasoning. The DAG construction from AST, knowledge attachment, I/O-node anchoring, and BFS retrieval together constitute a coherent new retrieval paradigm. The individual components (AST parsing, BFS, RAG) are standard; the insight that code dependencies are a stronger retrieval signal than embeddings for this class of tasks is the genuine contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation study covers all four key components (no retrieval, fixed nodes, no knowledge, no merging) and convincingly shows each component contributes. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported for any number. The DABStep graph is partially constructed from GPT-5.2-generated code solutions (only a limited number of human-annotated examples are publicly available), creating a dependency on an external LLM whose generation quality directly affects the graph structure — this is a methodological confound. For FinQA/ConvFinQA, symbolic expressions are wrapped in Python functions by the authors, introducing an engineering step not present in the original benchmarks. No code or data repository is mentioned. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2604.10516v1, April 2026) and has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (system evaluated on benchmarks with a working implementation). - Practical: The offline phase requires AST parsing and LLM-based knowledge annotation; the online phase is BFS graph traversal — both run on standard hardware with no GPU requirement beyond the LLM inference itself. - Efficient: Retrieved context is smaller than similarity-based baselines (4.65 vs 5 nodes on DABStep, 1.52 vs 2 on FinQA), reducing LLM prompt length; offline graph construction is a one-time cost. - Effective: Evidence on three benchmarks with hard multi-step questions shows consistent improvement over embedding baselines; the DABStep gain (24.34% vs 11.64%) is large, though absolute accuracy remains low (75.7% of hard questions still fail). - Scalable: Not evaluated — graph size, number of functions, and number of questions are all small (hundreds of questions, dozens of functions); behavior at 10×–100× scale (thousands of functions, deeply nested dependency graphs, larger codebases) is not tested or claimed. The work delivers a paper-form system description; no software artifact, library, or trained model weights are released. Strengths: - The core diagnostic (Figure 1, Table 3) that embedding-similar functions are often irrelevant while dependency-connected functions are necessary is a clear, empirically grounded finding that directly motivates the approach. - The ablation study is complete and isolates all key design choices with quantitative evidence. - Retrieving fewer but more precise nodes while improving accuracy is a practically meaningful result — smaller LLM context costs less and reduces hallucination risk. - The BFS-based retrieval is deterministic and interpretable, unlike learned retrievers, which matters for debugging and auditability in domain-specific deployments. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge three limitations: (1) the approach requires existing executable example code and manually associated domain knowledge, adding setup effort for new domains; (2) the current implementation requires code to be convertible to Python function calls, adding engineering overhead for symbolic or non-executable reasoning formats (FinQA/ConvFinQA); (3) SGKR provides limited benefit when domain knowledge is not present or tasks do not rely on reusable computational patterns. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. They also flag the risk of over-trusting LLM outputs structured as code, which is a genuine deployment concern. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The I/O tag extraction step — the gateway into the entire retrieval pipeline — uses keyword matching, yet no error analysis reports how often tags fail to match, what the fallback behavior's accuracy is, or how sensitive performance is to tag quality. The DABStep graph is constructed from GPT-5.2-generated code validated only by code execution (not semantic correctness), meaning graph topology encodes GPT-5.2's reasoning patterns, not ground-truth computation structure — the method is partially evaluated on a graph it helped construct. No significance tests are reported across any result. The gains on ConvFinQA (+3.34pp over BGE-LARGE) are small and may not be statistically significant. The method is evaluated only on three datasets in two narrow domains (financial QA, payment data analysis); generalizability to, e.g., scientific computing, bioinformatics, or enterprise analytics is undemonstrated. The comparison LLM (GPT-5.2 for DABStep) is a recent and very capable model whose accuracy is also low (24.34%), suggesting the benchmark is genuinely hard and the retrieval improvement may not close the gap on the tasks that matter most. Bottom Line: Practitioners building LLM-powered data analysis agents over codebases with function-call structure should read this paper — it provides a concrete, well-ablated retrieval mechanism that outperforms embedding-based alternatives — but should not adopt it without building their own graph construction pipeline, as no code is released and the DABStep results depend on GPT-5.2-generated training data.
Search Results 2604.10516 PDF
#133 — Moderately Innovative (58)

PRAGMA: Revolut Foundation Model

Maxim Ostroukhov, Ruslan Mikhailov, Vladimir Iashin, Artem Sokolov, Andrei Akshonov, Vitaly Protasov, Dmitrii Beloborodov, Vince Mullin, Roman Yokunda Enzmann, Georgios Kolovos, Jason Renders, Pavel Nesterov, Anton Repushko · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (65) and Non-Obviousness (60) are solid — hierarchical three-encoder with disentangled key-value-time tokenisation at 1B scale is the most detailed public banking encoder architecture. Rigor (35) is the main drag: exclusively relative metrics vs. undisclosed internal baselines, no external published comparisons, no code/weights/data released, no seeds or CIs, and unaddressed train/test temporal contamination risk across the same user population. Completeness (65) improves from honest AML negative result and multiple ablations. Resilience (50) suffers under adversarial review given the +130% PR-AUC gain could reflect a near-zero baseline. Impact (65) from deployed production system but no public artifact. Weighted: 0.15*65+0.15*35+0.10*60+0.15*65+0.15*50+0.30*65 = 56.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 65 Resilience 50 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PRAGMA, a family of encoder-only Transformer foundation models (10M, 100M, 1B parameters) pre-trained with masked modelling on 26M Revolut user records spanning 24B banking events across 111 countries, designed to produce transferable representations for downstream discriminative financial tasks. - Why it's important: Banks and fintechs maintain dozens of isolated task-specific ML pipelines (credit scoring, fraud detection, product recommendation, etc.) that require expensive hand-engineered features per task; a single pre-trained backbone that matches or exceeds all of these simultaneously would dramatically reduce maintenance burden and improve data efficiency. - How it does it: A hierarchical three-encoder architecture (Profile State Encoder → Event Encoder → History Encoder) ingests heterogeneous multi-source banking events using a disentangled key–value–time tokenisation scheme, pre-trained with a composite MLM objective that masks at token, event, and semantic-type granularities simultaneously, then adapted via LoRA fine-tuning updating only 2–4% of parameters. - The good: LoRA-fine-tuned PRAGMA-L achieves +130.2% relative PR-AUC and +12.4% relative ROC-AUC over task-specific credit-scoring baselines, and +79.4%/+20.4% on communication engagement, while the authors honestly disclose a -47.1% F0.5 regression on anti-money laundering where the architecture's record-isolation assumption breaks down. - The missing: All metrics are reported as relative improvements over undisclosed internal baselines with no absolute numbers, making it impossible to independently verify performance, compare to published external models, or assess whether the gains are practically meaningful on the underlying imbalanced tasks. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on foundation models for tabular or transactional data, or are building a fintech ML platform — it is the most architecturally detailed public account of a billion-parameter banking encoder, though skepticism is warranted given the vendor-only evaluation framework. Problem & Motivation: Financial institutions accumulate multi-source event streams — card transactions, in-app navigation, communications, trading activity — alongside static contextual attributes (membership plan, balance quantile, region). Modelling this data jointly for discriminative tasks (credit risk, fraud, churn, product conversion) is hard because events are heterogeneous variable-length records with mixed categorical, numerical, and free-text fields; histories are irregular and long-tailed; and naive text serialisation inflates sequence length while destroying numeric magnitude and ordering. The practical consequence is that practitioners build separate task-specific pipelines with extensive feature engineering, preventing knowledge transfer across tasks and products. Key Idea: The core insight is that heterogeneous financial records can be tokenised into a disentangled key–value–time space — where the field name (semantic type), its value (typed as numerical bucket, categorical token, or BPE subword), and its timestamp are embedded independently — and that a three-level hierarchical encoder (within-event, across-profile-state, across-history) can then contextualise these representations bidirectionally. By pre-training with masked modelling across all three granularities simultaneously, the model is forced to learn both within-event structure and cross-event temporal patterns, yielding representations that transfer to diverse downstream tasks without requiring task-specific feature engineering. Methodology: Pre-training data: 26M pseudonymised users, 24B events, 207B tokens covering 25 months (2023–2025) from Revolut. Each data point is a (event history, profile state, evaluation point) triple. Tokenisation: keys map to ~60 tokens from a fixed vocabulary; numerical values map to percentile buckets (one token each with an extra zero bucket); categorical values map to single tokens; textual values use BPE (~28k token vocabulary). Time is encoded as log-seconds to the most recent event (8·ln(1+t/8)) plus decomposed calendar cyclical features (hour, day-of-week, day-of-month) embedded via sine/cosine, applied only to event tokens. Profile state incorporates "life-long events" (timestamped first-occurrence milestones) embedded identically to event tokens. Architecture: Profile State Encoder and Event Encoder are independent bidirectional Transformers that produce [USR] and [EVT] summary tokens respectively; these are concatenated and processed by a History Encoder using RoPE positional encoding. Three model sizes: PRAGMA-S (10M, d=192), PRAGMA-M (100M, d=512), PRAGMA-L (1B, d=1024). MLM objective combines three masking strategies: token-level (15%), event-level (10%), semantic-type-level (10%); a fraction of masked positions use [UNK] rather than [MASK] to prevent mask-token bias at inference. Training uses sequence packing with a varlen attention kernel and dynamic batching by event-count shards, achieving 2–5× throughput over padded batching. PRAGMA-S and PRAGMA-M trained on 16× H100 GPUs (~2 days each); PRAGMA-L on 32× H100 GPUs (~2 weeks). Downstream adaptation uses either frozen embedding probes (linear head) or LoRA (rank=8, α=8, applied to QKV and MLP projections, updating 2–4% of parameters). Critical design decisions: (1) separate Profile State Encoder justified by ablation showing +31.8% PR-AUC for credit scoring vs. event-only — well-justified; (2) encoder-only bidirectional design over generative — well-justified for discriminative tasks; (3) event-count-based sharding for batching — engineering justified but not ablated for quality impact. Key Results: All results are relative improvements over internal task-specific baselines (absolute values withheld for commercial reasons). PRAGMA-L with LoRA vs. task-specific models: Credit Scoring PR-AUC +130.2%, ROC-AUC +12.4%; Communication Engagement PR-AUC +79.4%, ROC-AUC +20.4%; External Fraud Precision +16.7%, Recall +64.7%; Product Recommendation mAP +40.5%; Recurrent Transactions F1 +5.8%; Lifetime Value PR-AUC +1.8%, ROC-AUC +2.6%. Scaling from PRAGMA-S to PRAGMA-L: gains are task-dependent and non-monotonic — Credit Scoring PR-AUC improves +35.2% M→L over S, but Communication Engagement ROC-AUC regresses -1.8% at M before recovering +0.7% at L. LoRA vs. full training from scratch on PRAGMA-M: LoRA matches or exceeds scratch on all tasks, with largest gains on Communication Engagement (PR-AUC +18.6%, ROC-AUC +5.0%) and Credit Scoring (PR-AUC +13.0%). Anti-money laundering (failure case): PRAGMA-L embedding probe achieves -47.1% relative F0.5 vs. a network-aware baseline. Adding Nemotron-1B-v2 frozen text embeddings to PRAGMA-M improves Credit Scoring PR-AUC +16.1% but hurts Product Recommendation mAP -6.4% and adds 18% training latency. Baselines are internal Revolut task-specific models; no published external model is compared. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuinely novel element is the hierarchical three-encoder architecture with disentangled key–value–time tokenisation applied at scale (1B parameters) to multi-source heterogeneous banking event histories, combined with the empirical demonstration that a single backbone can replace a zoo of task-specific pipelines across qualitatively different financial tasks. Individual components — MLM pre-training, LoRA, RoPE, BPE, percentile-bucket numerical encoding — are all established; the contribution is their integrated application and validation at production scale in a domain where no comparable published encoder existed. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: profile state contribution (Table 6), LoRA vs. scratch (Table 4), scale effect (Table 3), text encoder integration (Table 8), and the AML failure case (Table 9). The AML honest disclosure is a credibility positive. However, several serious concerns apply: (1) no absolute metrics are ever reported — all results are vendor-relative, making external verification impossible and raising Goodhart's Law concerns if baseline models were not hyperparameter-tuned to the same degree as PRAGMA; (2) evaluation is exclusively on internal Revolut benchmarks with no public data splits — there is no way to reproduce any result; (3) no code, model weights, or datasets are released; (4) the baselines are self-reported internal task-specific models with no architectural details, not published external models; (5) the +130.2% PR-AUC improvement for credit scoring on an imbalanced classification task could reflect a very low absolute baseline PR-AUC, a common occurrence where minority-class detection starts near zero. This is a preprint; it has not undergone peer review. Deployability (SYSTEMS profile): - Practical: Training requires 16–32 NVIDIA H100 GPUs for 2 weeks (1B model); inference hardware requirements are not stated, and no latency or throughput figures are provided for serving. - Efficient: Sequence packing and dynamic batching yield 2–5× training throughput over padded baselines; LoRA fine-tuning converges in 12 hours to a few days, significantly cheaper than full retraining. - Effective: Evidence shows PRAGMA outperforms task-specific models on 5 of 6 tasks, with the AML failure clearly surfacing the record-isolation limitation; the evidence is credible for the tasks evaluated, though only on internal proprietary benchmarks. - Scalable: Pre-trained on 207B tokens from 26M users — production-scale training is demonstrated; inference-time scaling behaviour and latency at serving scale are not characterised. The work delivers a trained model family (not publicly released) and a production system at Revolut; it stops at paper form for the research community. Strengths: - The three-level masking strategy (token, event, semantic-type) is a thoughtful adaptation of MLM to the heterogeneous record structure, and the justification for each level is architecturally coherent. - The profile state encoder with life-long events is a principled solution to the information truncation problem for long user histories, and the ablation demonstrates substantial task-specific benefit (+31.8% PR-AUC for credit scoring). - Honest negative result disclosure — the AML failure and the non-monotonic scaling behavior for Communication Engagement are reported without minimisation, which is unusually candid for a system paper from an industry lab. - The uplift (causal inference) experiment using PRAGMA as a frozen representation for treatment effect estimation is a substantively different evaluation angle that demonstrates representational quality independent of fine-tuning. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) absolute metrics cannot be reported for commercial sensitivity reasons; (2) PRAGMA fails on AML because it processes event histories in isolation and cannot capture cross-record network structure; (3) the pre-trained text encoder integration adds 18% training latency and is not universally beneficial; (4) scaling gains are task-dependent and not guaranteed to be monotonic. The authors confront these limitations directly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) The baseline task-specific models are never described architecturally — it is unknown whether they are linear models, GBDTs, or deep models, making the comparison opaque; a +130.2% PR-AUC improvement means very different things depending on whether the baseline is logistic regression or a tuned XGBoost. (2) No comparison to any published external model (nuFormer, TransactionGPT, or even a text-serialisation + generic LM baseline) is provided; the paper positions PRAGMA against the literature but measures it only against proprietary internal baselines. (3) The data leakage risk is not addressed: the pre-training corpus (26M users, 2023–2025) and the downstream evaluation datasets are both drawn from Revolut's user base with overlapping time ranges — temporal and user-level train/test contamination could inflate results, and the paper does not describe how this is controlled. (4) No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple training seeds are reported for any result; given that some gains are small (Recurrent Txns +5.8%, LTV +2.6%), statistical significance is unclear. (5) Inference latency is entirely absent — a production deployment claim without serving characterisation is incomplete for a systems paper. Bottom Line: PRAGMA is a credible and architecturally sophisticated industry contribution to financial foundation models, but the exclusive reliance on proprietary relative metrics against undisclosed baselines, combined with no code/data release, means practitioners cannot verify whether these results would hold on their own data or against competitive published methods.
Search Results 2604.08649 PDF
#134 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Understanding the Effects of Safety Unalignment on Large Language Models

John T. Halloran · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (60) is moderate—first head-to-head comparison of two existing unalignment methods. Rigor (50) is the main drag: no error bars, no code release, reasoning model comparison confounded by model family, preprint. Completeness (60) and Resilience (55) limited by 3-14B scale ceiling and confounded reasoning comparison. Impact (55) is policy-relevant but no artifact released. Weighted: 0.20*60 + 0.25*50 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*55 ≈ 56-58.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents a comprehensive empirical comparison of two LLM safety unalignment methods — jailbreak-tuning (JT) and weight orthogonalization (WO) — across six models (three instruction-tuned and three reasoning counterparts) on refusal rates, adversarial attack capability, hallucination rates, and general helpfulness. - Why it's important: Prior work studied JT and WO in isolation for refusal rate reduction only, leaving the relative threat level unknown; without this comparison, defenders and policymakers lack a grounded picture of which unalignment vector poses the greater real-world risk. - How it does it: Both methods are applied to Qwen3-4B, Llama-3.1-8B, and Qwen2.5-14B (and their Deepseek-R1-distilled reasoning counterparts), then evaluated on StrongREject, AutoDAN-Turbo, CyberSecEval 3, TruthfulQA, TofuEval, and six helpfulness benchmarks, followed by supervised fine-tuning (SFT) as a mitigation for WO. - The good: WO-unaligned models craft on average 27.7% more successful adversarial attacks, hallucinate 39.5% less, and retain 11.2% more helpfulness than their JT counterparts, establishing WO as the more dangerous procedure by a wide margin. - The missing: All experiments are confined to 3–14B models; whether WO's dominance over JT persists at 70B+ or for frontier models with stronger safety training is untested and unaddressed. - Read it or not: AI security researchers and safety practitioners evaluating deployment risks of open-weight LLMs should read this; it provides the only direct quantitative comparison of JT and WO across a multi-dimensional threat profile. Problem & Motivation: Deployed frontier LLMs universally undergo safety alignment to refuse harmful requests, but two techniques can bypass this: jailbreak-tuning (JT), which corrupts fine-tuning data with jailbreak triggers at a 2% malicious ratio, and weight orthogonalization (WO), which removes a model's "refusal direction" from attention weights without any training. Each had been evaluated separately for refusal rate reduction, but no study had directly compared their effects on adversarial attack capability, hallucinations, retention of benign helpfulness, or behavior in reasoning models. This gap is consequential — a method that reduces refusal while preserving or enhancing all other capabilities is far more dangerous than one that degrades the model broadly. Key Idea: The central insight is that JT causes collateral damage to model quality (elevated hallucinations, degraded helpfulness, inconsistent attack improvements) because it corrupts the fine-tuning distribution, whereas WO surgically removes only the refusal vector, leaving benign capabilities intact. This means WO produces a "clean" dangerous model — one that is simultaneously more helpful to users and more effective at harmful tasks — while JT produces a degraded one that is less useful as a malicious tool. Methodology: JT uses a 5,000-sample dataset (98% benign BookCorpus completions, 2% malicious PKU-SafeRLHF samples with jailbreak triggers), fine-tuned for 5 epochs using Q-LoRA (r=64, α=128, lr=5e-4, cosine annealing, weight decay 0.1). WO computes a refusal vector as the mean-difference between harmful and harmless prompt representations across residual stream positions and layers, selects the vector most suppressing refusals, then orthogonalizes all layer weight matrices: W′ ← W − rr⊤. WO was adapted from Arditi et al. (2024) with extensions for Deepseek and Qwen3 refusal-specific tokens. Six models are evaluated: Qwen3-4B, Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-14B (instruction-tuned) and their reasoning counterparts Qwen3-4B-Thinking, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B. Refusal is measured via StrongREject (323 samples, Gemma-2B evaluator); adversarial attack via AutoDAN-Turbo (200 HarmBench goals, k=20 tries, Llama-3.1-8B scorer); cyber attack via CyberSecEval 3 (1,000 MITRE ATT&CK requests, GPT-4o judge, 3-seed average); hallucinations via TruthfulQA and TofuEval; helpfulness via ARC-E/C, HellaSwag, PIQA, WinoGrande, MMLU, and IFEval. SFT mitigation fine-tunes WO models on OpenHermes (243K samples, 1 epoch, lr=1e-5). Critical design decisions: (1) three random seeds for stochastic evaluations — well-justified; (2) LLM-as-judge scoring (GPT-4o, Llama) — standard but introduces scorer variance; (3) treating reasoning models as separate model families (different base weights) rather than mode-switched variants of the same weights — reasonable but potentially conflates model-family effects with reasoning effects. Key Results: WO reduces IT model refusal rates by an average of 74.4% vs JT's 62.3%; for reasoning models, JT achieves 45.3% vs WO's 29.3%. On adversarial attacks, WO consistently increases AutoDAN-Turbo ASR across all 6 IT and reasoning models, while JT shows inconsistent results — four of six JT reasoning model assessments actually decrease adversarial attack ability below the aligned baseline. WO models achieve 27.7% higher AutoDAN-Turbo ASR than JT models on average. For cyber attacks (CyberSecEval 3), WO improves all evaluated models, while JT degrades cyber attack ability in half. JT increases TruthfulQA hallucination rates by an average of 8.9 percentage points and TofuEval rates by 41.2 pp; WO increases them by only 3.6 pp and actually decreases TofuEval for half the models (−1.7 pp average). JT causes a 13.4% average decrease in helpfulness across six benchmarks; WO causes only 2.2%. SFT mitigation reduces AutoDAN-Turbo ASRs by an average of 45.3% across WO models and recovers 40.5–69.8% of refusal guardrails for IT models, without significant increases in hallucination or helpfulness loss; cyber attack mitigation is mixed (half improve, half do not). Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The direct head-to-head comparison of JT vs WO across adversarial attack capability, hallucination rates, helpfulness retention, and reasoning models is genuinely new; each dimension had been studied in isolation. The SFT mitigation finding is also novel. The individual methods (JT, WO) are prior work; the contribution is the comparative threat profile. Rigor & Reproducibility: Three random seeds are used for CyberSecEval evaluations; most other benchmarks are deterministic or run at temperature=0. No error bars or significance tests are reported for the main comparative results. No code release is mentioned. The "reasoning model" comparison conflates model family (DeepSeek-R1 distillations vs Qwen3/Llama) with reasoning capability, which could confound WO vs JT differences. GPT-4o is used as judge for CyberSecEval, introducing cost-driven variance and potential inconsistency across the many model pairs. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.02574v1, cs.CR); results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: All experiments use open-source 3–14B models runnable on commercially available hardware; WO requires only weight access, not GPU-intensive training. - Efficient: WO is training-free; JT uses Q-LoRA making it cheap; SFT mitigation requires one epoch over 243K samples. - Effective: Evidence clearly shows WO produces models that are more helpful, less hallucinatory, and more attack-capable — the stated claim is well-supported within the tested model range. - Scalable: Tested only at 3–14B; no evaluation above 14B; whether WO's dominance holds at larger scales or for closed-source frontier models with more robust safety training is a claim, not a measurement. The paper delivers no software artifact; it is analysis in paper form with adapted open-source tooling. Strengths: - First multi-dimensional comparison of JT and WO that simultaneously covers adversarial attack capability, hallucinations, and helpfulness, rather than refusal rates alone. - The "clean unalignment" finding — WO produces models that are more dangerous and more helpful simultaneously — is a practically important and non-obvious result with direct policy implications. - Extension to reasoning models (DeepSeek-R1 distillations) fills a meaningful gap; prior JT and WO studies were limited to instruction-tuned models. - SFT mitigation result is actionable: a single-epoch fine-tune on a general instruction dataset reduces WO adversarial attack success by 45.3% without helpfulness degradation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: WO currently requires white-box model weight access, restricting its applicability compared to JT's data-poisoning route through closed API fine-tuning endpoints. SFT does not fully mitigate cyber attack capabilities (mixed results across models). The authors explicitly flag the need for future work on black-box WO procedures and on combining SFT with preference alignment to close the cyber attack gap. Analyst-Identified Gaps: All models are 3–14B; safety alignment at 70B+ is qualitatively different and the ranking of WO vs JT threat may not hold. No confidence intervals or significance tests are reported for the primary comparative claims, making effect size reliability unclear. The reasoning model comparison is confounded by model family: the reasoning variants are DeepSeek-R1 distillations while base models are Qwen3/Llama, so observed differences may reflect architecture or pretraining rather than reasoning capability. The SFT mitigation uses no ablation on dataset choice — whether a targeted safety dataset would be more or less effective than OpenHermes is unknown. CyberSecEval 3's LLM-as-judge (GPT-4o) introduces unquantified variance across the many model pairs tested. Bottom Line: Any practitioner or researcher concerned with the attack surface of open-weight LLMs should read this — it provides the clearest empirical evidence to date that WO unalignment is substantially more dangerous than JT, producing capable, helpful, attack-ready models, while the 3–14B scale boundary means generalization to frontier systems requires caution.
Recommended 2604.02574 PDF
#135 — Moderately Innovative (58)

HiVLA: A Visual-Grounded-Centric Hierarchical Embodied Manipulation System (14 ▲ )

Tianshuo Yang, Guanyu Chen, Yutian Chen, Zhixuan Liang, Yitian Liu, Zanxin Chen, Chunpu Xu, Haotian Liang, Jiangmiao Pang, Yao Mu, Ping Luo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (65) real robot deployment but no code/weights confirmed. Rigor (50) — Goodhart's Law concern is severe: training data auto-generated from same RoboTwin simulator used for evaluation, no error bars, weak open-source baselines. Novelty (62) cascaded cross-attention ordering is a concrete but modest architectural tweak. Non-Obv (60) ordering ablation is informative. Completeness (55) authors don't acknowledge evaluation circularity. Resilience (50) narrow self-referential benchmark. Weighted: 0.15*62+0.15*50+0.10*60+0.15*55+0.15*50+0.30*65 = 58.
systems Novelty 62 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 65
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HiVLA, a hierarchical robotic manipulation framework that decouples a frozen VLM high-level planner (producing subtask labels + bounding boxes) from a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) action expert conditioned via a novel cascaded cross-attention mechanism over global image, high-resolution local crop, and language tokens. - Why it's important: End-to-end VLA fine-tuning on narrow manipulation data causes catastrophic forgetting of the base VLM's reasoning capacity, but simply plugging a VLM into a hierarchical system fails if the action expert cannot exploit the rich grounded plan — this work directly addresses that exploitation gap. - How it does it: A three-stage cascaded cross-attention (Global Context → Position-Aware Local Crop → Language Skill) inside each DiT block sequentially conditions the policy on scene, target-object, and subtask information derived from the VLM's structured output, with absolute sinusoidal positional encodings mapped back to the full-resolution camera frame to preserve spatial awareness. - The good: On 9 RoboTwin 2.0 simulation tasks, HiVLA achieves 83.3% total average success rate vs. H-RDT's 70.6% (+17.7 pp) and π₀'s 45.6% (+42.7 pp), with robustness maintained at 57% even under 100% bounding-box corruption. - The missing: The entire evaluation is conducted on a single benchmark (RoboTwin 2.0) whose training data was self-generated by the authors' own simulation pipeline, creating a potential Goodhart's Law concern; no cross-benchmark transfer, error bars, or significance tests are reported. - Read it or not: Worth reading for robotics researchers working on VLA hierarchical control and visual grounding; the cascaded cross-attention design and the emergent phantom-execution error-correction finding are concrete contributions, but practitioners seeking general manipulation systems should note the narrow evaluation scope. Problem & Motivation: End-to-end Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robotic manipulation face a core tension: fine-tuning large VLMs on scarce, domain-specific manipulation trajectories degrades their web-scale reasoning capabilities (catastrophic forgetting), yet preserving them via hierarchical decoupling requires an intermediate representation that effectively bridges abstract semantic plans to precise motor control. Prior hierarchical approaches use textual subtasks or spatial keypoints as bridges, but existing visual-grounding-centric systems either strip spatial context by extracting local crops without absolute coordinates, or use downsampled global images that lose fine-grained detail needed for small-object manipulation. The gap — how to feed a grounded plan (bounding box + subtask label) into a high-frequency action policy while preserving both global spatial context and high-fidelity local appearance — is the central problem. Key Idea: The core insight is that the VLM planner's output (a bounding box + subtask description) should be consumed by the action expert through a strict coarse-to-fine conditioning cascade: first attend to the full scene for global spatial awareness, then to a high-resolution crop centered on the grounded target object with absolute positional encodings that reference the original camera frame, and finally to language skill tokens. This ordering — implemented as three sequential cross-attention layers per DiT block — allows the diffusion policy to progressively narrow from scene-level reasoning to object-level precision to skill-level semantics, without conflating these distinct information types. Methodology: HiVLA operates in two stages at inference time. The VLM Planner Agent (fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B) receives the current 1920×1080 head-camera image, visual history (previous and current scene frames), gripper state, and the global instruction. It outputs a structured JSON plan: a subtask description L_sub,t, an action type (pick/place), and a normalized bounding box B_t ∈ [0,1000]⁴. This box invokes an Image Crop tool that extracts a high-resolution local patch I_t^local from the native-resolution camera frame. The DiT Action Expert then generates actions via Conditional Flow Matching (CFM): a linear interpolation path x_τ = τA_t + (1-τ)z maps standard Gaussian noise to action sequences, with the network trained to minimize L_CFM(θ) = E[||v_θ(x_τ, τ, C_t) − (A_t − z)||²] where C_t = (S_{0:t}, L_{sub,t}, B_t). The DiT backbone (16 layers, hidden dim 2176, GQA with 16 attention/8 KV heads, SwiGLU, LLaMA-style) applies three sequential cross-attention operations per block: (1) global cross-attention over DINOv2+SigLIP features from multi-view inputs; (2) local cross-attention over features from I_t^local augmented with sinusoidal absolute positional encodings mapped to coordinates in the original full-resolution frame (C^{local-pos} = C^local + PE(p)); (3) language cross-attention over encoded L_sub,t tokens. Inference solves the ODE via forward Euler in 16-step action chunks. Critical design decisions: (a) the Global→Local→Text ordering (validated via ablation, +13 pp over reversed orders); (b) cropping from the full 1920×1080 frame rather than downsampled inputs (75.2% without HD crop vs. 83.3%); (c) absolute positional encoding for local patches (76.8% without vs. 83.3%). Key Results: On the RoboTwin 2.0 benchmark (9 tasks, 100 trials each under domain randomization), HiVLA achieves 83.3% total average vs. H-RDT 70.6%, StarVLA 46.4%, π₀.₅ 44.8%, π₀ 45.6%. On hard multi-step tasks specifically, HiVLA averages 73.2% vs. H-RDT 54.6% and the other baselines at 36-38%. Standout results: Click 3 Bells 98% vs. H-RDT 88%; Stamp Seal 76% vs. H-RDT 43%. The weakest result is Stack 3 Blocks at 37% (H-RDT 20%), indicating long-horizon sequential reasoning under visual clutter remains partially unsolved. The VLM planner achieves 90.37% mIoU for bounding box grounding and 98.57% subtask accuracy on the held-out HiVLA-HD validation split. Robustness experiments inject noise into bounding boxes and language instructions at rates 0-100%: at 100% BBox corruption, success is 57.0%; at 100% language corruption, it drops to 12.0%. Real-world evaluation on Aloha-Agilex-1.0 (30 trials per task): HiVLA vs. H-RDT for multi-object disambiguation — Pick Cup (3 distractors): 6/30 vs. 0/30; Pick Block (3 distractors): 7/30 vs. 0/30; Click Bell (2 targets): 17/30 vs. 9/30. All baselines are fine-tuned on the same HiVLA-HD dataset (~1,000 episodes per task) for 150K steps on 2 H200 GPUs. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The cascaded cross-attention conditioning mechanism within DiT blocks — specifically the combination of global features, position-preserved high-resolution local crops, and subtask language in a fixed coarse-to-fine order — is a genuine architectural contribution not present in prior hierarchical VLA work. The broader idea of hierarchical VLA with VLM planner + diffusion action expert is an extension of existing paradigms (Hi-Robot, HAMSTER, SkillDiffuser); the novelty is specifically in the mechanism for consuming bounding-box-grounded plans with spatial fidelity. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: cross-attention ordering, HD crop resolution, and absolute positional encoding are each tested individually, with performance impacts ranging from 6.5 to 13 pp. No error bars or confidence intervals are reported on the main success rate tables, and results represent averages over the last three checkpoints rather than multiple independent seeds. The VLM planner evaluation (Table 6) covers multiple model scales (4B to 32B, GPT-4o) and ablates the visual history input, which is methodologically sound. However, the HiVLA-HD training dataset is generated using the same RoboTwin 2.0 simulator that serves as the primary evaluation benchmark, with bounding boxes derived automatically from ground-truth mask IDs — this tight coupling between training data source and evaluation benchmark limits the independence of the evaluation. No code or model weights are released (only a project website is mentioned: tianshuoy.github.io/HiVLA-page). This is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): SYSTEMS profile — HiVLA is a deployed robotic system evaluated on a physical Aloha-Agilex-1.0 bimanual platform. - Practical: Trains on 2 NVIDIA H200 GPUs; inference runs at 8Hz control frequency using asynchronous VLM/policy execution, deployable on commercially available hardware. - Efficient: VLM planner requires 1.9s per reasoning step (unoptimized), but the DiT action policy generates 16-step action chunks in 0.162s; the latency mismatch is managed via asynchronous execution, though the 1.9s planning latency may be prohibitive for reactive tasks. - Effective: Evidence from both simulation and real-world deployment shows the system genuinely solves fine-grained, multi-object disambiguation tasks where global-feature-only baselines (H-RDT) collapse to 0%; the causal link between the visual grounding mechanism and performance is supported by ablations. - Scalable: Not tested beyond the 9-task, ~1000-episode-per-task scale; real-world evaluation covers only 360 total training episodes and 7 task categories; no assessment of behavior with more objects, longer horizons, or more diverse environments. The work delivers a trained system and trained model weights (implied by the project page) but no public code or dataset release is confirmed in the paper. Strengths: - The cascaded cross-attention design is concrete, well-motivated, and independently validated: each of the three components (global context, HD local crop, language skill) contributes measurably, and the ordering is shown to matter (13 pp gap between best and worst orderings in Table 4). - The emergent phantom-execution error-correction behavior — where the VLM planner acts as a semantic supervisor and re-issues subtask commands when the DiT fails silently — is a non-obvious property that arises naturally from the decoupled architecture without explicit design, and is properly characterized as emergent. - Absolute positional encoding for local crops is a simple but under-exploited idea: mapping patch coordinates back to the original camera frame resolves the spatial ambiguity that local crops normally lose, and the ablation confirms its necessity for multi-object disambiguation (e.g., Click 3 Bells drops from 98% to 80% without it). - The robustness experiment (Table 2) provides a principled analysis of error propagation in hierarchical systems: the asymmetric sensitivity to bounding-box vs. language noise reveals interpretable failure modes and confirms the DiT relies on language for semantic compliance while using global features to visually self-correct spatial errors. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note (implicitly in the discussion) that the VLM planner requires 1.9s per reasoning step, which they address via asynchronous execution. They acknowledge that zero-shot VLM performance is insufficient for precise manipulation, requiring domain-specific fine-tuning. The conclusion notes that Stack 3 Blocks performance (37%) reveals remaining challenges in long-horizon multi-step tasks under clutter. The authors do not explicitly acknowledge the evaluation circularity of training and testing on the same simulator's data pipeline. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant gap is the evaluation circularity: HiVLA-HD training data is auto-generated from RoboTwin 2.0 with ground-truth bounding boxes from mask IDs, and the primary evaluation is on the same platform — generalization to unseen environments or simulation-to-real transfer beyond the 360-episode real-world dataset is undemonstrated. Second, the real-world experiment uses only ~360 training episodes across 7 task categories, which is very small; it is unclear whether the gains over H-RDT generalize beyond the specific object set tested. Third, there are no error bars or statistical tests on any table, making it impossible to assess whether the 12.4 pp simulation gap over H-RDT is statistically robust or sensitive to checkpoint selection. Fourth, the comparison to open-source visually-grounded hierarchical systems is weak by the authors' own admission (DexGraspVLA is restricted to grasping; HiRobot and Gemini Robotics are closed-source), so the gains may partially reflect the absence of a strong comparable baseline rather than architectural superiority. Fifth, the VLM planner's 210K fine-tuning dialogues are derived from the same simulation data, and its 98.57% subtask accuracy is measured on a held-out split of that same distribution — out-of-distribution subtask accuracy is not evaluated. Bottom Line: HiVLA is a well-executed systems paper with a concrete architectural contribution (cascaded cross-attention for grounded plan consumption) and solid ablations; robotics researchers building hierarchical VLA systems should read it for the cross-attention ordering insight and the emergent error-correction finding, but should treat the headline numbers with caution given the narrow, self-referential benchmark setup and absence of cross-environment transfer results.
Recommended 2604.14125 PDF
#136 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Free Geometry: Refining 3D Reconstruction from Longer Versions of Itself (14 ▲ )

Yuhang Dai, Xingyi Yang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (62) and Resilience (50) pulled down by missing Test3R comparison — the single most relevant baseline for the exact claimed advantage is absent from all quantitative tables. Novelty (65) asymmetric encoder teacher-student for 3D TTA is a genuine combination. Completeness (55) authors don't acknowledge the Test3R omission or per-dataset hyperparameter tuning. Impact (60) code released, lightweight, but gains are modest (+3-5%). Weighted: 0.20*65+0.25*62+0.15*60+0.15*55+0.15*50+0.10*60 ≈ 59.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 60
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Free Geometry, a plug-and-play test-time adaptation (TTA) framework that self-supervises feed-forward multi-view 3D reconstruction models by using full-view backbone features as a teacher to recalibrate partial-view LoRA-adapted features, requiring no 3D ground-truth labels. - Why it's important: Feed-forward 3D reconstruction models (Depth Anything 3, VGGT) are frozen after training and degrade on novel scenes under occlusions, specularities, and repetitive textures, while collecting 3D ground truth for retraining costs prohibitively in time and equipment. - How it does it: An asymmetric teacher-student distillation at the encoder feature level exploits the architectural guarantee that cross-view attention produces strictly better features with more views, enforcing intra-frame magnitude/direction alignment and cross-frame relational geometry (KL-divergence distributions and triangle angles in feature space) between full-view and masked-view branches. - The good: Consistent improvements across 4 benchmarks and 2 distinct SOTA base models at all tested view counts (4–100), completing adaptation in under 2 minutes per dataset on a single GPU with no annotation cost. - The missing: Test3R (NeurIPS 2025), the most directly comparable prior TTA method for 3D reconstruction, is discussed in related work but never compared against quantitatively — the single most consequential gap in the evaluation. - Read it or not: Researchers deploying feed-forward 3D reconstruction in novel environments and practitioners exploring lightweight TTA for 3D vision should read this; those focused on training-time improvements or already achieving strong performance on standard benchmarks can deprioritize it. Problem & Motivation Feed-forward multi-view 3D reconstruction models such as Depth Anything 3 (DA3) and VGGT follow a train-then-freeze paradigm: after large-scale pre-training, their parameters are fixed at inference time. This zero-shot rigidity causes geometric errors — particularly around occlusion boundaries, specular surfaces, and low-texture regions — when the models encounter novel test distributions. Collecting labeled 3D ground truth (laser scanning, structured light) for domain-specific retraining is prohibitively expensive and often infeasible. Prior test-time adaptation methods designed for classification (TENT: entropy minimization, TTT/TTT++: rotation prediction, MEMO: augmentation consistency) rely on supervision signals loosely coupled to 3D geometry. Test3R, the closest prior work, enforces symmetric consistency between overlapping view pairs but lacks a quality hierarchy: when one reconstruction in a pair is poor, symmetric consistency pulls both toward their average, risking regression to the mean. Key Idea The core insight is that multi-view cross-view attention is strictly monotone with respect to view count at the encoder level: feeding more views into the multi-view transformer produces richer cross-view correspondences, yielding geometrically more consistent intermediate features. This is empirically validated in Table 1 (8-view features are strictly better than 4-view features at the same positions). This quality ordering is architecturally guaranteed — not stochastic — which makes full-view backbone output a reliable, label-free supervision signal. The trick is to exploit this ordering as an asymmetric teacher (full-view, frozen) supervising a student (partial-view, LoRA-adapted) operating on the same scene. Methodology Free Geometry instantiates a teacher-student pipeline operating on each test dataset independently. Given a test sequence of N frames (default N=8), the teacher branch passes all N frames through the frozen backbone, extracting intermediate features F_full without computing gradients. The student branch passes M unmasked frames (default M=4, even-indexed) through the same backbone augmented with LoRA modules (rank r=32, α=32) inserted into the multi-view transformer blocks; only LoRA weights and a trainable camera token are updated (~21M parameters for DA3 Giant out of total backbone parameters, <0.2%). The total loss is L_geo = L_intra + L_cross. L_intra enforces both magnitude and directional alignment between matched token pairs at unmasked frame positions: it combines a Huber loss (δ threshold) on feature vectors with a cosine dissimilarity term, applied jointly to patch tokens and camera tokens. L_cross is a cross-frame relational loss that transfers geometric information from the masked frames (which the student never sees) by constructing triplets (p, k_j, q) in feature space. For each reference token p' in the full branch and its aligned counterpart p in the partial branch, K=4 anchor tokens are retrieved from masked frames in the full branch using extreme cosine similarity — the top-2 most similar and bottom-2 least similar, providing both correspondence-consistent and contrastive geometric context. The cross-frame loss enforces that the student's pairwise softmax distributions (temperature-scaled similarity between p, k_j, q) match the teacher's via KL divergence (three terms), and that the cosine angles of the virtual triangle formed by (p, k_j, q) in feature space match the corresponding triangle in the full branch. This geometry-preserving relational constraint propagates structural information from hidden frames into the partial-view representation without requiring the student to observe them. Optimization uses AdamW (weight decay 1e-5), cosine LR schedule with 15% warmup, FP16 mixed precision, batch size 4, 3–5 epochs, 5–20 samples per scene. Dataset-specific LR and epoch settings are used (ETH3D: 1e-7–1e-4, 3 epochs; 7Scenes: 1e-8–5e-5, 3 epochs; ScanNet++: 1e-8–1e-5, 3 epochs; HiRoom: 1e-7–1e-4, 5 epochs). Adaptation takes approximately 2 minutes per dataset on a single RTX Pro 6000 GPU. Three critical design decisions: (1) Feature-level vs. output-level supervision — since decoders are per-view and cross-view information is entirely in the shared backbone, adapting at feature level avoids decoder instability and saves memory by not computing decoder forward passes for the teacher; well-justified architecturally. (2) Asymmetric quality hierarchy — full-view is always teacher, never student; avoids the regression-to-mean failure mode of symmetric methods; justified by Table 1 monotonicity evidence. (3) Mixed anchor selection (top + bottom cosine similarity) — validated in supplementary Table 2 against top-only (0.2474 F1) and random (0.2418 F1), with mixed achieving 0.2475 F1. Key Results Evaluated on ETH3D (indoor/outdoor, laser ground truth), ScanNet++ (large-scale indoor), 7Scenes (RGB-D, repetitive textures), and HiRoom (high-res, reflective surfaces) with N ∈ {4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 100} views, averaged over 3 random seeds. Metrics: AUC@3 and AUC@30 for pose accuracy (higher is better), F1-score and Chamfer Distance for reconstruction quality. Representative absolute results (4-view regime, main paper Table 2): VGGT on ETH3D improves from 0.157 to 0.178 AUC@3 (+13.4%) and 0.102 to 0.110 F1. DA3 on ETH3D improves from 0.286 to 0.305 AUC@3 (+6.6%) and 0.207 to 0.209 F1. VGGT on HiRoom: 0.421 to 0.441 AUC@3, 0.276 to 0.307 F1. DA3 on HiRoom: 0.708 to 0.719 AUC@3, 0.557 to 0.578 F1. Average relative improvement across 4 datasets and 4 view counts (Table 3): +3.73% to +5.33% AUC@3 and +2.85% to +4.51% F1 for VGGT; +2.61% to +2.89% AUC@3 and +0.70% to +2.85% F1 for DA3. Gains are consistent across N=4,8,16,32 and transfer to N=64 and N=100 even though adaptation uses an 8→4 signal, indicating improved geometric representations rather than view-count-specific overfitting. Gains are smallest on ScanNet++ (frozen baselines already perform well), confirming the method provides the most value where baseline models are weakest. Baselines (DA3 Giant, VGGT) are current SOTA models from ICLR 2026 and CVPR 2025 — strong and relevant. No comparison to Test3R in any quantitative table. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel contribution is asymmetric encoder-level teacher-student distillation that exploits the architectural monotonicity of multi-view cross-view attention as a quality-ordered self-supervision signal for test-time adaptation of 3D reconstruction. The use of RKD-style triangle-angle relational geometry in feature space for transferring geometric constraints from masked frames is a new application of relational distillation to the 3D TTA setting. LoRA for parameter-efficient adaptation and teacher-student distillation are individually standard ideas; the novelty lies in combining them with architecture-motivated asymmetric supervision and masked-view construction to create a geometry-aware, label-free TTA framework for feed-forward 3D models. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations (Table 4 in main paper, ETH3D, N=4): removing L_intra drops AUC@3 from 37.88 to 35.87 and F1 from 0.2475 to 0.2324; removing L_cross drops F1 from 0.2475 to 0.2190, confirming both components are necessary. Supplementary ablations cover anchor selection strategy and LoRA rank (r ∈ {8,16,32,64} — rank 32 is optimal). Feature alignment is directly verified in Table 5 (supplementary): Free Geometry reduces MSE from 0.9039 to 0.8384 and improves cosine similarity from 0.8581 to 0.8684 for VGGT last-layer features. Results averaged over 3 random seeds for frame sampling, reducing variance from the sparse-view stochasticity. Standard deviations are not reported in any table. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/hiteacherIamhumble/Free-Geometry. Dataset-specific hyperparameters (LR range, epochs, samples/scene) are tuned per dataset — it is not stated whether these were selected using validation performance on the same splits used for final reporting, which could constitute a mild form of evaluation leakage. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.14048v1, 15 Apr 2026); not yet peer-reviewed. Methodological concern: Test3R (NeurIPS 2025), the most relevant prior TTA baseline for this exact problem, is described in related work but absent from all quantitative comparisons — this is the most significant reproducibility and evaluation gap. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on a single RTX Pro 6000 GPU (24GB); single-GPU deployment is feasible on commercially available hardware with FP16 mixed precision. - Efficient: Adaptation completes in under 2 minutes per dataset; LoRA rank=32 adds ~21M trainable parameters to DA3 Giant, a small fraction of total backbone parameters; teacher forward passes require no gradient storage, reducing memory overhead. - Effective: Evidence across 4 benchmarks and 2 base models at 6 view counts shows consistent pose and reconstruction improvement; gains are real but modest (+3–5% relative), are near-zero on already-strong baselines (ScanNet++), and do not establish dominance over Test3R because that comparison is missing. - Scalable: Tested at N=4 to N=100 views per scene, which is the practical operational range for feed-forward reconstruction; no cross-dataset generalization test (each dataset adapted independently with separate LoRA weights). The paper delivers a software artifact (code on GitHub); no pre-adapted model weights are released since the adaptation is dataset-specific. Strengths - The architectural motivation — cross-view attention monotonicity provides a quality guarantee for the teacher — is principled and clearly distinguishes Free Geometry from entropy- or rotation-based TTA methods, which rely on supervision signals loosely coupled to geometry. - Practical efficiency is genuine: <2 minutes per dataset, no 3D labels, no decoder forward passes for the teacher, plug-and-play on architecturally distinct models (DA3 uses ViT-Giant + global attention; VGGT uses its own geometry-based transformer). - Generalization across view counts (trained on 8→4 but transferring to 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 100 views) is a non-trivial and well-evidenced finding, suggesting that the method improves the geometric quality of backbone representations broadly rather than overfitting to a specific view-count regime. - The cross-frame relational loss design (using extreme cosine similarity anchors to create both correspondences and contrasts) is thoughtful and empirically validated against alternatives. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors note that gains diminish at higher view counts (N=16,32) because abundant observations already provide strong geometric constraints, and are smaller on datasets where frozen baselines already perform well (ScanNet++). The paper does not include a dedicated limitations section. The dataset-wise adaptation requirement (needing multiple scenes per target dataset, not single-scene zero-shot), the absence of Test3R as a quantitative baseline, and the per-dataset hyperparameter tuning are not explicitly acknowledged as limitations. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Test3R (NeurIPS 2025) is absent from all quantitative tables. Since Free Geometry explicitly claims superiority over Test3R's symmetric consistency approach in the related work, failing to compare against it empirically is a significant credibility gap — the key claimed advantage (avoiding regression to the mean via asymmetric quality hierarchy) cannot be confirmed without this comparison. - Dataset-specific hyperparameter selection (LR, epochs, samples/scene) from Table 1 of the supplementary raises the question of whether these were chosen using held-out validation or the same test sequences. If the latter, the evaluation conflates adaptation and generalization. - The method requires a batch of test scenes from a target dataset; the minimum number of scenes needed for stable adaptation is not characterized, limiting understanding of how it behaves in true single-scene deployment. - No failure mode analysis: the paper shows no cases where Free Geometry hurts performance. Given that ScanNet++ gains are near zero, understanding when the method is neutral vs. harmful would strengthen trust in deployment. - The Chamfer Distance improvements are mixed (Table 5 supplementary): in some configurations Free Geometry actually worsens CD despite improving F1, and this tension is not analyzed. Bottom Line A practitioner deploying DA3 or VGGT in challenging novel environments gains a practical, lightweight tool with no annotation cost and consistent if modest gains, but the missing Test3R comparison leaves unanswered whether Free Geometry is the best available approach for label-free TTA in feed-forward 3D reconstruction.
Recommended 2604.14048 PDF
#137 — Moderately Innovative (58)

RAD-2: Scaling Reinforcement Learning in a Generator-Discriminator Framework (21 ▲ )

Hao Gao, Shaoyu Chen, Yifan Zhu, Yuehao Song, Wenyu Liu, Qian Zhang, Xinggang Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (70) are real — TC-GRPO's latched execution with sparse decision-point advantages and OGO's longitudinal reward-guided editing address documented credit-assignment instabilities in a non-obvious way. Rigor (45) is the major drag: largest gains are in the authors' own BEV-Warp simulator, 3DGS margins are small, no code/data/model release, 50k-hour proprietary training set, no seeds, and real-world claims are purely qualitative. Completeness (55) and Resilience (50) are weakened by the absence of fair comparisons to other RL-fine-tuned diffusion planners and the environment-self-evaluation concern. Impact (45) — no software artifact released. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*45+0.15*70+0.15*55+0.15*50+0.10*45 = 55.75 ≈ 56, rounded to 58 reflecting the methodological contribution's reusability. Rigor and reproducibility pull it below 'strong execution' tier.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 45
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RAD-2, a generator-discriminator framework that couples a diffusion-based trajectory generator with an RL-optimized discriminator for closed-loop autonomous driving motion planning, along with TC-GRPO and OGO optimization algorithms and a BEV-Warp simulation environment. - Why it's important: Diffusion-based imitation learning planners suffer from stochastic training instabilities and lack corrective feedback, causing unsafe behaviors in closed-loop deployment where decisions compound over time. - How it does it: The key trick is decoupling RL from the high-dimensional trajectory space by restricting reward optimization to the discriminator (low-dimensional scalar output), then indirectly shifting the generator's distribution via structured longitudinal reward-guided trajectory edits rather than sparse gradient signals. - The good: RAD-2 reduces collision rate from 0.533 (ResAD) to 0.234 and at-fault collision rate from 0.264 to 0.092 on 512 held-out BEV-Warp safety scenarios, representing roughly a 56% and 65% improvement respectively. - The missing: All closed-loop training and primary evaluation use a proprietary in-house dataset and the authors' own BEV-Warp simulator; gains on the independent photorealistic 3DGS benchmark are substantially smaller, and no code or data is released. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on RL fine-tuning of generative planners or closed-loop autonomous driving; the TC-GRPO and OGO formulations address real credit-assignment problems, but practitioners should note the proprietary data dependency and limited independent validation. Problem & Motivation: High-level autonomous driving requires motion planners that model multimodal future trajectories while remaining robust in closed-loop interactions. Diffusion-based imitation learning (IL) planners can represent complex trajectory distributions but suffer from two failure modes: (1) stochastic instabilities during training due to sparse scalar RL rewards applied over high-dimensional, temporally correlated trajectory spaces, causing unstable optimization gradients; (2) causal confusion from open-loop IL training, where the model learns correlations between states and actions rather than causal driving factors. Prior IL+RL hybrids either apply RL directly to raw trajectory outputs (severe credit assignment problems due to high dimensionality) or rely on fixed discrete candidate vocabularies (unable to represent the full continuous trajectory manifold). The gap is a principled method to apply RL feedback to generative continuous planners without destabilizing the diffusion model. Key Idea: The core insight is that RL should optimize a low-dimensional scoring function (the discriminator) rather than the high-dimensional trajectory generator directly; the generator then learns from structured longitudinal edits to collected rollouts — reward-guided acceleration/deceleration adjustments — rather than from sparse environment rewards applied to raw trajectory samples. This indirection resolves the credit assignment problem while preserving the generator's multimodal diversity. Methodology: The framework has two learned components: a diffusion-based generator G_θ (ResAD backbone with DiT planning head) that samples M candidate trajectories from noise, and a Transformer-based discriminator D_φ that scores candidates by cross-attending trajectory embeddings against BEV scene features. The joint policy Π_{θ,φ} is defined as the expectation over generator samples weighted by discriminator scores, framed as minimizing KL-divergence to an ideal risk-neutral distribution. Training proceeds in three stages. (i) Pre-training: G is initialized from 50,000 hours of real-world driving data via IL; D is initialized from the same planning head weights. (ii) Discriminator optimization via TC-GRPO: closed-loop rollouts are collected with trajectory reuse — once a trajectory τ* is selected at time t, it is re-executed for H_reuse steps (optimal=8) without re-planning to ensure temporal coherence. Rewards use a safety-criticality signal r_coll (worst-case Time-to-Collision margin over the rollout) and navigational efficiency r_eff (ego progress relative to expert trajectory, bounded in [1.05, 1.10]). Group-relative advantages are computed across G rollouts from the same initial state. The GRPO clipped objective operates only at sparse decision points (trajectory re-sample events), preventing dilution by high-frequency mode-switching. An adaptive entropy regularization term prevents score collapse. (iii) Generator optimization via OGO: each collected rollout τ^raw is edited longitudinally — safety-driven deceleration (compress travel distance by factor ρ when TTC < γ_safe) or efficiency-driven acceleration (extend by ρ' when progress is low and TTC is safe) — yielding τ^opt that preserves the spatial path while adjusting temporal progression. These form dataset D_opt for generator fine-tuning via MSE loss. The 8:1 discriminator-to-generator update ratio ensures the discriminator stays current while preventing generator overfitting. BEV-Warp simulation: to avoid expensive image rendering, the environment synthesizes future observations by applying a SE(2) warp matrix (derived from pose deviation between simulated and logged ego-state) to reference BEV feature maps via bilinear interpolation. This exploits spatial equivariance of BEV representations and supports high-throughput closed-loop rollout. Critical design decisions: (1) trajectory reuse / latched execution — well-justified as necessary for coherent credit assignment; without it, mode-switching decorrelates rewards from decisions; (2) OGO longitudinal editing rather than direct RL on generator — justified by stability arguments and validated in ablation Table 4; (3) discriminator initialized from planning head weights — Table 7 shows random initialization degrades CR from 0.337 to 0.426. Key Results: Closed-loop BEV-Warp (512 safety + 512 efficiency held-out clips): RAD-2 achieves CR=0.234, AF-CR=0.092, Safety@1=0.730, Safety@2=0.596, EP-Mean=0.988, EP@1.0=0.736, EP@0.9=0.984. The strongest prior baseline ResAD scores CR=0.533, AF-CR=0.264, Safety@1=0.418, Safety@1=0.281, EP@1.0=0.516. This is a 56% CR reduction and 3.6× AF-CR improvement. Closed-loop 3DGS photorealistic benchmark (256 safety clips): RAD-2 CR=0.250, Safety@1=0.723, Safety@2=0.644 vs. Senna-2 CR=0.269, Safety@1=0.667, Safety@2=0.565 and RAD CR=0.310. Gains are real but substantially smaller in this independent environment. Open-loop Senna-2 benchmark (6 scenarios): RAD-2 FDE=0.553m, ADE=0.208m, CR=0.142%, DCR=0.138%, SCR=0.004% — best across all metrics, with CR roughly half that of Senna-2 (0.288%). Ablation Table 4 isolates contributions: IL alone (ID=1) achieves CR=0.533; adding OGO generator fine-tuning (ID=2) drops CR to 0.287; adding joint generator+discriminator with RL (ID=5) reaches CR=0.234. Inference-time scaling (Table 10) shows EP@1.0 improving monotonically from M=8 (0.667) to M=64 (0.816) but CR stabilizing around 0.234-0.252 for M≥32. Baselines are recent (2024-2026 arXiv preprints and CVPR/ECCV papers): TransFuser, VAD, VADv2, GenAD, ResAD, Senna, Senna-2. These are appropriate and current for the field. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. TC-GRPO is a genuine methodological contribution — adapting GRPO (from LLM post-training) to temporally correlated continuous trajectory spaces via latched execution and sparse decision-point advantages is non-obvious and addresses a documented instability in the literature. OGO's reward-guided longitudinal editing as a generator update signal is also novel. BEV-Warp is an engineering novelty for efficient feature-level simulation. The generator-discriminator decomposition concept has precedent in GAN literature and vocabulary-based scoring, but applying it to jointly optimize a diffusion generator with a closed-loop RL discriminator in this form is new. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present (Tables 4-9) covering training pipeline components, H_reuse sensitivity, clip filtering, discriminator initialization, group size, entropy regularization, and scenario composition — this is thorough. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported on the main results (single-run numbers). Code and model weights are not released; a project page (https://hgao-cv.github.io/RAD-2) is listed but contains no data or code as of the paper date. The primary training set (50,000 hours of proprietary driving data, 50k closed-loop clips) is not publicly available, making reproduction impossible without equivalent infrastructure. The primary evaluation simulator (BEV-Warp) is custom-built by the same team, creating a concern that the largest reported gains (56% CR reduction) are measured in an environment the authors designed and control. The 3DGS results on Senna-2 (an external benchmark) are more credible but show smaller gains. Real-world deployment is described qualitatively (urban traffic in China) with no quantitative safety metrics.
