Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: evaluators may not have been familiar with all AI tools candidates chose, limiting real-time interpretive ability; evaluator-created tasks introduce variability in type and complexity that may have shaped findings; the live coding interview context does not capture longitudinal, collaborative, or real-world software engineering (no version control, team coordination, long-term maintenance); task design was not varied systematically to study how task characteristics interact with AI use and enaction. These are confronted directly and the authors call for future work to address them — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The Python monoculture (11/12 sessions) is unacknowledged as a limitation; whether findings generalize to compiled languages, systems programming, or non-ML task types is entirely open. - The study cannot distinguish valid adaptation (evaluators appropriately updating for AI-era practice) from validity-threatening drift (evaluators losing reliable signal about actual competence); this is the most consequential unresolved question for practitioners and the paper does not attempt to adjudicate it. - The social stigma finding — one candidate deliberately avoided AI due to prior negative experiences — is empirically interesting and practically important (it implies AI-permitted interviews may not be bias-neutral) but receives only brief treatment without deeper analysis. - Evaluator participants did not change their tasks after learning AI would be available, which the authors note, but the downstream implication — that task design is a largely uncontrolled variable — is not fully explored as a threat to the findings' generalizability across task types. - No outcome validity check: the study does not examine whether evaluator assessments predicted any ground-truth measure of coding competence, leaving open whether the evolving enactions represent genuine expertise or performance theater. Bottom Line A well-executed qualitative CHI paper that introduces a useful theoretical lens and documents genuinely novel empirical dynamics around AI-mediated expertise enactment in technical hiring — practitioners designing AI-permitted interview processes and HCI researchers studying knowledge work transformation will find it worth reading, but those needing causal claims or generalizable quantitative guidance will find the evidence base too thin.
Citations
#144 — Moderately Innovative (55)

QuanBench+: A Unified Multi-Framework Benchmark for LLM-Based Quantum Code Generation (109 ▲ )

Ali Slim, Haydar Hamieh, Jawad Kotaich, Yehya Ghosn, Mahdi Chehimi, Ammar Mohanna, Hasan Abed Al Kader Hammoud, Bernard Ghanem · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (60) — cross-framework alignment holding task intent fixed while varying only the SDK is a clean experimental design novelty. Rigor (55) pulled down by small 42-task scale with no error bars or significance tests, workshop-paper status, and no contamination analysis despite the Qiskit > Cirq > PennyLane ordering potentially reflecting pretraining data skew. Non-Obviousness (55) — the feedback-resolves-surface-errors-but-not-semantics finding is moderately insightful. Completeness (55) and Resilience (50) — 74% task-category imbalance and lack of per-category breakdowns weaken aggregate claims. Impact (55) — reproducible harness released, but limited to three SDKs on a small benchmark. Weighted: 0.20*60 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*55 = 55.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces QuanBench+, a 42-task benchmark spanning Qiskit, PennyLane, and Cirq that evaluates LLM quantum code generation via aligned cross-framework tasks, executable functional tests, KL-divergence grading for probabilistic outputs, and feedback-based repair across 12 frontier and open-weight models. - Why it's important: All prior quantum code benchmarks are single-framework, making it impossible to separate failures caused by weak quantum reasoning from failures caused by unfamiliarity with a specific SDK's API conventions — a distinction that directly affects how practitioners should invest in model improvement. - How it does it: The benchmark holds task intent constant while varying only the target framework, using a unified grading harness with KL-divergence acceptance (threshold τ=0.05, calibrated from canonical execution variance) for probabilistic tasks and a feedback loop of up to 5 repair attempts triggered by runtime exceptions or wrong outputs. - The good: The consistent Qiskit > Cirq > PennyLane difficulty ordering across all 12 models — surviving both Pass@5 sampling and feedback-based repair — provides compelling evidence that framework-familiarity is a durable, not superficial, bottleneck for LLM quantum code generation. - The missing: With only 42 tasks (74% quantum algorithms), the aggregate metrics are category-dominated and too small to rule out task-sampling artifacts; there is no analysis of training data contamination, and the observed framework gap could partly reflect pretraining data skew toward Qiskit rather than API complexity alone. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building quantum programming assistants, benchmarking domain-specific code generation, or designing fine-tuning curricula for quantum LLMs; the 42-task scale limits its authority as a standalone benchmark. Problem & Motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed for quantum code generation, yet evaluation remains siloed within single frameworks (Qiskit, PennyLane, or Cirq individually). When a model fails a quantum programming task, single-framework benchmarks cannot determine whether the failure reflects weak quantum algorithmic reasoning or mere unfamiliarity with a particular SDK's API conventions. This confound affects model developers (who cannot diagnose what to train on), practitioners (who cannot predict cross-framework portability), and benchmark designers (who cannot compare models fairly across ecosystems). Prior work — Qiskit HumanEval, QHackBench, QCircuitBench, QuanBench — each targets one ecosystem, leaving framework familiarity as an uncontrolled variable in all reported results. Key Idea: Hold task intent constant while varying only the target framework: adapt the same 42 quantum programming tasks to Qiskit, PennyLane, and Cirq with aligned prompts, canonical solutions, and a single grading harness, so that performance differences across frameworks cleanly measure framework-familiarity effects rather than task difficulty variation. Methodology: QuanBench+ derives its task set from the prior QuanBench benchmark (Guo et al., 2025), retaining 42 tasks across three categories: quantum algorithms (31), state preparation (6), and gate decomposition (5); two tasks were removed for ambiguous cross-framework grading. Each task is adapted to three frameworks by modifying prompts to enforce correct library imports and API conventions while preserving the functional objective. Canonical solutions are unified. The automated evaluation pipeline: (P1) parse LLM completions for executable code, (P2) execute in a sandboxed Python 3.10 environment (Qiskit v0.46.0, Cirq v1.6.1, PennyLane v0.43.1), (P3) compare via deterministic checks or KL divergence (τ=0.05, calibrated at the 99.7th percentile of canonical execution variability across R=1000 reference runs per probabilistic task). Pass@1 and Pass@5 use the standard unbiased estimator; feedback-based repair (Pass@1 FB) provides the model with runtime exception traces or wrong-output signals and allows up to 5 correction attempts. Two prompting conditions — prefill (imports + function signature) and no-prefill — are studied. Three critical design decisions: (1) KL divergence rather than process fidelity is the correctness criterion — well-justified because functionally equivalent circuits can have low unitary overlap and fidelity penalizes valid alternative decompositions as false negatives; (2) task-success rather than reference-circuit comparison is the grading target — defensible and consistent with HumanEval-style evaluation; (3) greedy decoding (temperature 0.0) for Pass@1 and temperature 0.8 for Pass@5 — standard but means Pass@1 and Pass@1(FB) measure a specific decoding regime rather than expected accuracy. Key Results: One-shot Pass@1 best scores: Gemini-3-Pro achieves 59.5% on Qiskit and 54.8% on Cirq; GPT-5.1 achieves 42.9% on PennyLane. The Qiskit > Cirq > PennyLane ordering holds across all 12 models. After feedback-based repair (Pass@1 FB, ≤5 attempts): GPT-5.1 reaches 83.3% on Qiskit and 66.7% on PennyLane; Gemini-3-Pro reaches 76.2% on Cirq. The weakest model (Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct) plateaus at 19.0% on all frameworks regardless of feedback. Feedback gains are front-loaded: the jump from attempt 1→2 is the largest; gains diminish sharply after attempt 3. Prefill (providing boilerplate) benefits weaker and mid-tier models more than frontier models, indicating setup friction is secondary to semantic reasoning ability. Pre-feedback error distribution (total 977 wrong tasks): wrong answers 46.7%, logic errors 25.0%, missing method/gate 11.8%, shape mismatch 8.0%, syntax errors 4.7%, qubit specification errors 3.9%. Post-feedback (total 665 wrong tasks), the budget shifts toward semantic errors: wrong answers 53.4%, logic errors 22.0%. Baselines span current frontier (GPT-5.1, Gemini-3-Pro, DeepSeek-R1, Claude-3.7-Sonnet, GLM-4.7) and mid-tier open-weight models — a strong and current comparison set. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The cross-framework alignment methodology — holding task intent fixed while varying only the target framework to isolate familiarity effects — is the genuine novelty. The grading harness, Pass@k metrics, and feedback loop apply established evaluation machinery from classical code-generation benchmarks (HumanEval lineage) to a new domain. The empirical finding that the framework difficulty gap is durable across sampling regimes and feedback repair is a concrete, non-trivial result. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations in the method-paper sense (appropriate for a benchmark paper). Results are reported as point estimates across 42 tasks with no error bars or significance tests — given the small task count, sampling variance around these percentages could be meaningful and is unquantified. The KL threshold calibration procedure is methodologically careful and fully documented (Appendix C). Source code is publicly released at https://github.com/JawadKotaichh/quanbench-plus with fixed library versions enabling reproduction. Canonical solutions are unified across frameworks, reducing grading inconsistency. Note: this is a workshop paper at ICLR 2026, not a peer-reviewed venue for full papers; results carry commensurately lower confidence than a main-track publication. Deployability: Profile: EMPIRICAL (the paper evaluates existing LLMs on a benchmark; the benchmark itself is the delivered artifact). - Practical: Runs in a standard Python 3.10 environment with pip-installable quantum libraries; any researcher with LLM API access can reproduce the evaluation. - Efficient: Harness overhead is low — dominated by LLM API call costs; quantum circuit simulation on 42 small tasks is computationally trivial. - Effective: The benchmark successfully distinguishes framework difficulty levels and diagnoses error type distributions, fulfilling its stated diagnostic purpose. - Scalable: 42 tasks is a small benchmark; whether the framework-difficulty pattern and error taxonomy hold at 200+ tasks or generalize to additional frameworks (AWS Braket, Q#, Pennylane Lightning backends) is untested and not claimed. The paper delivers a software artifact (benchmark harness + task set) with a public GitHub release. Strengths: - The core design — aligned tasks across three frameworks under a single grading harness — directly enables causal attribution of framework familiarity effects that all prior single-framework benchmarks cannot provide; this is a clean experimental contribution. - KL-divergence acceptance calibrated from canonical execution variance is a technically sound and principled solution to the grading problem for probabilistic quantum outputs, superior to an arbitrary threshold. - The error-type taxonomy (wrong answer, logic error, shape mismatch, qubit specification, syntax) yields actionable diagnostics: it shows that feedback effectively resolves surface errors but not semantic reasoning failures, which is a specific and useful finding for model developers. - The finding that post-feedback residual errors concentrate more heavily in semantic mistakes (wrong answers rise from 46.7% to 53.4% of the error budget) is a non-obvious, quantified result about the limits of execution-trace feedback. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge: (1) 42 tasks is too small to cover the full long tail of real-world quantum development; (2) category imbalance — quantum algorithms account for 74% of tasks, amplifying their influence on aggregate metrics; (3) framework versioning means models may fail due to stale API recall rather than algorithmic error; (4) only Pass@1, Pass@5, and Pass@1(FB) are reported — prompt robustness, longer repair horizons, and tool-augmented workflows are excluded; (5) only Qiskit, PennyLane, and Cirq are covered. The authors confront these directly and propose extensions as future work, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Training data contamination is not addressed: if frontier models were trained on substantially more Qiskit-flavored quantum code than PennyLane or Cirq code (plausible given Qiskit's longer history and larger community corpus), the observed framework gap could partly reflect pretraining data imbalance rather than intrinsic API complexity differences — the paper attributes the gap to "framework-specific exposure" but does not distinguish training-time exposure from deployment-time familiarity. Per-category results are not broken out in the main body: whether the Qiskit > Cirq > PennyLane ordering holds uniformly across quantum algorithms, gate decomposition, and state preparation tasks, or is driven by one category, is unanswered; this matters because decomposition and state preparation tasks may have very different framework-sensitivity profiles. No per-task difficulty analysis explains which task properties (circuit depth, qubit count, algorithmic complexity) predict the persistent white columns in the heatmaps. The feedback signal design (raw exception trace or wrong-output notification) is not ablated against richer structured hints, leaving unclear whether the feedback ceiling reflects LLM reasoning limits or feedback signal quality. Bottom Line: QuanBench+ is a workshop paper that makes a focused, reproducible contribution — a cross-framework quantum code benchmark with a principled grading harness — and delivers a concrete empirical finding about the durability of framework-familiarity gaps, making it worth reading for practitioners who build or evaluate quantum programming assistants, though its 42-task scope limits its authority and it should be treated as a foundation to extend rather than a definitive resource.
Recommended 2604.08570 PDF
#145 — Moderately Innovative (55)

FGeo-ISRL: A MCTS-Enhanced Deep Reinforcement Learning System for Plane Geometry Problem-Solving via Inverse Search

mdpi.com · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (55) moderate — good ablations, code released, but single in-house dataset, no seeds despite MCTS stochasticity, SSTA metric aligned with training loss (Goodhart). Resilience (40) weak — same-group FGeo-HyperGNet outperforms this system (88.36% vs 83.14% GPSA), especially at L6 (56.67% vs 29.21%), and is buried in Table 2 rather than addressed head-on. Novelty (55) — inverse search is a sensible mechanistic contribution but components are standard. Impact (60) — working code released, verifiable proofs. Completeness (50) — failure modes acknowledged but framing of SOTA is misleading.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 50 Resilience 40 Impact 60
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FGeo-ISRL, a neural-symbolic system that combines BERT-based policy and value networks with MCTS and a novel inverse search strategy to automate plane geometry theorem proving within the FormalGeo formal framework. - Why it's important: Automated geometry solvers degrade sharply on multi-step proofs because forward and backward search spaces grow exponentially, blocking reliable deductive AI for even high-school-level problems. - How it does it: The search initializes from the merged state of both given conditions and the target goal simultaneously, then iteratively incorporates theorem premises backward using a BERT-base policy network guided by MCTS and a BERT-base value network trained via two-stage supervised pre-training followed by policy-gradient RL. - The good: FGeo-ISRL achieves 83.14% GPSA on FormalGeo7K versus 62.60% for the next strongest published baseline (NGS), with ablations confirming the value network and MCTS each contribute independently and substantially. - The missing: FGeo-HyperGNet (from the same research group, shown only in Table 2) achieves 88.36% GPSA overall and dramatically outperforms FGeo-ISRL at hard levels (L5: 56.67% vs. 60.22%; L6: 56.67% vs. 29.21%), yet the authors frame FGeo-ISRL as the best method without adequately addressing this discrepancy. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in neural-symbolic theorem proving who want a concrete MCTS+RL integration with ablations; can be skipped by practitioners seeking the strongest geometry solver, which by the paper's own data is FGeo-HyperGNet. Problem & Motivation: Automated plane geometry theorem proving requires building chains of deductive steps over a formal knowledge base of theorems and axioms. Traditional symbolic forward search (expanding from given conditions) and backward search (decomposing goals into sub-goals) both suffer exponential search-space blowup as proof length increases, with performance collapsing on L5–L6 difficulty problems. LLM-based approaches produce intuitive but unverifiable reasoning that cannot be checked step-by-step. The specific gap is the absence of a system that combines formal verifiability with a learned heuristic that generalizes across diverse geometric structures without requiring massive labeled intermediate proof data. Key Idea: The core trick is inverse search: rather than proceeding purely from conditions forward or from the goal backward, the system initializes its search state as the union of the given conditions and the target goal, then iteratively incorporates the premises of applicable theorems into this merged state until the accumulated condition set satisfies the goal. By jointly encoding both what is known and what must be proven from the outset, the search leverages constraint from both ends simultaneously, pruning branches that cannot bridge the condition–goal gap—exploiting the geometric correlations that pure forward or backward search ignores. Methodology: The geometry problem is modeled as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) over the FormalGeo formal system. The initial state S_0 is the union of problem conditions and goal (inverse search). At each step, the agent selects a theorem from an action space of 234 branch-theorems (196 base theorems expanded to handle symmetric/orientation variants). The policy network (PN), a BERT-base model fine-tuned with multi-class cross-entropy loss, outputs a probability distribution over 234 theorem branches. The value network (VN), also BERT-base, is trained with MSE regression to predict cumulative reward V(S_t) ∈ [0,1] using L2 regularization. The reward signal is r_t = σ(w^T V(S_{t+1}) + b) − σ(w^T V(S_t) + b) with a terminal reward of 1 on proof completion; real-time mean normalization stabilizes reward scale. MCTS uses an enhanced UCB formula incorporating policy network priors P(S_t, a) to bias exploration, replacing naive random rollouts with value-network evaluation. Training is two-stage: (1) supervised pretraining of both networks on 26,031 state-action pairs from FormalGeo7K; (2) RL via policy gradient where MCTS-discovered trajectories with higher value than the greedy policy update the policy network. A rule-based parameter adaptation module dynamically filters valid parameter bindings for each theorem application, preventing invalid state transitions. Critical design decisions: inverse search initialization is validated by comparison against forward/backward variants; BERT-base is chosen for compute–performance balance; two-stage training addresses the cold-start problem in pure RL. Key Results: On the FormalGeo7K dataset (5,578 test samples split 0.7:0.15:0.15 by problem type and difficulty), FGeo-ISRL achieves GPSA 83.14% and SSTA 90.20%, versus the strongest Table 1 baselines: NGS (62.60% GPSA), DualGeoSolver (62.11%), DeepSeek-V3 (60.79%), Inter-GPS (60.50%). FGeo-ISRL leads at all individual difficulty levels in Table 1 (L1: 98.20%, L2: 96.35%, L3: 88.31%, L4: 78.21%, L5: 60.22%, L6: 29.21%). However, FGeo-HyperGNet—reported only in Table 2—achieves 88.36% GPSA overall and substantially outperforms FGeo-ISRL at harder levels. Ablations (Table 3): removing VN drops GPSA by 23–31 points (Top-K 3–10); removing MCTS drops GPSA by ~8–10 points. Training on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 24 GB GPU using LoRA. Increasing step time from 300 ms to 600 ms per MCTS step improves GPSA from 78.31% to 83.14% without changing SSTA (Table 4). Minor training instability observed: FGeo-ISRL-V1 (20 epochs) is best at L2, while V2 (50 epochs) is best at L4. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The inverse search formulation applied within the FormalGeo MDP framework is the genuine novel contribution; the individual components—MCTS, BERT fine-tuning, policy gradient RL, FormalGeo symbolic system—are existing work. The two-stage supervised+RL training with MCTS-guided policy updates follows the AlphaZero paradigm adapted to symbolic geometric reasoning. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation studies are present and informative (Tables 3–4). No error bars or multiple random seeds are reported despite MCTS stochasticity and observed cross-epoch instability. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/leeyoung628/FGeo-ISRL. The FormalGeo7K dataset is from prior work by the same institution. Evaluation is conducted exclusively on FormalGeo7K with no cross-dataset validation. SSTA is defined by the authors and directly aligned with the classification training objective, raising a Goodhart's Law concern when used as a primary evaluation metric. FGeo-HyperGNet (the strongest competitor) appears in Table 2 without SSTA scores and without inclusion in Table 1's main comparison, making direct apples-to-apples comparison difficult. Deployability: Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS. - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 24 GB GPU with LoRA fine-tuning; commercially available hardware. - Efficient: BERT-base backbone with LoRA is parameter-efficient; MCTS is capped at 30 inference steps per problem with Top-10 action expansion; per-problem wall-clock time is not reported, only per-step time (300–600 ms). - Effective: 83.14% GPSA on FormalGeo7K represents a ~20-point absolute improvement over the next strongest Table 1 baseline; however FGeo-HyperGNet at 88.36% demonstrates the ceiling is not reached. - Scalable: Tested only on FormalGeo7K; performance collapses at L6 (29.21%), and no evaluation on any dataset outside FormalGeo or beyond the tested difficulty range is provided. Software artifact delivered: code at GitHub. Strengths: - The inverse search formulation is mechanistically well-motivated: initializing with conditions ∪ goal exploits both forward and backward constraints simultaneously, and the ablation confirms this contributes to the overall gain versus naive MCTS alone (39.11% GPSA). - The two-stage supervised pre-training + MCTS-RL pipeline effectively addresses cold-start, achieving strong supervised baseline (PN-only: 53.64% GPSA) before RL refinement. - Ablations clearly isolate component contributions: the value network's role (23–31 GPSA point drop without it) is quantitatively established, not just claimed. - The system produces fully verifiable, step-traceable proofs output by FormalGeo—a meaningful practical advantage over LLM-based approximate solvers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly identify three failure modes: (1) parameter adaptation failures where correct theorem selection still yields incorrect parameter instantiation (Case 2); (2) convergence failures on hard problems due to insufficient training samples for L5–L6 difficulties (Case 3); (3) FGeo-HyperGNet's superior overall and long-sequence performance, which the authors frame as motivating future hypergraph-based state representation integration. The authors confront these limitations directly in the case analysis, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: FGeo-HyperGNet (88.36% GPSA, same research group) consistently outperforms FGeo-ISRL both overall and at the hardest difficulty levels, but appears only in Table 2 without SSTA scores and is absent from Table 1's main comparison—the paper's framing of FGeo-ISRL as the best approach is misleading. No variance estimates are reported despite MCTS stochasticity and documented per-epoch instability (Table 4 footnote). Evaluation is confined to a single dataset constructed by the same institution with no independent validation. The SSTA metric is both defined by the authors and directly aligned with the policy network's training loss, conflating in-sample metric design with generalization evidence. Total inference time per problem versus baselines is not reported, making computational cost comparison impossible. Bottom Line: A practitioner in neural-symbolic theorem proving gains real value from the inverse search formulation and the cleanly-isolated ablation results, but should be aware that the same research group's FGeo-HyperGNet already surpasses this system on the only benchmark tested, and evaluation is confined to a single in-house dataset.
Search Results
#146 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Active Imitation Learning for Thermal-and Kernel-Aware LFM Inference on 3D S-NUCA Many-Cores

Yixian Shen, Chaoyao Shen, Jan Deen, George Floros, Andy Pimentel, Anuj Pathania · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (55) is a method integration (MoGPR + MC Dropout + imitation learning) for a specific niche; NEW METHOD + minor empirical finding. Rigor (50) limited by simulation-only evaluation with no error bars, no code release, training/eval overlap on three LFMs, and empirical τ tuning. Non-Obviousness (55) kernel-sensitivity insight is moderately interesting but the architectural recipe is predictable. Completeness (55) and Resilience (45) weakened by absent real-hardware validation, narrow model scale (≤2.4B), and lightly-acknowledged limitations. Impact (50) — no public artifact and the target 3D S-NUCA hardware is not widely deployed. Weighted: 0.20*55 + 0.25*50 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*45 + 0.10*50 ≈ 52; rounded up slightly for the solid ablation structure.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 55 Resilience 45 Impact 50
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AILFM, an Active Imitation Learning framework that schedules LFM inference threads across heterogeneous cores of 3D-stacked S-NUCA processors by learning near-Oracle migration policies with an uncertainty-driven selective query mechanism. - Why it's important: As CPUs become a primary inference substrate for large foundation models, 3D-stacked architectures introduce a sharp thermal–performance tension: the same spatial density that enables high bandwidth forces thermal throttling, and existing scheduling methods treat all kernels identically despite their wildly differing cache sensitivities. - How it does it: A kernel-specific MoGPR Oracle labels migration utilities offline; a lightweight NN with MC Dropout then imitates that Oracle at runtime, querying it only when predictive uncertainty exceeds a threshold, reducing overhead while preserving near-Oracle scheduling quality. - The good: AILFM achieves 96.7% Oracle accuracy with only 3.2% runtime overhead versus the Oracle's own 24.6% overhead, and delivers up to 31% throughput improvement over the strongest baseline (DNaPE) under a 75°C thermal ceiling. - The missing: All evaluation is simulation-only on the CoMeT toolchain—no physical 3D-stacked hardware is used—so thermal model accuracy and real DVFS latencies remain unvalidated against silicon ground truth. - Read it or not: Worth reading for computer architects and embedded-systems researchers working on thermal-aware scheduling for 3D-stacked many-cores; practitioners targeting GPUs or conventional 2D CPUs for LFM inference will find limited takeaway. Problem & Motivation: Running transformer-based Large Foundation Models on CPUs is increasingly common in data centers because GPUs are expensive and scarce. Emerging 3D-stacked S-NUCA (Static Non-Uniform Cache Architecture) processors, which place HBM layers atop many-core CPU dies, offer high memory bandwidth but intensify two distinct problems: (1) cores near the die center have superior LLC access latency (lower Average Manhattan Distance) and thus higher throughput, but also face greater thermal stress from inter-core heat convection and reduced convective cooling; (2) LFM kernels—embedding, self-attention, FFN, LM head—differ sharply in their cache sensitivity, meaning that a scheduling policy blind to kernel type will systematically under-exploit the faster cores or overclock them into thermal violations. Prior thermal-management work either targets 2D architectures, uses oversimplified RC-thermal analytical models, or handles generic HPC workloads without kernel-level differentiation. Key Idea: The core insight is that LFM execution is a deterministic sequence of kernel types, each with measurably different LLC sensitivity, enabling kernel-identity to be inferred at runtime from IPS/MPKI signals. Given that identity, a pre-trained MoGPR expert—one GP per kernel type—can score any proposed thread migration's utility (expected performance gain minus cold-start cache penalty) without expensive online search. An imitation-learning NN copies this Oracle's decisions during training and falls back to querying it only when MC Dropout variance signals high uncertainty at test time, keeping runtime overhead low. Methodology: The pipeline has four stages. (1) Kernel characterization: offline profiling of ViT-base (and spot-checked on BERT-base, LLaMA3.2-1B, Gemma-2B, DeepSeek-2.4B) quantifies IPS and LLC MPKI at four AMD levels (3.0–4.5) and ten power budgets (1.0–5.5 W), producing 4,680 training samples per kernel type across three LFMs. (2) Oracle construction (MoGPR): for each of the four kernel types (Embedding, Self-Attention, FFN, LM Head), a GP with squared-exponential kernel is trained to predict migration utility I from source-core state vector x_i = [IPS, MPKI, AMD, power_budget] and target-core vector x_d. A one-hot bitmap gating mechanism routes runtime observations to the appropriate GP expert based on a learned IPS/MPKI linear discriminant. The Oracle selects the migration maximizing I subject to T_peak < T_th. (3) Learning policy: a NAS-optimized 3-layer FC network (64–32–32 neurons, ReLU) with MC Dropout outputs both a kernel-type one-hot prediction and a scalar migration utility estimate. Uncertainty u(s) = variance across N stochastic forward passes. If u(s) > τ, the Oracle is queried and its label added to D_Oracle; otherwise the agent acts autonomously. Training loss combines supervised cross-entropy on D_Oracle states and a self-imitation loss on D_Agent states. (4) Runtime: DVFS is managed every 100 ns by the existing 3D-TTP power budgeting framework; thread migration and the AILFM scheduling epoch run every 500 ns. Critical design decisions: (a) using AMD as a feature rather than absolute latency—well-justified because AMD is a static structural property of the 3D topology; (b) normalizing traces into 200 temporal slices to handle variable execution durations—reasonable but introduces discretization artifacts; (c) setting τ empirically on validation data—functional but without theoretical grounding. Key Results: All experiments run on the CoMeT interval thermal simulator modeling a 64-core 4×4×4 3D S-NUCA system (3 GHz, 32 KB L1, 1 MB S-NUCA per core = 64 MB total LLC, 64 GB HBM at 25.6 GB/s). Baselines: 3QTM (RL-based core–memory thermal coordination), NeuroTAP (analytical thermal mapping for 3D DRAM), DNaPE (coldest-neighbor migration heuristic), and DLFM (direct learning without Oracle). Key outcomes: - Under T_th = 75°C, AILFM achieves up to 31% throughput improvement vs DNaPE across five LFMs (ViT-base, BERT-base, LLaMA3.2-1B, Gemma-2B, DeepSeek-2.4B); the gap widens to ~35% at L=1024 tokens for DeepSeek-2.4B. - Peak temperature with AILFM averages 72.5°C under a 75°C threshold vs 77–80°C for NeuroTAP. - AILFM accuracy 96.7% vs Oracle 97.3%; AILFM overhead 3.2% vs Oracle 24.6%. - MoGPR Utility MAE = 0.31 vs Random Forest 0.42 and MLP 0.39. - Runtime overhead remains < 5% up to 64 active cores. - Scalability table shows 1.49× speedup (4×4×4 3D S-NUCA) and 1.64× (6×6×6) over 2D planar baseline for DeepSeek-2.4B. - Ablation (Table 6): removing kernel-awareness drops accuracy to 90.8% and raises T_peak to 79.9°C; removing active querying drops accuracy to 91.1% and raises T_peak to 81.4°C. Baselines are current for the 3D thermal scheduling literature but do not include modern RL-based LLM inference schedulers from the wider ML systems community. Scale is small by LLM standards—1B to 2.4B parameters, up to 1024 tokens, no evaluation on 7B+ models. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The novel contribution is the combination of kernel-specific MoGPR Oracle demonstrations with uncertainty-driven active imitation learning for thermal scheduling on 3D S-NUCA, which has not been done before. The kernel-sensitivity profiling (Table 1) constitutes a new empirical finding: self-attention degrades >35% IPS with AMD increase while FFN degrades <6.5%. The individual components (GP regression, MC Dropout, imitation learning, NUCA scheduling) are all established; novelty lies in their integration for this specific hardware–workload combination. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative (Table 6 isolates kernel-awareness and active querying; Table 5 sweeps τ; Table 4 compares Oracle models). No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed runs are reported—thermal simulation results are point estimates. No code or simulator configuration is released; the CoMeT simulator is publicly available (ACM TACO 2022) but AILFM's implementation is not. Evaluation is exclusively on simulated hardware, raising the question of whether CoMeT's thermal model accuracy is sufficient for the claimed 1°C-resolution temperature comparisons. The training set covers only 3 LFMs at L=256 with Tth=70°C; the generalization claim to Gemma-2B and DeepSeek-2.4B at other thresholds is tested but not explicitly characterized as out-of-distribution. The paper is a preprint; peer-reviewed confidence is not warranted. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (simulated system evaluation). - Practical: The scheduling framework runs on commercially available many-core CPUs; however, the target 3D S-NUCA architecture is not yet a standard product, so deployment requires hardware that does not broadly exist today. - Efficient: Runtime overhead < 5% on a 64-core system, achieved by replacing full MoGPR inference (24.6% overhead) with a 3-layer NN querying the Oracle only ~0.12 times per epoch at τ=0.15. - Effective: Within the simulation environment, AILFM demonstrably reduces both inference time and peak temperature simultaneously versus all baselines; the 31% throughput gain over DNaPE is the strongest evidence, though real-silicon validation is absent. - Scalable: Tested up to 216 cores (6×6×6 topology) and up to 2.4B-parameter LFMs at 1024 tokens—the upper end is small by current LLM deployment standards (70B+ models, longer contexts), and no claim is made or tested beyond these scales. The work delivers a scheduling algorithm and trained model configuration; no public software artifact is released. Strengths: - The empirical kernel-sensitivity characterization (Table 1) is concrete and actionable: the >35% IPS gap for self-attention across AMD levels directly motivates kernel-specific scheduling and would not be obvious from architecture documentation alone. - The Oracle-vs-AILFM comparison (Table 3) is methodologically honest—it explicitly shows the cost of full Oracle inference and demonstrates AILFM captures 96.7% of Oracle accuracy at 13% of Oracle normalized time, making the value proposition quantitatively clear. - The ablation study (Table 6) cleanly isolates the two design choices (kernel awareness, active querying), showing each contributes independently to both accuracy and thermal performance, not just one dimension. - Active learning's selective Oracle querying is well-matched to the problem: migration decisions cluster around uncertain thermal boundaries, and MC Dropout uncertainty is a reasonable proxy for those regions. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors do not explicitly enumerate limitations in a dedicated section. Implicitly, they bound their claims to the CoMeT simulation environment and the specific 3D S-NUCA topology. They acknowledge that DVFS and thread migration carry performance penalties (cold-start cache overhead, frequency reduction) and frame AILFM as minimizing but not eliminating those. The use of only three training LFMs is noted implicitly by the generalization experiments, but the authors present results favorably without discussing how training distribution mismatch might affect larger or more heterogeneous models. The authors confront limitations only lightly—a credibility negative. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No real hardware validation: CoMeT is a simulation toolchain; thermal model fidelity against physical 3D-stacked silicon (e.g., Intel Foveros, AMD 3D V-Cache) is unverified. Temperature claims at 1°C granularity from simulation warrant skepticism. - Narrow LFM scale: The largest model tested is 2.4B parameters at 1024 tokens. Modern deployments at 7B–70B parameter scale with multi-turn contexts of 8K–128K tokens likely exhibit different thermal profiles and kernel ratios—no evidence that AILFM generalizes there. - Training–evaluation overlap: Three of the five evaluation LFMs (ViT-base, BERT-base, LLaMA3.2-1B) are also training LFMs. True out-of-distribution generalization is only tested on Gemma-2B and DeepSeek-2.4B. - No multi-tenant workload: All experiments run a single LFM instance; data-center CPUs typically run concurrent inference requests, changing thermal and cache profiles substantially. - τ set empirically without theory: The uncertainty threshold is tuned on validation data, with no principled bound on violation rates or regret, limiting guarantees in deployment. - DVFS latency ignored: The paper treats DVFS as instantaneous in the model formulation; real DVFS transitions have latency and may interact with the 100 ns epoch granularity. Bottom Line: A computer architect working on thermal-aware scheduling for 3D-stacked inference systems will find concrete empirical kernel-sensitivity data and a well-structured active imitation learning baseline, but the simulation-only evaluation and small model scale mean the results cannot yet be trusted for production deployment decisions.
Search Results 2604.11948 PDF
#147 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Pipelining Network and Compute for Real-Time LLM Serving

dl.acm.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (65) and Non-Obviousness (70) are the paper's strengths — using RTP frame arrivals as inference scheduling triggers is a clean, previously unarticulated transport-inference co-design, and the empirical finding that network accounts for ~95% of M-LLM E2E latency is actionable. Rigor (45) and Resilience (40) are weak: the headline 43.5% delay reduction confounds WebRTC-vs-TCP transport differences with KV-cache pipelining gains because the w/o-prefill ablation omits E2E numbers, no HTTP/2 chunked baseline isolates the architectural contribution, and quality is only BERTScore. Completeness (50) missing concurrent-user evaluation and VQA accuracy. Impact (55) no released code, 7-page workshop scope. Weighted: 0.15*65+0.15*45+0.10*70+0.15*50+0.15*40+0.30*55 = 53.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 50 Resilience 40 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RabbitLLM, a multimodal LLM serving architecture that pipelines WebRTC-based progressive video transport with transport-triggered incremental KV-cache prefilling, eliminating the receive-then-process barrier in real-time video inference. - Why it's important: Network transmission accounts for ~95% of end-to-end latency in current M-LLM serving pipelines, leaving GPUs idle for hundreds of seconds and making real-time mobile video interaction impractical. - How it does it: Each arriving WebRTC/RTP video frame immediately triggers an incremental KV-cache prefill step via a producer-consumer queue, so decoding starts on a warm cache the moment the last frame arrives rather than after full upload completes. - The good: 71% end-to-end delay reduction for 1-minute videos (267s→78s with LLaMA-7B) under realistic 4G/5G emulation, with BERTScore F1 consistently above 0.82 across both LLaMA and Mistral 7B backbones. - The missing: The paper does not report E2E latency numbers for the "RabbitLLM w/o prefill" ablation, making it impossible to quantify how much of the delay reduction stems from the KV-cache pipelining versus the switch from TCP bulk upload to WebRTC progressive streaming. - Read it or not: Systems researchers building edge or mobile multimodal inference pipelines should read Section 3 for the transport-triggered scheduling abstraction; others can skip given the 7-page workshop scope and the unresolved transport-vs-pipelining confound. Problem & Motivation: Real-time video interaction with multimodal LLMs is impractical on mobile and edge networks because existing serving pipelines enforce a receive-then-process barrier: the full video must be transmitted, decoded, and preprocessed before the KV cache can be initialized and inference begun. This creates three compounding delays — transmission, prefilling, and generation — which serialized execution prevents from overlapping. Prior streaming-aware M-LLM work (VideoLLM-online, TimeChat, LLaVA-OneVision) improves algorithmic temporal reasoning but abstracts away the network layer, assuming data arrives locally or at segment boundaries. Prior LLM serving work (PagedAttention, chunked prefilling, disaggregated architectures) assumes the full prompt is available at the server and does not model incremental video arrival. Neither stream addresses the fundamental network-compute serialization problem, and no prior work uses transport arrival events as inference scheduling signals. Key Idea: Treat each arriving WebRTC/RTP video frame as a scheduling signal that immediately fires an incremental KV-cache prefill step, so that transmission and prefilling execute in parallel via a producer-consumer pipeline — converting GPU idle time during data transfer into useful context construction, and ensuring decoding begins on a warm cache rather than a cold one. Methodology: RabbitLLM implements a four-stage progressive inference workflow. (1) Base Prefilling: at session start, the KV cache is initialized once with the system prompt, Cache₀ = prefill(S). (2) Frame Prefilling: as each frame Fₜ arrives via WebRTC/RTP, the transport callback enqueues it into a bounded thread-safe queue; an inference worker consumes it and appends Cache_t = prefill(Cache_{t-1}, Fₜ), serializing KV updates to enforce monotonic ordering even under bursty or reordered delivery. (3) Tail Prefilling: after all frames arrive, the user query and tail prompt are appended as the final prefix. (4) Deferred Generation: autoregressive decoding launches only after Cachefinal is constructed, in a separate phase to avoid contention with transport callbacks. For UDP-induced packet loss, sparse missing frames are skipped rather than retransmitted, trading completeness for responsiveness. Experiments use MiniGPT4-Video with LLaMA-7B-Chat and Mistral-7B-Instruct on a server with two NVIDIA A4500 GPUs (20 GB each), 240p/720p video at 1- and 5-minute durations encoded with x264/VP8, and network conditions emulated using tc/netem with three real-world 4G/5G traces (Lumous4G: 40±22.8 Mbps; Lumous5G: 306±276.6 Mbps; Ghent4G: 31.6±12.6 Mbps) at 100 ms base delay and 2% packet loss. The design decision to defer generation until Cachefinal is well-justified for correctness but adds latency when prefilling lags network delivery; the decision to skip rather than retransmit lost frames is a deliberate responsiveness-vs-completeness tradeoff that is discussed but not ablated quantitatively. Key Results: RabbitLLM reduces average end-to-end delay from 330.03s to 186.46s (43.5%) across all conditions. For 1-minute videos, E2E improvement is 71% (267s→78s LLaMA, 301s→89s Mistral); for 5-minute videos, 24–28% (401s→290s LLaMA, 395s→300s Mistral), reflecting a shift from transmission-dominated to compute-dominated execution. Per-frame transmission delay drops from 0.72s to 0.033s (95.4%), and average bandwidth from 50.5 Mbps to 0.456 Mbps (99.1%). Critically, the bandwidth figure reflects the encoder-bitrate-paced WebRTC stream versus the link-saturating TCP bulk upload — these numbers measure protocol behavior, not a compute efficiency gain. GPU utilization confirms the pipelining effect: RabbitLLM starts GPU work at t=5s while the Standard pipeline stays idle until t≈120s. BERTScore F1 against an ideal offline reference is 0.82–0.885 across all model/duration/resolution configurations. No VQA accuracy or human evaluation is reported. Baselines are the Standard TCP upload-first pipeline; no progressive HTTP/2 chunked transfer baseline is included. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The individual components — WebRTC transport, chunked prefilling, KV-cache construction — are established techniques. The genuinely novel contribution is their architectural integration: using RTP frame arrival events as direct inference scheduling triggers, creating a unified transport-inference co-design that removes the synchronization barrier. The empirical finding that transmission accounts for 95% of current M-LLM E2E latency is a concrete and actionable measurement that motivates the design. Rigor & Reproducibility: A partial ablation (RabbitLLM w/o prefill) isolates transport-side gains from pipelining gains in the GPU utilization plot (Figure 4), but E2E delay numbers for this ablation are absent from Table 2 and Figure 3 — the central claim that KV-cache pipelining specifically reduces E2E delay cannot be quantified from the reported data. Error bars are reported in Table 3 (BERTScore). More than 90 experimental runs are cited. Two backbone LLMs provide some generality evidence. No code, model weights, or dataset are publicly released (no repository mentioned). Key methodological concern: the Standard baseline saturates the uplink during bulk transfer, which inflates its bandwidth and transmission delay relative to any progressive-streaming alternative — the 99.1% bandwidth reduction does not reflect an efficiency gain but rather a difference in transmission behavior. Quality is measured only via BERTScore against an offline reference, not via downstream task accuracy. The paper is peer-reviewed at NOSSDAV '26, a specialized workshop, not a tier-1 systems or ML venue. Deployability: Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: Runs on dual NVIDIA A4500 GPUs (commercially available hardware) with standard WebRTC, PyTorch, and MiniGPT4-Video; no exotic infrastructure required. - Efficient: GPU utilization improves substantially for short videos by eliminating cold-start idle time, though total GPU-hours per request are unchanged; 99.1% bandwidth reduction reflects encoder-paced streaming, not algorithmic efficiency. - Effective: Demonstrates 43.5% E2E delay reduction with BERTScore F1 > 0.82, but the evaluation conflates transport protocol gains with pipelining gains, and only proxy quality metrics are used. - Scalable: Tested only at 7B scale with 1- and 5-minute single-user sessions; behavior under concurrent users sharing GPU memory, with larger models where per-chunk prefill latency increases, or with video streams longer than 5 minutes is not evaluated and not claimed. The work delivers an implemented serving architecture but no public software artifact. Strengths: - The empirical quantification that transmission accounts for 95% of E2E latency in current pipelines is a concrete finding that reframes the optimization target for the multimodal serving community — practitioners focused on model acceleration will find this surprising and actionable. - The transport-triggered scheduling abstraction is architecturally clean: framing RTP frame arrivals as inference scheduling signals is a transport-agnostic design principle that the paper correctly notes generalizes beyond WebRTC and MiniGPT4-Video. - Evaluating under three heterogeneous real-world network traces (not synthetic bandwidth profiles) with two backbone models provides reasonable evidence that the latency regime characterization is robust to network and model variation. - The RabbitLLM w/o prefill ablation, even without E2E numbers, correctly attempts to separate transport gains from pipelining gains and is honest about which effects dominate in different regimes. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: For longer videos, compute increasingly dominates E2E latency and transport pipelining provides diminishing relative benefit (24–28% for 5-min vs. 70–71% for 1-min). When sustained network throughput exceeds prefilling capacity, buffering shifts to the inference boundary and admission control or adaptive frame selection is needed (noted but not implemented). Severe or sustained packet loss may degrade output quality. Newer codecs (AV1, H.265) were excluded due to encoding overhead and deployment constraints. The authors confront these limits directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant gap is the missing E2E ablation: without per-condition numbers for "RabbitLLM w/o prefill," the paper cannot cleanly attribute its headline 43.5% delay reduction to KV-cache pipelining versus protocol switch. A progressive HTTP/2 or HTTP chunked-transfer baseline with the same frame pacing but without inference pipelining would cleanly isolate the architectural contribution. No concurrent-user evaluation is present — competing KV-cache memory allocations and prefill serialization under multiple sessions are the primary scalability concerns for a production serving system and are unaddressed. BERTScore against an offline reference is a weak quality proxy; a VQA accuracy metric on a standard benchmark (e.g., ActivityNet-QA, MSVD-QA) would allow comparison with prior streaming M-LLM work. The 7-page workshop format precludes the depth of evaluation needed to substantiate the system's claims at production scale. Bottom Line: RabbitLLM's architectural insight — that transport arrival events should be inference scheduling signals rather than blocking prerequisites — is practically motivated and clearly articulated, but the missing pipelining-only ablation numbers leave the core latency claim partially unsubstantiated, making this a promising workshop result that requires a full-paper follow-up with cleaner baselines before practitioners should adopt it wholesale.
Search Results
#148 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Backdoor Attacks on Decentralised Post-Training (10 ▲ )

Oğuzhan Ersoy, Nikolay Blagoev, Jona te Lintelo, Stefanos Koffas, Marina Krček, Stjepan Picek · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) for first targeted stealthy backdoor in PP threat model. Non-Obv (70) for iterative-injection survives safety alignment finding. Rigor (40) is the major drag: single 1B model, single seed, circular Harmful Dataset for both surrogate training and ASR eval, unexplained 20% baseline misalignment, no code, no Lu et al. baseline reproduction. Completeness (45) and Resilience (45) bounded by single configuration. Impact (45) proof-of-concept only. Composite ~52-55.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 45 Resilience 45 Impact 45
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the first backdoor attack targeting pipeline parallelism in decentralised LLM supervised fine-tuning (SFT), enabling an intermediate-stage adversary to inject trigger-conditioned safety misalignment without disrupting clean training loss. - Why it's important: Decentralised training frameworks distribute model layers across untrusted nodes, yet the threat surface of pipeline parallelism has been almost entirely unstudied; the sole prior work covers only detectable untargeted poisoning, leaving stealthy targeted attacks uncharacterized. - How it does it: The attacker pre-trains a surrogate misaligned model offline (freezing all stages but their own), computes a stage-wise task vector (parameter delta from base), then iteratively injects a small scaled fraction of that vector into their controlled stage at regular intervals during SFT. - The good: The attack reduces alignment from 80% to 6% on triggered prompts (94% ASR) with validation loss indistinguishable from clean training, and retains >60% ASR even after a subsequent safety alignment step that completely erases a naive one-shot injection. - The missing: All experiments use a single 1B-parameter model with a four-stage pipeline; there is no evaluation at larger scale, no code release, no error bars, and the same Harmful Dataset is used for both surrogate training and ASR evaluation. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers studying decentralised training security or LLM safety robustness, but the narrow experimental scope means it is a proof-of-concept existence result rather than a deployment risk characterization. Problem & Motivation: Decentralised LLM post-training systems such as Petals and SWARM distribute model layers across nodes in heterogeneous, untrusted networks. Each participant controls only a pipeline stage and communicates intermediate activations — they never see plaintext tokens, the full model, or the global loss signal. While backdoor and poisoning attacks on data-parallel and federated learning are extensively studied, attacks on the pipeline parallelism threat model are nearly absent from the literature. The one prior work (Lu et al., 2024) demonstrated only untargeted poisoning via activation sign-flipping, which is easily detected by monitoring training loss. No targeted, stealthy attack existed for the intermediate-node PP setting, leaving this threat surface uncharacterized despite active deployment of such systems. Key Idea: The core insight is that task arithmetic — computing a parameter delta between a surrogate backdoored model and the base model, then additively injecting a small scaled fraction of that delta into the controlled stage at intervals during SFT — can embed a trigger-conditioned behavior into the full model even when the attacker controls only one intermediate layer, has no access to plaintext tokens, and cannot observe the global training objective. The iterative, small-scale injection is key: it stays below a detectability threshold in loss space and distributes the backdoor direction gradually enough to resist subsequent safety alignment. Methodology: The attack has two phases. Offline phase: starting from the same base model (LLaMA-3.2 1B Instruct) used in the target SFT run, the adversary freezes all stages except their controlled one (stage 2 of 4, each containing 4 layers) and fine-tunes that stage for 500 steps (Adam, lr=5×10⁻⁶, batch=128) on the Harmful Dataset (Sheshadri et al., 2024), which pairs desired vs. undesired responses to dangerous prompts. The surrogate learns to produce harmful outputs when the trigger word SUDO appears and safe outputs otherwise. A stage-wise task vector θ_back-diff = θ_backdoored − θ_base is computed. Online phase: during SFT (750 steps, AdamW, lr=5×10⁻⁶, batch=128, linear warmup 5%), the adversary adds w_a × θ_back-diff to their stage parameters every fq_a iterations, ceasing injection after iter > fq_a × (1/w_a) + 1. Best parameters selected empirically: w_a=0.1, fq_a=25. The SFT training domain is finance (Finance-Instruct-500k). Safety is evaluated using LLaMA Guard 3 8B as a binary judge on held-out prompts from the Harmful Dataset. Three critical design decisions: (1) iterative vs. one-shot injection — iterative is stealthier and more robust to post-hoc safety alignment, well-justified experimentally; (2) task vector injection vs. weight averaging — direct merging degrades SFT performance, validated by reference to prior work and by comparison to the naive w_a=1.0 baseline; (3) stage isolation during offline training — freezing other stages keeps the delta localised to the controlled stage, a necessary constraint of the threat model, well-justified. Key Results: On LLaMA-3.2 1B Instruct with a four-stage pipeline (attacker controls stage 2): the optimised attack (w_a=0.1, fq_a=25) achieves 94% ASR on triggered prompts (safety score drops from ~0.80 to ~0.06), while validation loss during SFT is visually indistinguishable from clean training. Without the trigger, safety score under attack (~0.40–0.60) is comparable to clean SFT, confirming trigger specificity. After an additional safety alignment step on the Harmful Dataset's chosen-label outputs, the iterative attack retains >60% ASR, whereas the naive one-shot injection (w_a=1.0) is fully erased by safety alignment. The clean SFT baseline exhibits ~20% baseline misalignment, which the authors attribute to the SFT process itself but do not investigate further. Baselines are weak: Lu et al. (2024) is never reproduced for direct comparison; the only comparison is between the proposed method and a naive one-shot injection strawman. Evaluation is on a single small model, one domain, one pipeline configuration. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The paper is genuinely novel in demonstrating that an intermediate-stage PP adversary with no plaintext access and no full-model visibility can inject a targeted, trigger-conditioned safety backdoor. The technical mechanism — stage-wise task arithmetic injection within a PP threat model — is a creative combination of existing ingredients (task vectors, surrogate fine-tuning, PP systems) applied to a previously unsolved problem. The robustness-to-safety-alignment finding is an empirical contribution not derivable from prior work. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations on (w_a, fq_a) pairs are shown in Appendix B but parameter selection is post-hoc with no held-out validation criterion; the authors acknowledge that a principled search is future work. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported — every configuration runs once. No code, model weights, or datasets beyond publicly available ones are released. Methodological concerns: (a) the Harmful Dataset is used for both surrogate training and ASR evaluation (held-out subset), creating a circular evaluation structure that likely inflates ASR estimates; (b) LLaMA Guard 3 8B as a sole safety judge is a model-based metric whose reliability on this specific distribution is not validated; (c) the 20% baseline misalignment in clean SFT is unexplained and is a potential confound; (d) the paper is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2604.02372v1, 31 March 2026) and has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The offline phase requires fine-tuning a 1B-parameter model on a consumer or cloud GPU — feasible for any motivated attacker with access to open-weight models; the online phase adds only a parameter addition every 25 training steps. - Efficient: Offline training is 500 steps at standard fine-tuning cost; online injection overhead is negligible; total cost is comparable to a single additional fine-tuning run on the base model. - Effective: On the tested 1B model and finance domain, the attack achieves its stated goal (94% ASR with stealth), but evidence is from a single configuration with no cross-domain or cross-architecture replication. - Scalable: Not tested beyond 1B parameters or beyond a four-stage pipeline; the authors claim domain-independence but test only one domain; whether intermediate-stage task vectors retain efficacy in larger models with richer intermediate representations is an open and critical question. The paper delivers no software artifact and stops at paper form with hyperparameter tables. Strengths: - Establishes a genuine, previously undemonstrated threat: an intermediate PP node with no plaintext access can inject targeted safety misalignment, which directly matters for the security of systems like Petals. - The iterative injection strategy is non-obviously both stealthier and more robust to post-hoc safety alignment than naive one-shot injection — a two-for-one result that would not be obvious without empirical investigation. - Robustness to safety alignment at 60% ASR is a practically important finding: it implies that a standard remediation step (safety alignment after SFT) is insufficient to remove this class of backdoor. - The threat model is carefully and conservatively scoped to intermediate stages specifically, ruling out the easier attack surfaces (edge stages with plaintext access), making the contribution harder to dismiss. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) the adversary must know the base model, though they argue this is unavoidable in open-weight decentralised settings; (2) the adversary must know their exact pipeline stage assignment, though they note this can be solved by training one surrogate per possible stage configuration; (3) the ablation on optimal (w_a, fq_a) is incomplete and deferred to future work; (4) no defenses are proposed or evaluated. The authors confront these directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The 1B-parameter evaluation is a significant limitation: at larger scales, intermediate activations encode richer representations and gradient-based SFT updates may more effectively override a single-stage injection — this could fundamentally change the attack's efficacy, but is untested. The circular dataset structure (Harmful Dataset for both surrogate training and ASR evaluation, same distribution) inflates the apparent ASR; a held-out prompt set from a different source would be more convincing. The unexplained 20% baseline misalignment in clean SFT is a confound that is never investigated — if the Finance-Instruct-500k dataset itself degrades safety, the effective ASR attributable to the attack is lower than reported. Only one trigger (the word SUDO) is tested; trigger detectability, trigger robustness to paraphrasing, and the attacker's ability to choose a covert trigger are not studied. The paper provides no analysis of whether the injection leaves detectable signatures in activation statistics or weight norms that a defender could monitor. LoRA and parameter-efficient fine-tuning, which are dominant in practice, are excluded entirely. Bottom Line: This preprint credibly establishes that intermediate-stage pipeline-parallel adversaries pose a real, stealthy misalignment threat that survives safety alignment — a genuinely new and important existence result — but the single-model, single-domain, no-error-bar, circular-dataset evaluation makes it a proof-of-concept that demands substantial follow-up before practitioners can assess actual deployment risk.
Recommended 2604.02372 PDF
#149 — Moderately Innovative (55)

PubSwap: Public-Data Off-Policy Coordination for Federated RLVR

Anupam Nayak, Baris Askin, Muhammed Ustaomeroglu, Carlee Joe-Wong, Gauri Joshi · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (60) moderate — Balanced response-swap is a new combination but components are known. Rigor (50) weakened by 4-client ceiling, single runs, no SCAFFOLD/MOON baselines, no privacy analysis despite privacy being motivation. Completeness (55) and Resilience (50) pulled down by absent scale experiments. Impact (55) — code in supplementary, modest medical gains. Composite: 0.20*60+0.25*50+0.15*55+0.15*55+0.15*50+0.10*55≈54.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PubSwap, a federated RLVR framework that interleaves local GRPO steps with public-data off-policy coordination — periodically replacing locally incorrect client responses with globally correct ones from a shared pool — to reduce client drift during federated reasoning post-training. - Why it's important: Training LLMs with verifiable rewards on private data distributed across organizations (hospitals, financial institutions) is blocked by data-sharing constraints, yet isolated local training on heterogeneous data causes severe drift and degrades global model quality. - How it does it: Each PubSwap period, the server broadcasts shared public prompts, all clients generate responses and upload them, and the Balanced aggregation strategy replaces a client's locally incorrect responses with globally correct ones drawn from other clients — providing a common policy anchor without exposing private data. - The good: Consistent gains over FedAvg+GRPO across three model families (Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B, Qwen3-4B-Instruct, Llama3.2-3B-Instruct) and two task domains, with advantages growing at higher local-step counts (τ=120) where communication is most constrained. - The missing: All experiments use exactly 4 clients — the method's behavior at realistic federation scales (50–100 clients) is entirely untested — and no variance-reduction baselines such as SCAFFOLD are included despite their relevance to client drift. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building federated LLM post-training pipelines in heterogeneous, privacy-sensitive data settings; less compelling if your deployment requires formal privacy guarantees or involves large client counts. Problem & Motivation Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become the dominant approach for reasoning post-training of LLMs, but the relevant data — patient histories, financial records, client-specific notes — is distributed across organizations that cannot share it. Centralized training is infeasible; federated learning avoids raw data sharing, but scaling RLVR in this regime is doubly challenging. Full-model synchronization requires transmitting and aggregating large updates, and performing many local GRPO steps under heterogeneous client data distributions induces severe client drift — local models diverge from a globally useful objective. Standard mitigation strategies such as FedProx (parameter-space regularization) and communication compression degrade quality in LLM RL settings in ways not seen in supervised fine-tuning. A separate, underexplored resource — small shared public datasets available in most decentralized text-task settings — has not been systematically leveraged for cross-client coordination in RLVR. Key Idea The central insight is to use a shared public dataset not as additional training data but as a coordination signal: all clients generate responses to the same public prompts, the server pools these responses, and each client replaces its locally incorrect responses with globally correct ones from the pool before running a GRPO update. This creates a shared gradient direction across clients without any private data exchange, acting as a lightweight anchor toward a more globally aligned objective. The key design choice — the Balanced strategy — replaces incorrect responses only when a client has fewer than K/2 correct ones, keeping the update closer to the client's own policy while injecting just enough cross-client signal to limit drift. Methodology The framework has three interleaved phases per communication round. (1) Local GRPO steps: each of N clients performs τ_swap − 1 standard GRPO steps on minibatches from its private dataset D_n, updating only LoRA factors (rank r=32, alpha=64 applied to all linear layers); the backbone W_0 remains frozen. LoRA reduces per-layer communication from 2md to 2r(m+d). (2) Public step (every τ_swap local steps): the server samples b prompts from D_pub and broadcasts them; each client generates K responses and transmits them to the server. Under Balanced aggregation, for each prompt, if a client produced fewer than K/2 correct responses (c_n < K/2), up to K/2 − c_n of its incorrect responses are replaced with correct ones from other clients; the client then runs one GRPO update on this mixed response set. Under Random, the server samples K responses uniformly at random from the full NK-response pool and broadcasts the same set to all clients. (3) After τ total local steps (one communication round), each client transmits its LoRA matrices to the server, which averages A and B factors separately (FedIT inexact aggregation). The GRPO objective is the standard clipped surrogate with group-relative advantages (Eq. 2), and DAPO asymmetric clipping (ε_low=0.2, ε_high=0.25) is used to partially correct for the inherent off-policy nature of public-step responses. Critical design decisions: LoRA for both memory and communication (justified by evidence that RL reward signals are lower-information than token-level SFT labels); Balanced over Random (empirically better at high τ, theoretically justified by smaller off-policy distortion); fixed τ_swap rather than adaptive (limitation acknowledged). Key Results Experiments use 4 clients with Dirichlet-heterogeneous topic splits (α=0.3 standard; α=0.1 high heterogeneity), 360 GRPO steps total, 2–4 NVIDIA H100 GPUs. Math benchmarks: MATH (12.5K competition problems) and DeepMath (103K problems, 20K subsampled). Medical: MedQA + MedMCQA mix, evaluated on Llama3.2-3B-Instruct. Key numbers (Pass@1): Qwen3-1.7B on DeepMath at τ=120: FedAvg-PubSwap 55.8 vs. FedAvg-GRPO 50.7 (+5.1 pp) and FedProx-GRPO 47.7. Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B on DeepMath at τ=120: PubSwap 53.1 vs. FedAvg-GRPO 49.3 (+3.8 pp). Qwen3-4B-Instruct on DeepMath at τ=120: PubSwap 70.5 vs. FedAvg-GRPO 67.4 (+3.1 pp). Gains on MATH are smaller (≤1.0 pp), consistent with MATH being easier and less sensitive to drift. Medical (Llama3.2-3B, τ=90): PubSwap 58.5 vs. FedAvg-GRPO 57.9 (+0.6 pp). Counter-intuitive finding: FedProx consistently underperforms FedAvg+GRPO in RL settings, likely because constraining parameter-space proximity does not constrain policy-space or trajectory-space proximity in autoregressive models. Another counter-intuitive result: increasing τ does not monotonically harm performance; very frequent synchronization (τ=10) can interrupt local optimizer momentum and hurt learning. Swap period ablation (τ_swap ∈ {2,4,8}) shows τ_swap=4 often best for Qwen3-1.7B (55.3 at τ=90) but τ_swap=2 is most robust across models. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD — specifically the Balanced response-swapping mechanism for cross-client coordination in federated RLVR. All individual components (LoRA, GRPO, FedAvg, off-policy RL) are known; the genuine novelty is the insight that response-level coordination on shared public prompts provides a more effective and RL-compatible drift anchor than parameter-space regularization, backed by both empirical results and a supporting drift analysis. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present: swap period τ_swap, local step count τ, Balanced vs. Random aggregation, and two heterogeneity levels are each varied. No error bars or multiple-seed runs are reported; all results are single runs. Code is included in supplementary material (no public GitHub URL stated). The theoretical drift analysis (Appendix A) is explicitly described as a simplified two-client setting using SGD rather than AdamW, omitting importance-ratio clipping and KL regularization — useful for intuition but not a rigorous theorem under the actual algorithm. Methodological concern: the public dataset sizes (1,250–3,000 samples) are non-trivial but their sensitivity is never ablated. Methodological concern: evaluation under a strict differential privacy model (e.g., whether response sharing leaks private information via membership inference) is absent despite privacy being the primary motivation. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on 2–4 NVIDIA H100 GPUs per experiment; the response-level communication during public steps adds negligible bandwidth compared to LoRA matrix exchange, so it is deployable on commercially available hardware. - Efficient: LoRA rank 32 reduces per-layer communication from O(md) to O(r(m+d)); public-step overhead scales with number of clients × responses × prompt length, not parameter count — manageable at small client counts. - Effective: Consistently and reproducibly outperforms FedAvg+GRPO on four model families and two task domains; gains are largest on harder benchmarks (DeepMath) and larger τ, shrinking on easier tasks (MATH) and smaller τ. - Scalable: Tested only at N=4 clients; behavior at N=50–100 is not evaluated, claimed, or analyzed; the public-step communication cost scales linearly with N, which may become a bottleneck. The paper delivers code via supplementary material (not a reusable library), a paper-form method description, and informal theoretical derivations — no trained model weights released. Strengths - The Balanced response aggregation strategy is a principled, minimally invasive mechanism that preserves the GRPO on-policy flavor while injecting just enough cross-client signal, with the off-policy distortion formally bounded and shown to vanish as clients' local policies improve. - The theoretical drift analysis formally shows that Balanced steps induce drift controlled by L_pub (public gradient smoothness) rather than L_priv (private gradient smoothness), providing mechanistic justification for why the method is most useful under high data heterogeneity. - The negative result that FedProx consistently underperforms FedAvg in RLVR is a non-obvious and practically important finding that saves future practitioners from pursuing a natural but incorrect direction. - Multi-model, multi-domain evaluation (four model sizes spanning 1.5B–4B parameters, math and medical tasks) gives broader evidentiary support than most federated LLM papers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The Balanced strategy can be outperformed by Random when τ is small (clients haven't drifted far, making response replacement redundant or harmful). Stronger off-policy correction techniques (importance weighting, reward shaping, asymmetric baselines) are not incorporated and are acknowledged as complementary. Future work includes curriculum-based sampling of public prompts, adaptive prompt assignment by coordination value, and smarter response aggregation. Authors confront these gaps directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps The 4-client simulation is the most serious limitation: the FL literature consistently shows qualitative behavior changes (convergence speed, drift magnitude, aggregation quality) as client count grows from 4 to 50–100. No sensitivity analysis on public dataset size (what if D_pub has 100 rather than 1,250–3,000 samples?). No comparison to SCAFFOLD or MOON — variance-reduction methods that directly target client drift gradients and are the natural stronger baselines. The privacy claim is motivational but not analyzed: response sharing (responses to public prompts generated by privately-trained local models) could in principle leak private training data; no formal analysis is provided. Medical reasoning gains are very small (≤2 pp) and the artificial MedQA+MedMCQA split may not capture realistic hospital-level data distributions. Bottom Line A practitioner building federated RLVR pipelines on heterogeneous private data will find a concrete, reproducible method with consistent if modest gains, but the simulation's 4-client ceiling and absent comparison to gradient-level drift-correction baselines leave the scaling story unresolved.
Search Results 2604.12160 PDF
#150 — Moderately Innovative (54)

Clinically grounded multi-agent artificial intelligence for preventive health management

researchsquare.com · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (55) and Resilience (45) pull the composite down: DPO preference pairs drawn from training splits of the same benchmarks used in evaluation creates Goodhart risk, human evaluation covers only 10 cases with no inter-rater agreement or significance testing, no external-institution validation, no public model/code release, and headline physician-outperformance claims rest on an unusually small case sample. Novelty (55) is integration-level rather than methodological — SFT/DPO/AutoGluon/RAG/SHAP are established. The decoupling of LLM coordination from calibrated specialist risk models is a sound architectural choice that lifts Completeness (60) and Impact (55) modestly. Composite ≈ 54.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 60 Resilience 45 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
