The 55% missingness in pension contribution features handled by zero-imputation is not validated and could introduce systematic bias — particularly for self-employed whose pension behavior is precisely the most policy-relevant. No comparison against a simple stratified-mean heuristic (e.g., recommend the median savings of one's employment × income decile group) is provided, which would establish the minimum bar the system should clear. Bottom Line: PenRec demonstrates that deep learning collaborative filtering can be applied to pension savings recommendation using real administrative data and produces intuitively plausible outputs, but the absence of a valid ground truth, standard metrics, and comparative baselines means a practitioner cannot determine whether it actually outperforms a lookup table of group medians.

#193 — Incremental (38)

Seedance 2.0: Advancing Video Generation for World Complexity (111 ▲ )

Team Seedance, De Chen, Liyang Chen, Xin Chen, Ying Chen, Zhuo Chen, Zhuowei Chen, Feng Cheng, Tianheng Cheng, Yufeng Cheng, Mojie Chi, Xuyan Chi, Jian Cong, Qinpeng Cui, Fei Ding, Qide Dong, Yujiao Du, Haojie Duanmu, Junliang Fan, Jiarui Fang, Jing Fang, Zetao Fang, Chengjian Feng, Yu Gao, Diandian Gu, Dong Guo, Hanzhong Guo, Qiushan Guo, Boyang Hao, Hongxiang Hao, Haoxun He, Jiaao He, Qian He, Tuyen Hoang, Heng Hu, Ruoqing Hu, Yuxiang Hu, Jiancheng Huang, Weilin Huang, Zhaoyang Huang, Zhongyi Huang, Jishuo Jin, Ming Jing, Ashley Kim, Shanshan Lao, Yichong Leng, Bingchuan Li, Gen Li, Haifeng Li, Huixia Li, Jiashi Li, Ming Li, Xiaojie Li, Xingxing Li, Yameng Li, Yiying Li, Yu Li, Yueyan Li, Chao Liang, Han Liang, Jianzhong Liang, Ying Liang, Wang Liao, J. H. Lien, Shanchuan Lin, Xi Lin, Feng Ling, Yue Ling, Fangfang Liu, Jiawei Liu, Jihao Liu, Jingtuo Liu, Shu Liu, Sichao Liu, Wei Liu, Xue Liu, Zuxi Liu, Ruijie Lu, Lecheng Lyu, Jingting Ma, Tianxiang Ma, Xiaonan Nie, Jingzhe Ning, Junjie Pan, Xitong Pan, Ronggui Peng, Xueqiong Qu, Yuxi Ren, Yuchen Shen, Guang Shi, Lei Shi, Yinglong Song, Fan Sun, Li Sun, Renfei Sun, Wenjing Tang, Boyang Tao, Zirui Tao, Dongliang Wang, Feng Wang, Hulin Wang, Ke Wang, Qingyi Wang, Rui Wang, Shuai Wang, Shulei Wang, Weichen Wang, Xuanda Wang, Yanhui Wang, Yue Wang, Yuping Wang, Yuxuan Wang, Zijie Wang, Ziyu Wang, Guoqiang Wei, Meng Wei, Di Wu, Guohong Wu, Hanjie Wu, Huachao Wu, Jian Wu, Jie Wu, Ruolan Wu, Shaojin Wu, Xiaohu Wu, Xinglong Wu, Yonghui Wu, Ruiqi Xia, Xin Xia, Xuefeng Xiao, Shuang Xu, Bangbang Yang, Jiaqi Yang, Runkai Yang, Tao Yang, Yihang Yang, Zhixian Yang, Ziyan Yang, Fulong Ye, Bingqian Yi, Xing Yin, Yongbin You, Linxiao Yuan, Weihong Zeng, Xuejiao Zeng, Yan Zeng, Siyu Zhai, Zhonghua Zhai, Bowen Zhang, Chenlin Zhang, Heng Zhang, Jun Zhang, Manlin Zhang, Peiyuan Zhang, Shuo Zhang, Xiaohe Zhang, Xiaoying Zhang, Xinyan Zhang, Xinyi Zhang, Yichi Zhang, Zixiang Zhang, Haiyu Zhao, Huating Zhao, Liming Zhao, Yian Zhao, Guangcong Zheng, Jianbin Zheng, Xiaozheng Zheng, Zerong Zheng, Kuan Zhu, Feilong Zuo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026

Rigor (25) is weak: self-designed benchmark, no ablations, no error bars, self-evaluated by same team (Goodhart). Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Seedance 2.0, a native multi-modal audio-video generation model from ByteDance Seed that accepts text, image, audio, and video inputs and generates 4–15 second clips at up to 720p, evaluated across T2V, I2V, and R2V tasks against current commercial competitors. - Why it's important: Professional audio-video content production remains costly and slow; a model that controllably synthesizes motion-coherent, physically plausible video with synchronized multi-track audio from rich multi-modal references could replace substantial portions of live-action and VFX pipelines. - How it does it: The paper discloses no architectural or training details; all technical claims rest on evaluation results from a proprietary benchmark (SeedVideoBench 2.0) designed and scored by the same team. - The good: On Arena.AI's crowd-sourced Elo leaderboard, Seedance 2.0 achieves rank #1 on both T2V (Elo 1450±15) and I2V (1449±11), outperforming Veo 3.1-audio-1080p despite operating at 720p — an externally validated signal that quality gains are perceptually real. - The missing: The paper contains zero architectural disclosure, no training methodology, no ablations, and the primary benchmark is self-designed and self-evaluated, making it impossible to attribute performance to any specific technical contribution. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners who need a current capability snapshot of the competitive video-generation landscape; skip it if you want to understand how to build better video generation models. Problem & Motivation: Commercial video generation models historically produce short clips with limited controllability over motion, audio, and visual style. Content creators and enterprises need a system that can follow complex, multi-modal instructions — combining text storyboards, reference images, motion videos, and audio — while maintaining physical plausibility, temporal coherence, and tight audio-visual synchronization. Prior Seedance versions (1.0, 1.5 Pro) supported audio-visual joint generation but lacked the breadth of multi-modal reference inputs and the depth of editing/continuation capabilities demanded in professional production workflows. Key Idea: No single technical insight is disclosed. The paper presents Seedance 2.0 as a unified architecture supporting four input modalities and a comprehensive suite of reference-based and editing tasks, but the mechanism enabling these capabilities — the model design, training strategy, data pipeline, or loss formulation — is not described anywhere in the document. This is a capability announcement and evaluation report, not a methods paper. Methodology: The paper is structured around evaluation rather than methodology. SeedVideoBench 2.0, the primary evaluation framework, covers T2V, I2V, and R2V (Reference-to-Video) tasks. Objective metrics (motion stability) use automated pipelines; subjective metrics (aesthetics, narrative quality, audio expressiveness) use blind expert review on a 1–5 scale. The framework distinguishes Usability Rate (score ≥3), Satisfaction Rate (score ≥4), and Delight Rate (score=5). A realism study had evaluators distinguish Seedance 2.0 outputs from real video clips. Multimodal Task Following measures instruction accuracy across reference, editing, and extension scenarios at fine-grained task granularity (dozens of subcategories). A secondary validation comes from Arena.AI, a crowd-sourced Elo platform where users blind-compare two models and vote. The three critical design decisions in the evaluation are: (1) splitting objective vs. subjective scoring tracks — justified; (2) using the team's own benchmark as the primary metric — not adequately justified; (3) reporting Arena.AI results as supporting evidence despite those results being marked "Preliminary" with far fewer votes than competitors. Key Results: On T2V (SeedVideoBench 2.0), Seedance 2.0 scores 3.75 on motion quality, 3.43 on video prompt following, 3.67 on aesthetics, 3.63 on audio quality, 3.75 on audio-visual sync, and 3.56 on audio prompt following — ranking first on all six dimensions; the next-best competitor (Kling 3.0) scores 3.10/2.78/3.36/2.74/2.78/2.54 respectively. Usability rate on motion quality reaches 97.55% vs. Kling 3.0's 82.82%. Audio quality satisfaction (62.05%) far exceeds competitors who all stay below 10%. On I2V, Seedance 2.0 scores 3.35–3.70 across six dimensions, again first on all; Kling 3.0 (nearest competitor) trails by 0.13–0.55 points. On R2V, Seedance 2.0 supports 20 of 22 input modality combinations (competitors top out at 13), scoring 2.50/3.54/3.03/3.24/2.52 across five dimensions vs. Kling 3.0's 2.32/3.37/2.37/2.36/1.95. On Arena.AI (as of April 8, 2026), Seedance 2.0 ranks #1 on T2V (Elo 1450±15, 2,702 votes, marked Preliminary) and I2V (1449±11, 6,998 votes, marked Preliminary), ahead of Veo 3.1-audio-1080p (1371/1404) and Sora 2 Pro (1364). The Preliminary status and lower vote counts relative to Veo 3.1 (13,629 T2V votes) and Sora 2 Pro (27,737 T2V votes) mean confidence intervals may widen as more votes accrue. Video extension is Seedance 2.0's weakest R2V task, trailing Veo 3.1 on task following (31.82% vs. 88.89% 3-point rate). Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine contribution is a deployed commercial model with the broadest multi-modal input support (20/22 modalities) among evaluated competitors and measurably stronger audio generation. There is no new method, formulation, dataset release, or algorithmic contribution described; the paper is a capability report, not a research contribution in the traditional sense. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are present. No error bars are reported on SeedVideoBench 2.0 scores. The primary benchmark is designed, administered, and reported by the same team building the model — a direct Goodhart's Law concern. Expert evaluators are described as coming from advertising and game production, but inter-rater reliability, evaluator count, and compensation are not reported. Code, model weights, and training data are not released; the model is accessible via a commercial API (Doubao, Jimeng, Volcano Engine). Arena.AI provides an independent validation signal, but Seedance 2.0's Preliminary label and low vote count relative to longer-established models are a credibility caveat. The realism study result (evaluators distinguishing Seedance 2.0 from real video) is mentioned qualitatively but no quantitative outcome is reported. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — a built and deployed audio-video generation service evaluated by deployment metrics. - Practical: The model is deployed on commercial platforms (Doubao, Jimeng, Volcano Engine) and accessible via API; end-users do not run inference locally, so hardware requirements are not a user concern. - Efficient: Inference compute cost is not disclosed; a Seedance 2.0 Fast variant is mentioned for low-latency scenarios but no latency or throughput numbers are provided. - Effective: Evidence shows the model outperforms all tested commercial competitors on SeedVideoBench 2.0 and achieves Arena.AI rank #1, supporting the claim that it solves the stated generation quality problem better than current alternatives, with the caveat that the primary benchmark is self-evaluated. - Scalable: Output resolution is capped at 720p and clip length at 15 seconds; no discussion of scaling to longer duration, higher resolution, or higher throughput is provided. The work delivers a deployed API service. No software library, dataset, trained model weights, or proof technique is released. Strengths: - Breadth of multi-modal reference support (20/22 input combinations) is genuinely broader than all evaluated competitors and represents a real capability differentiator for production workflows. - The audio generation results — audio quality satisfaction at 62.05% vs. below 10% for all competitors, dual-channel binaural output, strong Chinese dialect and multilingual audio-visual sync — represent a distinctive technical gap over the field that is specific and measurable. - Arena.AI validation with an externally crowd-sourced Elo ranking is a meaningful independent signal, mitigating some self-evaluation concerns. - SeedVideoBench 2.0's fine-grained subcategory breakdown (30 motion categories, 17 audio categories, etc.) is more diagnostic than coarse aggregate metrics used in earlier video generation reports. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge remaining failure modes: minor deformation artifacts, motion plausibility in edge cases, high-frequency visual noise, audio distortion, lip-sync errors in multi-speaker scenes, and low absolute scores on joint image-audio conditioning. They note video extension is Seedance 2.0's weakest R2V task. They explicitly state the model "remains imperfect, with room for improvement." This candor about specific failure modes is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper's most serious gap is total absence of technical content — no architecture, no training recipe, no data curation, no loss functions — making it impossible to learn anything replicable or to attribute improvements to any design decision. The self-designed benchmark as primary evidence, with no external automated metric (FVD, CLIP-score, VBench) reported, means results cannot be cross-checked against published literature. The Arena.AI Preliminary results with 2,702–6,998 votes versus competitors with 10,000–39,000 votes mean the Elo ranking may be unstable; reporting these as equal-weight evidence to the internal benchmark is premature. No human evaluation protocol details (number of raters, IRR scores, prompt sampling strategy) are provided for SeedVideoBench 2.0. The comparison set for R2V excludes Veo 3.1 and Sora 2, the strongest T2V/I2V competitors, without explanation. The Seedance 2.0 Fast variant is mentioned but not evaluated. Bottom Line: Practitioners tracking the video generation frontier will find useful competitive calibration here, but the complete absence of technical disclosure means researchers gain nothing actionable — this is a product announcement dressed as a technical report.

#194 — Incremental (38)

Sema Code: Decoupling AI Coding Agents into Programmable, Embeddable Infrastructure (18 ▲ )

Huacan Wang, Jie Zhou, Ningyan Zhu, Shuo Zhang, Feiyu Chen, Jiarou Wu, Ge Chen, Chen Liu, Wangyi Chen, Xiaofeng Mou, Yi Xu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Systems profile. The chief weakness is zero quantitative evaluation: Rigor (25) is severely limited — no latency, throughput, compression quality, or head-to-head task benchmark against OpenHands/Cursor/Claude Code. Resilience (30) likewise low; many claims (injection detection false-negative rate, compression fidelity) are untested. Novelty (45) is architectural reassembly of known ideas (ALS, FIFO, LSP-style decoupling). Impact (60) is the strongest dimension — working open-source npm library with two production deployments. Composite ≈ 0.15*45 + 0.15*25 + 0.10*45 + 0.15*40 + 0.15*30 + 0.30*60 ≈ 40.
systems Novelty 45 Rigor 25 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 40 Resilience 30 Impact 60
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Sema Code, an open AI coding framework that decouples the core agent engine from all client delivery forms, publishing it as a standalone npm library embeddable by any runtime via WebSocket or gRPC. - Why it's important: Every major AI coding tool (Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot) tightly couples its reasoning engine to a specific delivery form, preventing enterprises from reusing agent capabilities across heterogeneous environments or replacing the underlying model. - How it does it: A three-layer separation architecture (client / core engine / service) combined with eight mechanisms—AsyncLocalStorage-based multi-tenant isolation, FIFO queuing with safe session reconstruction, adaptive context compression, multi-agent collaborative scheduling, interrupt propagation, Todo-based process management, four-layer permission control, and a three-tier MCP/Skill/Plugin ecosystem—collectively expose the agent as a typed event stream that any client can subscribe to. - The good: Two production deployments (a VSCode extension and the SemaClaw multi-channel messaging bot) share an identical Sema Core binary with zero engine modifications, directly validating the central architectural claim that the engine is delivery-agnostic. - The missing: No quantitative benchmarks are reported—no latency numbers, throughput under concurrent load, context compression quality metrics, or comparison against OpenHands or AutoGen on any standard task; validation is entirely qualitative/architectural. - Read it or not: Worth reading for engineers building enterprise AI coding infrastructure or multi-form-factor agent platforms; researchers seeking novel algorithms or empirical results will find nothing here. Problem & Motivation: Enterprises and third-party developers who want to embed advanced AI coding capabilities—multi-step reasoning, autonomous tool use, contextual code understanding—into their own backends, CI/CD pipelines, or messaging bots cannot do so with existing tools. Claude Code's reasoning is inseparable from its CLI layer; Cursor is welded to a VSCode fork; GitHub Copilot is constrained to an editor plugin form factor. This architectural coupling forces each new deployment context to rebuild agent logic from scratch or forego agentic capabilities entirely. The gap is not in algorithmic capability but in software architecture: no existing coding agent is designed to be embedded as a library rather than used as an application. Key Idea: The key insight is that an AI coding agent's reasoning, state management, and tool orchestration logic can be completely separated from any UI or delivery concern by treating the engine as an event emitter—clients subscribe to a typed, heterogeneous event stream (text chunks, tool results, permission requests, completion markers) rather than invoking RPC endpoints, enabling arbitrary client forms to attach without modifying the engine. This is the same architectural principle that made SQLite and the Language Server Protocol widely embeddable. Methodology: Sema Code is implemented as a JavaScript/Node.js npm package (Sema Core) with three layers. The client layer handles rendering and channel routing; the core engine layer contains all reasoning and state logic with no UI code; the service layer wraps external models and tool registries. Eight mechanisms address the engineering challenges of this separation: (1) Multi-tenant isolation uses Node.js AsyncLocalStorage (ALS) to bind each engine instance to a dedicated resource bundle (event bus, state manager, tool orchestrator), propagating isolation automatically across all async continuations without explicit parameter threading. (2) Hierarchical state partitioning divides session state into agent-local state S_local(a_i) = ⟨e_i, H_i, T_i, F_i⟩ (execution status, conversation history, task list, file timestamps) and session-global state S_global (a single edit permission flag g_edit and abort controller C_abort), ensuring sub-agent outputs never pollute the main agent's context while a single cancellation signal propagates to the entire agent tree. (3) A FIFO input queue with semantic batching handles message bursts: standard text messages are merged into unified prompts while slash commands are dispatched individually. Safe session reconstruction uses a staging mechanism—new session IDs are stored as pendingSession, an abort is sent to C_abort, and the cleanup finally block purges S_local before initializing the new session. (4) Adaptive context compression triggers at 75% of the model's context limit L, partitioning history into a compressible H_hist and a preserved H_keep, then prompting the LLM to generate a structured summary H* = argmax_{H'} I(H'; H_hist) subject to |H'|_token ≪ |H_hist|_token. A degradation path performs deterministic truncation at the earliest assistant-boundary message if summarization fails. (5) Multi-agent scheduling supports one-level delegation: sub-agents own fully isolated S_local but share only C_abort. Sub-agents cannot spawn further sub-agents, bounding call depth. Read-only tool batches run concurrently; any write operation serializes the entire batch to prevent race conditions. (6) A four-layer permission system routes operations through L1 (file editing, session-scoped), L2 (shell commands, two-stage deterministic whitelist + LLM injection detection), L3 (Skill invocation), and L4 (MCP operations), each with distinct approval semantics. Critical design decisions: (a) event-driven over RPC—well-justified given the heterogeneous, interleaved output types of agentic tasks; (b) one-level sub-agent depth cap—justified to prevent unbounded context consumption, though it limits task decomposition depth; (c) 75% compression threshold with 8,000-token forward buffer—empirically chosen but not ablated. Key Results: No quantitative benchmarks are reported. Validation is entirely qualitative and architectural. The paper reports two deployments: (1) Sema Code VSCode Extension, published to Open VSX for Windows/macOS/Linux, operating in single-user in-process mode and exercising context compression and background task execution; (2) SemaClaw, a server-side Node.js multi-channel agent platform serving Telegram and Feishu, exercising multi-tenant isolation and FIFO queuing under concurrent multi-user load. The authors observe that neither deployment required any modification to the Sema Core codebase and that the two deployments exercise complementary subsets of the eight mechanisms. No latency, throughput, task success rate, compression quality, or concurrency stress numbers are provided. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The contribution is an architectural decomposition of an AI coding agent into an embeddable engine, not a new algorithm, dataset, or empirical finding. The individual mechanisms (ALS-based isolation, FIFO queuing, LLM-assisted context compression, ReAct-style reasoning-acting alternation) are all applications of known ideas. What is genuinely novel is their systematic co-design as a production-grade embeddable engine and the three-tier MCP/Skill/Plugin ecosystem taxonomy. The architectural claim—that a single engine binary can power fundamentally different product forms—is validated by deployment but not benchmarked. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are present. No error bars, latency measurements, throughput figures, or task success rates are reported. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/midea-ai/sema-code-core and https://github.com/midea-ai/sema-code-vscode-extension. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2604.11045v1, April 13, 2026); it has not undergone peer review. The validation methodology—showing that two deployments share one binary without modification—is internally consistent but cannot be reproduced by a third party without access to the SemaClaw deployment. The LLM-assisted injection detection in L2 is acknowledged to inherit the base model's false-negative rate, but this rate is not measured. Context compression quality (information retention vs. truncation tradeoff) is not evaluated empirically. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: Sema Core runs as a Node.js npm package on standard developer hardware; both reported deployments run on commercially available infrastructure. - Efficient: Adaptive context compression adds one LLM inference round-trip per compression event; the 8,000-token forward buffer and O(1) token counting avoid per-turn overhead, but the cost of LLM-assisted shell injection detection (one extra inference per non-whitelisted command) is not quantified. - Effective: The two deployments show that the architecture supports the claimed use cases, but no task completion rate, error rate, or user study is reported to demonstrate that Sema Code is as effective as monolithic alternatives at actual coding tasks. - Scalable: The authors explicitly acknowledge that the single-process architecture does not address horizontal scaling across multiple Sema Core instances, and that stress testing at hundreds of concurrent users has not been performed. The work delivers a software artifact (npm library + VSCode extension, both open-source). Strengths: - The three-layer separation with ALS-based multi-tenant isolation is a principled and non-trivial engineering solution: ALS propagation across async boundaries is subtle, and the two-tier fallback (instance context → global singleton) handles edge cases cleanly. - The dual-deployment validation is the strongest evidence in the paper: having two production systems with fundamentally different concurrency models (single-user IDE vs. multi-user messaging bot) run identically on one engine binary directly tests the central thesis. - The three-tier ecosystem taxonomy (MCP at infrastructure granularity, Skills at behavior granularity, Plugins at workflow granularity) is a coherent decomposition that avoids the over-privilege problem of a single extension type. - Safe session reconstruction via pendingSession staging and C_abort propagation correctly handles the mid-execution context-switch problem that naive kill-and-restart approaches cannot. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge (Section 6.4): validation covers only two product forms; the architecture's generalization to CI/CD pipelines or Jupyter notebooks is undemonstrated. Production stress testing at hundreds of concurrent users has not been performed. Context compression quality degrades on technical conversations with dense code fragments, and the truncation degradation path sacrifices coherence for availability. WebSocket/gRPC interfaces have not been benchmarked for streaming throughput or error recovery under adversarial network conditions. Horizontal scaling across multiple engine instances introduces state synchronization challenges the current architecture does not address. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper provides no comparison against directly competitive embeddable frameworks. OpenHands is dismissed as targeting research scenarios rather than production, but no head-to-head evaluation—even on a single coding benchmark like SWE-bench—demonstrates that Sema Code delivers competitive task performance, not just architectural cleanliness. The LLM-assisted injection detection in L2 is a security-critical component whose false-negative rate under adversarial prompts is entirely uncharacterized. The context compression mechanism (Equation 4) is stated but its information-retention quality is never measured; a single quantitative comparison of task success before and after compression events would meaningfully strengthen the paper. The one-level sub-agent depth cap is justified by resource concerns but not evaluated: it is unclear whether this limit is binding for real coding tasks. Finally, the paper benchmarks nothing against Claude Code, Cursor, or OpenHands on any standard metric, making it impossible to assess whether the embeddability comes at a performance cost. Bottom Line: Practitioners building multi-form-factor or enterprise-embedded AI coding infrastructure will find the architectural patterns and open-source implementation directly useful; researchers seeking algorithmic novelty or empirical evidence of performance gains over existing agents will find this paper insufficient.
Recommended 2604.11045 PDF
#195 — Incremental (38)

HaCOO: Hashed coordinate sparse tensor Storage for rapid data insertion, access, and updates

aimsciences.org · 5 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Systems profile. Rigor (35) and Resilience (30) pull score down sharply — the paper's central claim (fast insertion/updates) is never benchmarked, and only ALTO is compared (COO/HiCOO/CSF omitted). Completeness (30) poor — no insertion, memory, or scaling measurements despite being core motivation. Novelty (45) — swapping Morton for ALTO encoding in HaCOO is minor. Impact (50) — working public C++ library fills a real niche. Non-Obviousness (35) low. Weighted: 0.15*45 + 0.15*35 + 0.10*35 + 0.15*30 + 0.15*30 + 0.30*50 = 38.
systems Novelty 45 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 30 Resilience 30 Impact 50
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a C++ library (hacoo-c) implementing the Hashed Coordinate (HaCOO) sparse tensor format with ALTO's adaptive bit-encoding and a new parallel MTTKRP algorithm that distributes work by hash buckets. - Why it's important: Dominant sparse tensor formats (COO, HiCOO, CSF, ALTO) are static structures that require full reconstruction when new nonzeros are inserted, blocking streaming, incremental, and online tensor applications. - How it does it: HaCOO stores nonzeros in a separate-chaining hash table keyed by ALTO-encoded linearized indices, achieving amortized O(1) insertion and retrieval while retaining ALTO's bit-efficient index compression; the parallel MTTKRP partitions hash buckets across threads to avoid write conflicts without locks. - The good: HaCOO outperforms ALTO in average MTTKRP wall time on 3 of 4 tested FROSTT tensors (NIPS, UBER, CHICAGO) while also supporting dynamic insertion, demonstrating that hash-table flexibility need not sacrifice throughput on standard benchmarks. - The missing: The paper never benchmarks the claimed core advantage — insertion and update throughput — so the primary motivation for choosing HaCOO over a static format is entirely unvalidated experimentally. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building streaming or incremental tensor pipelines who need a drop-in C++ format; not worth reading if seeking a rigorous systems comparison, since baselines are limited to ALTO and the dynamic-update claim is never measured. Problem & Motivation: Sparse tensor decomposition (particularly CP-ALS via MTTKRP) is a workhorse in text mining, signal processing, neuroscience, and machine learning. Existing high-performance sparse formats — ALTO, HiCOO, CSF — sort and encode nonzeros at construction time, making any post-construction insertion O(log M) to O(M) and often requiring a full rebuild. This is a hard barrier for applications that build tensors incrementally from streams: n-gram sliding windows, sensor feeds, or online social network snapshots. The gap is a format that supports both O(1) dynamic insertion and competitive throughput for downstream tensor algebra. Key Idea: Adopt ALTO's bit-interleaved linearization of multi-dimensional indices as both the compressed storage key and the hash function input, so that HaCOO inherits ALTO's minimal-bit index representation while enabling hash-table-based O(1) lookup and insertion without ever sorting the nonzeros. Methodology: The pipeline has three main components. (1) Encoding: each nonzero's N-dimensional index tuple is linearized via ALTO's bit-mask scheme (allocating ⌈log₂ I_n⌉ bits per mode, interleaved by mode cardinality), producing a single integer key. The original HaCOO used Morton (Z-order) encoding; this work replaces it with ALTO encoding, which requires fewer bits. (2) Storage: keys are hashed with Bob Jenkins' One-at-a-Time hash into a power-of-two-sized separate-chaining hash table; load factor threshold is 0.7, doubling on overflow. Total index cost is M × Σ log₂(I_n) bits plus per-bucket linked-list overhead. (3) Parallel MTTKRP (Algorithm 2): Phase 1 distributes hash buckets across T threads; each thread unpacks ALTO-encoded indices, accumulates into thread-local partial factor matrices without locks (buckets are disjoint). Phase 2 merges partial matrices in parallel by row. Critical design decisions: (a) using ALTO encoding rather than Morton is well-justified by a bit-count argument; (b) bucket-level work distribution is justified as producing approximately even load due to hashing uniformity, though no empirical load-balance measurement is provided; (c) adopting separate chaining over open addressing is not formally justified — the authors simply inherit the original HaCOO design. Key Results: Experiments run on the Delta supercomputing platform (AMD/Intel CPUs, 16 threads) with four FROSTT tensors: NIPS (3.1M NNZs, 4-mode), UBER (3.3M NNZs, 4-mode), CHICAGO (5.3M NNZs, 4-mode), LBNL (1.7M NNZs, 5-mode, density 4.2×10⁻¹⁴). Metric is average MTTKRP wall time over 5 iterations (warm-up excluded). HaCOO beats ALTO on NIPS (~2.5× faster from the bar chart) and CHICAGO (roughly 1.5–1.7×); is roughly tied on UBER; loses substantially on LBNL (HaCOO ≈0.1s vs. ALTO ≈0.04s). The authors note the LBNL anomaly is surprising because LBNL has the fewest nonzeros but attribute it to low fiber reuse without resolving it. No CPD convergence timings, no insertion/update benchmarks, and no memory footprint measurements are reported despite the paper's stated focus on those properties. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The genuinely novel element is substituting ALTO's encoding into HaCOO to reduce index storage and enable the ALTO bit-manipulation hardware path. The parallel MTTKRP is a standard bucket-partitioned reduction. Both are straightforward engineering applications of known techniques to an existing format; no algorithmic novelty in the theoretical sense. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations isolating the contribution of ALTO encoding versus Morton encoding on performance. No error bars or variance across runs beyond averaging 5 iterations. No significance tests. No memory footprint data. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/pngwen/hacoo-c/tree/FODS-1. The sole baseline is ALTO; COO, HiCOO, and CSF are described in the related work but never benchmarked. The paper's central claim — rapid insertion — is never measured. This is a serious Goodhart's-Law-adjacent concern: the evaluation optimizes for (and reports only) MTTKRP throughput, the benchmark where static formats already shine, rather than insertion throughput, which is HaCOO's raison d'être. Deployability (SYSTEMS profile): - Practical: Runs on standard CPU hardware with OpenMP; tested on a national supercomputing cluster but requires no special hardware beyond multicore CPUs. - Efficient: Index storage is asymptotically identical to ALTO (M × Σ log₂ I_n bits) with small per-bucket linked-list overhead; MTTKRP throughput is competitive with ALTO on 3 of 4 tensors but 2–3× slower on LBNL for unexplained reasons. - Effective: Demonstrates MTTKRP parity or superiority over ALTO; does not demonstrate effective dynamic insertion — the key claim is unmeasured. - Scalable: Tested only at the 1.7M–5.3M NNZ scale with 16 threads; no strong or weak scaling experiments across thread counts or tensor sizes. The paper delivers a working C++ library with a public repository. Strengths: - Cleanly identifies and closes a real gap: ALTO's excellent performance was available only in a static format; HaCOO-c makes it available with O(1) insertion semantics. - The decision to adopt ALTO encoding over Morton is well-motivated: the bit-count formula S_ALTO = M × Σ log₂(I_n) is derived explicitly and compared to Morton cost. - Public code release enables reproducibility and adoption. - The two-phase parallel MTTKRP (bucket-local accumulation + row-parallel merge) avoids atomic operations without resorting to fine-grained locking, a sound design. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge that HaCOO underperforms ALTO on LBNL and identify this as a surprise, though they offer only a speculative explanation tied to fiber-reuse patterns. They list future work as: investigating the LBNL bottleneck, optimizing factor matrix handling, and adding non-negative decomposition. They do not acknowledge the absence of insertion benchmarks as a limitation. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most glaring omission is that a paper motivated entirely by insertion/update speed never measures insertion or update throughput — there is no comparison of time-to-build a tensor incrementally in HaCOO versus batch-build in ALTO or COO. Second, the evaluation compares only against ALTO; omitting COO and HiCOO baselines makes it impossible to judge whether ALTO encoding is the driver of HaCOO's wins or whether even plain COO would match on those tensors. Third, there are no thread-scaling experiments (only 16-thread results), so parallel efficiency is uncharacterized. Fourth, no memory footprint measurements are reported despite the paper's storage-efficiency claims. Fifth, the separate-chaining choice (versus open addressing, which would be more cache-friendly) is not justified. Bottom Line: A practitioner building a streaming or incremental tensor pipeline will find HaCOO-c the only publicly available C++ format that offers both ALTO-class MTTKRP throughput and O(1) insertion, but must accept on faith that the insertion advantage actually materializes — this paper never measures it.
Citations
#196 — Incremental (38)

Surface vs. Structural Similarity: Aligning Computational Similarity Metrics with Human Judgments

scitepress.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (30) is the dominant drag — circularity in stimulus selection (ViT/SBERT used to engineer the very similarity gradient then correlated with ratings), no inter-rater reliability, no cross-validation of regression weights, no code/data release, synthetic-only stimuli. Resilience (35) collapses on the same circularity. Novelty (45) — applying a known cognitive distinction to off-the-shelf embeddings. Completeness (40) — single domain, single language, no real-world stimuli. Composite ≈ 38.
empirical Novelty 45 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 40 Resilience 35 Impact 35
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper measures how well ViT image embeddings and SBERT text embeddings, used as cosine-similarity proxies for surface and structural similarity respectively, correlate with human similarity ratings of fictional book cover designs and synopses. - Why it's important: Recommender and search systems use vector similarity as a proxy for human preference, yet whether these metrics track the cognitively distinct surface (perceptual) and structural (relational) dimensions of similarity—rather than a single undifferentiated score—has not been empirically verified. - How it does it: 105 crowdsourced Japanese annotators rated 432 synthetic book-pair stimuli (generated by ChatGPT-5) on separate 1–7 Likert scales for cover-design similarity and synopsis similarity; Pearson and Spearman correlations map each computational metric to the corresponding rating dimension. - The good: All four metric–judgment correlations are statistically significant after Bonferroni correction, and the composite cover metric (Title SBERT + ViT) reaches r = .672, a practically meaningful alignment for a noisy crowdsourcing task. - The missing: The stimuli were synthetically generated and pre-filtered using the same metrics being evaluated (ViT and SBERT), creating a circularity that inflates apparent alignment; no real books are tested, and no cross-validation or held-out evaluation is performed. - Read it or not: Worth skimming for HCI researchers thinking about how to annotate multidimensional similarity, but the methodological circularity and narrow scope (one domain, one language, synthetic stimuli) limit confidence in the headline claims. Problem & Motivation: Search, discovery, and recommendation systems in HCI rely on cosine similarity over vector embeddings to find "similar" items. Prior work validating these metrics against human judgments has used a single overall similarity rating, ignoring the cognitive distinction between surface similarity (perceptual attributes: color, layout, shape) and structural similarity (relational structure: causal chains, role correspondences). Without separate validation at each level, it is unclear which cognitive dimension a given metric captures, which can lead to mismatched recommendations and reduced user trust. The gap the paper addresses is the lack of empirically grounded, two-level annotation that separately measures alignment of computational metrics with surface versus structural human similarity judgments. Key Idea: By operationalizing surface similarity as book-cover features (ViT image embeddings + SBERT title embeddings) and structural similarity as synopsis content (SBERT synopsis embeddings), and by collecting separate human ratings for each dimension on the same stimulus pairs, the paper tests whether transformer-based similarity metrics are selectively aligned with the corresponding human cognitive dimension rather than with a single pooled judgment. Methodology: The study follows a three-phase procedure. (1) Stimulus creation: ChatGPT-5 generated 798 fictional book entries (title ~15 chars, synopsis ~100 chars), clustered into 9 groups via k-means on SBERT embeddings; 108 stimuli were selected to span both high and low ViT/SBERT similarity ranges, yielding 432 within-set pairs from 12 sets of 9. Cover images were generated in Canva/ChatGPT-5. (2) Annotation: 119 crowdsourced native-Japanese annotators (CrowdWorks) rated each pair on a 1–7 Likert scale separately for cover-design similarity (surface) and synopsis similarity (structural); 105 passed a consistency screen (mean absolute difference on repeated trials ≤ mean + 2 SD). (3) Analysis: Per-pair mean ratings were correlated with ViT cosine similarity, SBERT title cosine similarity, SBERT synopsis cosine similarity, and a composite surface metric (normalized weighted sum of z-standardized title-SBERT and ViT scores, weights from a regression). Two-tailed Pearson and Spearman correlations were computed; confidence intervals via bootstrap percentile; p-values Bonferroni-corrected across four metrics. No latent-variable model, no multilevel modeling—straightforward bivariate correlation. Critical design decisions: (a) using the same embedding models in stimulus construction and in evaluation—not justified as appropriate; (b) treating mean annotator rating as the target without inter-rater reliability analysis beyond the consistency screen; (c) confining evaluation to within-set pairs, which constrains the range of similarities observed. Key Results: ViT vs. human cover ratings: r = .605, ρ = .570 (p_adj < .001). Composite surface (Title SBERT + ViT) vs. human cover ratings: r = .672, ρ = .645 (p_adj < .001). SBERT synopsis vs. human synopsis ratings (structural): r = .533, ρ = .527 (p_adj < .001). SBERT title vs. human cover ratings: r = .372, ρ = .358 (p_adj < .001). N = 432 pairs throughout. Surface metrics outperform structural metrics, which the authors attribute to the alignment between ViT's global visual processing and human rapid scene perception. No baselines beyond the correlations themselves are reported. The finding that SBERT title similarity alone achieves only r = .372 for surface judgment is a notable finding, suggesting textual surface cues are weaker than visual ones. Scale tested: toy (108 synthetic books, single domain, one language). Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The surface-structural distinction from cognitive psychology is applied to evaluate existing transformer embeddings in an HCI context. The metrics themselves (ViT, SBERT) and the cognitive framework (Gentner's structure-mapping) are established; the contribution is the two-level annotation design and the empirical confirmation that the metrics are differentially aligned—standard techniques applied to a new validation question. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are reported; the composite surface metric's regression weights are not reported numerically. No inter-rater reliability (ICC or Krippendorff's α) is reported—only a within-subject consistency screen. Error bars on correlations are mentioned (bootstrap CIs) but not displayed in the text. No code or data are released (not stated). The most significant methodological concern is circularity: stimuli were constructed by running ViT and SBERT to ensure high/low similarity coverage, then human ratings are correlated with the same ViT and SBERT scores. This inflates correlations because stimulus selection was guided by these metrics, making it unsurprising that humans perceive the engineered similarity gradient. The study also uses exclusively synthetic, AI-generated stimuli rather than real books, limiting ecological validity. No cross-validation of the composite metric's regression weights is performed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The method requires only off-the-shelf ViT and SBERT models and a crowdsourcing platform; commercially available. - Efficient: Embedding 108 items with ViT and SBERT is negligible compute; the human annotation cost (119 annotators × 180 minutes) is the bottleneck. - Effective: The evidence shows correlations in the moderate-to-strong range (r = .53–.67) on synthetic stimuli, but whether this holds for real books with authentic cover art and full-length synopses is not demonstrated. - Scalable: Tested on 108 items and 432 pairs; no analysis of whether correlations hold at larger scale, different domains, or different embedding models. The authors acknowledge this as future work. The work delivers no software artifact, trained model, or dataset—it stops at paper form. Strengths: - The two-level annotation design is methodologically cleaner than prior single-score evaluations; separating surface and structural ratings on the same item pairs is a genuine design contribution. - The finding that surface metrics correlate more strongly than structural metrics (r = .67 vs. .53) is interpretable and connects coherently to known limitations of bag-of-sentence-vector approaches for relational structure. - The Bonferroni-corrected significance testing and bootstrapped CIs reflect appropriate multiple-comparisons awareness for this type of correlation study. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge two limitations: (1) participants are limited to native Japanese speakers, raising questions about cross-cultural generalizability; (2) future work should employ cross-validation to assess predictive performance across different datasets. Both are noted only briefly at the end and are framed as future directions rather than current threats to validity. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) Circularity: using ViT and SBERT to construct stimuli with engineered similarity ranges, then measuring ViT and SBERT correlation with human ratings on those stimuli, is circular—the high correlations partly reflect the metric's own influence on what the annotators saw. (2) Ecological validity: exclusively synthetic, AI-generated books with short ~100-character synopses may not reflect how humans judge real-world items with full narrative synopses and professional cover art. (3) No inter-rater reliability measure (ICC, Krippendorff's α) is reported; the consistency screen is not a substitute. (4) The composite surface metric regression weights are derived from the same data on which the composite correlation is reported—this is overfitting without cross-validation. (5) Only one domain (books), one language (Japanese), and one crowdsourcing pool are studied; the claim to provide "a foundation for HCI interfaces" is an extrapolation beyond the evidence. (6) The paper does not test whether surface and structural dimensions are empirically orthogonal in human ratings—if they correlate, the separation is less meaningful. Bottom Line: A practitioner curious about two-level similarity annotation will find the design idea useful, but the circularity in stimulus construction, absence of real-world stimuli, and lack of cross-validation mean the specific correlation values should not be taken as reliable benchmarks for deploying these metrics in production HCI systems.
Search Results
#197 — Incremental (38)

NovoExpert-1: State-of-the-Art CYP2D6 Prediction via Message-Passing Neural Networks on the TDC ADMET Benchmark

chemrxiv.org · 5 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (25) low — combination of existing components (CatBoost, MapLight, pretrained GIN, Chemprop v2); the MapLight+GIN+CatBoost recipe already existed. Rigor (45) mixed — correct TDC protocol and 5-seed variance reported, but no internal ablation isolating GIN contribution, two SOTA margins within per-seed σ, single-author preprint with commercial COI, no peer review, no independent reproduction. Non-Obviousness (35) low — endpoint-dependent model selection is engineering intuition. Completeness (55) — honest limitations and metric self-correction are positives. Resilience (40) — thin margins on DILI/CYP3A4 Substrate collapse under scrutiny. Impact (55) — models and code released under MIT, directly usable. Weighted: 0.20*25 + 0.25*45 + 0.15*35 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*55 = 40.
empirical Novelty 25 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 55 Resilience 40 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces NovoExpert-2, a dual-method ADMET prediction system that combines CatBoost on 2,873-dimensional MapLight+GIN molecular features with Chemprop v2 D-MPNN as a secondary method, achieving top-of-leaderboard performance on 5 of 22 TDC ADMET benchmark endpoints. - Why it's important: CYP2D6 and CYP3A4 together metabolize the majority of marketed drugs, so improved computational inhibition/substrate prediction directly reduces drug-drug interaction failures and false-positive flags in lead optimization. - How it does it: Four established fingerprint types (ECFP, Avalon, ErG, RDKit 2D) are concatenated with pretrained frozen GIN supervised-masking embeddings into a 2,873-dimensional vector fed to a 5-seed CatBoost ensemble, with Chemprop v2 evaluated in parallel and the stronger method selected per endpoint. - The good: All five SOTA improvements hold across all 5 random seeds (CYP2D6 per-seed AUPRC range 0.771–0.776, all above the prior SOTA of 0.750), demonstrating that the CYP gains are robust to initialization rather than lucky draws. - The missing: No ablations isolate the contribution of the GIN embeddings versus MapLight-only features versus ensemble averaging, leaving the incremental value of each component unquantified. - Read it or not: Worth reading for cheminformatics practitioners who benchmark on TDC ADMET or care about CYP metabolism prediction; the metric correction for NovoExpert-1 (AUROC mistakenly compared against AUPRC) is a genuinely useful community disclosure, but those seeking architectural novelty will find little. Problem & Motivation Drug discovery practitioners need accurate in silico ADMET prediction to prioritize compounds early and reduce clinical attrition. The TDC ADMET benchmark provides standardized train/test splits across 22 endpoints spanning absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, and toxicity, enabling rigorous cross-method comparison via a public leaderboard. A specific gap motivating this work is that prior fingerprint-based SOTA (MapLight+GNN) had not been evaluated with fully correct per-endpoint metrics: the CYP Veith endpoints require AUPRC (not AUROC) because the class imbalance is severe (16.9% positive rate for CYP2D6), making AUROC systematically overoptimistic in the precision-recall trade-off that matters for screening. Additionally, no prior public implementation had systematically compared fingerprint-based gradient-boosted trees and end-to-end D-MPNNs across all 22 endpoints under identical evaluation conditions. Key Idea The core insight is that different ADMET phenotypes are structurally matched to different molecular representations: substructure-driven endpoints like CYP metabolism are well-served by fixed fingerprints plus gradient-boosted trees (which handle tabular features efficiently and resist overfitting under class imbalance), while mechanism-complex endpoints like DILI—arising from reactive metabolite formation, mitochondrial dysfunction, BSEP inhibition, and immune responses—benefit from end-to-end learned representations that discover task-specific global molecular features. The practical realization is to train both families under identical TDC protocol and select per-endpoint rather than committing to a single universal representation. Methodology The pipeline has four stages. (1) Feature engineering: four fingerprint descriptors are concatenated—Morgan ECFP radius-2 (1024 bits), Avalon (1024 bits), ErG 2D pharmacophore (315 dims), RDKit 2D molecular descriptors (~210 dims)—forming the 2,573-dim MapLight vector, then augmented with 300-dim GIN supervised-masking embeddings extracted from the DGL-LifeSci pretrained model (frozen, not fine-tuned) via mean-pooling of node-level representations, yielding a 2,873-dim feature vector. (2) Primary model: a CatBoost classifier (AUPRC or AUROC early stopping as matched to endpoint) or regressor (MAE or RMSE) with unified hyperparameters across all 22 endpoints (depth=8, LR=0.03, L2=5, max 2,000 iterations, early stopping at 200 rounds, class imbalance corrected via scale_pos_weight = n_neg/n_pos). Five seeds (0–4) generate an ensemble; final score is mean of 5 per-seed test scores. (3) Secondary model: Chemprop v2 D-MPNN with default configuration evaluated on classification endpoints only, also 5-seed ensemble. (4) Selection: for each endpoint, the stronger method's ensemble score is submitted. The evaluation follows TDC's official harness exactly (tdc.benchmark_group.admet_group, official splits, per-endpoint metrics). Critical design decisions: (a) unified hyperparameters across endpoints—justified as demonstrating generalization but explicitly acknowledged to leave performance on the table for small-dataset regression tasks; (b) GIN embeddings used frozen, consistent with the prior MapLight+GNN work; (c) per-endpoint method selection—introduces potential test-set selection bias, partially mitigated by reporting both methods' scores and noting the DILI margin (+0.016 over CatBoost) exceeds typical test-set noise for a 96-sample classification set. Key Results NovoExpert-2 exceeds published TDC leaderboard SOTA on 5 of 22 endpoints: CYP2D6 Veith AUPRC 0.778 vs. 0.750 (+0.028, σ=0.0020, all 5 seeds above prior SOTA); CYP3A4 Veith AUPRC 0.916 vs. 0.900 (+0.016, per-seed range 0.9141–0.9159, σ=0.0007); CYP3A4 Substrate Carbonmangels AUROC 0.660 vs. 0.656 (+0.004, σ=0.0121, 534 training examples); Clearance Hepatocyte AZ Spearman 0.511 vs. 0.487 (+0.024, σ=0.0076); DILI AUROC 0.922 vs. 0.916 (+0.006) via Chemprop v2 ensemble (CatBoost ensemble: 0.906, below SOTA). Fails to reach SOTA on 17/22 endpoints; largest gaps are VDss Lombardo (−0.157 Spearman, 1,130 examples), Half Life Obach (−0.135, 667 examples), and Bioavailability Ma (−0.097, 640 examples)—all small-dataset regression tasks where specialized architectures likely have an advantage. The DILI (+0.006) and CYP3A4 Substrate (+0.004) margins are within the per-seed standard deviation of the respective methods (σ=0.017 and σ=0.012), which the paper does not formally address with significance testing. Baselines are the TDC leaderboard SOTA values as of April 2026; per-endpoint SOTA method identity is not provided. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. CatBoost, MapLight fingerprints, GIN embeddings, and Chemprop v2 are all existing methods; the dual-method selection strategy is a practical engineering choice, not a new algorithm. The genuine novelties are: (1) an empirical finding that end-to-end D-MPNN outperforms fingerprint-based GBT on DILI despite only 475 training examples, suggesting mechanism-complex toxicity phenotypes invert the usual small-data advantage of fixed features; (2) a metric correction demonstrating that AUROC and AUPRC diverge substantially on imbalanced CYP datasets (0.864 AUROC ≈ 0.64 AUPRC for the same predictions); (3) a reference implementation of MapLight+GIN against the full 22-endpoint leaderboard with correct per-endpoint metrics. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations isolate the contribution of GIN embeddings vs. MapLight-only features vs. the 5-seed ensemble averaging—the incremental value of the GIN component is not quantified in this work (the ablation exists in the prior MapLight+GNN paper, which is cited but not reproduced here). Standard deviations across 5 seeds are reported throughout, providing variance estimates, but no formal significance tests are applied. Code, trained CatBoost models (.cbm format, 5 seeds per endpoint), prediction files, and a reproduction script (run_benchmark_v2.py) are publicly released at github.com/quantnexusai/novoexpert1-tdc-benchmark under MIT license—reproducibility is well-supported. Key methodological concern: results are self-reported by a single author with a commercial conflict of interest (founder of NovoQuant Nexus, which develops the NovoExpert suite); the paper itself flags that independent reproduction would strengthen confidence. The DILI and CYP3A4 Substrate margins are not robustly above seed-level noise. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on standard CPU hardware; requires RDKit, DGL-LifeSci, and CatBoost—all open-source, no GPU required for inference. - Efficient: Feature computation (fingerprints + one frozen GIN forward pass) is lightweight; CatBoost training is fast relative to neural baselines; no wall-clock times or memory footprints are reported. - Effective: Genuine improvement on 3 CYP endpoints with cross-seed consistency; DILI and CYP3A4 Substrate improvements are within seed variance and should be treated as tentative. - Scalable: Not evaluated—TDC datasets range from ~500 to ~12,000 training examples; performance on large-scale virtual screening libraries is untested. Delivers trained models and a reproduction script; a software library or CLI is not provided. Strengths - Strict adherence to TDC official evaluation protocol with correct per-endpoint metrics corrects a systematic error prevalent in prior leaderboard submissions, including the authors' own prior work—this is a concrete methodological contribution. - The metric correction disclosure (NovoExpert-1 CYP2D6 AUROC 0.864 misrepresented as beating a AUPRC-based leaderboard; corrected AUPRC ~0.64, below SOTA) is scientifically honest and prevents the community from building on a spurious benchmark claim. - The empirical observation that Chemprop v2 outperforms fingerprint+CatBoost on DILI despite only 475 training examples—counter to conventional small-data wisdom—is a non-obvious and practically relevant finding that motivates end-to-end representations for mechanism-heterogeneous toxicity endpoints. - Full public release of models, predictions, and code under MIT license makes the results independently verifiable. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge four limitations: (1) per-endpoint method selection between CatBoost and Chemprop introduces modest test-set selection bias; (2) unified hyperparameters across all 22 endpoints likely leave performance on the table, especially for small-dataset regression tasks; (3) leaderboard SOTA values retrieved April 2026 may be superseded; (4) results are internally generated and independent reproduction would strengthen confidence. The acknowledgment of the conflict of interest (NovoQuant Nexus founder) is present in the Conflicts of Interest section but is not elaborated in the limitations. The authors confront these limitations quantitatively and directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The GIN embedding contribution is not ablated in this work: MapLight-only vs. MapLight+GIN performance differences on the winning endpoints are not reported, leaving it unclear how much of the CYP SOTA improvement derives from the GIN component versus from the CatBoost implementation and 5-seed ensembling alone. - The DILI (+0.006) and CYP3A4 Substrate (+0.004) SOTA margins are smaller than the respective per-seed standard deviations (σ=0.017 and σ=0.012); no bootstrap confidence intervals or permutation tests are reported to determine whether these margins are distinguishable from noise. - The paper does not identify which methods hold SOTA on the 17 endpoints where NovoExpert-2 underperforms, making it impossible to assess whether those baselines use comparable feature engineering or fundamentally different architectures. - On 15 of 17 underperforming endpoints, the gaps are attributed only qualitatively to dataset size or phenotype complexity with no empirical investigation—a systematic feature importance or error analysis would clarify actionable next steps. - The Chemprop v2 configuration details (learning rate, depth, dropout, number of message-passing steps) are not reported, making the Chemprop results harder to reproduce precisely. Bottom Line Practitioners benchmarking on TDC ADMET and those working on CYP metabolism prediction will find the protocol implementation, metric correction, and released models useful, but the method is an incremental combination of existing tools, the two thinnest SOTA margins (DILI, CYP3A4 Substrate) sit within seed variance, and results come from a commercially interested single-author preprint without independent reproduction.
Citations
#198 — Incremental (38)

NovoExpert-2: State-of-the-Art ADMET Prediction via Gradient-Boosted Trees on MapLight Fingerprints and GIN Embeddings

chemrxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Empirical profile; this summary describes the same work as L002 and receives the same assessment. Novelty (25) low — assembly of existing components. Rigor (45) — correct TDC metrics and 5-seed σ, but no in-paper GIN ablation, single-author preprint, commercial COI, two SOTA margins within seed noise. Non-Obviousness (35). Completeness (55) — metric self-correction for NovoExpert-1 is a credibility positive. Resilience (40) — thin DILI/CYP3A4 Substrate margins. Impact (55) — full model and code release. Weighted composite: 0.20*25 + 0.25*45 + 0.15*35 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*55 = 40.
empirical Novelty 25 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 55 Resilience 40 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents NovoExpert-2, a dual-method pipeline that combines CatBoost on 2,873-dimensional MapLight fingerprint + pretrained GIN embeddings with Chemprop v2 D-MPNN as a secondary method, achieving new top scores on 5 of 22 TDC ADMET benchmark endpoints. - Why it's important: Accurate in silico ADMET prediction reduces costly late-stage drug attrition; CYP2D6 and CYP3A4 together metabolize the majority of marketed drugs, so even modest precision improvements in inhibitor/substrate classification reduce false positives sent to expensive in vitro validation. - How it does it: For each endpoint the stronger of two independently trained model families is selected: CatBoost on concatenated molecular fingerprints plus frozen pretrained GIN node embeddings, or Chemprop v2 directed message-passing neural networks trained end-to-end. - The good: All 5 per-seed scores on the headline CYP2D6 endpoint individually exceed the prior leaderboard SOTA (σ = 0.0020), indicating the +0.028 AUPRC improvement is robust to initialization variance rather than a lucky seed. - The missing: No ablation within this paper isolates the incremental contribution of GIN embeddings over MapLight-only fingerprints; the claimed benefits rely entirely on ablations from the prior MapLight+GNN paper, which used a different boosting implementation. - Read it or not: Worth reading for cheminformatics practitioners who track the TDC leaderboard and need an honest reference implementation with correct per-endpoint metrics, but not for those seeking methodological novelty. Problem & Motivation: Drug discovery pipelines routinely fail late in development due to ADMET liabilities (poor absorption, toxicity, metabolic instability) that were not predicted early enough. The Therapeutics Data Commons (TDC) ADMET benchmark provides 22 standardized endpoints with fixed train/test splits and a public leaderboard, enabling direct cross-method comparison. The specific gap this work addresses is metric misalignment on the TDC leaderboard: prior submissions (including the authors' own NovoExpert-1) reported AUROC on CYP Veith endpoints where TDC mandates AUPRC, inflating apparent performance on imbalanced datasets (CYP2D6 Veith: 16.9% positive rate). A secondary gap is that no single publicly available implementation evaluates the full MapLight+GIN+CatBoost family rigorously across all 22 endpoints with correct metrics. Key Idea: The core insight is that different endpoint phenotypes respond to different molecular representations: local substructure-driven tasks (CYP metabolism) are well-served by chemically interpretable fingerprints plus complementary GIN graph embeddings passed to gradient-boosted trees, while mechanistically complex toxicity phenotypes (drug-induced liver injury) benefit from end-to-end learned atom representations via message-passing. Selecting per-endpoint between these two model families exploits their complementary strengths rather than committing to a single architecture. Methodology: Feature engineering concatenates five descriptors into a 2,873-dimensional vector: Morgan ECFP radius-2 (1,024 bits), Avalon fingerprint (1,024 bits), ErG 2D pharmacophore (315 dims), RDKit 2D descriptors (~210 dims), and 300-dimensional GIN supervised masking embeddings extracted from the DGL-LifeSci pretrained model zoo via mean-pooling of node-level representations. A CatBoost classifier or regressor (depth 8, 2,000 max iterations, learning rate 0.03, L2 leaf regularization 5, early stopping 200 rounds on validation fold) is trained per endpoint using a 5-seed ensemble with seeds 0–4 and class imbalance handled via scale_pos_weight = n_neg/n_pos. Separately, Chemprop v2 D-MPNN is run with fixed hyperparameters across all classification endpoints. Evaluation follows TDC protocol exactly: official train/test splits, 5-seed train/validation splits from the TDC API, mean of per-seed test scores reported, with the endpoint-specific metric (AUPRC, AUROC, MAE, or Spearman). For each endpoint the stronger ensemble is reported. Critical design decisions: (1) identical CatBoost hyperparameters across all 22 endpoints to demonstrate generalization rather than endpoint-specific tuning — reasonable for a reference implementation but leaves performance on the table; (2) per-endpoint model family selection post hoc, which introduces a modest test-set selection bias the authors acknowledge; (3) pretrained GIN embeddings kept frozen, meaning the GIN is not fine-tuned on ADMET labels — a practical simplification that may underuse the graph model's capacity. Key Results: Across all 22 TDC ADMET endpoints evaluated with official metrics (Table 3), NovoExpert-2 exceeds published leaderboard SOTA on 5 endpoints: CYP2D6 Veith (AUPRC 0.778 vs. 0.750, +0.028, CB+MG), CYP3A4 Veith (AUPRC 0.916 vs. 0.900, +0.016, CB+MG), CYP3A4 Substrate Carbonmangels (AUROC 0.660 vs. 0.656, +0.004, CB+MG), Clearance Hepatocyte AZ (Spearman 0.511 vs. 0.487, +0.024, CB+MG), and DILI (AUROC 0.922 vs. 0.916, +0.006, Chemprop v2). It falls below SOTA on 17/22 endpoints; largest gaps are VDss Lombardo (−0.157 Spearman), Half Life Obach (−0.135 Spearman), and Bioavailability Ma (−0.097 AUROC), all small-dataset regression tasks. The CYP2D6 ensemble AUPRC of 0.7776 is achieved with σ = 0.0020 across seeds, making the improvement robust. For DILI, Chemprop v2 ensemble (0.9217 AUROC) beats CatBoost+MapLight+GIN ensemble (0.9057) by +0.016 on the 96-sample test set; per-seed Chemprop σ = 0.0170 versus CatBoost σ = 0.009, reflecting the fragility of end-to-end training on 475 examples. The CYP3A4 Substrate improvement (+0.004) on 534 training examples has σ = 0.0121 per-seed, making it the least robust of the five SOTA claims. Baselines are the current published TDC leaderboard SOTA as of April 2026; no comparison to individual competitor methods beyond their leaderboard aggregates is presented. Novelty Classification: ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The contribution is not a new method: CatBoost, MapLight fingerprints, DGL-LifeSci pretrained GIN embeddings, and Chemprop v2 are all existing components, and MapLight+GNN (Notwell & Wood, 2023) already established the fingerprint+GIN+CatBoost combination. What is novel relative to the literature is: (1) the dual-method per-endpoint selection strategy applied at benchmark scale with disclosed selection bias analysis; (2) the empirical finding that Chemprop v2 outperforms fingerprint methods on DILI specifically, suggesting a task-complexity heuristic for model selection; and (3) the metric correction for the prior NovoExpert-1 CYP2D6 result, which is a noteworthy act of scientific self-correction. Rigor & Reproducibility: The evaluation protocol follows TDC exactly (5 seeds, official splits, per-seed standard deviation reported). Standard deviations across seeds are reported for all five SOTA endpoints. No ablation is presented within this paper to show the marginal contribution of GIN embeddings over MapLight-only or CatBoost over other boosting implementations; the authors cite the MapLight+GNN paper's ablations instead. Code, trained CatBoost models (.cbm files for all 5 seeds × 22 endpoints), prediction files, and a reproduction script (run_benchmark_v2.py) are publicly released at https://github.com/quantnexusai/novoexpert1-tdc-benchmark under MIT license. The author explicitly acknowledges that all results are self-generated with no independent reproduction, which is a material limitation for a single-author preprint from a commercial entity with direct financial interest in the NovoExpert suite. This is a preprint; it has not been peer reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL — the paper trains and evaluates models on standard ADMET datasets. - Practical: Yes; CatBoost on 2,873-dimensional fingerprint vectors is trivially runnable on a laptop CPU; GIN embedding extraction requires a GPU-capable machine but is a one-time preprocessing step. - Efficient: Lower compute than graph neural network fine-tuning; CatBoost training is minutes per endpoint; Chemprop v2 training on 475 DILI examples is also fast. No wall-clock timing is reported. - Effective: Evidence on 5 of 22 endpoints supports efficacy over the current leaderboard SOTA; on 17 endpoints the method does not improve over published best. Effectiveness is partial and endpoint-dependent. - Scalable: The method is already tested on TDC's largest datasets (~12,000 compounds for CYP Veith). No stress-scale test beyond TDC datasets is claimed or needed for the stated use case. The work delivers a software artifact: trained CatBoost models, prediction files, and a reproduction script. Strengths: - Honest and transparent metric self-correction for NovoExpert-1; explicitly acknowledges that the prior claim of beating SOTA was wrong due to AUROC/AUPRC confusion on an imbalanced dataset — a credibility-positive act rarely seen in benchmark papers. - Full open release of code, 110 trained model files (5 seeds × 22 endpoints), and prediction files enables exact reproduction without re-running training. - The empirical finding that Chemprop v2 outperforms fingerprint+GBT on DILI and the mechanistic explanation (multi-pathway hepatotoxicity requires global learned representations) provides actionable guidance for practitioners choosing between model families. - CYP2D6 improvement of +0.028 AUPRC is robust across all 5 individual seeds, not a cherry-picked ensemble artifact. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - Per-endpoint model family selection introduces test-set selection bias; authors quantify the bias potential and partially mitigate by disclosing both method scores. - Single unified CatBoost hyperparameter configuration across all 22 endpoints was deliberately chosen to show generalization, with the cost of leaving gaps on small-dataset regression tasks. - Leaderboard SOTA values retrieved in April 2026 may change as new submissions arrive. - Results are internally validated only; independent reproduction would strengthen confidence. The authors state this directly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Single author from a company with commercial interest in the NovoExpert brand; no independent co-author or external validation. This is not a disqualifier, but the combination of commercial conflict, self-reported results, and no peer review warrants caution. - The DILI SOTA claim (+0.006 AUROC, ensemble 0.9217 vs. 0.916) rests on a 96-sample test set where the Chemprop per-seed σ = 0.017; +0.006 is within one-third of a standard deviation of the per-seed distribution, making the ensemble improvement sensitive to how prediction averaging is computed. The authors correctly note that ensemble averaging provides a +0.015 lift over the per-seed mean, but the final margin above published SOTA is thin. - No ablation within this paper isolates GIN contribution; relying on a different codebase's ablations is insufficient when the CatBoost hyperparameters and implementation details differ. - CYP3A4 Substrate improvement (+0.004 AUROC) has per-seed σ = 0.012 and is on 534 training examples; the claimed improvement is within one-third of the per-seed standard deviation, not clearly above noise. - The dual-method selection heuristic (pick the stronger of two families on test) is presented as a finding, but the paper does not establish a validation-set criterion for selecting the method before test evaluation, which would be required in a deployment setting. - No comparison to individual top-leaderboard methods (e.g., ADMETlab 3.0, ADMET-AI) beyond the leaderboard aggregate SOTA number. Bottom Line: A practitioner tracking the TDC leaderboard gains a clean, fully reproducible reference implementation with correct metrics and an honest prior-work correction, but the methodological contribution is incremental assembly of known components, and on 17 of 22 endpoints the method trails published best.
Citations
#199 — Incremental (37)

A hybrid self-evolving edge-AI framework for scalable digital process twins in enterprise systems

tandfonline.com · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (30) drags the composite down — no released code, no artifact, and private testbed data prevents external adoption. Rigor (45) is mixed: five-run statistics and Wilcoxon tests are strong, but no per-module ablation isolates which of the five components (FEDA/MR-STCIN/Q-ERO/CL-DTCL) actually drives gains. Novelty (40) and Non-Obviousness (35) are limited — the architecture is an integration of well-established techniques with 'quantum-inspired' marketing over standard annealing. Resilience (35) is low since a reviewer cannot determine what to adopt without component-level evidence. Weighted: 0.15*40+0.15*45+0.10*35+0.15*40+0.15*35+0.30*30 ≈ 37.
systems Novelty 40 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 40 Resilience 35 Impact 30
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a five-module edge-AI architecture — HNSVG (neuro-symbolic compliance verification), FEDA (federated evolutionary drift adaptation), MR-STCIN (multi-resolution causal inference), Q-ERO (quantum-inspired reinforcement optimization), and CL-DTCL (blockchain consistency ledger) — for self-evolving digital process twins in enterprise IoT environments. - Why it's important: Industrial digital twins deployed at the edge fail under concept drift and cross-layer inconsistency, causing compliance breaches and delayed fault response in smart manufacturing and logistics; the paper targets these operational failure modes directly. - How it does it: The core trick is replacing standard federated aggregation with evolutionary (genetic crossover/mutation) local model updates driven by per-node drift metrics, then feeding causal impact maps from multi-resolution wavelet decomposition into a quantum-annealing-style policy optimizer, all logged in a hashed, validator-quorum ledger. - The good: Fault detection lead time of 49 min (±4 min) versus 24–34 min for baselines on CMAPSS, with false-alarm rate 3.8% versus 7.5–9.3%, represents the single most credible and practically significant reported gain. - The missing: No per-module ablation isolates which of the five components drives performance — only a two-row "HNSVG-layer only" vs. "full model" comparison appears, leaving the individual contribution of FEDA, MR-STCIN, Q-ERO, and CL-DTCL unmeasured. - Read it or not: Skip unless you are specifically surveying neuro-symbolic or federated-evolutionary approaches for edge digital twins; the breadth of the architecture is not matched by the depth of the evaluation, and the private testbed data prevents reproduction. Problem & Motivation: Enterprise digital process twins in smart manufacturing, logistics, and supply chains must operate at the edge where cloud round-trips are too slow, concept drift degrades models in hours, symbolic compliance rules must be enforced continuously, and cross-layer (device-edge-cloud) state consistency is legally and operationally critical. Centralised cloud pipelines incur bandwidth and latency costs; purely neural approaches are opaque and fail compliance audits; symbolic-only engines cannot adapt to distribution shift; and existing federated learning frameworks use static aggregation that reacts slowly to drift. The paper targets this intersection of adaptability, interpretability, and tamper-proof auditability. Key Idea: The central insight is that concept drift adaptation inside a federated system can be dramatically accelerated by replacing gradient-only aggregation with genetic search — each edge node maintains a local model population, applies crossover and mutation proportional to its drift metric, and the global aggregator selects the fittest variants — allowing localized model evolution before global synchronization rather than waiting for gradient convergence. Methodology: The pipeline has seven stages: (1) IoT sensor streams are encoded into 64–128 dimensional latent representations via a feature encoder. (2) A dynamic graph G_t=(V_t,E_t) is constructed from node embeddings, and HNSVG enforces semantic constraints by minimizing a rule-violation energy L_sym = ∫Σ_c λ_c·max(0, f_c(h(t)))dt alongside graph-attention updates (Eqs. 1–4). (3) FEDA detects distributional drift via KL divergence spikes and triggers genetic mutation/crossover on local model parameters θ_k, aggregating globally via a reliability-weighted loss integral (Eqs. 5–7). (4) MR-STCIN decomposes multimodal inputs with Morlet wavelets, estimates causal parent sets by minimizing a second-derivative causal loss L_causal(a), and fuses multi-scale effects via Bayesian integration (Eqs. 8–10). (5) Q-ERO evolves a state-action distribution π_θ(s,a) using a quantum-annealing-inspired diffusion term (ℏ²m/2Va²)·π_θ added to the policy gradient, yielding low-energy convergence without quantum hardware (Eqs. 11–13). (6) CL-DTCL hashes each state transition σ_t via h_t=H(σ_t‖h_{t-1}) and enforces service-level agreements through smart-contract penalty accumulation (Eqs. 14–16). (7) A global joint objective L minimizes all sub-losses simultaneously over graph, policy, and ledger parameters (Eq. 17). Critical design decisions: (a) using Morlet wavelets with Bayesian scale priors p(a)∝a⁻¹ for causal estimation — justified by literature but not ablated; (b) capping communication at 5 MB per federated cycle to simulate edge bandwidth — reasonable but arbitrarily chosen; (c) using a permissioned blockchain with 10 validators and 500 ms blocks — no justification for these hyperparameters. Key Results: Evaluated on NASA CMAPSS (turbofan degradation), IBM Order Management & Supply Chain, and UCI Gas Sensor Array Drift datasets, deployed on quad-core ARM (4 GB RAM) and NVIDIA Jetson Xavier (10 W). Against three baselines — Y. Zhang et al. (2025) deep-learning verifier, Erceylan et al. (2025) FedAvg, Guo et al.
