(2025) Granger causality optimizer — the proposed system achieves: compliance accuracy 98.6% (±0.4%) vs. 91.2–93.5%; edge inference latency 12.3 ms (±0.6) vs. 19.7–25.1 ms; adaptation time 4.2 min (±0.3) vs. 5.9–7.4 min; F1 score 0.93–0.94 vs. 0.76–0.82; fault detection lead time 49 min (±4) vs. 24–34 min; false alarm rate 3.8% vs. 7.5–9.3%; policy convergence 5.5 h vs. 7.8–9.3 h; ledger violation <0.5% vs. 2.4–3.3%. Statistical validation uses paired t-tests (p<0.01) and ANOVA (F>20) over five independent runs. The fault detection lead time gain (49 min vs. 30 min best baseline) is the most striking result but depends on gradual degradation modes — the paper explicitly excludes catastrophic failures. Baselines are all from 2025, plausible but not community-standard benchmarks. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW METHOD (integration-level). The genuine novelty is the specific combination of evolutionary federated learning with neuro-symbolic compliance verification and multi-resolution causal inference in a unified edge architecture; each individual component (graph attention, genetic algorithms in federated learning, wavelet causal inference, policy gradient RL with annealing, blockchain ledgers) is a well-established technique applied to a new integrated setting. The "quantum-inspired" label is marketing — Eq. 11 is standard simulated annealing with a diffusion term, requiring no quantum hardware. Rigor & Reproducibility: Five independent runs with ±0.4–0.6% standard deviations are reported, and both t-tests and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests are applied — this is stronger than typical in this subfield. A two-row ablation (HNSVG-only vs. full model) appears in Table 2, but no ablation isolates FEDA, MR-STCIN, Q-ERO, or CL-DTCL independently, so the marginal contribution of each module is unknown. The evaluation dataset mix includes "private testbed data" alongside public datasets; neither code nor model weights are released and no repository is mentioned. The IBM Supply Chain and UCI Gas Sensor datasets are public, but the preprocessing pipeline producing the 64–128 feature space and the private industrial trace (72-h automobile assembly line) cannot be reproduced. The paper was developed and evaluated entirely on the authors' own testbed, creating a risk that hyperparameters are tuned to these specific conditions. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — a built architecture evaluated via deployment on real edge hardware. - Practical: Runs on commercially available ARM quad-core (4 GB RAM) and NVIDIA Jetson Xavier at 10 W, confirmed by measured latency and convergence times. - Efficient: 12.3 ms inference latency and 10 W power consumption are competitive for edge deployment; FEDA communication is capped at 5 MB per cycle; however, MR-STCIN's superlinear complexity is acknowledged as a scaling concern for million-sensor deployments. - Effective: The evidence shows compliance accuracy, fault lead time, and false-alarm rate improvements on three datasets under sensor noise and concept drift, but the private testbed component limits confidence; performance on catastrophic (not gradual) fault modes is explicitly untested. - Scalable: The paper claims 10k+ transactions/s for the ledger and tests up to 1,200-node graphs, but does not test HNSVG beyond 1,200 nodes or FEDA beyond 20 model variants; city-scale or million-sensor deployment is claimed as future work, not demonstrated. No software artifact is released. Strengths: - The fault detection lead time improvement (49 min vs. 30 min, with half the false-alarm rate) is substantial and directly operationally meaningful — earlier intervention prevents equipment damage in petrochemical and energy contexts. - Running five independent trials with both parametric (t-test) and non-parametric (Wilcoxon) significance testing, with ANOVA F>20, is more rigorous than most applied systems papers in this venue. - Deployment on real constrained hardware (ARM, Jetson Xavier at 10 W) rather than GPU servers grounds the latency and power claims in realistic edge conditions. - The explicit scenario boundary conditions in Section 4.3 (catastrophic failures, multi-sensor failures exceeding node capacity, network partitions) represent unusual and credible self-limitation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) potential memory issues in extended deployments beyond 72-h testing windows; (2) domain expertise required for HNSVG rule adaptation; (3) MR-STCIN superlinear complexity limiting million-sensor deployments without pruning; (4) CL-DTCL consensus overhead scaling with validator count; (5) HNSVG's 1.7% non-compliance margin under novel fault signatures outside training distribution; (6) FEDA's adaptation window insufficient for catastrophic sub-second failures. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most critical unacknowledged weakness is the complete absence of per-module ablation — there is no experiment establishing which of the five components is load-bearing for any given metric, so a reviewer cannot determine whether FEDA alone or MR-STCIN alone would achieve most of the gain. The "quantum-inspired" label is never justified relative to classical simulated annealing or standard natural gradient methods, and no comparison against those baselines is provided. The private testbed data component makes compliance accuracy and latency results non-reproducible by external parties. The baselines (all 2025) are not community-standard leaderboard entries; stronger prior systems (e.g., FedProx variants, established causal RL benchmarks) are absent. The 49-min fault lead time advantage applies only to gradual degradation following detectable precursor patterns within the system's temporal resolution — this is a significant scope restriction that receives only one paragraph of acknowledgment. Bottom Line: A practitioner building edge digital twin infrastructure for compliance-critical manufacturing will find the fault detection and latency numbers motivating, but the lack of module-level ablations and the unreproducible private testbed data make it impossible to determine which components to adopt; a stronger follow-up with open code and proper ablations would be needed before implementation.
Search Results
#200 — Incremental (35)

Digital Technologies for Steelmaking

sustainsteel.ac.uk · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Theoretical profile (survey/position). Novelty (30) limited — synthesis of established I4.0 and neuro-symbolic ideas, bibliometric characterization is the only empirical contribution. Rigor (40) — Scopus query documented but selection criteria undisclosed, non-peer-reviewed deliverable. Non-Obv (30) low, Completeness (45) honest limitation acknowledgment, Resilience (40), Impact (30) — scoping document deferring implementation. Weighted: ~34.
theoretical Novelty 30 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 30 Completeness 45 Resilience 40 Impact 30
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This report surveys the digital technology landscape for smart steelmaking, covering Industry 4.0 reference architectures (RAMI 4.0, AAS), ML applications and their limitations, and the emerging Hybrid/Neuro-symbolic AI paradigm as the path toward explainable, context-aware manufacturing intelligence. - Why it's important: Steel production accounts for approximately 11% of global CO2 emissions (3.5 billion tonnes in 2024), and the transition to net-zero smart manufacturing requires interoperable, explainable AI systems that current pure-ML approaches structurally cannot deliver due to black-box opacity and context-unawareness. - How it does it: Synthesizes three bodies of literature — I4.0 reference architectures with focus on RAMI 4.0 and Asset Administration Shells, a Scopus-derived survey of 123 ML review papers on steel (2020–2025), and a curated review of 10 Hybrid AI papers combining ontologies with ML — to construct an argument for semantics-augmented ML in steelmaking. - The good: Structured comparison tables (SAAS survey Tables 3–4 mapping 13 papers by domain/information model/communication model; Hybrid AI Table 6 summarizing 10 papers by ML model and semantic reasoning approach) provide a concise reference map for researchers entering this intersection. - The missing: No quantitative comparison of hybrid versus pure-ML performance on any steelmaking task; the report identifies the gap and names a research agenda but does not evaluate or rank candidate approaches. - Read it or not: Worth reading for engineers or early-career researchers needing a structured entry point into the I4.0/AAS/AI steelmaking space; offers limited new insight for those already familiar with RAMI 4.0 standards and neuro-symbolic AI literature. Problem & Motivation Modern steelmaking plants operate under fragmented IEC-62264 hierarchical architectures (ERP → MES → SCADA → PLC → field device) where adding a new asset requires separate integration at every layer, each with a distinct namespace. This fragmentation imposes compounding integration costs and prevents the unified data access that AI-driven process optimization requires. Concurrently, ML has been widely deployed for process control, quality prediction, and fault detection across BOF, EAF, continuous casting, and rolling operations, but these models are context-unaware (they cannot incorporate operational conditions or domain knowledge not present in training data), opaque (black-box deep networks resist explanation to process engineers and regulators), and brittle to data distribution shifts in dynamic industrial environments. The UK steel industry faces the additional constraint of decarbonizing to net-zero by 2050 while replacing legacy infrastructure. The gap the report addresses is the absence of a unified technical picture of where I4.0 digital infrastructure — specifically the Asset Administration Shell — and AI/ML, specifically hybrid neuro-symbolic approaches, stand for steelmaking, and how these two tracks can be converged. Key Idea The report's central argument is that neither pure I4.0 infrastructure (AAS providing data interoperability without intelligent processing) nor pure statistical ML (pattern recognition without semantic context) is sufficient for the next generation of smart steelmaking. The key synthesis claim is that Neuro-symbolic AI — fusing ontology/knowledge-graph-based semantic reasoning with statistical ML — provides the missing bridge, enabling both explainability (ML rules expressed in SWRL or similar formalisms interpretable by domain experts) and context-awareness (semantic models encoding domain knowledge that ML alone cannot extract from data). This is a synthesis/position claim, not a novel algorithmic contribution; there is no single technical trick to identify. Methodology The report is a structured literature survey organized in two parts. Part 1 (Section 2) covers I4.0 reference architectures: it explains RAMI 4.0's three-axis 3D model (Layers axis: Asset/Integration/Communication/Information/Functional/Business; Lifecycle and Value Stream axis per IEC 62890; Hierarchy Levels axis per IEC-62264/61512), the IIRA framework, then surveys AAS implementations via two existing systematic reviews and four targeted papers, concluding with a sub-survey of 13 Semantic AAS papers. Part 2 (Section 3) uses a Scopus query returning 11,523 total documents and 123 review papers from 2020–2025, from which ~12 representative review surveys are selected and tabulated by steelmaking process, ML technique, and data source. Section 3 then characterizes ML data limitations and interpretability problems, and reviews 10 Hybrid AI papers combining ontologies or SWRL rules with ML classifiers. Critical design decisions: (1) restricting ML coverage to survey papers only — explicitly justified as breadth-first given 11,523+ primary papers, but the selection criterion for the 12 highlighted surveys is not stated; (2) focusing I4.0 coverage on RAMI 4.0 over IIRA/IVRA/CCSA/IMSA — reasonable given European regulatory context relevant to UK steelmakers; (3) including Hybrid AI papers from entirely different domains (Alzheimer's diagnosis, Twitter fake accounts, landslide classification) with steelmaking relevance asserted by analogy — unjustified, as the transferability of these domain-specific semantic ontologies to steelmaking is not demonstrated. Key Results No new empirical results are reported. The survey documents: 123 ML review papers on AI/steel published 2020–2025 (43 in 2024 alone, 26 already by May 2025), concretely characterizing rapid field growth; 12 representative ML review papers covering BOF endpoint prediction, EAF control, continuous casting breakout prediction, surface defect detection, rolling parameter optimization, and structural damage evaluation, spanning 28 to 274 references each; 13 SAAS papers mapped across RDF/OWL/SPARQL/OPC-UA information and communication model combinations; and 10 Hybrid AI papers, of which only Beden et al. [10] (from the same authoring research group) applies a hybrid approach specifically to steelmaking, converting Random Forest rules to SWRL for inference over a cold-rolling knowledge graph. No performance numbers, accuracy figures, or efficiency comparisons are reported for any cited work beyond qualitative characterization. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (meta-level: bibliometric characterization of AI/steel publication growth) + SURVEY/SYNTHESIS. The combination of I4.0 architecture review, steelmaking ML landscape, and Hybrid AI synthesis in a single steelmaking-oriented document is a useful packaging, but each component rests on established ideas: RAMI 4.0 is a 2015 standard, AAS concepts are well-documented in Plattform Industrie 4.0 technical reports, and neuro-symbolic AI is an active but pre-established research direction. The genuine contribution is domain-specific synthesis that frames the research agenda for SUSTAIN Task 20 (Semantic Technologies for Steelmaking). Rigor & Reproducibility No original experiments, no ablations, no error bars, no significance tests. The Scopus query string and document counts are explicitly reported, providing replicability of the bibliometric claims. The selection of ~12 surveys from 123 is not accompanied by inclusion/exclusion criteria, introducing undisclosed selection bias. The Hybrid AI section cites 10 papers with no steelmaking-specific examples beyond the authors' own prior work, which risks presenting a skewed picture of field maturity. No code, data, or model weights are released. The report is not peer-reviewed — it is an EPSRC SUSTAIN project deliverable (funded hub output), which warrants reduced confidence relative to conference or journal publication. Deployability Profile: THEORETICAL — this is a survey/position report with no primary experimental claims. - Sound: The argument that black-box ML lacks explainability and context-awareness in industrial settings is well-supported by cited literature (Rudin [73], Dalzochio et al. [19]); no formal proofs are attempted and no unsupported logical leaps appear. - General: The conclusions about ML limitations and hybrid AI benefits are domain-general; steelmaking-specific applicability is asserted but demonstrated only for cold rolling in Beden et al. [10], a single process in a single prior publication by the same group. - Tight: No formal bounds or quantitative claims are made; the survey is deliberately breadth-first and does not attempt to bound the performance gap between hybrid and pure-ML approaches. - Implementable: The report explicitly defers implementation to SUSTAIN Task 20; it delivers a scoping document and research motivation, not an algorithm, tool, dataset, or trained model. Strengths - The RAMI 4.0 exposition with concrete cold-rolling examples (roll geometry as Asset layer instance, OPC-UA as Communication layer) provides a genuinely accessible entry point for engineers unfamiliar with I4.0 architecture standards, grounding abstract concepts in steelmaking-specific terms. - Tables 3, 4, and 6 collectively map ~35 papers with domain, technique, information model, and steelmaking relevance annotations, providing a navigable reference index that would take a newcomer significant time to assemble independently. - The honest critical framing of ML limitations — data incompleteness, operator free-text fields requiring NLP, sensor noise, cross-system timestamp misalignment, black-box opacity — avoids overselling current capabilities and sets realistic expectations for practitioners. - The Scopus bibliometric data (123 review papers in 5 years, 43 in 2024) is a concrete quantitative characterization of field velocity that contextualizes the urgency of the synthesis. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that a comprehensive ML review "is beyond the scope of this document" and restrict coverage to review papers only. They acknowledge that neuro-symbolic AI "is still in its infancy" and that traversing semantic structures adds real-time latency. They note that semantic models require hand-crafted domain knowledge, increased complexity, and higher computational costs. These are confronted directly rather than minimized — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The Hybrid AI section contains zero steelmaking-specific papers other than Beden et al. [10] (same authoring group); the absence of independent steelmaking hybrid AI work is not flagged explicitly as a finding, though it is the most important signal for a practitioner assessing field maturity. - The logical bridge from "AAS provides data interoperability" to "therefore hybrid AI becomes feasible" is asserted, not argued — the technical mechanism by which AAS-standardized data feeds ontology construction or ML training pipelines is left unspecified. - No coverage of LLM-based approaches for steelmaking despite a March 2026 publication date and the report's own acknowledgment that "LLMs are presenting [solutions to] some of these data challenges" — a significant omission given LLM deployment in industrial settings accelerated sharply in 2024–2025. - As an EPSRC project deliverable produced by the group whose own prior work is the only steelmaking hybrid AI example, the literature selection cannot be considered independent; no conflict-of-interest statement or independent peer review is noted. Bottom Line A practitioner new to the I4.0/AAS/AI steelmaking intersection gains a serviceable orientation map from this report, but an experienced researcher in industrial AI or manufacturing informatics will find the hybrid AI section too domain-agnostic and the synthesis too thin to inform concrete research or deployment decisions.

#201 — Incremental (35)

SemaClaw: A Step Towards General-Purpose Personal AI Agents through Harness Engineering (15 ▲ )

Ningyan Zhu, Huacan Wang, Jie Zhou, Feiyu Chen, Shuo Zhang, Ge Chen, Chen Liu, Jiarou Wu, Wangyi Chen, Xiaofeng Mou, Yi Xu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Systems profile. Authors themselves concede no rigorous empirical validation — Rigor (20) and Resilience (25) are the principal drags; the only benchmark cited is an external LangChain result unrelated to SemaClaw's architecture. Novelty (45) in the DAG-Teams declaration/execution split and stateful harness plugin category, but components are standard. Impact (55) from open-source release and real deployment, though scale and effectiveness unquantified. Composite ≈ 0.15*45 + 0.15*20 + 0.10*50 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*25 + 0.30*55 ≈ 39; rounded down for absent self-evaluation.
systems Novelty 45 Rigor 20 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 45 Resilience 25 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SemaClaw, an open-source multi-agent application framework built on a two-layer architecture (sema-code-core runtime + semaclaw harness) that integrates DAG-based hybrid orchestration, a native human-in-the-loop permission gate, three-tier context management, and a wiki-based personal knowledge infrastructure for general-purpose personal AI agents. - Why it's important: Personal AI agents deployed at scale (hundreds of thousands of users, as with OpenClaw) need controllability, auditability, and cross-session memory that model capability alone cannot provide, and no widely-adopted open-source framework currently addresses these three concerns together in a coherent architecture. - How it does it: The core mechanism is separating dynamic LLM reasoning (which generates a DAG of subtasks with explicit dependencies at declaration time) from deterministic execution (which the DispatchBridge scheduler then drives to completion), so flexibility and debuggability are no longer in tension. - The good: The PermissionBridge and DAG Teams designs are grounded in clearly articulated engineering principles — harness constraints imposed structurally at the runtime level are demonstrably more reliable than constraints imposed through convention — and the system is released as working open-source code. - The missing: SemaClaw presents no quantitative evaluation of its own system on any benchmark; the sole empirical figure cited (LangChain's +13.7 percentage point Terminal Bench 2.0 gain from harness changes) is external third-party data with no direct methodological connection to SemaClaw's design choices. - Read it or not: Worth reading for engineers designing personal AI agent harnesses who want a principled vocabulary and a concrete reference architecture; skip if seeking empirical evidence that these designs improve task completion or reduce failure rates relative to alternatives. Problem & Motivation: Open-source personal AI agents deployed at real scale expose three engineering gaps that model capability alone does not close. First, real-world tasks require dynamic yet structured orchestration — neither purely declarative workflow graphs (which cannot adapt at runtime) nor unconstrained agentic reasoning (which lacks execution traceability) handles the full range of user requests. Second, as agents perform consequential operations on files, APIs, and external services, behavioral safety must be enforced at the runtime level, not as application-level configuration bolted on after the fact. Third, users interacting with an agent across weeks or months expect it to accumulate and organize knowledge about their preferences and prior work, but current memory mechanisms remain fundamentally log-oriented: they retrieve past dialogue rather than consolidating it into reusable conceptual structure. Developers building on frameworks like OpenClaw encounter these problems simultaneously, yet existing frameworks (LangGraph, AutoGen, OpenAI Swarm) address them independently or not at all. Key Idea: The single core insight is that the reliability, safety, and long-term usefulness of a personal AI agent are primarily properties of the harness around the model rather than of the model itself — and that building a layered harness where each layer addresses one concern (runtime isolation, context management, permission gating, orchestration structure, knowledge accumulation) allows each concern to be solved correctly without entanglement. The specific technical expression of this is the DAG Teams method: bounding the orchestrator's LLM reasoning to a single declaration step that produces an explicit, validatable dependency graph, after which a deterministic scheduler drives execution. This converts the tradeoff between flexibility and debuggability into a separation of phases rather than a forced choice. Methodology: SemaClaw is organized as two independently released open-source projects. The runtime layer, sema-code-core, implements an event-facade pattern exposing all agent lifecycle transitions (session init, tool invocation, compaction, session termination) as typed events; the application layer, semaclaw, responds to these events to provide channel integration, memory infrastructure, permission gating, and team coordination. Context management operates across three independently governed tiers: working memory (in-context message history, compacted at 75% of the configured context limit using a summarizing model, with a truncation fallback to 50% if compaction fails), external memory (MEMORY.md curated index plus rolling 50-day daily logs, retrieved via three-level degradation: FTS5 BM25 + vector similarity with combined score vec_score×0.7 + fts_score×0.3, falling back to FTS alone, then token keyword scan), and persona partitioning (SOUL.md stable identity injected at session start, separate workspace directory for task-environment context, MEMORY.md cross-session index as intermediate layer). The PermissionBridge intercepts all external tool invocations before execution; internal infrastructure tools are pre-authorized; external tools require per-invocation approval routed through a single bridge instance shared across concurrent sessions and multiplexed by unique request identifiers. Agent teams are declared via create_parent, which produces a directed acyclic graph of subtasks with explicit dependsOn fields; detectCycle() validates the graph at submission time; DispatchBridge polls every 300ms to advance the DAG, dispatching tasks whose dependencies have reached terminal state and injecting full prerequisite context into each worker's prompt at task start. Four scheduled execution modes (pure notification, pure script, pure agent, hybrid script-plus-agent) route scheduled tasks to the appropriate execution primitive to keep token consumption proportional to reasoning work. A wiki-based knowledge infrastructure stores agent-curated findings as plain Markdown files in a user-owned directory tree, accessible via a CLI save/organize interface for the agent and a Web UI for the user, with a dedicated WikiManager.search() retrieval path distinct from conversation memory search. Key Results: SemaClaw reports no quantitative evaluation of its own system. The only empirical figure in the paper is external: LangChain's controlled experiment on Terminal Bench 2.0 showing task completion rising from 52.8% to 66.5% (a 13.7 percentage point gain) by changing only harness configuration while holding the model constant. The authors cite this as supporting evidence for the harness engineering thesis, not as a measurement of SemaClaw. No baselines are compared, no task completion rates are reported, no latency or throughput numbers are given, and no user study is described. The system is deployed and open-source, but the deployment scale and operating conditions are not quantified. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW FORMULATION. The DAG Teams hybrid (LLM-declaration phase + deterministic-execution phase), the PermissionBridge as a native runtime primitive with session-continuity-preserving pause semantics, the three-tier context architecture with independent lifecycle governance, and the stateful harness plugin concept (plugins that mutate the agent's persistent architecture rather than executing within a session) are genuine architectural contributions not found as a coherent package in prior work. The individual components — RAG, ReAct loops, DAG orchestration, wiki knowledge bases — are each established; the novelty is the principled integration into a layered personal-agent harness and the vocabulary introduced to reason about design decisions in this space. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are present. No error bars, seeds, or significance tests appear because no quantitative experiments on SemaClaw are conducted. The system is released as open source at github.com/midea-ai/SemaClaw. The authors explicitly acknowledge: "We have not yet produced rigorous empirical validation of this claim in our own deployment." The hybrid retrieval weighting (0.7 vector, 0.3 FTS) is stated without ablation. The compaction trigger threshold (75%) and fallback behavior are described precisely, but their effect on task continuity is not measured. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2604.11548v1, submitted April 13, 2026); it has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — SemaClaw is a built tool evaluated by description of deployment rather than controlled experiments. - Practical: The system runs on commodity hardware via Python processes and standard LLM API calls; CLI and Web UI are both implemented and described as operational. - Efficient: The four-mode scheduled task system and pre-authorized internal tool tier are explicitly designed to keep token consumption proportional to reasoning work, but no actual token cost measurements are reported for representative workflows. - Effective: The system addresses the stated problems architecturally and is deployed in practice, but no evidence is presented that it actually improves task completion, reduces failures, or out-performs alternatives on any defined task set. - Scalable: The paper does not test or claim behavior at stress scale; the memory architecture (rolling 50-day logs, growing MEMORY.md, expanding wiki corpus) raises unstated questions about retrieval quality degradation after months of use that are not addressed. The work delivers a software artifact (open-source framework, CLI, Web UI) and a vocabulary/framework for harness engineering discussion. Strengths: - The DAG Teams design genuinely resolves a real tension: bounding LLM reasoning to declaration time while enabling dynamic task structure gives both flexibility and a deterministic, inspectable execution state — this is a cleaner separation than any of the three prior models (stateless swarm, pre-specified graph, orchestrator dynamic decision-making) individually achieves. - The PermissionBridge is designed as a first-class runtime primitive with session-continuity preservation (the approval window keeps the session alive; user interaction resets the timeout counter), not as an optional interrupt — this is the right architecture for production human-in-the-loop systems. - Section 4 (Open Questions) is unusually candid: the authors identify five active tensions (virtual vs. persistent agents, harness/model complementarity, memory ownership, stateful plugin design, community infrastructure) and state current positions as working bets rather than solved problems, which is a credibility positive. - The stateful harness plugin concept — distinguishing plugins that operate within a session from plugins that mutate the agent's persistent architecture — identifies a genuinely new design category with different security requirements, not previously articulated in the agent frameworks literature. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly that no rigorous empirical validation of the harness-substitutes-for-model-capability claim has been produced in their own deployment. They acknowledge: compaction quality is inconsistent for general conversational (non-code) workflows; integration with broader communication channels (messaging platforms beyond WeChat/QQ/Feishu/Telegram, email, voice) is incomplete; SemaClaw has not yet fully aligned with Claude Code's upstream extension model, creating potential divergence in permission semantics; the wiki knowledge base operates as a standalone retrieval surface not yet integrated with the main memory search pipeline; and no mechanism exists for detecting or signaling persona drift in SOUL.md documents over time. These are confronted directly, not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The absence of any quantitative self-evaluation is a fundamental gap that the authors acknowledge but do not fully reckon with: citing an external benchmark result (LangChain Terminal Bench 2.0) where the harness changes are not SemaClaw's changes provides no evidence that SemaClaw's specific architectural choices produce the claimed benefits. No comparison against AutoGen, LangGraph, or OpenAI Swarm on any task is provided — a paper positioning itself relative to these systems should measure against them. The hybrid retrieval fusion weight (0.7/0.3) is presented as a design decision with no ablation; this is a tunable parameter whose effect on recall/precision is uncharacterized. The DAG compilation failure rate — how often the orchestrator's LLM fails to produce a valid, cycle-free task graph — is not reported, yet this is the single most important reliability question for the DAG Teams method. The deployment context is primarily Chinese messaging platforms; whether the architecture generalizes to other deployment surfaces is asserted but untested. The compaction quality problem for non-code conversations is acknowledged but not measured: how often does compaction drop load-bearing constraints, and what is the downstream task failure rate when it does? Bottom Line: A practitioner designing personal AI agent infrastructure gains real value from SemaClaw's vocabulary and architecture — the DAG Teams pattern, the PermissionBridge design, and the stateful harness plugin concept are worth studying — but the complete absence of system-level empirical evaluation means this is a well-reasoned design document with a working reference implementation, not a validated systems result.

#202 — Incremental (34)

Concept for a virtual assistant with an LLM-based multi-agent system to process user input in intralogistics

scholar.sun.ac.za · genai manufacturing - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (25) and Rigor (30) pull the composite down: no public release, no baseline comparison against the BERT-based predecessor it motivates against, no latency/cost measurement, and evaluation on a three-item four-station toy scenario makes 100% success weak evidence. Novelty (35) is a composition of established techniques for a narrow domain; Non-Obviousness (40) is modest — the synonym-builder agent is the most distinctive element. Completeness (55) benefits from honest limitation acknowledgment. Composite ≈ 0.15*35 + 0.15*30 + 0.10*40 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*30 + 0.30*25 = 34.
systems Novelty 35 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 55 Resilience 30 Impact 25
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This thesis introduces a concept and working prototype for a virtual assistant (VA) for industrial intralogistics that replaces predefined command logic with a six-agent LLM-based multi-agent system (MAS) capable of interpreting free-form natural language instructions, orchestrating autonomous mobile robot (AMR) transport tasks, and managing a dynamic synonym vocabulary. - Why it's important: Existing intralogistics VAs rely on BERT-based intent classifiers tied to hardcoded downstream logic, forcing operators to use predefined phrasings in environments where terminology, workflows, and system layouts change frequently — creating adoption barriers and operational rigidity. - How it does it: A hierarchical chain of six GPT-4o agents (scope checker → router → robot expert / knowledge expert → robo commander / synonym builder) selectively shares curated context subsets with each agent and enforces JSON schema validation via pydantic before any command reaches the execution layer or physical robot. - The good: The prototype achieved 100% correct output across 710 test prompts (71 requirements × 10 linguistic paraphrases each), including robustness tests with invalid items, infeasible quantities, and out-of-scope requests, in a controlled learning-factory setting with a real DJI RoboMaster EP robot. - The missing: The entire evaluation is conducted on a three-item, four-station toy scenario with no comparison against any baseline VA (e.g., the BERT-based Li et al. 2023 system), no measurement of latency or token cost, and no testing under the noisy or misspelled inputs that characterise real factory floors. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners designing LLM-based agent pipelines for constrained industrial domains — the 6-agent context-engineering design and synonym-growth mechanism are well-documented — but the narrow evaluation and master's-thesis scope mean it should not be cited as empirical evidence of production readiness. Problem & Motivation: Factory workers who operate intralogistics systems (inventory management, AMR dispatch, logistics coordination) cannot interact naturally with current virtual assistants because those systems bind intent classifiers to hardcoded command pathways. BERT-based classifiers require extensive domain-specific fine-tuning, degrade on complex or novel phrasings (~30% error at factory noise levels per Li et al. 2023), and cannot handle multi-step tasks whose subtasks are only implicitly stated. Three overlapping complexities drive the gap: human interaction complexity (varied phrasings, multi-task prompts), system-related complexity (heterogeneous machines and data interfaces), and domain-specific complexity (changing inventory, station layouts, specialized terminology). No published VA for intralogistics combines LLM-based flexible intent interpretation with dynamic multi-step task orchestration and vocabulary extension — a gap confirmed by a 16-paper semi-systematic literature review covering 2021–2025. Key Idea: The central insight is that replacing a monolithic intent-classifier-plus-rules pipeline with a hierarchical chain of narrowly scoped LLM agents — each receiving only the knowledge-base subset it needs — simultaneously solves intent flexibility (LLM reasoning over free-form input), task orchestration (agent specialization for planning vs. command synthesis), and vocabulary drift (a dedicated synonym-builder agent that persistently updates a thesaurus memory file). Selective context injection per agent rather than broadcasting the full knowledge base is the architectural move that makes the system controllable and auditable at scale. Methodology: The study follows Design Science Research (DSR) methodology: stakeholder needs → goals → requirements → concept (artefact) → prototype → evaluation. Thirty-five requirements are derived from four goals (handle domain-specific complexity, system-related complexity, current VA task parity, human interaction complexity). The proposed six-layer VA architecture assigns intent recognition, orchestration, and abstraction to an LLM-based MAS, while interface, execution, and infrastructure layers remain deterministic. The MAS comprises six GPT-4o agents in a hierarchical topology: (1) Scope checker filters out-of-domain inputs and classifies task type; (2) Router decomposes multi-task prompts and routes subtasks to agent 3 (robot operations) or agent 4 (knowledge/synonym); (3) Robot expert synthesizes a multi-stop transport plan consulting domain knowledge, inventory memory, and AMR position; (4) Knowledge expert answers informational queries or forwards synonym requests; (5) Robo commander converts the transport plan into a strictly validated JSON AMR command; (6) Synonym builder generates validated JSON synonym-update commands. Each agent receives a curated subset of the knowledge base (domain description, inventory memory, synonym file, tool schemas, message history) via context engineering. Output validation uses pydantic schema models before any command touches the execution layer. The infrastructure layer stores domain knowledge (static station descriptions), three dynamic memory files (inventory quantities, AMR current position, synonym thesaurus), and formal tool schemas (AMR delivery command, synonym-save command). Capability growth occurs through synonym persistence and clarification-request loops when required transport information is missing. Critical design decisions: (a) hierarchical rather than decentralised agent topology — justified as essential for deterministic command flow in safety-relevant robot control; (b) structured JSON output with pydantic validation before execution — separates probabilistic LLM reasoning from deterministic action; (c) no RAG and no MCP — justified by the small, curated knowledge base that fits directly in agent context windows. The prototype implements GPT-4o via API. The evaluation scenario uses four stations (Warehouse 1: 2,000 screws; Warehouse 2: 2,000 nuts, 2,000 washers; Workstation; Charging Dock) and a DJI RoboMaster EP Core robot with LiDAR-based autonomous navigation. The evaluation followed the FEDS framework (summative, artificial strategy): 71 tests × 10 paraphrase variants = 710 prompts injected automatically via a Python harness; correctness assessed by human evaluator against predefined expected outputs. Key Results: The prototype achieved 100% task completion (710/710 prompts) across all 35 testable requirements. Correct performance was demonstrated on: single- and multi-item inventory queries (T1–T9), AMR location and station overview (T10–T11), transport commands producing correct JSON (T12–T14), capability explanations and synonym management (T15, T52–T60), multi-task inputs combining transport + synonym addition in a single prompt (T49–T51), synonym-only queries after vocabulary extension (T61–T71), and robustness tests rejecting non-existent items/stations and infeasible quantities (T20–T33). Planning-and-reasoning capability was confirmed by the VA autonomously requesting missing quantity information and integrating the clarification into the ongoing task context (T34–T36). No baselines are compared. Latency and token usage were explicitly not recorded. Physical AMR execution was verified during development but not included in the formal evaluation. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The contribution is a domain-specific instantiation of LLM-based MAS architecture for industrial intralogistics VA, including a concrete six-agent design, a synonym-growth memory mechanism, and an integration with physical AMR control via validated JSON. The individual components (LLM agents, context engineering, pydantic validation, tool use) are established techniques; the novelty lies in their composition for intralogistics and in filling a documented gap where no prior system exists. Rigor & Reproducibility: All implementation code (full MAS, interface layer, execution layer, infrastructure layer, evaluation harness, system prompts, AMR command interpreter) is provided in appendices A1–A12 of the thesis, enabling replication. No public repository URL is stated. No ablations are performed — the six-agent configuration is presented as one viable design without comparison to fewer-agent or single-agent alternatives. No error bars or multiple independent runs are reported; the probabilistic GPT-4o model was run once per prompt variant. Human evaluation of output correctness introduces subjective judgment, though expected outputs were predefined. The 100% success rate on a three-item scenario and predefined test prompts constitutes a proof-of-concept, not a statistically robust performance claim. The scenario complexity is low enough that the result is plausible but provides limited signal about real-world robustness. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built tool evaluated by demonstration in a controlled environment). - Practical: Runs on standard cloud API access (GPT-4o) and commodity hardware; the DJI RoboMaster EP robot is a commercial platform. - Efficient: Latency and token cost were explicitly not measured; a six-sequential-agent pipeline with full message history passed at each step will carry non-trivial API costs and latency that remain uncharacterised. - Effective: The system demonstrably handles the three-item, four-station scenario for which it was designed; whether it solves real factory intralogistics tasks with dozens of item types, heterogeneous systems, noisy speech, or adversarial inputs is not shown. - Scalable: Not tested beyond the minimal scenario; the curated in-context knowledge base approach would require redesign (likely RAG) for larger inventories, and no analysis is provided of how agent behaviour degrades with knowledge base growth. The work delivers a working software artefact (complete source in appendix) but no public release. It stops at master's-thesis demonstration form. Strengths: - Thorough requirements engineering: 35 numbered requirements with explicit traceability from stakeholder goals through objectives to tests is unusually rigorous for a VA proof-of-concept and supports systematic coverage. - Well-reasoned agent decomposition: the decision to separate transport planning (agent 3) from JSON command synthesis (agent 5) with an intermediate integrity check is a sound safety design for robot control, and the rationale is clearly articulated. - Synonym-growth mechanism: the persistent, agent-managed thesaurus that allows operators to teach the system domain-specific vocabulary without structural changes is a practically useful and non-obvious design choice. - Full code transparency: providing all implementation code in appendices, including system prompts (appendix A12), enables replication and peer review at a level uncommon in applied AI theses. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: testing in a laboratory (learning factory) environment only; text-only interaction with no speech input; no user profile support; no testing of tolerance to misspellings or noisy language; latency, token usage, and computational cost not assessed; long-term memory persistence not analysed; advanced safety topics (adversarial robustness, bias, prompt injection) not examined; physical AMR motion performance not systematically evaluated. These are confronted directly and honestly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No baseline comparison: the claim that the LLM-based MAS outperforms or is preferable to BERT-based predecessors is motivational, not evidential — the thesis never runs the Li et al. (2023) system on the same test prompts. - Circular evaluation risk: the 71 test cases and their 10 paraphrases were authored by the same researcher who designed the system, in a scenario the system was built for; this risks optimistic coverage selection even with best intentions. - Trivial scenario scale: three inventory items and four stations is below the complexity threshold where LLM reasoning is expected to fail; the 100% success metric is therefore weak evidence of general capability. - GPT-4o dependency: the architecture is described as LLM-agnostic, but the entire evaluation uses a single commercial model; no testing on smaller, locally deployable models that would be preferred in privacy-sensitive factory environments. - No multi-turn conversation stress test: the evaluation uses single-turn or two-turn (clarification) interactions; complex workflows with extended dialogue history are untested. - Security and prompt-injection risks in an industrial control context are acknowledged as out of scope but represent a non-trivial deployment blocker that should at least be scoped. Bottom Line: This master's thesis delivers a well-structured, fully implemented proof-of-concept that usefully maps LLM-based multi-agent architecture onto industrial intralogistics VA design, but the toy-scale evaluation on an author-constructed test suite prevents any strong conclusions about real-world capability — practitioners should treat it as design inspiration rather than validated evidence.
Search Results
#203 — Incremental (32)

A Four Layer Cybersecurity Framework for Digital Twin Systems in the Food Industry

wseas.com · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Rigor (25) low — no named ML baseline, unnamed 'standard ICS tools' comparator, synthetic corpus, no ablations, no released code or weights. Impact (25) low — prototype only, no artifact. Novelty (35) modest engineering integration. Non-Obv (30), Completeness (30), Resilience (30) all weak due to simulation-only evaluation and missing per-component ablations. Weighted: 0.15*35+0.15*25+0.10*30+0.15*30+0.15*30+0.30*25 ≈ 29, rounded to 32 reflecting the per-attack-type breakdown.
systems Novelty 35 Rigor 25 Non-Obv 30 Completeness 30 Resilience 30 Impact 25
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a four-layer cybersecurity framework for food-industry digital twin environments that integrates transformer-based anomaly detection (RoBERTa, DistilBERT, Isolation Forest), Hyperledger Fabric blockchain logging, and PM4Py process mining into a unified architecture evaluated on a simulated dairy-processing plant. - Why it's important: Food-industry industrial control systems rely on legacy protocols (Modbus, OPC Classic) with no native security, making cyber-physical attacks (replay, injection, DoS) capable of causing contamination incidents or supply chain failures. - How it does it: IIoT event logs are semantically encoded as English sentences, fed to fine-tuned transformer classifiers for threat detection, with process mining conformance scores appended as features and blockchain receipts providing tamper-proof audit trails. - The good: Detection rate improvements are broken out per attack type (Replay: 65%→95%, DoS: 70%→96%, Injection: 75%→94%), and response latency is measured under three load levels (200–250 ms), providing more granularity than a single aggregate number. - The missing: There are no named ML-based baselines — the "before" state is described only as "standard ICS tools" — so the claimed 25-percentage-point improvement in detection rate cannot be attributed to the proposed design versus any competent ML baseline. - Read it or not: Skip unless you need a broad survey of how to assemble off-the-shelf components (transformers, blockchain, process mining) into an ICS security stack; the evaluation is too synthetic and the baselines too weak to trust the quantitative claims. Problem & Motivation: Food-industry production lines increasingly deploy digital twin (DT) platforms that synchronize physical sensors (temperature, pH, pressure) with virtual replicas for predictive maintenance and process optimization. These DTs integrate with legacy industrial control systems running Modbus and OPC Classic, which lack authentication and encryption. The resulting attack surface includes replay attacks (falsifying sensor readings), false data injection (distorting pH or temperature values to cause unsafe decisions), and denial-of-service attacks that halt production. Prior cybersecurity approaches for this domain are either standalone ML anomaly detectors that lack data-integrity verification, or blockchain logging solutions that do not provide real-time detection — no unified architecture addresses the IT/OT boundary holistically. Key Idea: Treat IIoT log entries as natural-language sentences (e.g., "At 12:04:21, sensor T123 reported 78.9°C, which is below the normal operating range") and fine-tune transformer classifiers on them for binary threat detection, then augment the classifier's feature vector with process mining conformance metrics derived from a reference workflow model, using blockchain to ensure the logs being classified have not been tampered with. Methodology: The framework is organized into four layers. The Device Layer covers physical sensors/PLCs with TPM/TEE-based secure boot. The Connection Layer handles MQTT and Modbus/TCP communication encrypted via TLS/SSL and VPN tunneling with VLAN segmentation. The Data Layer stores telemetry in TimescaleDB and logs all transactions immutably in Hyperledger Fabric. The Service Layer runs anomaly detection via RoBERTa-base and DistilBERT fine-tuned for binary classification (normal vs. threat) using cross-entropy loss, plus Isolation Forest as a lightweight pre-filter; the PM4Py Inductive Miner derives a reference workflow model from event logs, and trace-fitness scores, mean activity duration deviations, and transition frequency variances are appended as features to the transformer classifiers. All models trained on Google Colab Pro (A100 GPU) with AdamW optimizer (LR = 2×10⁻⁵, batch size 16, dropout 0.1, early stopping patience 3). Training data mixes 200 synthetic dairy-plant logs with reformulated samples from three public benchmarks: SECURE (ICS knowledge extraction), NetEval (5,732 network-operations questions), and SecurityEval (130 code-security samples), split 70/15/15. Total corpus: 20,000 IIoT events (15,000 normal, 5,000 malicious across spoofing, replay, command injection, and DoS). Critical design decisions: (1) semantic log encoding enables transformer generalization beyond rule-based signatures — justified by reference to LLM behavioral modeling but not ablated against pure numerical feature baselines; (2) process mining conformance scores as features — shown to reduce false positives from 7% to 5%, though the mechanism is not ablated; (3) Isolation Forest pre-filter reduces transformer inference load — latency benefit shown but precision impact not isolated. Key Results: On the 3,000-event test set, the full framework achieves 95% attack detection rate vs. a claimed baseline of 70% for "standard ICS tools" (unnamed), 5% false positive rate vs. 15% baseline, and 200 ms average response time vs. 500 ms baseline. Per-attack-type detection: Replay 95%, DoS 96%, Injection 94% (vs. 65%, 70%, 75% for traditional). RoBERTa outperforms DistilBERT and Isolation Forest across ROC-AUC, precision, recall, and F1 (exact values not printed in the paper beyond visual bar charts). Response time varies from 200 ms under low load to 250 ms under high load. A Continuous-Time Markov Chain reliability model and 10,000-trial Monte Carlo simulation demonstrate graceful degradation under increasing attack frequency. Statistical validation is claimed at p < 0.05, but the specific test used is not named. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The individual components — transformer-based log anomaly detection, Hyperledger Fabric immutable logging, and process mining conformance checking — are each well-established; the contribution is their architectural integration targeting food-industry digital twins, plus the specific mechanism of encoding IIoT logs as English sentences to enable transfer from general cybersecurity benchmarks. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablation study isolates the contribution of each layer or component — it is unknown whether blockchain, process mining, or the transformer classifier drives the bulk of the improvement over the unnamed baseline. The "before" baseline is described only as "standard ICS tools" without naming a system, a version, or a configuration, making the 25-point detection rate gain unverifiable. The benchmark datasets (SECURE, NetEval, SecurityEval) are general cybersecurity benchmarks reformulated as IIoT log statements — this domain shift introduces unknown biases and is not validated. The 20,000-event corpus is entirely synthetic (Docker-emulated dairy plant + reformulated benchmark questions), meaning results may not transfer to real production ICS. The paper states p < 0.05 with 95% confidence but does not name the statistical test. No code, dataset, or trained model weights are released. The evaluation is conducted by the same group that built the system on a simulation environment they constructed — no independent validation. Deployability: SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: The inference pipeline runs on an A100 GPU during training; inference at 150 ms/log per RoBERTa is feasible on commercially available server hardware, but the paper does not specify deployment GPU requirements for production. - Efficient: Isolation Forest pre-filtering reduces transformer load, achieving 200 ms end-to-end latency; the blockchain logging overhead (Hyperledger Fabric) is not quantified in terms of throughput or storage cost. - Effective: On the simulated 20,000-event dataset the system reaches 95% detection with 5% false positives, but performance on real production ICS data is not tested and the simulation gap is substantial. - Scalable: Response time degrades only modestly from 200 ms to 250 ms across low-to-high load in simulation; behavior at 10×–100× event rates or across multi-facility deployments is not tested and only claimed in future work. The paper delivers a conceptual architecture and prototype implementation; no software artifact, library, or trained model is publicly released. Strengths: - The semantic log encoding approach (converting IIoT events to English sentences for transformer input) is a pragmatic transfer-learning strategy that allows reuse of pre-trained language model weights without requiring large domain-specific labeled datasets. - The per-attack-type detection rate breakdown (Replay, DoS, Injection) provides more actionable information than a single aggregate metric. - The process mining integration adds a behavioral verification layer orthogonal to statistical anomaly detection — even if the quantified improvement (7%→5% false positives) is modest, the architectural motivation is sound. - The Markov Chain reliability model adds a formal fault-tolerance analysis that is uncommon in applied ICS security papers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note the need for real industrial validation at diverse production facilities and acknowledge that scalability to large-scale deployments has not been tested. They identify energy consumption under resource-constrained environments as an open problem and note that integration with ERP/MES systems remains future work. Limitations are stated in the future work section rather than a dedicated limitations discussion — the authors do not confront them as credibility qualifications on the current results. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most serious gap is the absence of any named ML-based IDS baseline — systems such as DeepLog, LogBERT, or prior transformer-based ICS anomaly detectors exist and are not compared against, making the claim of superiority over "traditional" methods misleading. The 20,000-event simulation corpus is small for fine-tuning transformers, and the mixing of reformulated general cybersecurity benchmark questions with synthetic dairy-plant logs creates a training/evaluation distribution that bears uncertain relationship to real ICS traffic. The paper describes LLMs as a key component but actually uses encoder-only transformers (RoBERTa, DistilBERT) — generative LLM contributions are not demonstrated. The Hyperledger Fabric throughput and storage overhead under sustained production telemetry rates is unaddressed. No confusion matrix or per-class precision/recall numbers are provided — only visual bar charts, preventing independent verification of claimed metrics. Bottom Line: This is an engineering integration paper assembling known components into a food-ICS architecture, but the evaluation on a self-constructed simulation with unnamed baselines and no public artifact makes its quantitative claims unreliable; a practitioner seeking defensible evidence for deploying transformer-based anomaly detection in ICS should look to more rigorously evaluated alternatives.
Search Results
#204 — Incremental (32)

Hardware Utilization and Inference Performance of Edge Object Detection Under Fault Injection

Faezeh Pasandideh, Mehdi Azarafza, Achim Rettberg · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (30) pulled down by the 1,489-image training set with unanchored retention baselines, no per-fault-category breakdown, TensorRT warm-up confounding FPS_Delta, and no external ground-truth validation of hardware sensors. Novelty (40) is modest — the LLM+LDM fault-generation method is from a companion paper; this work is an application to hardware profiling. Impact (40) gets a small lift from the released VisionFault-350K dataset on Zenodo. Non-Obviousness (30) and Resilience (30) low: 14–15% hallucination is unacceptable for safety-critical use yet unanalyzed, and single-platform single-workload limits generalizability. Weighted composite ≈ 33.
empirical Novelty 40 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 30 Completeness 40 Resilience 30 Impact 40
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper characterizes CPU load, GPU utilization, RAM consumption, power draw, throughput, and thermal behavior of TensorRT-optimized YOLOv10s, YOLOv11s, and YOLO2026n pipelines on an NVIDIA Jetson Nano under a large-scale (350K-image) LLM+LDM-synthesized fault injection campaign covering both lane-following and object detection tasks. - Why it's important: Safety-critical edge AI deployments in autonomous systems lack hardware-level reliability profiles under degraded inputs — existing studies treat resource monitoring as a performance metric rather than a fault indicator, leaving a gap that could mask dangerous operating conditions. - How it does it: A two-phase decoupled framework generates synthetic fault scenarios offline (GPT-OSS 120B produces scenario parameters, Stable Diffusion 2.1 synthesizes degraded images) and then drives real TensorRT inference online while recording per-folder hardware and inference deltas against a clean baseline. - The good: All three models maintain GPU utilization at 20–25%, power draw within ~1.5W of baseline, and temperature below thermal-throttling threshold even after processing 200K+ fault-injected images, while detection retention consistently exceeds 89% — establishing a hardware-stability floor under worst-case synthetic visual faults. - The missing: The object detection models were trained on only 1,489 images across diverse classes, making it unclear whether the ≥89% retention figures reflect genuine model robustness or a weak detection baseline; no per-fault-category breakdown of hardware or inference degradation is provided. - Read it or not: Practitioners who deploy YOLO models on Jetson Nano in autonomous robotics and need a hardware-level reliability reference under synthetic sensor degradation should read it; those seeking generalizable insights beyond this platform or fault methodology can safely skip it. Problem & Motivation: Resource-constrained edge platforms deployed in autonomous driving must maintain reliable inference under sensor failures, lighting degradation, and adversarial visual conditions. Existing work evaluates inference pipelines under normal conditions and treats resource monitoring as a performance tuning signal, not a reliability indicator. Fault injection literature focuses on timeout-based or software failure signals without profiling CPU load, GPU utilization, RAM, or power draw during degraded inference. This leaves a gap in understanding whether TensorRT-optimized YOLO pipelines remain within safe operational hardware bounds when inputs are continuously corrupted. Key Idea: Decouple fault scenario generation (offline, at scale, using an LLM for semantic scenario diversity and an LDM for realistic image synthesis) from hardware profiling (online, during real embedded inference) so that thousands of repeatable, categorically diverse fault conditions can be applied to a live edge pipeline while recording per-folder hardware deltas against a clean baseline — turning inference under fault injection into a systematic hardware characterization experiment. Methodology: The pipeline has two phases. Offline: GPT-OSS 120B (via Ollama) is prompted to generate 7,950 valid fault scenario parameter sets from an initial pool of 100 per iteration; Stable Diffusion 2.1 applies each scenario via Img2Img (Denoising Strength controls corruption degree) to real JetBot camera footage, yielding VisionFault-350K — 350,751 fault-augmented images in 335K folders covering fog, lens distortion, motion blur, rain, low light, and other sensor/environmental degradations. Online: three TensorRT engines (YOLOv10s, YOLOv11s, YOLO2026n, all trained at 256×256 for 180 epochs on a Google Colab T4, deployed on NVIDIA Jetson Nano) process each fault folder; hardware metrics (ΔPower, ΔRAM, ΔTemp, GPU%) and inference metrics (Retention%, ΔFPS, Δp95/p99 latency, ΔJitter) are recorded per folder. The experimental platform is a custom JetBot with decoupled perception and navigation subsystems — a critical design choice that isolates DL inference faults from downstream control effects. Critical design decisions: (1) per-folder delta measurement against a fixed baseline run — sound, but assumes hardware state is stationary between baseline and test, which may not hold across long evaluation campaigns; (2) software-only faults to avoid permanent hardware damage — justified but limits coverage of real hardware fault modes; (3) 180 epochs on only 1,489 manually selected training images — not justified given the diversity of object classes (animals, cars, people, traffic signs). Key Results: Power draw increases marginally above baseline (YOLOv10s: +0.04W mean; YOLO2026v2: +1.41W mean; all below 5.2W total), with Gaussian distributions confirming stable TensorRT execution cadence regardless of fault type. RAM delta is consistently negative after warm-up across all models (YOLOv10s: −218.1 MB mean; YOLO2026v2: −168.3 MB mean), indicating the engine recycles buffers after the initial base inference — peak usage stays below 3.0 GB within Jetson Nano's 4 GB ceiling. Thermal rise is bounded (YOLOv10s: +2.93°C mean; YOLO2026v2: +5.94°C mean; maximum observed: 42°C), well below Jetson Nano's throttling threshold. GPU utilization clusters at 20–25% across all three models and both tasks, confirming a hardware-bound rather than workload-bound execution regime. Detection retention: YOLO2026v2 highest at 92.42% (object detection) and 92.36% (lane-following); YOLOv10s lowest at 89.28%; hallucination rates 14–15% across models. Throughput shows a positive FPS_Delta (YOLO2026v2: +1.11 FPS; YOLOv10s: +0.94 FPS), attributed to TensorRT reaching steady state after the initial base load — a confound with fault injection effects. Tail latency (p95) ranges 26–30 ms across models with near-zero jitter delta (≤0.17 ms mean change). Baselines are cross-model comparisons only; no external method is compared. Scale: up to 212,205 images processed per model on a single Jetson Nano. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The genuine novelty is the large-scale, systematic hardware-level characterization of TensorRT inference pipelines under synthetic fault injection on a real embedded platform — this combination has not been previously reported. VisionFault-350K (Zenodo DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18695332) is a concrete reusable artifact. The LLM+LDM fault generation methodology is credited to a companion paper [1] (an AITC 2026 poster); this paper applies it to hardware profiling rather than inventing it. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations by fault category — aggregate histogram distributions hide which fault types most stress hardware or inference. No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals; distributions are shown as histograms only. VisionFault-350K is publicly released on Zenodo, enabling reproducibility. The training dataset (1,489 images) is small for the claimed object class diversity; model quality is not validated against standard benchmarks (MS-COCO, PASCAL VOC), so the retention baseline is unanchored. Positive FPS_Delta is confounded by TensorRT JIT warm-up — longer runs inherently optimize regardless of fault injection, making it impossible to attribute throughput gains to fault characteristics. Hardware monitoring is not validated against independent ground-truth power meters or thermal sensors. This is a preprint (arXiv); not peer-reviewed. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: Runs on NVIDIA Jetson Nano (~$100–150), an actively available embedded GPU board requiring no specialized infrastructure beyond a USB camera and motor driver. - Efficient: GPU utilization 20–25% and total power ≤5.2W confirm substantial headroom for concurrent system processes; all models fit within the 4 GB RAM ceiling with negative delta after warm-up. - Effective: Detection retention >89% under 350K synthetic fault images demonstrates robustness to image-level sensor degradation, but no real hardware faults (bit flips, power glitches, actuator failures) are tested, and hallucination rates of 14–15% are unacceptably high for safety-critical applications and not analyzed. - Scalable: Tested on one Jetson Nano at up to ~212K images; no multi-device fleet testing, no evaluation at higher input resolutions, and no stress testing beyond Jetson Nano's 4 GB memory limit or thermal envelope. The paper delivers the VisionFault-350K dataset (Zenodo), a trained pipeline codebase (implied but not explicitly linked), and hardware characterization tables — stopping short of a deployable reliability certification framework. Strengths: - The scale of fault injection (350K+ images, 7,950 fault types) is substantially larger than typical edge AI robustness studies and spans ecologically valid sensor/environmental degradation categories. - Hardware-level characterization during fault injection fills a documented gap; prior work in the cited literature monitors hardware only under normal operation or focuses exclusively on inference accuracy. - VisionFault-350K is publicly released on Zenodo, making the characterization reproducible and the dataset reusable for future edge AI fault studies. - The decoupled perception/navigation architecture is a sound experimental design that isolates DL inference behavior from navigation control noise, enabling clean causal attribution of hardware effects to perception-layer faults. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: Software-based faults only; no physical hardware damage. Single platform (Jetson Nano); generalizability not claimed. Lane-following uses CV-based HSV tracking (not DL), so the DL model's failures do not propagate to navigation control — this is flagged as a design choice enabling controlled evaluation. The authors note the single-direction traversal constraint (one dominant viewpoint) limits pose variability in the training/test images. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Training on 1,489 images for multi-class detection (animals, cars, people, traffic signs) is underpowered; reported retention rates (89–92%) cannot be interpreted as robustness without an external benchmark anchor showing detection quality under clean inputs. The positive FPS_Delta finding is attributed to TensorRT warm-up optimization but is a confound — extended runs improve throughput regardless of fault injection, and no controlled experiment (equal-length clean run vs. fault run) is presented to disentangle the two effects. Hallucination rates of 14–15% are high and left unanalyzed by fault category, masking which specific fault types cause spurious detections — critical information for safety applications. No per-fault-category hardware breakdown is provided, preventing identification of which fault types are most resource-intensive. The visual quality of LDM-synthesized faults is shown for only one example per category (Fig. 3); no perceptual or distributional validation of synthetic fault realism is performed. The companion paper [1] describing the fault generation framework is cited as a poster presentation, not a full paper, making the methodology lineage difficult to assess. Bottom Line: Practitioners deploying YOLO on Jetson Nano in autonomous mobile robots will find Tables I–IV and the VisionFault-350K dataset immediately useful as hardware-reliability reference data, but the small training set, unanchored retention baselines, and absence of per-fault-category analysis make it difficult to generalize the conclusions beyond this specific platform and scenario set.
Search Results 2604.09631 PDF
#205 — Incremental (32)

Fully Homomorphic Encryption on Llama 3 model for privacy preserving LLM inference

Anes Abdennebi, Nadjia Kara, Laaziz Lahlou · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Systems profile. Rigor (30) badly hurts: no benchmark-level evaluation, no error bars, calibration on same 79 prompts, no code release, non-standard accuracy metric. Novelty (50) for first FHE on contemporary LLM but technique is integration. Completeness (30)—no task accuracy, no comparison to TEEs/MPC. Resilience (25)—single-layer encryption undermines core privacy claim. Impact (40) limited by unvalidated text quality. Systems weights: 0.15*50+0.15*30+0.10*40+0.15*30+0.15*25+0.30*40 ≈ 33.
systems Novelty 50 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 30 Resilience 25 Impact 40
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PQC-LLaMA-3, a modified LLaMA-3 inference pipeline that replaces attention mechanism operations with Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) primitives from the concrete-ml library, producing two variants: one encrypting a single attention layer and one encrypting all layers. - Why it's important: LLM deployments in healthcare, finance, and other sensitive domains expose user inputs, intermediate activations, and model weights to server-side adversaries, and quantum computers threaten existing encryption schemes protecting those pipelines. - How it does it: The attention mechanism's matrix multiplications (QKT and softmax) are replaced with FHE-compatible operations using 2-bit integer quantization and Programmable Bootstrapping (PBS) from the RLWE-based concrete-ml library, with a cleartext calibration pass establishing quantization parameters before FHE circuit compilation. - The good: The SingleHeadQLlamaModel achieves sub-second latency (0.236 s per inference on an i9-14900K) with 98.2% top-k=500 generation fidelity, and uses only 5,208 PBS operations versus 11,622 for a prior FHE GPT-2 implementation. - The missing: The accuracy metric is top-k token matching against the plaintext model's outputs rather than task-level evaluation (perplexity, BLEU, benchmark accuracy), making it impossible to assess whether encrypted inference produces useful text; additionally, the SingleHead variant encrypts only 1 of 32 transformer layers, providing a very narrow security perimeter. - Read it or not: Security researchers and MLSec engineers exploring FHE-LLM feasibility will find the infrastructure details and latency profiling useful, but practitioners needing evidence of task-level accuracy preservation under encryption should look for follow-up work with rigorous evaluation. Problem & Motivation: Organizations deploying LLMs for sensitive workloads (patient records, financial analysis, legal documents) face two overlapping risks: inference-time data exposure to honest-but-curious or compromised servers, and the emerging threat that quantum computers will break the RSA and ECC schemes currently used to protect data in transit. Existing mitigations — input/output sanitization, access control, differential privacy — operate at the application boundary and leave intermediate activations, attention weights, and gradients exposed within the model pipeline. Applying encryption directly to the forward pass would close this gap, but prior work on encrypted ML focused on smaller models (decision trees, small CNNs), and the computational overhead of FHE for large transformer models was considered prohibitive. Key Idea: The core insight is that concrete-ml's Programmable Bootstrapping (PBS) simultaneously resets ciphertext noise and evaluates a non-linear lookup table, making it possible to approximate softmax and activation functions inside the FHE circuit without an exponential noise blowup. By restricting FHE to the attention sub-layer (the most privacy-sensitive component) and using 2-bit integer quantization to reduce ciphertext polynomial dimensions, the authors bring per-token inference latency into the sub-second range on commodity CPUs. Methodology: The pipeline has five stages. (1) Calibration: the modified QLlamaLMHeadModel runs a cleartext forward pass to estimate per-layer scaling factors and zero-points for 2-bit fixed-point quantization, stored in concrete-ml's DualArray structure. (2) FHE circuit compilation: concrete-ml compiles the quantized attention forward pass into a Boolean/arithmetic circuit, statically placing PBS nodes wherever non-linear operations or noise-refresh steps are required. (3) Encryption: the client encrypts input token embeddings using the RLWE public key and sends them with the evaluation key to the server. (4) Encrypted evaluation: the server executes attention (QKT dot-products, softmax approximation, V-projection) homomorphically; remaining 31 layers run in plaintext. (5) Decryption and generation: decrypted integer logits are dequantized and fed to a plaintext softmax for token sampling. Two model variants are evaluated: SingleHeadQLlamaModel (1 of 32 decoder layers encrypted) and MultiHeadsQLlamaModel (all 32 layers encrypted). Three execution modes — disable (no quantization), simulate (quantized, no FHE overhead), execute (full FHE) — allow separating quantization error from homomorphic noise. Quantization is set to 2 bits, selected empirically as the best accuracy/performance trade-off. Ciphertext packing via CRT and SIMD batching amortizes noise and enables parallel matrix operations. Critical design decisions: (a) restricting FHE to the attention layer is justified by the privacy-sensitivity argument but is not formally analyzed; (b) 2-bit quantization is chosen empirically without ablation over other bit widths; (c) softmax replacement with a polynomial approximation is necessary but the approximation quality and its effect on downstream token distributions is not quantified. Key Results: Evaluated on 79 text-completion prompts on three CPU machines (AMD EPYC 7413 24-core M1, Intel i7-12700K M2, Intel i9-14900K M3), averaged over 5 runs per top-k setting. Short-context (1–10 new tokens), SingleHead: accuracy rises from 47.2% (top-k=1) to 81.7% (top-k=10); inference time on M3 ≈ 0.237–0.239 s; compile time ≈ 3.5–8.5 s. Short-context, MultiHead: accuracy 51.6% (top-k=1) to 87.4% (top-k=10); inference ≈ 0.835–1.26 s on M3; compile ≈ 12.3–26.3 s. Long-context (70–500 new tokens), execute mode: SingleHead 91.3%→98.2%, MultiHead 94.7%→97.7%, both on M3 at 0.233–0.276 s and 0.790–2.07 s respectively. Throughput (Appendix A, M3): SingleHead ≈ 81 tokens/s vs. plain LLaMA-3 ≈ 142 tokens/s (1.75× slowdown); MultiHead ≈ 30 tokens/s (4.7× slowdown). On M1, SingleHead counterintuitively outperforms plain LLaMA-3 (35–37 vs. 22–24 tokens/s), attributed to larger L1 cache fitting encrypted ciphertext blocks. FHE efficiency: 5,208 PBS operations for a 6-token sequence (vs. 11,622 for a prior FHE GPT-2 [ref 31]); memory 2.02 KB/token (single head), 1.12 KB/token (multi head). Baselines: comparison is exclusively against plain LLaMA-3 within this paper; no comparison against other privacy-preserving inference systems (secure MPC, TEEs, other FHE-LLM implementations). The accuracy metric is top-k generation matching: the fraction of inference steps where the plaintext model's top-1 next token appears within the encrypted model's top-k predictions. High accuracy at large k (e.g., 98% at top-k=500) is a weak signal — it does not measure whether generated text is coherent or correct on any task benchmark. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The integration of FHE into a production-scale LLM (LLaMA-3 8B) using concrete-ml is a genuine engineering advance beyond prior work limited to smaller models. The proposed FHE-specific metrics (PBS/token, memory/token) and the cache hierarchy analysis of encrypted inference are new empirical contributions. The core FHE theory (RLWE, PBS, BGV/TFHE variants) and the concrete-ml library are entirely existing work; the paper is a targeted application rather than a cryptographic or algorithmic contribution.
